source_article.title: The stimulation by synaptic transmitters of the incorporation of oleate into the phospholipid of synaptic membranes.
source_article.abstract: Noradrenaline stimulated the incorporation of oleate into choline glycerophospholipids of guinea-pig brain synaptic membranes incubated in sodium phosphate buffer. In the presence of 1 mm-NaF, noradrenaline stimulated the incorporation of oleate into the choline glycerophospholipids, phosphatidylinositol, ethanolamine glycerophospholipids, phosphatidylserine and phosphatidic acid of synaptic membranes incubated in 10 mm-Tris-HCl buffer. In Tris-CHl containing 1 mm-NaF, stimulation of incorporation of oleate into choline glycerophospholipids by noradrenaline was enhanced by ATP, CaCl2, MgCl2 and CoA plus dithiothreitol. The optimum concentration of CaCl2 for stimulation by 10 mum-noradrenaline was 10 mum. In the presence of CaCl2, the optimum concentration of ATP-2MgCl2 was in the range 0.1-1 mm. Acetylcholine, carbamoylcholine, 5-hydroxytryptamine, dopamine, histamine and gamma-aminobutyric acid also stimulated the incorporation of oleate into choline glycerophospholipids of synaptic membranes. Sigmoidal dose-response curves were obtained, similar to those obtained previously for stimulation by the same agonists of the hydrolysis of phosphatidylcholine by phospholipase A2 (Gullis & Rowe, 1975a). The initial rate of transfer of oleate from oleoyl-CoA to choline glycerophospholipid was similar to the initial rate of transfer from oleate-albumin, stimulated by noradrenaline. Transfer of oleate from oleoyl-CoA was not appreciably stimulated by noradrenaline, but was stimulated by ATP and MgCl2.

statements.text: Noradrenaline stimulates incorporation of oleate into choline glycerophospholipids of guinea-pig brain synaptic membranes.
statements.support_summary: One abstract directly reports that noradrenaline stimulated incorporation of added oleate into position 2 of choline glycerophospholipid in brain synaptic membranes, which is closely aligned with the claim, although the abstract does not explicitly restate guinea-pig origin in that sentence (PMID: 173287). However, another cited abstract from the same research area states that previously reported stimulation by 10 micromolar noradrenaline of the phospholipase A2-acylation system in isolated guinea-pig synaptic membranes was not reproducible, which conflicts with its placement in the support set and weakens the overall supporting case (PMID: 195582). The duplicate entry for PMID: 195582 does not add independent support and instead reinforces this discrepancy. Overall, support rests mainly on a single direct abstract, with an important internal conflict from a later non-replication report.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. Notably, one study placed in the support group actually reports that earlier findings of noradrenaline-stimulated phospholipase A2-acylation activity in guinea-pig synaptic membranes were not reproducible, so there is implicit contradictory evidence within the provided support set (PMID: 195582).
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly supported overall. A single abstract directly supports noradrenaline-stimulated oleate incorporation (PMID: 173287), but this is substantially undermined by a later abstract reporting that the relevant noradrenaline stimulation was not reproducible (PMID: 195582).

statements.text: In the presence of sodium fluoride, noradrenaline stimulates incorporation of oleate into multiple phospholipid classes of guinea-pig brain synaptic membranes, including choline glycerophospholipids, phosphatidylinositol, ethanolamine glycerophospholipids, phosphatidylserine, and phosphatidic acid.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Several synaptic transmitters, including acetylcholine, carbamoylcholine, 5-hydroxytryptamine, dopamine, histamine, and gamma-aminobutyric acid, also stimulate incorporation of oleate into choline glycerophospholipids of guinea-pig brain synaptic membranes.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that cyclic nucleotides and adenosine stimulate oleate incorporation into choline glycerophospholipids of brain synaptic membranes, and it also states that adenosine, guanosine, ADP, and 5'-AMP inhibited the stimulation by noradrenaline, acetylcholine, 5-hydroxytryptamine, dopamine, and histamine (PMID: 173287). This abstract therefore actually implies that several transmitters such as acetylcholine, 5-hydroxytryptamine, dopamine, and histamine do stimulate oleate incorporation, which conflicts with its placement in the contradicting group. However, the abstract does not mention carbamoylcholine or gamma-aminobutyric acid, so it does not fully support the entire claim as stated (PMID: 173287).
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear but leans slightly away from contradiction because the only cited abstract appears misclassified: it suggests transmitter-stimulated oleate incorporation rather than refuting it. Still, the claim is only partially addressed because carbamoylcholine and gamma-aminobutyric acid are not mentioned in the available evidence.

matched_article.title: Conversion of [U-14C]threonine into 14C-labelled amino acids in the brain of thiamin-deficient rats.
matched_article.abstract: In confirmation of the findings of Gaitonde et al. (1974), a decrease in the brain concentration of threonine and serine, and an increase in glycine, were observed in rats maintained on a thiamin-deficient diet. Similar changes were found in the blood, and the concentration of several other amino acids in the blood decreased significantly. There was a correlation between the concentrations of threonine, serine, aspartate and asparagine in the brain and blood. In experiments in which [U-14C]threonine was injected into rats most of the radioactivity in the brain and blood of control rats was, as expected, in threonine in the acid soluble metabolites. In contrast, a considerable proportion of radioactivity was also found in other amino acids, namely glutamate, glutamine, aspartate, gamma-aminobutyrate and alanine, in the brain of thiamin-deficient rats. [U-14C]Threonine was also converted into 14C-labelled lactate and glucose, but the extent of this conversion was severalfold higher in thiamin-deficient than in control rats. This finding gave evidence of the stimulation in thiamin-deficient rats of the catabolism of [U-14C]threonine to [14C]lactate by the aminoacetone pathway catalysed by threonine dehydrogenase, and into succinate via propionate by the alpha-oxobutyrate pathway catalysed by threonine dehydratase (deaminase). The measurement of specific radioactivities of glutamate, aspartate and glutamine after injection of [U-14C]threonine, indicated a stimulation of the activities of threonine dehydrogenase and threonine dehydratase (deaminase) in the brain of thiamin-deficient rats. The specific radioactivities of glutamate, asparatate and glutamine int he brain were consistent with an alteration in the metabolism of threonine, mainly in the 'large' compartment of the brain of thiamin-deficient rats. The measurement of relative specific radioactivity of proteins after injection of [U-14C]threonine indicated a marked decrease in the synthesis of proteins, mainly in the liver of thiamin-deficient rats.

statements.text: Thiamin-deficient rats show decreased brain threonine and serine concentrations and increased brain glycine concentration, with similar amino acid changes in blood.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: After [U-14C]threonine administration, thiamin-deficient rats convert threonine to other labeled amino acids and to labeled lactate and glucose in the brain more extensively than control rats, indicating stimulated threonine catabolism in the brain.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.

statements.text: Thiamin deficiency is associated with decreased protein synthesis, mainly in the liver, after [U-14C]threonine administration.

source_article.title: Bone graft substitutes: What are the options?
source_article.abstract: Currently, a number of bone grafting materials are available in the clinical setting to enhance bone regeneration, varying from autologous bone to several bone graft substitutes. Although autologous bone remains the "gold standard" for stimulating bone repair and regeneration, the morbidity from its harvesting and its restricted availability generated the need for the development of other materials or strategies either to substitute autologous bone graft or expand its limited supply. Bone graft substitutes can possess one or more components: an osteoconductive matrix, acting as a scaffold; osteoinductive proteins and other growth factors to induce differentiation and proliferation of bone-forming cells; and osteogenic cells for bone formation. Based on their distinct properties, all these bone grafting alternatives have specific indications, and can be used either alone or in combination. In this review, we summarise the available bone grafting materials, focussing mainly on the various bone substitutes and their characteristics, in an effort to specify the indications for their use.

matched_article.title: Exsanguinators and tourniquets: do we need to change our practice?
matched_article.abstract: Exsanguinators and tourniquets are regularly used in orthopaedic theatres. A good understanding of their application and contraindications must be ensured to prevent injury to limb or life. However, the level of staff understanding is not well documented. The aims of this study were to assess knowledge of their use between theatre personnel and assess their sterility at our institution. A previously published questionnaire was distributed to various orthopaedic theatre personnel responsible for exsanguinator and tourniquet application. Microbiology culture and sensitivity swabs were also taken. Mean questionnaire score for all participants was 30.9%. None of the 74 participants scored more than 49% in the questionnaire. Exsanguinators grew more positive cultures than the tourniquets. Exsanguinators and tourniquets are used widely in the field of orthopaedics. Lack of their understanding amongst operating theatre personnel involved with their use strongly supports the need for providing and ensuring adequate education to provide the best patient care. In consideration of our findings, we propose a solution addressing these issues.

statements.text: Orthopaedic theatre personnel responsible for applying exsanguinators and tourniquets had poor knowledge of their correct use and contraindications, indicating a need for better education and training.
statements.support_summary: Direct questionnaire evidence shows suboptimal orthopaedic staff knowledge about tourniquet use: orthopaedic specialist registrars and operating department assistants scored only 41.3% and 46.7%, respectively, and the authors explicitly called for better training (PMID: 20223078). Several survey studies also found practices inconsistent with evidence-based recommendations, especially use of excessive cuff pressures and reliance on tradition rather than literature, supporting knowledge gaps and need for education/protocols in orthopaedics (PMID: 25429571, PMID: 16983305, PMID: 26969951, PMID: 38578023). Additional indirect support comes from reviews/audits noting that recommended guidelines were not well known or that staff training on safe-use guidelines was needed (PMID: 22118651, PMID: 38807391). One supporting item is somewhat off-target because it concerns maxillofacial rather than orthopaedic surgeons, though it similarly reports poor knowledge of contraindications and lack of formal training (PMID: 31358373). Overall, the support is thematically consistent around poor or uneven knowledge, nonstandard practice, and calls for formal education, though only a few studies directly assess knowledge and contraindications in the exact target population.
statements.contradict_summary: One questionnaire study from Norway reported that orthopaedic surgeons mostly followed modern tourniquet guidelines, which weakens the claim that knowledge was broadly poor in all settings (PMID: 16877612). However, the abstract focuses more on national practice patterns and complication rates than on formal testing of knowledge or contraindication awareness, so its contradiction is limited and context-specific.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence is a direct orthopaedic questionnaire study showing poor knowledge among theatre-related personnel and trainees (PMID: 20223078), reinforced by multiple surveys/audits showing non-evidence-based tourniquet practice and repeated recommendations for improved education, while the lone contradictory study suggests acceptable practice in one national setting rather than definitively disproving knowledge deficits.

statements.text: Exsanguinators were less sterile than tourniquets in an orthopaedic theatre setting, as exsanguinators yielded more positive microbiology cultures.
statements.support_summary: One abstract directly supports the claim: in an assessment of tourniquets and exsanguinators across orthopedic hospitals, exsanguinators were reported as the most heavily contaminated devices, with potentially pathogenic organisms including coagulase-negative staphylococci, Staphylococcus aureus, Proteus spp., resistant Acinetobacter, and Candida identified (PMID: 19404812). The study specifically compared these theater devices microbiologically and found higher contamination burdens on exsanguinators than tourniquets, consistent with more positive cultures from exsanguinators. It also showed that decontamination wipes were effective, reinforcing that the detected contamination was measurable and modifiable. No obvious discrepancy is present between the abstract and its categorization as supporting evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The available evidence is limited to a single directly relevant abstract, but that study explicitly reports heavier contamination of exsanguinators than tourniquets, which matches the claim.

source_article.title: Machine Learning-Based Ensemble Model for Zika Virus T-Cell Epitope Prediction.
source_article.abstract: Zika virus (ZIKV), the causative agent of Zika fever in humans, is an RNA virus that belongs to the genus Flavivirus. Currently, there is no approved vaccine for clinical use to combat the ZIKV infection and contain the epidemic. Epitope-based peptide vaccines have a large untapped potential for boosting vaccination safety, cross-reactivity, and immunogenicity. Though many attempts have been made to develop vaccines for ZIKV, none of these have proved to be successful. Epitope-based peptide vaccines can act as powerful alternatives to conventional vaccines due to their low production cost, less reactogenic, and allergenic responses. For designing an effective and viable epitope-based peptide vaccine against this deadly virus, it is essential to select the antigenic T-cell epitopes since epitope-based vaccines are considered safe. The in silico machine-learning-based approach for ZIKV T-cell epitope prediction would save a lot of physical experimental time and efforts for speedy vaccine development compared to in vivo approaches. We hereby have trained a machine-learning-based computational model to predict novel ZIKV T-cell epitopes by employing physicochemical properties of amino acids. The proposed ensemble model based on a voting mechanism works by blending the predictions for each class (epitope or nonepitope) from each base classifier. Predictions obtained for each class by the individual classifier are summed up, and the class with the majority vote is predicted upon. An odd number of classifiers have been used to avoid the occurrence of ties in the voting. Experimentally determined ZIKV peptide sequences data set was collected from Immune Epitope Database and Analysis Resource (IEDB) repository. The data set consists of 3,519 sequences, of which 1,762 are epitopes and 1,757 are nonepitopes. The length of sequences ranges from 6 to 30 meter. For each sequence, we extracted 13 physicochemical features. The proposed ensemble model achieved sensitivity, specificity, Gini coefficient, AUC, precision, F-score, and accuracy of 0.976, 0.959, 0.993, 0.994, 0.989, 0.985, and 97.13%, respectively. To check the consistency of the model, we carried out five-fold cross-validation and an average accuracy of 96.072% is reported. Finally, a comparative analysis of the proposed model with existing methods has been carried out using a separate validation data set, suggesting the proposed ensemble model as a better model. The proposed ensemble model will help predict novel ZIKV vaccine candidates to save lives globally and prevent future epidemic-scale outbreaks.

statements.text: A machine-learning ensemble model using physicochemical amino acid features can accurately distinguish Zika virus T-cell epitopes from nonepitopes.
statements.support_summary: The supporting evidence consists of a single duplicated study reporting a decision tree ensemble model that uses physicochemical amino acid properties to classify experimentally verified Zika virus T-cell epitopes versus non-epitopes from the ViPR database, directly matching the claim (PMID: 35552469). The abstract reports strong held-out test performance, including accuracy 0.9789, AUC 0.984, sensitivity 0.981, specificity 0.987, and MCC 0.948, along with a five-fold cross-validation mean accuracy of 0.97864 (PMID: 35552469). The same study also states that the ensemble outperformed standard machine-learning algorithms, reinforcing the claim of accurate discrimination (PMID: 35552469). Because both support items are duplicates of the same article, the support is based on one study rather than independent replication.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The available evidence directly describes an ensemble machine-learning model using physicochemical amino acid features with very high reported performance for distinguishing Zika T-cell epitopes from non-epitopes, although the support comes from a single duplicated study without independent contradiction.

statements.text: The ensemble model outperforms existing Zika virus T-cell epitope prediction methods on an independent validation set.
statements.support_summary: One unique study reports that a decision-tree ensemble model for Zika virus T-cell epitope prediction achieved high performance on a test set and in 5-fold cross-validation, and that it outperformed standard machine-learning algorithms in direct comparison (PMID: 35552469). However, the abstract does not clearly state that this comparison was done on an independent external validation set, so support for that specific part of the claim is only partial. The two supporting entries are duplicates of the same paper (PMID: 35552469), so they do not represent independent corroboration.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for the underlying ensemble-model article, indicating that the findings should not be considered reliable evidence (PMID: 37860387). This does not provide new comparative performance data against other methods, but it substantially weakens confidence in the reported superiority claim. Thus, the contradiction is indirect but important, because it challenges the validity of the main supporting study rather than presenting opposite benchmark results.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although one abstract reports that the ensemble model outperformed standard ML methods, the evidence is duplicated, does not clearly establish superiority on an independent validation set, and the core study has been retracted, which seriously undermines confidence in the claim.

statements.text: The ensemble model may support identification of novel Zika virus vaccine candidates for epitope-based vaccine development.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a study describing an ensemble decision-tree model trained on experimentally verified Zika virus T-cell epitope data, which achieved high test performance and explicitly states it can aid prediction of novel and immunodominant T-cell epitopes that may serve as prospective vaccine targets (PMID: 35552469). Multiple additional Zika studies using immunoinformatics, docking, and epitope-screening pipelines report identification of novel or conserved B- and T-cell epitopes and multi-epitope vaccine constructs, consistently concluding these computational approaches can generate candidate peptides for vaccine development, though they do not specifically test the same ensemble model (PMID: 32778040, PMID: 27485738, PMID: 34997041, PMID: 31962160, PMID: 27590716, PMID: 27633296, PMID: 28435428, PMID: 37486711, PMID: 38827811). Experimental and computational epitope-mapping studies also identify novel Zika T-cell or neutralizing epitopes with stated implications for vaccine design, further supporting the general premise that epitope prediction/discovery can inform vaccine candidate selection (PMID: 32753403, PMID: 28231312, PMID: 32090677). Reviews likewise argue that computational and machine-learning epitope prediction can accelerate peptide or mRNA vaccine design for Zika and other pathogens (PMID: 34089044, PMID: 31623241). A notable discrepancy is that many items are only indirect support for the specific claim about an ensemble model, and several entries are duplicates (e.g., PMID: 35552469, PMID: 39050858, PMID: 28435428).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. This is primarily because there is one directly relevant study showing an ensemble model can predict novel Zika T-cell epitopes as prospective vaccine targets (PMID: 35552469), and it is reinforced by a broader literature showing computational epitope discovery commonly yields plausible Zika vaccine candidates, although most of that evidence is indirect rather than model-specific.

matched_article.title: Kinematic Evaluation via Inertial Measurement Unit Associated with Upper Extremity Motor Function in Subacute Stroke: A Cross-Sectional Study.
matched_article.abstract: Kinematic evaluation via portable sensor system has been increasingly applied in neurological sciences and clinical practice. However, conventional kinematic evaluation rarely extends the context beyond the motor impairment level. In addition, kinematic tasks with numerous items could be complex and time consuming that pose a burden to test applications and data processing. The study aimed to explore the correlation of finger-to-nose task (FNT) kinematics via Inertial Measurement Unit with upper limb motor function in subacute stroke. In this study, six FNT kinematic variables were used to measure movement time, smoothness, and velocity in 37 participants with subacute stroke. Upper limb motor function was evaluated with the Fugl-Meyer Assessment for Upper Extremity (FMA-UE), Action Research Arm Test (ARAT), and modified Barthel Index (MBI). As a result, mean velocity, peak velocity, and the number of movement units were associated with the clinical assessments. The multivariable linear regression models could estimate 55%, 51%, and 32% of variance in FMA-UE, ARAT, and MBI, respectively. In addition, age, gender, type of stroke, and paretic side had no significant effects on these associations. Results show that FNT kinematic variables measured via Inertial Measurement Unit are associated with upper extremity motor function in individuals with subacute stroke. The objective kinematic evaluation may be suitable for predicting clinical measures of motor impairment and capacity to understand upper extremity motor recovery and clinical decision making after stroke. This trial is registered with ChiCTR1900026656.

statements.text: In individuals with subacute stroke, finger-to-nose task kinematic variables measured with an inertial measurement unit are associated with upper extremity motor function.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study in subacute stroke found that finger-to-nose test kinematic components were significantly associated with upper-extremity motor function measures, including FMA-UE, ARAT, and MBI, in 37 stroke participants (PMID: 33912550). The study used principal component analysis of six finger-to-nose kinematic metrics and concluded that these kinematic components are associated with upper-extremity motor function in subacute stroke (PMID: 33912550). However, the abstract does not clearly state that the kinematics were measured specifically with an inertial measurement unit, so this is a partial rather than direct match to the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because the available study directly links finger-to-nose kinematic variables with upper-extremity motor function in subacute stroke (PMID: 33912550). Confidence is moderate rather than strong because the abstract does not explicitly confirm the use of an inertial measurement unit for those measurements.

statements.text: In individuals with subacute stroke, mean velocity, peak velocity, and movement units from finger-to-nose task kinematics are associated with clinical measures of upper limb motor impairment and functional capacity.
statements.support_summary: Direct support comes from a subacute stroke study showing that principal components derived from finger-to-nose kinematics were associated with upper-extremity clinical measures; the main component was dominated by mean velocity, peak velocity, and number of movement units and related to FMA-UE, ARAT, and MBI, matching the claim closely (PMID: 33912550). Additional but more indirect support comes from broader stroke studies where movement speed and smoothness metrics were associated with clinical impairment/activity scales, although these used reaching or pointing tasks rather than finger-to-nose and were not limited specifically to subacute stroke (PMID: 31262320, PMID: 34770362). Another finger-to-nose study in people post-stroke found peak speed and number of movement units differed from controls, and number of movement units related to stroke severity/total movement time, supporting construct validity but not directly the exact set of clinical associations claimed (PMID: 28183337). Overall, the recurring theme is that velocity- and smoothness-related kinematics, especially in finger-to-nose performance, track upper-limb impairment and functional capacity after stroke, with the strongest and most specific evidence from PMID: 33912550.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence is a directly relevant subacute-stroke finger-to-nose study linking mean velocity, peak velocity, and movement units to FMA-UE, ARAT, and MBI (PMID: 33912550), while the remaining studies provide consistent but more indirect support across related tasks and populations.

statements.text: In individuals with subacute stroke, finger-to-nose task kinematics measured with an inertial measurement unit may help predict clinical measures of upper extremity motor function.
statements.support_summary: One study in subacute stroke found that finger-to-nose kinematic components derived by principal component analysis were significantly associated with standard upper-extremity clinical measures, including FMA-UE, ARAT, and MBI, suggesting these movement features may help estimate motor function (PMID: 33912550). The abstract emphasizes associations and variance explained in regression models, which is consistent with a predictive or explanatory role for finger-to-nose kinematics in clinical assessment. However, the abstract does not clearly state that the kinematics were measured specifically with an inertial measurement unit, so support for the device-specific part of the claim is only partial (PMID: 33912550).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available study shows that finger-to-nose kinematic measures are associated with clinical upper-extremity motor outcomes in subacute stroke (PMID: 33912550). Still, support is moderate rather than strong because the evidence is limited to a single abstract and does not clearly confirm the inertial measurement unit aspect.

source_article.title: Point Tracking Technology of Sports Image Sequence Marks Based on Fuzzy Clustering Algorithm.
source_article.abstract: Fuzzy clustering algorithms have received widespread attention in various fields. Point tracking technology has significant application importance in sports image data analysis. In order to solve the problem of limited tracking performance caused by the fuzzy and rough division of moving image edges, this paper proposes a point tracking technology based on a fuzzy clustering algorithm, which is used for the point tracking of moving image sequence signs. This article analyzes the development status of sports image sequence analysis and processing technology and introduces some basic theories about fuzzy clustering algorithms. On the basis of the fuzzy clustering algorithm, the positioning and tracking of the marker points of the moving image sequence are studied. A series of experiments have proved that the fuzzy clustering algorithm can improve the recognition rate of the landmark points of the moving image. For the detection and tracking of moving targets, the fuzzy clustering algorithm can reach the limit faster under the same number of iterations, and the image noise can be reduced to 60% of the original by 5 iterations. This has excellent development value in application.

statements.text: In sports image sequence point tracking, a fuzzy clustering algorithm improves recognition of moving-image landmark points compared with conventional tracking approaches.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In tracking moving image targets in sports image analysis, a fuzzy clustering algorithm converges faster than conventional tracking approaches under the same iteration limit.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In sports image sequence analysis, a fuzzy clustering algorithm reduces image noise during moving-target detection and tracking compared with the unprocessed image sequence.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: EEG Feature Extraction and Data Augmentation in Emotion Recognition.
matched_article.abstract: Emotion recognition is a challenging problem in Brain-Computer Interaction (BCI). Electroencephalogram (EEG) gives unique information about brain activities that are created due to emotional stimuli. This is one of the most substantial advantages of brain signals in comparison to facial expression, tone of voice, or speech in emotion recognition tasks. However, the lack of EEG data and high dimensional EEG recordings lead to difficulties in building effective classifiers with high accuracy. In this study, data augmentation and feature extraction techniques are proposed to solve the lack of data problem and high dimensionality of data, respectively. In this study, the proposed method is based on deep generative models and a data augmentation strategy called Conditional Wasserstein GAN (CWGAN), which is applied to the extracted features to regenerate additional EEG features. DEAP dataset is used to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Finally, a standard support vector machine and a deep neural network with different tunes were implemented to build effective models. Experimental results show that using the additional augmented data enhances the performance of EEG-based emotion recognition models. Furthermore, the mean accuracy of classification after data augmentation is increased 6.5% for valence and 3.0% for arousal, respectively.

statements.text: Data augmentation with conditional Wasserstein GAN-generated EEG features improves EEG-based emotion recognition performance in models trained to classify emotional valence and arousal from EEG recordings.
statements.support_summary: Direct support comes from studies specifically using conditional Wasserstein GAN augmentation for EEG emotion recognition: both PMID: 30440924 and PMID: 33052888 report that CWGAN/cWGAN-generated differential-entropy or related EEG features significantly improved emotion recognition accuracy on public datasets including DEAP and SEED, with PMID: 33052888 also finding gains over other augmentation methods and noting optimal benefit when generated data were kept below about 10 times the original training size. Additional studies using other GAN-based augmentation methods also consistently reported better EEG emotion recognition after augmentation, though they are less specific to the claim: PMID: 34671415 found GAN-based sample generation improved deep-learning emotion recognition, PMID: 34955797 reported 1.5% and 3.5% accuracy gains on SEED and SEED-IV with VAE-D2GAN, and PMID: 37053054 reported about a 5% average accuracy improvement on SEED with DEVAE-GAN. A recurring theme is that EEG data scarcity and class imbalance can be mitigated by generative augmentation that produces realistic feature samples. A minor scope discrepancy is that several supporting papers assess general emotion recognition rather than explicitly valence/arousal classification, although DEAP-based studies are relevant to that framing.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. There is therefore no direct abstract-level evidence here arguing that GAN-based EEG augmentation fails to improve emotion recognition performance or harms valence/arousal classification.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall strongly supported. The strongest evidence is from two abstracts directly evaluating conditional Wasserstein GAN augmentation in EEG emotion-recognition datasets and reporting significant performance improvements (PMID: 30440924, PMID: 33052888), with further indirect support from multiple other GAN-based augmentation studies showing the same general pattern.

statements.text: Using additional augmented EEG feature data can increase classification accuracy for emotion recognition compared with using the original limited EEG data alone.
statements.support_summary: Several studies directly support the claim by showing that synthetic or augmented EEG data improve emotion-recognition accuracy relative to training on the original data alone, especially GAN/VAE-based augmentation on SEED/DEAP/SEED-IV datasets (PMID: 33052888, PMID: 30440924, PMID: 34671415, PMID: 34955797, PMID: 37053054). These abstracts report explicit gains such as significant improvement with CWGAN-generated EEG (PMID: 30440924), 1.5-3.5% higher accuracy with VAE-D2GAN augmentation (PMID: 34955797), and ~5% improvement with DEVAE-GAN augmentation (PMID: 37053054). A broader but somewhat less direct theme is that richer/fused/derived EEG feature representations also often outperform simpler or raw inputs, including multi-feature models, cross-frequency or connectivity-based features, and feature fusion approaches (e.g., PMID: 33505263, PMID: 32898573, PMID: 36161187, PMID: 38885847). However, many items in the support set are only indirect support because they test feature engineering, multimodal fusion, or alternative model architectures rather than true augmentation of limited EEG data; this is a noticeable discrepancy in the evidence grouping. Overall, the strongest support comes from the multiple emotion-recognition studies explicitly comparing original versus augmented EEG training sets and finding higher classification accuracy.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This judgment is driven by multiple directly relevant augmentation studies in EEG emotion recognition showing improved accuracy after adding generated/augmented EEG feature data, while no contradictory evidence was provided.

source_article.title: Long Noncoding RNA FGD5-AS1 Acts as a Competing Endogenous RNA on microRNA-383 to Enhance the Malignant Characteristics of Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma by Increasing SP1 Expression.
source_article.abstract: PURPOSE: Previous studies have identified the important roles of a long noncoding RNA called FGD5 antisense RNA 1 (FGD5-AS1) in several types of human cancer. Nonetheless, to our knowledge, the expression and functions of FGD5-AS1 in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) have not been clarified. In this study, we aimed to determine the expression status of long noncoding RNA FGD5-AS1 in ESCC, determine its participation in ESCC progression, and uncover the underlying mechanisms. METHODS: ESCC tissue samples and paired normal adjacent tissues were collected to quantify FGD5-AS1 expression by reverse-transcription quantitative PCR. The effects of FGD5-AS1 on ESCC cell proliferation, apoptosis, migration, and invasion in vitro as well as tumor growth in vivo were studied using a Cell Counting Kit-8 assay, flow cytometry, Transwell migration and invasion assays, and an in vivo tumor xenograft experiment. RESULTS: FGD5-AS1 was found to be aberrantly upregulated in both ESCC tumors and cell lines compared to the control groups. Increased FGD5-AS1 expression manifested a close association with tumor size, TNM stage, and lymph node metastasis in patients with ESCC. Overall survival of patients with ESCC was shorter in the FGD5-AS1 high-expression group than in the FGD5-AS1 low-expression group. An FGD5-AS1 knockdown markedly attenuated ESCC cell proliferation, migration, and invasion and promoted apoptosis in vitro as well as slowed tumor growth in vivo. Mechanism investigation revealed that FGD5-AS1 can increase SP1 expression by sponging microRNA-383 (miR-383), thus functioning as a competing endogenous RNA. An miR-383 knockdown and recovery of SP1 expression attenuated the inhibition of the malignant characteristics of ESCC cells by the FGD5-AS1 knockdown. CONCLUSION: Thus, FGD5-AS1 enhances the aggressive phenotype of ESCC cells in vitro and in vivo via the miR-383-SP1 axis, which may represent a novel target for ESCC therapy.

statements.text: In esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, high FGD5-AS1 expression is associated with more aggressive disease and poorer patient survival compared with low FGD5-AS1 expression.
statements.support_summary: Two indirect evidence items support the claim in a broad, cross-cancer sense, but neither abstract provides esophageal squamous cell carcinoma-specific data. A review reports that FGD5-AS1 is highly expressed in many cancers and is associated with lymph node metastasis, tumor invasion, survival time, and recurrence, with experimental evidence linking it to proliferation, invasion, metastasis, and tumor growth (PMID: 35475392). A meta-analysis of 12 studies across eight cancer types found that high FGD5-AS1 expression was significantly associated with poor overall survival, advanced stage, lymph node metastasis, and larger tumor size (PMID: 39224806). The main recurring theme is that elevated FGD5-AS1 tracks with more aggressive clinicopathologic features and worse prognosis across malignancies. However, because these studies are not specific to esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, the support for this exact claim is indirect and limited.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall weakly supported. The available evidence consistently links high FGD5-AS1 expression to aggressive disease and poorer survival across multiple cancers, but there is no direct evidence here from esophageal squamous cell carcinoma abstracts specifically.

statements.text: In esophageal squamous cell carcinoma cells, reducing FGD5-AS1 expression suppresses proliferation, migration, invasion, and tumor growth while promoting apoptosis compared with control conditions.
statements.support_summary: One review reports that FGD5-AS1 is overexpressed across multiple cancers and is associated with proliferation, metastasis, invasion, poor prognosis, and in vivo tumor growth, with mechanistic discussion centered on miRNA sponging (PMID: 35475392). This indirectly supports the claim because if FGD5-AS1 promotes malignant behaviors, reducing it could be expected to suppress proliferation, migration/invasion, and tumor growth and potentially favor apoptosis. However, the abstract is a broad review rather than a primary study in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, and it does not explicitly describe FGD5-AS1 knockdown effects in ESCC cells or apoptosis outcomes, so support is indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall weakly supported. The only cited evidence is an indirect review-level summary suggesting FGD5-AS1 has oncogenic functions in cancer broadly (PMID: 35475392), but there is no direct abstract-level evidence here specifically showing that FGD5-AS1 reduction in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma suppresses proliferation, migration, invasion, and tumor growth while increasing apoptosis.

statements.text: In esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, FGD5-AS1 promotes malignant behavior by sponging microRNA-383 and increasing SP1 expression; disrupting microRNA-383 or restoring SP1 counteracts the effects of FGD5-AS1 loss.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Anticancer Effects of ACT001 via NF-κB Suppression in Murine Triple-Negative Breast Cancer Cell Line 4T1.
matched_article.abstract: ACT001 is a novel sesquiterpene lactone derivative with anticancer effects, including the reversal of tamoxifen resistance in estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer cells. However, few studies have investigated the anticancer effects of ACT001 in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), a highly aggressive cancer with a poor prognosis. This study aimed to investigate the effects of ACT001 on TNBC and the potential mechanism underlying these effects. The anticancer effects of ACT001 on the murine TNBC cell line 4T1 were evaluated by Cell Counting Kit-8 assay, animal experiments, TUNEL staining, flow cytometry, immunofluorescence, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, and Western blotting analysis. ACT001 induced apoptosis in 4T1 cells by upregulating B cell lymphoma 2-associated X protein expression. Moreover, ACT001 markedly decreased levels of secretory granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor (GM-CSF) in 4T1 tumors, decreased the number of myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), and reduced angiogenesis. Furthermore, GM-CSF promoted angiogenesis and the proliferation of MDSCs in a dose-dependent manner. Finally, ACT001 suppressed phospho-NF-κB and IκB-α levels in 4T1 cells, thereby further decreasing GM-CSF levels. Our results suggest that ACT001 exerts its anticancer effects by inducing apoptosis in murine TNBC cell line 4T1 and regulates the tumor microenvironment by attenuating angiogenesis and accumulation of MDSCs in 4T1 tumors. The underlying mechanism may involve the suppression of NF-κB activity.

statements.text: ACT001 induces apoptosis in murine triple-negative breast cancer 4T1 cells and suppresses tumor growth-related malignant behavior.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: ACT001 reduces angiogenesis and the accumulation of myeloid-derived suppressor cells in murine triple-negative breast cancer 4T1 tumors.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: ACT001’s anticancer effects in murine triple-negative breast cancer 4T1 cells may be mediated by suppression of NF-κB signaling and downstream granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor production.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that Cordyceps sinensis suppresses 4T1 triple-negative breast tumor growth while activating NF-κB signaling in the tumor microenvironment, apparently through promotion of M1 macrophage polarization rather than direct effects of ACT001 on 4T1 cells (PMID: 32422358). This creates only indirect contradiction: it shows an anticancer context in which NF-κB activation, not suppression, is associated with tumor inhibition. However, the study involves a different intervention and mechanism, and the abstract does not address ACT001 or downstream GM-CSF production, so its relevance to the specific claim is limited. This is therefore best viewed as a discrepancy in mechanism across studies rather than strong direct refutation of the claim.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear and insufficient for this specific claim. There is no direct supporting evidence here, and the lone contradicting item is only indirectly relevant because it studies a different agent and mechanism in 4T1 tumor biology.

source_article.title: Experimental infection of mice with avian paramyxovirus serotypes 1 to 9.
source_article.abstract: The nine serotypes of avian paramyxoviruses (APMVs) are frequently isolated from domestic and wild birds worldwide. APMV-1, also called Newcastle disease virus, was shown to be attenuated in non-avian species and is being developed as a potential vector for human vaccines. In the present study, we extended this evaluation to the other eight serotypes by evaluating infection in BALB/c mice. Mice were inoculated intranasally with a prototype strain of each of the nine serotypes and monitored for clinical disease, gross pathology, histopathology, virus replication and viral antigen distribution, and seroconversion. On the basis of multiple criteria, each of the APMV serotypes except serotype 5 was found to replicate in mice. Five of the serotypes produced clinical disease and significant weight loss in the following order of severity: 1, 2>6, 9>7. However, disease was short-lived. The other serotypes produced no evident clinical disease. Replication of all of the APMVs except APMV-5 in the nasal turbinates and lungs was confirmed by the recovery of infectious virus and by substantial expression of viral antigen in the epithelial lining detected by immunohistochemistry. Trace levels of infectious APMV-4 and -9 were detected in the brain of some animals; otherwise, no virus was detected in the brain, small intestine, kidney, or spleen. Histologically, infection with the APMVs resulted in lung lesions consistent with broncho-interstitial pneumonia of varying severity that were completely resolved at 14 days post infection. All of the mice infected with the APMVs except APMV-5 produced serotype-specific HI serum antibodies, confirming a lack of replication of APMV-5. Taken together, these results demonstrate that all APMV serotypes except APMV-5 are capable of replicating in mice with minimal disease and pathology.

statements.text: In BALB/c mice inoculated intranasally with prototype strains of avian paramyxovirus serotypes, avian paramyxovirus serotypes 1, 2, 6, 7, 9, and other serotypes except serotype 5 were able to replicate in mice with minimal disease and pathology.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that both a variant APMV-6 strain and the prototype APMV-6 strain replicated in mouse respiratory tissues and induced respiratory disease, sometimes causing death, which conflicts with the claim's characterization of replication as causing only minimal disease and pathology (PMID: 28685285). This is only a partial contradiction because the abstract addresses serotype 6 specifically rather than the broader set of serotypes in the claim, but it directly challenges the 'minimal disease' portion for prototype APMV-6.
statements.conclusion: The available evidence more contradicts than supports the statement, but only narrowly and for serotype 6. The contradiction is based on a single abstract showing prototype APMV-6 can cause notable respiratory disease and occasional death in mice (PMID: 28685285), while no supporting items were provided for the broader claim.

statements.text: In BALB/c mice inoculated intranasally with prototype strains of avian paramyxovirus serotypes, avian paramyxovirus serotype 5 did not replicate and did not induce serotype-specific antibody responses.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In BALB/c mice inoculated intranasally with prototype strains of avian paramyxovirus serotypes, several replicating serotypes caused short-lived clinical disease and lung lesions, with the most severe effects produced by serotypes 1, 2, 6, 7, and 9.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: The effects of Th17 cytokines on the inflammatory mediator production and barrier function of ARPE-19 cells.
matched_article.abstract: Th17 cells have emerged as a key factor in the pathogenesis of uveitis as well as other autoimmune disorders. They secrete a number of cytokines including IL-17A, IL-17F and IL-22 and until now the effects of these cytokines on resident cells of the eye were not yet clear. The purpose of this study was to investigate the effects of Interleukin (IL)-17A, IL-17F and IL-22 on the production of inflammatory mediators and barrier function of retinal pigment epithelium cells. We showed that ARPE-19 cells, a spontaneously arisen cell line of retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), constitutively expressed IL-17RC and IL-22R, but not IL-17RA. IL-17A significantly enhanced the production of CXCL8, CCL2, CCL20 and IL-6 by these cells. IL-17F had a similar effect on the production of CXCL8, CCL2 and IL-6 by ARPE-19 cells, but did not influence the expression of CCL20. Both IL-17A and IL-17F significantly decreased the transepithelial electrical resistance (TER) of the ARPE-19 monolayer and increased the diffusion rate of fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC)-dextran. They also disrupted the distribution of the junction proteins zonula occludens (ZO)-1 and occludin at the interface of adjacent cells. IL-22 did not have a detectable effect on the production of the tested inflammatory mediators by ARPE-19 cells, TER of the ARPE-19 monolayer, the diffusion rate of FITC-dextran or the distribution of ZO-1 and occludin. This study demonstrates that IL-17A and IL-17F, but not IL-22, significantly promoted ARPE-19 cells to secrete inflammatory mediators and compromised the ARPE-19 monolayer barrier function in association with a disrupted distribution of ZO-1 and occludin. These results suggest that both IL-17A and IL-17F may play a role in posterior segment inflammation of the eye.

statements.text: In ARPE-19 retinal pigment epithelial cells, IL-17A and IL-17F increase production of inflammatory mediators, including CXCL8, CCL2, and IL-6, and IL-17A also increases CCL20.
statements.support_summary: One ARPE-19 study directly supports the claim that IL-17A increases inflammatory mediator production, showing increased CXCL8, CCL2, and IL-6 and implicating Erk1/2, p38 MAPK, PI3K/Akt, and NF-κB signaling in this response (PMID: 22162626). A second study in primary human RPE cells, not specifically ARPE-19, found that IL-17A upregulated IL-8, CCL2, and CCL20 mRNA and activated similar signaling pathways, which partially supports the IL-17A/CCL20 part of the claim but in a different RPE model and without IL-6 data (PMID: 26830368). Across the supporting studies, the recurring theme is that IL-17A promotes a proinflammatory RPE phenotype with increased chemokine/cytokine output. However, none of the provided abstracts addresses IL-17F, so that part of the claim is not directly supported here.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. There is therefore no direct abstract-level evidence here disputing the claim, although absence of contradiction should not be taken as confirmation of the unsupported IL-17F component.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall partially supported. The evidence directly supports IL-17A-induced increases in CXCL8, CCL2, and IL-6 in ARPE-19 cells and provides partial support for IL-17A-induced CCL20 in RPE cells, but the IL-17F component is not supported by the provided abstracts.

statements.text: In ARPE-19 retinal pigment epithelial cells, IL-17A and IL-17F impair epithelial barrier function by reducing transepithelial resistance, increasing paracellular permeability, and disrupting ZO-1 and occludin localization.
statements.support_summary: Two studies support the portion of the claim involving IL-17A in ARPE-19/RPE barrier dysfunction. A study of a SOCS1-derived peptide reports that IL-17A damages ARPE-19 barrier properties, with altered ZO-1 staining and reduced transepithelial electrical resistance, and that the peptide protects against this damage (PMID: 30048621). Another study directly shows that IL-17A treatment of ARPE19 cells reduces TEER, increases FITC-tracer permeability, and disrupts ZO-1 distribution, consistent with blood-retinal barrier injury in vitro and in vivo (PMID: 34356895). However, neither abstract provides evidence for IL-17F in ARPE-19 cells, and neither clearly confirms occludin disruption in these abstracts, so support for the full claim is only partial.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only partially. The available abstracts consistently support that IL-17A impairs ARPE-19/RPE barrier function by lowering electrical resistance, increasing permeability, and disrupting tight-junction organization, yet they do not substantiate the IL-17F and occludin-specific parts of the claim.

statements.text: In ARPE-19 retinal pigment epithelial cells, IL-22 does not detectably affect inflammatory mediator production or barrier function.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study directly contradicts the claim by reporting that ARPE-19 cells express IL-22Rα and respond to recombinant IL-22 with increased proliferation and clearly elevated MCP-1 production, indicating a detectable effect on inflammatory mediator production (PMID: 27166675). The same abstract also states that IL-22 increased IL-22Rα expression via PI3K/Akt and promoted inflammatory cell migration indirectly through MCP-1 induction (PMID: 27166675). However, this abstract does not mention barrier function, so the contradiction is strong for inflammatory mediator production but only indirect/incomplete for the barrier-function part of the claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although the available evidence is limited to a single study, it directly shows that IL-22 alters ARPE-19 inflammatory behavior by increasing MCP-1 production, while providing no abstract-level evidence about barrier function.

source_article.title: The Serological Confirmation and Outcome of the Pediatric Dengue Patients Presenting to Emergency Department: A Cross-Sectional Study.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: In the emergency department, it is very uncommon perform a differential diagnosis to serologically differentiate between dengue, dengue hemorrhagic fever, and dengue shock syndrome. Prompt differential diagnosis and treatment is essential with the presentation of dengue. This study aims to determine the serological confirmation and outcome of the dengue epidemic in the pediatric population presenting to the ED in a tertiary care hospital. METHODS: A single-center cross-sectional study was conducted. All pediatric patients aged less than 18 years presented to ED with clinical features suggestive of DF, DFF, and DSS while also doing the serological confirmation for the dengue were enrolled in the study. Data was collected on demographics, clinical characteristics, diagnosis, and outcomes of 324 pediatric patients. Multivariable binary logistic regression was applied for the analysis. RESULTS: Out of 324 patients, 191 (59.13%) underwent NS1 testing and 132 (40.87%) did the IgM test. Most participants were in the age range of 13 to 18 years in both groups. Fever was the most common complaint in both groups 191 (100%) and 132 (100%). In each group, around one-third of the participants complained about body aches 69 (36.13%) and 44 (33.33%). The patient having a history of traveling within the past 14 days created a 1.51 (95% CI: 1.27-2.25) times higher odds of contracting dengue fever as compared to no history of travel. CONCLUSION: The serologic confirmation of dengue in the ED helps in both the adequate and timely treatment as well as patient disposition and ultimately saves lives.

statements.text: Serological confirmation of dengue in pediatric emergency department patients with suspected dengue improves timely treatment and patient disposition and may help save lives.
statements.support_summary: The supporting studies consistently indicate that laboratory or serologic confirmation helps distinguish dengue from other febrile illnesses in children and may enable earlier recognition of severe disease, but most evidence is indirect rather than a direct test of improved emergency-department disposition or survival. A multicenter pediatric surveillance study found that many febrile children with dengue were not clinically diagnosed, supporting the need for laboratory confirmation in suspected cases (PMID: 23936565), while outbreak and hospital-based pediatric studies reported use of NS1/IgM/IgG testing to confirm cases and emphasized that timely diagnosis, strict monitoring, and prompt supportive management were associated with low mortality or reduced mortality risk in severe dengue/DSS (PMID: 16391444, PMID: 15510763, PMID: 27621562, PMID: 35780359). One outbreak study in Brazil suggested serologic confirmation could avoid faulty diagnosis and stated that stable demographic/hematologic patterns helped improve management during the epidemic, though it was not pediatric-ED specific (PMID: 27593529). A COVID-19/dengue emergency-department case series recommended dengue serologic screening in endemic settings, but it involved mixed-age patients and did not directly assess disposition or mortality benefits (PMID: 33457380). Overall, the theme is that confirmation and early detection are clinically valuable, but there is a discrepancy between this indirect support and the stronger causal claim that serologic confirmation itself improves pediatric ED treatment timing, disposition, and survival.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported, but only moderately. The available evidence supports that serologic/laboratory confirmation improves diagnostic accuracy and may contribute to earlier appropriate management of pediatric dengue, yet none of the abstracts directly demonstrate improved emergency-department disposition or lifesaving benefit attributable specifically to serologic confirmation.

statements.text: Recent travel history is associated with higher odds of dengue fever in pediatric emergency department patients with suspected dengue.
statements.support_summary: Two studies provide indirect support that recent travel to dengue-endemic areas is an important risk indicator for dengue, including in children. In a non-endemic setting, febrile patients with travel history to endemic regions had a substantial number of serologically confirmed dengue cases, and the authors concluded that travel history is an essential criterion for suspecting dengue, though this study was not limited to pediatric emergency patients and did not estimate odds ratios (PMID: 25538925). A pediatric single-center Korean study found that many children had recent international travel and identified serologically confirmed dengue cases, concluding that dengue should be suspected in children with travel to endemic areas; however, it also did not specifically study emergency department patients or quantify the association as higher odds (PMID: 21189941). Overall, the recurring theme is that travel history is clinically useful for identifying possible dengue in non-endemic settings, but the evidence is indirect relative to the specific claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall somewhat supported, but only indirectly. The available studies consistently link travel history with dengue suspicion or imported pediatric dengue cases, yet they do not directly test whether recent travel increases the odds of dengue specifically among pediatric emergency department patients with suspected dengue.

matched_article.title: The Impact of Adverse Childhood Experiences on Health and Development in Young Children.
matched_article.abstract: In recent years, there have been advances in research regarding the prevalence of Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) and resulting poor outcomes for children who have experiences ACES. ACE exposure has been connected with poor academic performance, poor health outcomes, and certain diseases. However, there is still relatively little known about the prevalence and impact of ACE exposure in early childhood. Using the 2016 National Survey of Children's Health, this study assessed the prevalence of adverse childhood experiences for children under the age of six years. This research measured the impact of individual ACEs on health and developmental outcomes and found that financial hardship and parental mental illness both had increased odds of having health and developmental difficulties. Furthermore, this work finds that there is a dose-response relationship between ACE count and increased risk of health and developmental difficulties. Conversely, children who were reported as affectionate with their parent had decreased risk for health and developmental problems. Programs supporting families with young children should focus efforts on enhancing quality of attachment, especially for children experiencing ACEs.

statements.text: Among children under age six years, exposure to financial hardship and parental mental illness is associated with increased risk of health and developmental difficulties compared with children not exposed to those adverse childhood experiences.
statements.support_summary: The support evidence is mostly indirect but consistently links adverse childhood experiences, including economic/material hardship and parental mental illness, with poorer child health and development. The most directly relevant study found that children exposed to poverty plus parental mental illness had especially high risk of poor health/special health care needs compared with other ACE profiles (PMID: 28929420). In young children specifically, greater ACE burden in ages 18-71 months was associated with worse mental health, more chronic medical conditions, and poorer social development (PMID: 26183001), and ACE exposure before age 3 predicted later behavioral problems in a longitudinal cohort of children living in poverty (PMID: 31521904). Additional population-based studies reported that material hardship and parental mental illness were associated with greater care coordination needs (PMID: 35273805), and broader reviews/methods papers noted ACEs are associated with poorer child health outcomes across income groups (PMID: 28865661), though several included papers are reviews, qualitative studies, intergenerational analyses, or studies not limited to children under 6, so their relevance is partial rather than direct.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The evidence includes one study directly implicating the combination of poverty and parental mental illness in worse child health (PMID: 28929420), plus multiple studies showing that early-life ACE exposure is associated with health, behavioral, and developmental difficulties, although much of the evidence is indirect or not restricted exactly to children under age six.

statements.text: Among children under age six years, a greater number of adverse childhood experiences is associated with a higher risk of health and developmental difficulties compared with fewer adverse childhood experiences.
statements.support_summary: Several studies directly support a dose-response association between more ACEs and worse health/developmental outcomes in children under 6. In nationally representative or large samples of young children, higher ACE counts were linked to greater odds of developmental delay (children aged 3-5; one ACE aOR 2.03, two or more ACEs aOR 2.34) (PMID: 38142261), worse mental health/chronic medical conditions/social development among children 18-71 months in child welfare (per additional ACE: OR 1.32 for behavior problems, 1.21 for chronic medical conditions, 1.77 for low socialization in ages 36-71 months) (PMID: 26183001), and long-term internalizing/externalizing behavior problems with a dose-response pattern among children birth to 6 years (PMID: 25798504). Longitudinal evidence also found that ACEs before age 3 predicted higher behavioral problems at age 5, with stronger associations for 3+ ACEs than 2 ACEs (PMID: 31521904). Many additional items are broader reviews, mixed-age child studies, or intergenerational/parental-ACE studies that are only indirectly relevant to the specific under-6 claim, so they add context but are weaker direct support; one such item on maternal ACEs concerns mothers' ACEs rather than children's own ACEs and should be treated as only indirect evidence despite being grouped as support (PMID: 28919342).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided. Some supporting-group articles are only indirectly relevant because they address broader age ranges, specific subpopulations, reviews, or parental ACEs rather than children's own ACE exposure, but none of their abstracts clearly report findings that contradict the claim.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is strongly supported. The most directly relevant studies in children under 6 consistently show graded or dose-response associations between higher ACE counts and increased developmental, behavioral, mental health, and some physical health difficulties, while no direct contradictory evidence was provided.

statements.text: Among children under age six years, being reported as affectionate with a parent is associated with a lower risk of health and developmental problems compared with children not reported as affectionate with a parent.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: [Association between magnesium, calcium, phosphorous, copper and zinc in plasma and erythrocytes of umbilical cord blood and biometric parameters of newborns].
source_article.abstract: The correlation between magnesium, calcium, phosphorous, copper, and zinc in umbilical cord plasma and erythrocytes and gestational age, birth height, weight and head circumference were calculated in 52 newborn infants (27 girls, and 25 boys). No infant had a congenital malformation, all pregnancies were uncomplicated, mothers were healthy and had no history of serious diseases. There were no significant sex-related differences at birth among the variables studied. Gestational age was positively correlated with erythrocyte zinc. Birthweight positively correlated with erythrocyte magnesium and zinc. Height of neonate positively correlated with calcium in plasma. Plasma calcium was positively correlated with erythrocyte copper. The most significant regressors accounting for birthweight were erythrocyte zinc followed by plasma zinc. Reference values in umbilical cord plasma and erythrocytes for magnesium, calcium, phosphorous, copper and zinc was also determined.

statements.text: In healthy newborn infants with uncomplicated pregnancies, higher erythrocyte zinc in umbilical cord blood was associated with greater gestational age.
statements.support_summary: A directly relevant study of 66 full-term newborns selected for optimal maternal conditions and uncomplicated pregnancies reported that gestational age was positively correlated with umbilical cord erythrocyte zinc (P<0.001), closely matching the claim (PMID: 1733586). A second study found a low positive correlation between cord blood zinc and gestational age (ρ=0.351, p=0.001), but it measured serum rather than erythrocyte zinc, included low-birth-weight and preterm infants, and concluded the association was too weak for firm inference, so it only partially supports the claim and is less directly applicable (PMID: 25617232). Overall, the strongest abstract-level support comes from one highly concordant study, with limited additional support from a less comparable serum-zinc study.
statements.contradict_summary: Several studies reported no association or an inverse association between gestational age and cord blood zinc, but most assessed plasma/serum zinc rather than erythrocyte zinc and often included preterm or mixed-risk populations, making them only partially comparable to the claim. Cord plasma zinc was reported to decrease with gestational age across preterm-to-term infants in one study (PMID: 12324179), while other studies found no significant correlation between gestational age and cord serum/blood zinc (PMID: 11450383; PMID: 12008981). Another preterm-only study found serum zinc inversely correlated with gestational age (PMID: 28851072). One item appears miscategorized as contradiction: it studied erythrocyte zinc protoporphyrin/heme ratio rather than erythrocyte zinc concentration, so its inverse association with gestational age does not directly address the claim (PMID: 16061597).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The key reason is that the most directly matched study—healthy full-term infants from uncomplicated pregnancies measuring cord erythrocyte zinc—found the claimed positive association, whereas most opposing studies used different zinc compartments (serum/plasma), different populations, or indirect biomarkers.

statements.text: In healthy newborn infants with uncomplicated pregnancies, higher erythrocyte magnesium and erythrocyte zinc in umbilical cord blood were associated with greater birthweight, and erythrocyte zinc was the strongest mineral correlate of birthweight.
statements.support_summary: The strongest supporting abstract is the closely matched study in healthy full-term newborns with uncomplicated pregnancies, which found that birth weight was most strongly explained by erythrocyte zinc, followed by plasma zinc, in stepwise regression; it also reported gestational age correlated positively with erythrocyte zinc (PMID: 1733586). Additional studies reported positive associations between cord zinc measures and birth weight, including significant positive relationships for cord blood zinc (though magnesium was not associated, only trending upward) (PMID: 7712952), cord tissue zinc and birth weight (PMID: 3817258), and maternal/cord zinc with birth weight or lower zinc in low-birth-weight deliveries (PMID: 24958541). However, these supporting studies often measured plasma, whole cord blood, or tissue zinc rather than erythrocyte zinc, and they generally do not show that erythrocyte magnesium is positively associated with birth weight. One item listed as support actually conflicts with the claim, reporting higher cord Zn and Mg in babies weighing <3 kg than in heavier babies (PMID: 14620318).
statements.contradict_summary: Several abstracts contradict the zinc component of the claim by reporting no positive relation between cord or newborn zinc and birth weight, including no correlation of maternal or cord zinc with neonatal birthweight in Nigerian term pregnancies (PMID: 1972116) and no significant correlation between birth weight and maternal or newborn plasma/tissue zinc in uncomplicated Chinese pregnancies (PMID: 2094142). A recent cohort sub-study explicitly stated that cord blood zinc and magnesium were not positively associated with birth weight and described a reverse correlation with zinc (PMID: 38192895). Another study of healthy full-term infants found no significant multivariable association between cord trace elements other than copper and birth weight, so it does not support zinc or magnesium as positive correlates of birth weight (PMID: 26346609). These contradicting studies also tend to assess plasma/whole cord blood or tissue rather than erythrocytes, but collectively they challenge the generalizability of a positive zinc-birthweight association and provide little support for magnesium.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the claim is mixed but leans more contradicted. The best-matched supporting study directly supports erythrocyte zinc as the strongest mineral correlate of birth weight (PMID: 1733586), yet multiple other studies fail to confirm a positive zinc association and the evidence for erythrocyte magnesium being positively associated with greater birthweight is weak to absent.

statements.text: In healthy newborn infants with uncomplicated pregnancies, greater neonatal length was associated with higher plasma calcium, and plasma calcium was positively associated with erythrocyte copper in umbilical cord blood.
statements.support_summary: One study in full-term newborns selected for optimal maternal conditions and uncomplicated pregnancies found that plasma calcium in umbilical cord blood was positively correlated with erythrocyte copper, directly supporting that component of the claim (PMID: 1733586). That same study did not clearly report a direct positive association between neonatal length and plasma calcium; instead, plasma copper was the strongest regressor for birth height, so it only partially supports the full claim (PMID: 1733586). Two additional term-infant studies reported that cord blood calcium was significantly positively associated with birth length, supporting the greater-length/higher-calcium part of the claim, although they did not assess erythrocyte copper (PMID: 25358844, PMID: 21736799). Overall, the evidence supports each component of the claim across studies, but no single abstract fully confirms both relationships together except partially in the original-type population.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support comes from one abstract directly linking plasma calcium with erythrocyte copper in healthy full-term newborns from uncomplicated pregnancies and from additional studies linking higher cord blood calcium with greater birth length, although the full two-part claim is assembled across multiple studies rather than conclusively demonstrated in a single abstract.

matched_article.title: [Examining the effects of tobacco smoking on levels of certain hormones in serum of young men].
matched_article.abstract: Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA), testosterone (T), 17 beta-estradiol (E), prolactin (PRL) and gonadotropins (FSH, LH) were examined in 30 smokers and 25 nonsmokers. The mean E level was higher in smokers than nonsmokers, whereas the levels of T and DHEA are similar and did not differ significantly in smokers compared to nonsmokers. Smokers have also lower mean levels of LH, FSH and PRL than nonsmokers. However, smokers with low prolactin levels have also low sperm percent motility, these results suggest that changes in endocrine profile due to cigarette smoking can reduce the fertility.

statements.text: Young men who smoke cigarettes have higher serum 17 beta-estradiol levels than young men who do not smoke, while serum testosterone and dehydroepiandrosterone levels are not meaningfully different between smokers and nonsmokers.
statements.support_summary: Several studies report higher estradiol in male smokers than nonsmokers, including young adult samples and reproductive-age men, with no significant testosterone difference where testosterone was measured (PMID: 6496540, PMID: 2782983, PMID: 3169284, PMID: 8070715). One study specifically found significantly higher estradiol production and serum estradiol in smokers but no significant difference in testosterone (PMID: 3169284).
