Evidence for unchanged DHEA is much thinner and comes mainly from older men with benign prostatic hyperplasia rather than young men; that study found higher estradiol, nonsignificantly lower testosterone, and no significant DHEA/DHEA-S difference in smokers (PMID: 9284188). Overall, the recurring supportive theme is elevated estradiol among smokers, while testosterone is often reported as unchanged; however, direct support for the DHEA part of the claim in young men is limited.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is mixed and often comes from populations that do not closely match the claim, such as middle-aged men or infertility cohorts. Some studies report no relationship between smoking and estradiol (PMID: 1535737, PMID: 3360176), although one of these tested only acute 30-minute smoking effects rather than chronic smoker-versus-nonsmoker differences, limiting direct comparability (PMID: 1535737). Several other studies contradict the claim's 'no meaningful difference' hormone component by finding higher testosterone and/or DHEA in smokers (PMID: 7962322, PMID: 12042277, PMID: 24279617) or lower testosterone with smoking (PMID: 22578234, PMID: 30399168), indicating inconsistency rather than a single opposing pattern. The strongest contradiction on DHEA comes from a large cross-sectional study of middle-aged men showing higher DHEA, DHEAS, and testosterone in smokers (PMID: 7962322).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall partially supported but with important uncertainty. Evidence more consistently supports higher estradiol in smokers, especially in younger/reproductive-age men, but the claim that testosterone and particularly DHEA are not meaningfully different is less secure because multiple contradictory studies report smoking-associated changes in those hormones, often in older or infertile populations.

statements.text: Young men who smoke cigarettes have lower serum luteinizing hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, and prolactin levels than young men who do not smoke.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Several studies contradict the claim, though not all evaluate all three hormones simultaneously. Smokers had higher prolactin in some studies (PMID: 2782983, PMID: 22534361), while one infertility cohort found lower prolactin in smokers (PMID: 12042277), creating a discrepancy specifically for prolactin. For gonadotropins, one study in healthy men found no significant differences in LH or FSH between smokers and nonsmokers (PMID: 24279617), whereas others reported higher LH and/or FSH in smokers rather than lower values (PMID: 22578234, PMID: 22534361, PMID: 30399168). Notably, some studies involve infertile men or broader adult populations rather than specifically young healthy men, so population differences may contribute to inconsistent findings, but the available abstracts do not support lower LH/FSH/prolactin in smokers.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. There is no direct supporting evidence here, and multiple abstracts report either unchanged or increased LH/FSH and mixed but often increased prolactin in smokers, which does not align with the claim that young male smokers have lower levels of all three hormones.

statements.text: Cigarette smoking in young men may adversely affect fertility by altering the endocrine profile and is associated with reduced sperm motility in men with low prolactin levels.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies report that cigarette smoking is associated with poorer semen quality, especially reduced sperm motility, lower sperm concentration/count, and in some studies more abnormal morphology, in smokers versus nonsmokers (PMID: 4054326, PMID: 8070715, PMID: 8172343, PMID: 23183716, PMID: 22578234, PMID: 17081174). Several abstracts also support endocrine disruption in male smokers, including higher estradiol and prolactin with normal testosterone (PMID: 2782983), higher LH/prolactin/testosterone in current smokers (PMID: 22534361), and higher FSH/LH with lower testosterone in infertile smokers (PMID: 22578234), while another study found lower testosterone without prolactin differences (PMID: 30399168). Overall, the recurring themes are impaired semen parameters plus altered reproductive hormones in smokers, supporting an adverse fertility effect. However, the specific component of the claim concerning reduced sperm motility specifically in men with low prolactin levels is not directly demonstrated in these abstracts, and some hormone findings differ across studies.
statements.contradict_summary: One large birth-cohort study of 20-22-year-old men found that smoking was not associated with significant semen variables, testicular volume, or circulating reproductive hormones (PMID: 26409015). This directly contradicts the claim's suggestion that smoking in young men adversely affects fertility through endocrine alterations. It also does not support a smoking-related association with reduced sperm motility. Thus, the main contradictory theme is a null association in a relatively population-based young male cohort.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Most studies indicate smoking is linked to reduced sperm motility/other semen impairments and to altered reproductive hormones, but the evidence is weaker for the claim's more specific prolactin-related mechanism, and one substantial cohort study reports no significant smoking effect.

source_article.title: RETRACTED: Differential Regulation of the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN Pathway between Low- and High-Grade Gliomas.
source_article.abstract: Gliomas represent 70% of all central system nervous tumors and are classified according to the degree of malignancy as low- or high-grade. The permanent activation of the EGFR/PI3K/AKT pathway by various genetic or post-translational alterations of EGFR, PI3KCA, and PTEN has been associated with increased proliferation and resistance to apoptosis. The present study aimed to analyze the molecular/genetic changes in the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN pathway between low-grade and high-grade gliomas in a sample of Colombian patients. A total of 30 samples were tested for PI3K and PTEN mutations, EGFR, PI3K, and AKT gene amplification, AKT, PI3K, BAX, Bcl2 expression levels, and phosphorylation of AKT and PTEN, EGFR and/or PI3K gene amplification was found in 50% of low-grade and 45% of high-grade ones. AKT amplification was found in 25% of the low-grade and 13.6% of the high-grade. The expression of PI3K, AKT, Bcl2, and BAX was increased particularly to a high degree. AKT phosphorylation was found in 66% of low-grade and 31.8% of high-grade. Increased phosphorylation of PTEN was found in 77% low-grade and 66% high-grade. Our results indicate that alterations in the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN pathway could be important in the initiation and malignant progression of this type of tumor.

statements.text: In glioma samples, alterations in the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN signaling pathway were associated with glioma initiation and malignant progression.
statements.support_summary: Multiple human glioma studies report that components of the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN axis are altered and track with tumor grade or aggressive behavior. In astrocytomas, EGFR expression rose from low- to high-grade tumors while PTEN loss and Akt activation were common, leading the authors to conclude these are early events and that EGFR helps drive progression (PMID: 17822324). In diffuse gliomas, Akt activation correlated significantly with glioma grade and was linked functionally to PTEN/PI3K signaling, with the abstract explicitly stating that PTEN loss and EGFR amplification contribute to progression (PMID: 15184909). Additional studies found PTEN promoter methylation or loss of PTEN expression associated with PI3K/Akt pathway activation, higher grade, secondary GBM evolution, higher proliferation indices, worse survival, or poorer outcome in pediatric and adult gliomas (PMID: 22753230, PMID: 17504928, PMID: 18519768, PMID: 25823655, PMID: 14655756, PMID: 12084351). Several other items are reviews, in vitro/in vivo mechanistic studies, classification papers, or non-human/canine data that generally align with the claim but are more indirect or lower-level evidence, and one supportive item appears discrepant because its abstract is truncated and does not by itself document the claimed association (PMID: 34209611; also PMID: 26248734, PMID: 34913078, PMID: 21842313, PMID: 18565348, PMID: 36916430, PMID: 39061577).
statements.contradict_summary: There is no substantive biological study here directly disputing the association. The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a study on differential regulation of the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN pathway in low- versus high-grade gliomas, which undermines that specific paper but does not itself provide evidence against the broader claim (PMID: 40536529). Thus, this item is best viewed as a reliability concern about one prior report rather than affirmative contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The evidence includes several human tumor studies showing EGFR upregulation, PTEN loss/methylation, and Akt/PI3K pathway activation associated with glioma grade, progression, and aggressive features, whereas the only opposing item is a retraction notice that does not directly refute the broader pathway-level association.

statements.text: Low-grade gliomas and high-grade gliomas both showed frequent EGFR or PI3K gene amplification and increased expression of PI3K, AKT, Bcl2, and BAX, indicating that this pathway is commonly dysregulated across glioma grades.
statements.support_summary: One pediatric glioma study found PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway activation in both high-grade and low-grade tumors, with activation markers present in 80% of high-grade cases and roughly 44-50% of low-grade cases, alongside frequent PTEN promoter methylation across grades (PMID: 22753230). This supports the narrower idea that the PI3K/Akt pathway is commonly dysregulated in gliomas of different grades. However, the abstract does not report EGFR or PI3K gene amplification, nor increased expression of PI3K, AKT, Bcl2, or BAX specifically, so support for the exact claim is only partial.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a study on differential regulation of the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN pathway in low- versus high-grade gliomas (PMID: 40536529). Because the abstract is only a retraction notice and provides no replacement data, it does not itself present direct evidence that the claim is false; rather, it undermines confidence in findings from the retracted article. This is therefore best treated as a reliability concern or evidence gap, not strong biological contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported overall. There is limited abstract-level evidence that PI3K/Akt pathway dysregulation occurs in both low- and high-grade gliomas, but the specific assertions about frequent EGFR/PI3K amplification and increased PI3K, AKT, Bcl2, and BAX expression are not directly supported here, while the lone opposing item is a retraction notice rather than substantive contradictory data.

statements.text: Low-grade gliomas and high-grade gliomas showed abnormal AKT and PTEN phosphorylation, suggesting that post-translational changes in the EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN pathway are involved in glioma biology.
statements.support_summary: Several primary studies support dysregulation of the PI3K/AKT/PTEN axis across glioma grades. In astrocytomas, phosphorylated Akt was detected in most low-grade and high-grade tumors, while PTEN expression was absent in many cases, leading the authors to conclude that PTEN alteration and Akt activation occur early and persist through progression (PMID: 17822324). Pediatric gliomas of all grades also showed frequent PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway activation with reduced PTEN expression, and pathway activation increased with grade (PMID: 22753230). PTEN promoter methylation in grade II/III gliomas and secondary GBM correlated with Akt phosphorylation, indicating functional PI3K pathway activation in both lower- and higher-grade disease (PMID: 17504928), and a large diffuse glioma series found Akt activation positively associated with grade and linked functionally to PTEN status (PMID: 15184909). Additional items mainly provide indirect or mechanistic support in glioma/GBM models or reviews rather than direct evidence of AKT and PTEN phosphorylation in both low- and high-grade gliomas (PMIDs: 36801645, 26248734, 21842313, 25823655, 12084351, 36916430), and one cBioPortal item is too incomplete from the abstract to weigh strongly (PMID: 34209611).
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for an article on differential EGFR/PI3K/AKT/PTEN regulation between low- and high-grade gliomas (PMID: 40536529). This undermines that specific study as evidence, but the notice abstract itself does not present experimental data contradicting the claim. Thus, it is better interpreted as reducing confidence in one line of evidence rather than establishing the opposite conclusion.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. Multiple observational studies directly or indirectly show Akt activation/phosphorylation and PTEN loss or pathway dysregulation across low- and high-grade gliomas, whereas the lone opposing item is only a retraction notice and not substantive counterevidence.

matched_article.title: Cerebrospinal Fluid Biomarkers for Alzheimer's Disease in the Era of Disease-Modifying Treatments.
matched_article.abstract: Correct in vivo diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease (AD) helps to avoid administration of disease-modifying treatments in non-AD patients, and allows the possible use of such treatments in clinically atypical AD patients. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) biomarkers offer a tool for AD diagnosis. A reduction in CSF β-amyloid (marker of amyloid plaque burden), although compatible with Alzheimer's pathological change, may also be observed in other dementing disorders, including vascular cognitive disorders due to subcortical small-vessel disease, dementia with Lewy bodies and normal-pressure hydrocephalus. Thus, for the diagnosis of AD, an abnormal result of CSF β-amyloid may not be sufficient, and an increase in phospho-tau (marker of tangle pathology) is also required in order to confirm AD diagnosis in patients with a typical amnestic presentation and reveal underlying AD in patients with atypical or mixed and diagnostically confusing clinical presentations.

statements.text: In patients being evaluated for Alzheimer’s disease, an abnormal cerebrospinal fluid β-amyloid result alone is not sufficient to confirm Alzheimer’s disease because reduced β-amyloid can also occur in other dementing disorders.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies indicate that reduced CSF Aβ42 is not specific to Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and can also be seen in other dementing disorders, including vascular dementia, dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB), Parkinson’s disease dementia, idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus, and even prion disease (PMID: 26133663, PMID: 12411760, PMID: 14676043, PMID: 30741681, PMID: 30883350). Large cohorts and differential-diagnosis studies consistently report that Aβ42 alone has limited specificity, while combinations with tau/p-tau or ratios such as Aβ42/Aβ40 or p-tau/Aβ42 perform better for distinguishing AD from non-AD dementias (PMID: 20043812, PMID: 22646065, PMID: 24016466, PMID: 29604263, PMID: 31345148). Several DLB-focused studies further show overlap in amyloid abnormalities between AD and DLB, including reduced Aβ42 in demented-stage DLB and even no difference in Aβ42 between AD and DLB in one study, reinforcing that amyloid abnormality alone cannot confirm AD (PMID: 26923009, PMID: 29321140, PMID: 11487210, PMID: 31601267). Framework and validation papers also explicitly support a multi-biomarker approach, noting that both amyloid and tau evidence are needed for biologically defined AD and that AD-type biomarker profiles are found in substantial proportions of non-AD clinical syndromes (PMID: 31615545, PMID: 31686097, PMID: 25150736). A few cited support items are only indirectly relevant or are broad reviews/guidance rather than direct demonstrations of reduced Aβ in other dementias, but the overall abstract-level evidence strongly supports the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The evidence base includes multiple large cohorts, differential-diagnosis studies, and reviews showing that low CSF β-amyloid/Aβ42 occurs in several non-AD dementing disorders and that more specific diagnosis of AD requires additional biomarkers such as tau/p-tau or biomarker ratios.

statements.text: In patients with a typical amnestic presentation being evaluated for Alzheimer’s disease, increased cerebrospinal fluid phospho-tau is needed, together with abnormal cerebrospinal fluid β-amyloid, to confirm Alzheimer’s disease diagnosis.
statements.support_summary: Supportive abstracts largely describe CSF Aβ42, p-tau, and often total tau as the established core biomarker panel for biologically defining or improving diagnosis of Alzheimer’s disease, especially in prodromal, atypical, mixed, or diagnostically ambiguous cases (PMID: 24016466, PMID: 29562530). Several studies explicitly align with biomarker frameworks in which both amyloid and phosphorylated tau evidence are required to define AD biologically in living patients, though these are framework-oriented and not specific to a typical amnestic presentation (PMID: 31615545, PMID: 31686097). Other studies show that combining markers or using ratios such as p-tau/Aβ42 or tau/Aβ42 improves diagnostic accuracy compared with single markers alone, again implying added value of p-tau alongside amyloid abnormality (PMID: 26401938, PMID: 29154286, PMID: 28161476). Longitudinal and clinic-based studies also report that lower Aβ42 with higher p-tau/tau is associated with AD, worse cognition, or progression from MCI, including amnestic MCI in some analyses (PMID: 12603251, PMID: 31589110, PMID: 27472875). However, much of this support is indirect because many abstracts emphasize usefulness or improved accuracy rather than stating that increased CSF p-tau is strictly necessary to confirm AD in patients with a typical amnestic syndrome.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence includes a small Thai dementia cohort in which decreased CSF Aβ42 was common in AD but increased p-tau181 was essentially absent, directly arguing against p-tau being required for AD confirmation (PMID: 21721431). Another prospective study found that patients with elevated CSF p-tau but normal/high Aβ42 and normal Aβ42/Aβ40 often had negative amyloid PET, indicating that p-tau elevation without amyloid abnormality does not support AD and underscoring the primacy of amyloid evidence (PMID: 30963253). A differentiation study reported that p-tau was elevated only in probable AD versus controls, but concluded that p-tau, tau, and Aβ42 were insufficient to fully discriminate dementia etiologies in clinical practice, which weakens any claim of a strict required combination for confirmation (PMID: 12411760). One microvesicle study is only loosely relevant to the claim, but it reported no group difference in phospho-tau between AD and controls despite established AD diagnosis, creating a discrepancy with the notion that elevated p-tau is always needed (PMID: 34295239).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, mainly because multiple reviews, framework papers, and diagnostic studies treat combined amyloid and phosphorylated tau abnormalities as the preferred biological signature of AD. However, the evidence is not definitive for the narrower claim about typical amnestic presentations, and several studies show that AD can be present without elevated CSF p-tau or that biomarker constellations are more heterogeneous than the statement implies.

statements.text: In patients with atypical, mixed, or diagnostically confusing dementia presentations, increased cerebrospinal fluid phospho-tau helps reveal underlying Alzheimer’s disease when cerebrospinal fluid β-amyloid is abnormal.
statements.support_summary: Several abstracts support the diagnostic value of an Alzheimer-type CSF profile combining low Aβ42 with elevated p-tau/tau, especially when presentations are atypical, mixed, or clinically ambiguous. A directly relevant tertiary-center study found that among patients with an AD CSF profile (high tau/p-tau, low Aβ42), many had mixed, atypical, or unusual syndromes, and concluded CSF biomarkers are useful for antemortem identification of such cases (PMID: 36553018). Reviews and cohort studies similarly state that core CSF biomarkers, including p-tau181, add value in differential diagnosis of AD versus related disorders and are helpful in mixed pathologies or ambiguous dementia diagnoses (PMID: 29562530, PMID: 22646065), while large datasets show the highest p-tau and lowest Aβ42 in AD relative to other dementias (PMID: 26133663). Ratio-based studies further suggest p-tau181/Aβ42 improves discrimination of AD in heterogeneous dementia populations and in clinically mismatched cases (PMID: 31345148, PMID: 26401938), although several of these studies are indirect rather than specifically focused on confusing presentations.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence mainly emphasizes limits in the sensitivity or specificity of p-tau, and that abnormal amyloid alone can occur in non-AD disorders or that p-tau is not always elevated. A small Thai dementia cohort reported very low sensitivity of increased p-tau181 for AD despite frequent low Aβ42, directly challenging the idea that raised p-tau reliably reveals AD when amyloid is abnormal (PMID: 21721431). Other studies show p-tau is not universally informative: it did not differ from controls in one AD-vs-control microvesicle study (PMID: 34295239), was not associated with decline in Lewy body dementia even when AD biomarkers were assessed (PMID: 27296778), and biomarkers could not fully discriminate AD from vascular dementia in clinical practice (PMID: 12411760). However, several items in this group are only indirectly contradictory because they actually show p-tau is lower in non-AD mimics such as DLB or iNPH, which can also be interpreted as supporting its usefulness for differential diagnosis rather than refuting the claim outright (PMID: 26923009, PMID: 25428256, PMID: 30741681, PMID: 30883350).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence indicates that an AD-pattern CSF profile with low Aβ42 plus elevated p-tau/tau is useful for identifying underlying AD in atypical or diagnostically confusing dementias, though some studies show p-tau alone may have limited sensitivity or inconsistent performance across settings.

source_article.title: Structural characterization and optical properties of Ca2MgSi2O7:Eu(2+),Dy(3+) phosphor by solid-state reaction method.
source_article.abstract: Ca2MgSi2O7:Eu(2+),Dy(3+) phosphor was prepared by the solid-state reaction method under a weak reducing atmosphere. The obtained phosphor was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) techniques. The phase structure of the Ca2MgSi2O7:Eu(2+),Dy(3+) phosphor was akermanite type, which is a member of the melilite group. The surface morphology of the sintered phosphor was not uniform and phosphors aggregated tightly. EDX and FT-IR spectra confirm the elements present in the Ca2MgSi2O7:Eu(2+),Dy(3+) phosphor. Under UV excitation, a broadband emission spectrum was found. The emission spectra observed in the green region centered at 535 nm, which is due to the 4f-5d transition. The mechanoluminescence (ML) intensity of the prepared phosphor increased linearly with increases in the mechanical load. The ML spectra were similar to the photoluminescence (PL), which indicates that ML is emitted from the same emitting center of Eu(2+) ions as PL.

statements.text: Ca2MgSi2O7 phosphor doped with Eu(2+) and Dy(3+) exhibits green broadband photoluminescence under ultraviolet excitation, with emission arising from Eu(2+) ions.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract reports that Ca2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ prepared by solid-state reaction shows a main emission peak at 530 nm under UV excitation, described as a broad emission attributed to the Eu2+ 4f6 5d1 → 4f7 transition, and confirms green color by CIE analysis (PMID: 25693949). This directly supports the parts of the claim concerning green broadband photoluminescence under UV excitation and emission arising from Eu2+ ions. However, this study does not include Dy3+ doping, so it only partially addresses the full Eu2+/Dy3+-codoped claim.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted based on the available evidence. The support is limited because the cited study clearly supports green broad Eu2+-based UV-excited emission in Ca2MgSi2O7 (PMID: 25693949), but it does not evaluate the added Dy3+ component of the claim.

statements.text: Ca2MgSi2O7 phosphor doped with Eu(2+) and Dy(3+) shows mechanoluminescence whose intensity increases with mechanical load.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract reports that Ca2MgSi2O7:Eu2+ prepared by solid-state reaction exhibits mechanoluminescence, and that ML intensity increases linearly with increasing impact velocity, consistent with stronger mechanical stimulation producing stronger emission (PMID: 25693949). The same study also states that the ML spectrum resembles the photoluminescence spectrum, indicating Eu2+ as the emitting center (PMID: 25693949). However, this evidence is only a partial match to the claim because the abstract studies Eu2+ doping alone and does not mention Dy3+, so there is a discrepancy between the claim and the available supporting evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly to moderately supported overall. The available abstract supports load-dependent mechanoluminescence in Ca2MgSi2O7 with Eu2+ doping, but it does not address the specific Eu2+/Dy3+ co-doped material in the claim.

statements.text: The mechanoluminescence spectrum of Ca2MgSi2O7 phosphor doped with Eu(2+) and Dy(3+) is similar to its photoluminescence spectrum, indicating a common emitting center from Eu(2+) ions.
statements.support_summary: One related study on Ca2MgSi2O7 doped with Eu2+ alone reported that the mechanoluminescence spectrum was similar to the photoluminescence spectrum, and explicitly interpreted this as emission arising from the same Eu2+ luminescent center (PMID: 25693949). The abstract also identifies the main PL emission at 530 nm as the Eu2+ 4f(6)5d(1)→4f(7) transition, consistent with Eu2+ being the emitting center (PMID: 25693949). However, this study did not include Dy3+ co-doping, so it only partially supports the specific claim about Ca2MgSi2O7:Eu2+,Dy3+ and should be viewed as indirect rather than exact evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall weakly supported. The only available evidence directly supports the ML–PL spectral similarity and common Eu2+ emitting center in Ca2MgSi2O7, but it is limited because the study examined Eu2+ doping without Dy3+ co-doping.

matched_article.title: Derivative emission spectrofluorimetry for the simultaneous determination of guaifenesin and phenylephrine hydrochloride in pharmaceutical tablets.
matched_article.abstract: Rapid, simple and sensitive derivative emission spectrofluorimetric methods have been developed for the simultaneous analysis of binary mixtures of guaifenesin (GUA) and phenylephrine hydrochloride (PHE). The methods are based upon measurement of the native fluorescence intensity of the two drugs at λex = 275 nm in methanolic solutions, followed by differentiation using first (D1) and second (D2) derivative techniques. The derivative fluorescence intensity-concentration plots were rectilinear over a range of 0.1-2 µg/mL for both GUA and PHE. The limits of detection were 0.027 (D1, GUA), 0.025 (D2, GUA), 0.031 (D1, PHE) and 0.033 (D2, PHE) µg/mL and limits of quantitation were 0.089 (D1, GUA), 0.083 (D2, GUA), 0.095 (D1, PHE) and 0.097 (D2, PHE) µg/mL. The proposed derivative emission spectrofluorimetric methods (D1 and D2) were successfully applied for the determination of the two compounds in binary mixtures and tablets with high precision and accuracy. The proposed methods were fully validated as per ICH guidelines.

statements.text: Derivative emission spectrofluorimetric methods can simultaneously determine guaifenesin and phenylephrine hydrochloride in binary mixtures and pharmaceutical tablets with high precision and accuracy.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: The developed derivative emission spectrofluorimetric methods for guaifenesin and phenylephrine hydrochloride are sufficiently sensitive for low-level quantification in methanolic solution.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: The developed methods are suitable for validated routine analysis of guaifenesin and phenylephrine hydrochloride in tablet formulations.
statements.support_summary: Several studies describe validated analytical methods that include both guaifenesin and phenylephrine and report successful application to pharmaceutical dosage forms, which is broadly consistent with suitability for routine analysis. A robust HPLC/DAD and HPTLC study validated methods for mixtures containing guaifenesin and phenylephrine and successfully applied them to laboratory-prepared tablets, with validation covering linearity, precision, accuracy, sensitivity, and stability (PMID: 31504290). Additional support comes from a fully validated HPLC method for cough preparations including phenylephrine hydrochloride and guaifenesin, with satisfactory results in commercial combinations and no significant difference from official methods, although the abstract does not specifically emphasize tablets alone (PMID: 27404374). A green validated chromatographic method explicitly states it could be used for routine analysis of phenylephrine, paracetamol, and guaifenesin in a ternary pharmaceutical mixture (PMID: 27062581), and chemometric spectrophotometric methods were also validated and successfully applied to pharmaceutical formulation analysis without excipient interference (PMID: 26254602). Some evidence is indirect because several abstracts concern multicomponent mixtures or pharmaceutical formulations generally rather than specifically guaifenesin plus phenylephrine hydrochloride tablets, but none appears to conflict with the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Multiple abstracts report validated, precise, and accurate methods that include guaifenesin and phenylephrine and were applied successfully to pharmaceutical formulations or tablets, though some support is indirect because the methods often target broader multicomponent mixtures rather than the exact formulation context of the claim.

source_article.title: Tan IIA inhibits H1299 cell viability through the MDM4‑IAP3 signaling pathway.
source_article.abstract: Tanshinone IIA (Tan IIA), as a bioactive compound extracted from the dried roots of Salvia miltiorrhiza (also known as Danshen), is known to inhibit cancer cell proliferation and induce apoptosis. However, the mechanisms underlying the function of Tan IIA in cancer cell apoptosis remain to be elucidated The aim of the present study was to identify the molecular mechanisms underlying the anti‑cancer effects of Tan IIA in p53‑deficient H1299 cells. Tan IIA was demonstrated to suppress murine double minute 4 (MDM4) expression in a time‑ and dose‑dependent manner through the inhibition of MDM4 mRNA synthesis. Tan IIA‑induced downregulation of MDM4 resulted in an increase of P73α and a decrease of inhibitor of apoptosis 3 (IAP3). However, P73α was not activated as two P73α target genes, BCL2 binding component 3 and phorbol‑12‑myristate‑13‑acetate‑induced protein 1, were not significantly induced. Tan IIA‑induced inhibition of IAP3 expression may be involved in Tan IIA‑induced apoptosis and inhibition of H1299 cell viability. Notably, a combination of Tan IIA and doxorubicin (DOX) exposure resulted in further MDM4 overexpression in H1299 cells, indicating that Tan IIA sensitized p53‑deficient and MDM4‑overexpressing H1299 cells to DOX‑induced apoptosis.

matched_article.title: Role of autophagy in a model of obesity: A long‑term high fat diet induces cardiac dysfunction.
matched_article.abstract: Obesity may induce end‑organ damage through metabolic syndrome, and autophagy serves a vital role in the pathogenesis of metabolic syndrome. The purpose of the present study was to define the roles of autophagy and mitophagy in high fat diet (HFD)‑induced cardiomyopathy. Male, 8 week‑old C57BL/6 mice were fed either a HFD (60% kcal) or a diet of normal chow (NC; 10% kcal) for 42 weeks. Glucose tolerance tests were performed during the feeding regimes. Blood samples were collected for assaying serum triglyceride with the glycerol‑3‑phosphate oxidase phenol and aminophenazone (PAP) method and total cholesterol was tested with the cholesterol oxidase‑PAP method. Myocardial function was assessed using echocardiography and hemodynamic analyses. Western blot analysis was employed to evaluate endoplasmic reticulum stress (ERS), autophagy and mitochondrial function. Electron microscopy was used to assess the number of lipid droplets and the degree of autophagy within the myocardium. The body weight and adipose tissue weight of mice fed the HFD were increased compared with the NC mice. The serum levels of blood glucose, total cholesterol and triglyceride were significantly increased following 42 weeks of HFD feeding. The results of the glucose tolerance tests additionally demonstrated metabolic dysregulation in HFD mice. In addition, HFD mice exhibited hemodynamic and echocardiographic evidence of impaired diastolic and systolic function, including alterations in the cardiac output, end‑diastolic pressure, end‑diastolic volume and left ventricular relaxation time constant (tau) following HFD intake. Furthermore, a HFD resulted in increased ERS, and a downregulation of the autophagy and mitophagy level. The present study investigated cardiac function in obese HFD‑fed mice. These results aid the pursuit of novel therapeutic targets to combat obesity‑associated cardiomyopathy.

statements.text: Long-term high-fat diet feeding in mice causes obesity, metabolic dysregulation, and cardiac dysfunction compared with normal chow feeding.
statements.support_summary: Multiple mouse studies directly comparing high-fat diet (HFD) with normal/chow diets report increased body weight/adiposity plus metabolic abnormalities such as hyperglycemia, glucose intolerance/insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, and dyslipidemia, often alongside cardiac remodeling or dysfunction (e.g., hypertrophy, fibrosis, reduced ejection fraction/fractional shortening, diastolic dysfunction, arrhythmogenic/electrical changes) (PMID: 23593350, 26036798, 31792335, 27151515, 29118818, 25142225). Several longer-duration studies are especially aligned with the claim, including 16 months, 24 weeks, and 20+ week models showing obesity/metabolic syndrome features together with impaired cardiac reserve, fibrosis, systolic or diastolic dysfunction, and structural remodeling relative to chow-fed controls (PMID: 23593350, 26358164, 25782072, 26631050). Many intervention papers also implicitly support causality because the untreated HFD groups consistently exhibit obesity and metabolic/cardiac abnormalities that treatments partially reverse (PMID: 33688395, 24693333, 26157343, 30633969, 29989028). Recurring mechanistic themes include lipid accumulation/lipotoxicity, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, altered autophagy/mitophagy, and impaired cardiac insulin signaling in HFD-fed mice (PMID: 30786833, 31153974, 28826719, 32039238, 37534099). A few support-group items are only partial or indirect because they focus on non-cardiac outcomes or special strains/genotypes, but the overall pattern across abstracts strongly supports the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: The clearest contradictory abstract reports that more than six months of 60% fat feeding caused robust adiposity and impaired glucose disposal but no significant echocardiographic cardiac dysfunction, and did not worsen infarct-induced heart failure, directly challenging the cardiac-dysfunction portion of the claim (PMID: 24367585). Other contradiction items are more qualified: one pregnancy/postpartum study found that only obesity-prone HFHS mice, not diet exposure alone, developed glucose intolerance and transient systolic impairment, suggesting obesity rather than diet per se drove the phenotype in that context (PMID: 34508150). Another study used a juvenile HFD+streptozotocin two-hit model and found metabolic impairment without obesity, but it did not assess cardiac dysfunction, so it only partially conflicts with the obesity component of the claim (PMID: 37061011). One Plin5 transgenic study is miscategorized as contradiction/noisy because it actually reports long-term HFD-mediated contractile dysfunction in that genotype, although the mice were relatively protected from obesity and glucose intolerance compared with expectations (PMID: 37150323). Overall, contradiction centers on inconsistency of cardiac dysfunction across models rather than on the obesity/metabolic-dysregulation components, which are still often present.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Across many mouse abstracts, long-term HFD consistently produces obesity and metabolic dysregulation and frequently causes cardiac remodeling/dysfunction versus chow, although at least one substantial study suggests cardiac dysfunction is not inevitable and may depend on model, strain, duration, or physiological context.

statements.text: Long-term high-fat diet feeding in mice increases endoplasmic reticulum stress in the myocardium and reduces myocardial autophagy and mitophagy compared with normal chow feeding.
statements.support_summary: Several mouse studies support parts of the claim that high-fat diet (HFD) adversely alters cardiac stress and degradative pathways. In one 24-week HFD model, myocardial CHOP was increased, consistent with increased ER stress, while LC3-II and p62 accumulation indicated autophagy dysregulation rather than clearly preserved flux (PMID: 26358164). Another 5-month HFD study reported reduced myocardial autophagy markers, including decreased LC3-II conversion and Beclin-1 with increased p62, versus normal diet (PMID: 25018087). A separate study found decreased cardiac Parkin protein in HFD-fed obese mice versus low-fat controls, supporting impaired mitophagy machinery (PMID: 32039238). Overall, the supporting literature most consistently indicates increased ER-stress signaling and reduced general autophagy markers, with only indirect/partial support for reduced mitophagy.
statements.contradict_summary: Multiple studies directly contradict the claim regarding mitophagy, reporting that cardiac mitophagy increases during HFD feeding rather than decreases. Using Mito-Keima and related models, mitophagy was increased after 3 weeks of HFD and remained elevated through 2 months or even 20–24 weeks, despite evidence that general autophagic flux peaks early and later declines (PMID: 30786833; PMID: 34724805; PMID: 37232152). A review/commentary reiterates this pattern, stating that chronic HFD downregulates general macroautophagy while activating alternative mitophagy in the heart (PMID: 35025704). Another heart/liver time-course study also weakens the claim by finding no change in cardiac LC3 over 16 weeks and no clear induction of cardiac ER-stress markers, with PERK actually suppressed and procaspase-12 activation not seen in heart (PMID: 26264388). Thus, the main contradiction is a recurring dissociation: conventional autophagy may fall with prolonged HFD, but mitophagy appears maintained or increased via alternative pathways.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. While there is moderate evidence that long-term HFD can increase some myocardial ER-stress markers and reduce general autophagy, stronger and more consistent evidence indicates that cardiac mitophagy is not reduced during chronic HFD exposure and may instead increase through alternative mitophagy mechanisms.

statements.text: Cardiac dysfunction in high-fat diet-fed obese mice is associated with obesity-related cardiomyopathy.
statements.support_summary: Most supporting studies report that high-fat diet (HFD) feeding in mice induces obesity together with cardiac structural and functional abnormalities consistent with obesity-related cardiomyopathy, including hypertrophy, fibrosis, systolic and/or diastolic dysfunction, impaired strain, altered ECGs, lipid accumulation, oxidative stress, mitochondrial damage, and contractile defects (e.g., PMID: 26036798, PMID: 25018087, PMID: 27804274, PMID: 31792335, PMID: 29118818, PMID: 25782072, PMID: 39007149). Several abstracts explicitly frame these phenotypes as obesity-associated or obesity-induced cardiomyopathy and show that targeting implicated pathways (CD36, CPT-1b, mitophagy/DRP1-Ulk1-Rab9, autotaxin, autophagy, oxidative stress, inflammation, metabolism) ameliorates the cardiac phenotype, reinforcing the association (PMID: 34724805, PMID: 37232152, PMID: 31247591, PMID: 35057432, PMID: 33949876). Long-duration diet models also report reduced contractile reserve, fibrosis, hypertrophy, and remodeling resembling early diabetic/obesity cardiomyopathy (PMID: 23593350). Some evidence is indirect or less specific because it focuses on mechanisms, interventions, review articles, other species, or injury rather than explicit cardiomyopathy terminology, and one supporting item also notes a resistant mouse strain despite obesity, indicating heterogeneity across models (PMID: 26408147). Overall, the abstracts largely support that cardiac dysfunction in HFD-fed obese mice commonly co-occurs with an obesity-related cardiomyopathy phenotype.
statements.contradict_summary: One strong contradictory study found that more than 6 months of 60% fat feeding produced robust adiposity and impaired glucose disposal but no significant decrement in echocardiographic cardiac function, and HFD did not worsen infarct-induced heart failure, directly arguing that HFD-induced obesity does not necessarily cause cardiac dysfunction in mice (PMID: 24367585). A second study in pregnancy/postpartum mice reported that cardiac dysfunction occurred in the obesity-prone subgroup, while the high-fat/high-sucrose diet alone was insufficient, concluding that obesity rather than diet exposure per se negatively affected cardiac adaptation (PMID: 34508150). This second article is not a full contradiction to the claim because it still links dysfunction to obesity, but it does challenge any simple assumption that HFD alone reliably induces cardiomyopathy. Together, the contradictory evidence highlights model-, phenotype-, and context-dependence in whether HFD-fed mice develop measurable cardiac dysfunction.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Numerous mouse abstracts consistently describe HFD-induced obesity with cardiac remodeling/dysfunction framed as obesity-related cardiomyopathy, whereas the main contradiction is a notable but relatively isolated negative study showing that this phenotype is not universal across all murine HFD models.

source_article.title: Metallic Conductivity in a Two-Dimensional Cobalt Dithiolene Metal-Organic Framework.
source_article.abstract: Two-dimensional (2D) metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have received a great deal of attention due to their relatively high charge carrier mobility and low resistivity. Here we report on the temperature-dependent charge transport properties of a 2D cobalt 2,3,6,7,10,11-triphenylenehexathiolate framework. Variable temperature resistivity studies reveal a transition from a semiconducting to a metallic phase with decreasing temperature, which is unprecedented in MOFs. We find this transition to be highly dependent on the film thickness and the amount of solvent trapped in the pores, with density functional theory calculations of the electronic-structure supporting the complex metallic conductivity of the material. These results identify the first experimentally observed MOF that exhibits band-like metallic conductivity.

statements.text: A two-dimensional cobalt triphenylenehexathiolate metal-organic framework exhibits a temperature-dependent transition from semiconducting behavior to metallic conductivity, showing band-like metallic transport in a metal-organic framework.

matched_article.title: Diversity of Secondary Structure in Catalytic Peptides with β-Turn-Biased Sequences.
matched_article.abstract: X-ray crystallography has been applied to the structural analysis of a series of tetrapeptides that were previously assessed for catalytic activity in an atroposelective bromination reaction. Common to the series is a central Pro-Xaa sequence, where Pro is either l- or d-proline, which was chosen to favor nucleation of canonical β-turn secondary structures. Crystallographic analysis of 35 different peptide sequences revealed a range of conformational states. The observed differences appear not only in cases where the Pro-Xaa loop-region is altered, but also when seemingly subtle alterations to the flanking residues are introduced. In many instances, distinct conformers of the same sequence were observed, either as symmetry-independent molecules within the same unit cell or as polymorphs. Computational studies using DFT provided additional insight into the analysis of solid-state structural features. Select X-ray crystal structures were compared to the corresponding solution structures derived from measured proton chemical shifts, 3J-values, and 1H-1H-NOESY contacts. These findings imply that the conformational space available to simple peptide-based catalysts is more diverse than precedent might suggest. The direct observation of multiple ground state conformations for peptides of this family, as well as the dynamic processes associated with conformational equilibria, underscore not only the challenge of designing peptide-based catalysts, but also the difficulty in predicting their accessible transition states. These findings implicate the advantages of low-barrier interconversions between conformations of peptide-based catalysts for multistep, enantioselective reactions.

statements.text: Peptide-based catalysts with a central Pro-Xaa sequence can adopt a diverse range of ground-state conformations, including multiple conformers of the same sequence.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support conformational diversity in proline-containing peptide scaffolds, including multiple conformers accessible to the same sequence. Crystal, NMR, and computational studies show that heterochiral Pro-Pro or Pro-Xaa motifs can populate distinct turn, extended, helical, and cis/trans states, sometimes with multiple states observed for one peptide sequence (PMID: 18335426, PMID: 19562756, PMID: 16771506, PMID: 35880873). In catalyst-relevant systems, peptidic catalysts were described as conformationally mobile or heterogeneous, with multiple distinct conformers identified by MD/NMR and different ground-state conformations deliberately stabilized in designed analogues (PMID: 30687577, PMID: 34659874); related structural work on H-dPro-Pro-Xaa catalysts also found a common β-turn-like motif but with C-terminal residue-dependent variation, indicating flexibility within a preorganized framework (PMID: 27403581). Additional indirect support comes from modeling and structural analyses of cyclic Pro-Xaa motifs and prolyl peptide/peptoid hybrids that suggest ensembles of β-turn and coiled conformations or conformational features relevant to catalysis (PMID: 12932582, PMID: 24053491). A limitation is that several supporting papers concern model peptides or catalyst classes not exactly matching the specific central Pro-Xaa catalyst motif in the claim, so relevance is sometimes indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The evidence consistently indicates that proline-centered peptide scaffolds, including catalyst-related peptides, can access multiple ground-state conformations and in some cases multiple conformers of the same sequence, although several studies are indirect rather than exact matches to the specific Pro-Xaa catalyst context.

statements.text: Small changes in residues flanking the central Pro-Xaa loop region can substantially alter the conformation of catalytic tetrapeptides.
statements.support_summary: Structural analysis of a catalytic β-turn tetrapeptide and a residue-substituted analogue found that the more selective variant adopted a type I' β-turn in both crystal and solution, and that this conformation was also accessible to the original catalyst, indicating that sequence changes can shift conformational preferences in catalytic tetrapeptides (PMID: 26963788). A separate conformational study of proline-containing tetrapeptides showed that varying residues at positions flanking the proline-containing turn strongly affected cis/trans prolyl amide populations and whether type VIa β-turn conformations were adopted, supporting a broader sequence-dependence of Pro-centered loop geometry (PMID: 11890796). However, the latter study was not on catalytic tetrapeptides specifically, so its relevance is indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The support is moderate because one study directly examines catalytic tetrapeptides and shows residue substitutions alter accessible conformations (PMID: 26963788), while the second provides indirect but consistent evidence that small sequence changes around proline-containing turns can substantially affect peptide geometry (PMID: 11890796).

statements.text: The broad conformational diversity and conformational equilibria of simple peptide-based catalysts complicate the design and prediction of their accessible transition states in enantioselective catalysis.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support the idea that peptide catalyst conformational heterogeneity complicates mechanistic interpretation and prediction in enantioselective catalysis. A parameterization study of flexible β-turn tetrapeptide catalysts found that although one conformer may dominate enantioselectivity, multiple conformers may contribute to a complex transition-state ensemble, directly linking flexibility to difficulty in assigning a single reactive state (PMID: 29300461). Molecular dynamics and NMR work likewise identified multiple peptide and catalyst-substrate conformers, showed substrate-dependent shifts in conformational equilibrium, and concluded that catalytic properties correlate with the conformational ensemble, emphasizing the challenge these dynamic systems pose for study and modeling (PMID: 30687577). A further study states explicitly that relating conformational dynamics to selectivity in a flexible peptidic catalyst is challenging and uses macrocyclization to isolate conformers, though the abstract is truncated and provides only indirect support for the specific transition-state prediction claim (PMID: 34659874).
statements.contradict_summary: One review-like article provides partial contradiction by arguing that although conformational flexibility was initially viewed as a liability in peptide catalysis, peptide catalysts have nevertheless been developed for many highly site- and enantioselective transformations, and flexibility may even be advantageous for asymmetric induction in some cases (PMID: 30525436). However, this abstract does not directly show that accessible transition states are easy to design or predict; rather, it presents a more optimistic perspective on flexibility's functional consequences. Thus, this item is better viewed as tempering the claim than directly refuting it.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest abstract-level evidence consistently shows that flexible peptide catalysts populate multiple conformers and substrate-dependent equilibria, making selectivity analysis and transition-state assignment more complex, whereas the lone contrary item mainly argues that such flexibility can sometimes be beneficial rather than disproving the underlying complication.

source_article.title: Skullcapflavone I protects cardiomyocytes from hypoxia-caused injury through up-regulation of lincRNA-ROR.
source_article.abstract: Myocardial infarction (MI) is a serious heart disease in which cardiomyocytes are damaged, caused by hypoxia. This study explored the possible protective activity of Skullcapflavone I (SF I), a flavonoid isolated from the root of Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi, on hypoxia-stimulated cardiomyocytes cell injury in vitro. Viability and apoptosis of H9c2 cells and primary cardiomyocytes were tested using cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay and Guava Nexin Reagent, respectively. Quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) was used to measure the long non-coding RNA regulator of reprogramming (lincRNA-ROR) expression. si-ROR was transfected to knockdown lincRNA-ROR. Western blotting was conducted to assess the protein levels of key molecules related to cell proliferation, apoptosis, and mitogen-activated protein kinase/extracellular signal-regulated kinase (MEK/ERK) pathway. We discovered that hypoxia stimulation obviously reduced H9c2 cell and primary cardiomyocytes' viability and proliferation, but promoted cell apoptosis. SF I treatment mitigated the cell viability and proliferation inhibition, as well as cell apoptosis caused by hypoxia. Moreover, SF I promoted the hypoxia-caused up-regulation of lincRNA-ROR in H9c2 cells and primary cardiomyocytes. Knockdown of lincRNA-ROR reversed the influence of SF I on hypoxia-stimulated H9c2 cells and primary cardiomyocytes. Besides, SF I activated MEK/ERK pathway in H9c2 cells and primary cardiomyocytes via up-regulating lincRNA-ROR. To sum up, our research verified the beneficial activity of SF I on hypoxia-caused cardiomyocytes injury. SF I protected cardiomyocytes from hypoxia-caused injury through up-regulation of lincRNA-ROR and activation of MEK/ERK pathway.

statements.text: Skullcapflavone I protects hypoxia-exposed cardiomyocytes from injury by improving cell viability and proliferation and reducing apoptosis.

statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.

statements.text: Skullcapflavone I exerts protective effects against hypoxia-induced cardiomyocyte injury through up-regulation of lincRNA-ROR.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Two studies report that lncRNA-ROR worsens rather than protects against cardiomyocyte injury under hypoxic or hypoxia/reoxygenation conditions. In H9C2 cells, lncRNA ROR was upregulated during H/R injury, and its overexpression increased apoptosis by sponging miR-138 and increasing Mst1, while miR-138 overexpression counteracted this effect (PMID: 32240614). Another study found that ROR promoted hypoxic/pathologic injury responses in cardiomyocytes through the miR-145/HAX-1 axis, with ROR knockdown reducing injury-associated markers such as LDH, MDA, and CK and increasing SOD (PMID: 35693619). Neither abstract mentions Skullcapflavone I directly, so the contradiction is indirect but biologically relevant to the claim's proposed mechanism that up-regulation of lincRNA-ROR is protective.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported. Although there is no direct evidence here about Skullcapflavone I itself, the available studies consistently indicate that increased lncRNA-ROR aggravates hypoxia-related cardiomyocyte injury, which conflicts with the claim that protection occurs through up-regulation of lincRNA-ROR.

matched_article.title: Blocking of checkpoint receptor PD-L1 aggravates osteoarthritis in macrophage-dependent manner in the mice model.
matched_article.abstract: As a chronic degenerative joint disease, osteoarthritis is among the most common diseases all over the world. In osteoarthritis, inflammation plays an important role in the generation of joint symptoms and the development of disease. When the programmed cell death-1 (PD-1)/programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) immune checkpoint is blocked, the antitumor immunity will be enhanced. We aim to illustrate the function of PD-L1 in osteoarthritis. Osteoarthritis in mice was induced by the injection of collagenase or anterior cruciate ligament transection (ACLT). Anti-PD-L1 was employed to block the signal of PD-L1. Knee joints histological sections were stained by Safranin-O. The level of cytokine was checked by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and mRNA level was shown by quantitative reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction. The blockade of PD-L1 signal up-regulated inflammatory response and promoted the development of osteoarthritis in mice. The inflammatory cytokine interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-α expression were promoted by PD-L1 blocking in macrophages. Osteoarthritis was aggravated when the expression of inflammatory cytokine is elevated in macrophages. Our results indicated that the blockade of PD-L1 signal in macrophages elevates the expression of inflammatory cytokine and promotes the development of osteoarthritis in mice, which could be utilized as a potential diagnostic and therapeutic target for osteoarthritis patients.

statements.text: In mice with osteoarthritis, blocking PD-L1 signaling aggravates osteoarthritis and increases joint inflammation compared with unblocked PD-L1 signaling.
