statements.text: The aggravation of osteoarthritis associated with PD-L1 blockade appears to depend on macrophage-mediated inflammatory responses in mice.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study reports that PD-L1 blockade worsened arthritis severity in a collagen-induced arthritis mouse model, with increased immune cell infiltration, and identified synovial macrophages as the main PD-L1-expressing cells, consistent with a protective macrophage-associated role for PD-L1 in joint inflammation (PMID: 37997621). The same abstract indicates that genetic approaches were used to test the protective role of PD-L1-expressing macrophages, further linking macrophages to the effect of PD-L1 signaling in murine arthritis (PMID: 37997621). However, this evidence is indirect for the specific claim because it concerns inflammatory arthritis/CIA rather than osteoarthritis, and the abstract does not explicitly state that aggravation depends on macrophage-mediated inflammatory responses in an osteoarthritis model.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is somewhat supported, but only indirectly. The available evidence in mice links PD-L1 blockade to worsened joint disease and implicates synovial macrophages in arthritis (PMID: 37997621), yet it does not directly test osteoarthritis or clearly establish macrophage-mediated inflammation as the required mechanism for PD-L1 blockade–induced osteoarthritis aggravation.

source_article.title: Can European Union (EU) Enlargement Boost Regional Economic Common Growth? Multi-Period Difference-in-Difference (DID) Method.
source_article.abstract: Under the dual background of Britain's blatant "Brexit" and the steady "expansion" of the European Union (EU) audit list, the economic effect of the development of regional alliances, in the end, is a question worthy of in-depth discussion. Using data from a sample of 27 EU member states from 2000 to 2018, this study examines and compares the impact of EU enlargement on economic growth for countries as a whole, developed and developing countries, and Central and Eastern European (CEE) countries using a multi-period difference-in-difference (DID) method and explores the mechanisms underlying that. The results show that EU enlargement contributes to the expected regional economic growth, and the effect is more evident in developed countries and CEE countries; the robustness of the results is tested by the dynamic effect test and counterfactual method; EU enlargement improves the spatial allocation of factor markets through regional integration, increases productivity, and positively promotes the overall national economic growth. As a typical quasi-natural experiment of the development of regional integration, the research results of this study on the enlargement of the EU provide a useful reference for the promotion of the development of cross-administrative integration around the world.

statements.text: European Union enlargement is associated with higher economic growth across European Union member states.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study suggests limited, indirect evidence that EU enlargement may be linked to economic improvement in newer member states rather than across all EU members. It reports that after EU accession, some Central and Eastern European industries that co-specialized with other CEE countries experienced productivity increases, and that this pattern may contribute to productivity convergence between CEE and EU15 countries (PMID: 36712367). However, the same abstract also notes divergence in specialization between EU15 and CEE and says cross-group co-specialization was less related to productivity growth, so its support for broad, union-wide higher economic growth is only partial.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The only available study provides indirect evidence of productivity gains and convergence for some accession-country industries after EU accession, but it does not directly show that EU enlargement is associated with higher economic growth across EU member states as a whole.

statements.text: European Union enlargement has a stronger positive association with economic growth in developed countries and Central and Eastern European countries than in other member states.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports unintended negative effects of the 2004/2007 EU enlargement on cross-border scientific collaboration, finding collaboration levels may have been higher without enlargement and attributing part of the divergence to migration imbalance from entrant to incumbent states (PMID: 28439544). However, this article examines scientific integration and brain drain rather than economic growth across member-state groups, so its relevance to the specific claim about stronger positive associations with economic growth is indirect and somewhat discrepant.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear but leans weakly against the claim because the only cited study points to negative consequences of EU enlargement in a related domain, not directly to economic growth effects. With no direct supporting studies and only one indirect contradicting study, confidence in contradiction is limited.

statements.text: European Union enlargement appears to promote economic growth by improving regional factor allocation and increasing productivity.
statements.support_summary: One study provides partial support by showing that after EU accession, Central and Eastern European (CEE) industries that co-specialized with other CEE countries experienced productivity increases, and that this sectoral division of labor may contribute to productivity convergence with EU15 countries (PMID: 36712367). This is relevant to the claim because it suggests enlargement-related integration can improve specialization patterns and productivity, although the abstract is more about productivity convergence than direct overall economic growth. The same abstract also notes that co-specialization between CEE and EU15 countries was less related to productivity growth, which tempers the strength of support (PMID: 36712367).
statements.contradict_summary: The strongest contradictory evidence comes from a study of the 2004/2007 enlargement showing that cross-border scientific collaboration would have been higher without enlargement, with migration imbalances from entrant to incumbent states identified as a key mechanism, suggesting enlargement may worsen some forms of high-skill factor allocation rather than improve them (PMID: 28439544). However, this paper focuses on science-system integration rather than broad economic growth, so its contradiction to the claim is indirect. Another study reports that in EU healthcare systems, later-joining countries often had high technical efficiency but tended to experience declining efficiency and productivity, with productivity decline in central and eastern Europe driven by a negative technological frontier shift (PMID: 36612499). This provides sector-specific evidence against a simple enlargement-led productivity gain narrative, though it is not a direct test of enlargement effects on aggregate growth.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans slightly toward contradiction or at least toward the claim being overstated. Support is limited to one indirect study showing accession-linked co-specialization and productivity gains in some CEE industries, whereas the contradictory evidence points to negative integration effects in science and productivity declines in at least one important sector, without directly establishing broader growth benefits.

matched_article.title: Effects of Long-Term Regular Continuous and Intermittent Walking on Oxidative Stress, Metabolic Profile, Heart Rate Variability, and Blood Pressure in Older Adults with Hypertension.
matched_article.abstract: It is documented that regular exercise is beneficial for improving the antioxidant system, metabolic system, cardiac autonomic function, and blood pressure in those with hypertension. In this regard, low-intensity exercise is recommended for older adults, particularly those with chronic diseases. This study aimed to compare the effects of long-term regular continuous walking with intermittent walking on oxidative stress, metabolic profile, heart rate variability, and blood pressure in older adults with hypertension. Forty-three participants with hypertension aged 60-80 years were randomly divided into the continuous or intermittent walking (CON or INT) groups. Participants in the CON group walked for 30 min, 3 days/week for 12 weeks. Participants in the INT group split 30 min walking into 3 identical sessions punctuated by a 1 min rest after each session, 3 days/week for 12 weeks. Antioxidant and oxidative stress markers, metabolic markers, heart rate variability, and blood pressure were evaluated before and after the exercise program. Glutathione (GSH), GSH to GSH disulfide (GSSG) ratio, and total GSH increased significantly, and GSSG and malondialdehyde decreased significantly in both groups (p < 0.05) without significant differences between groups. Triglycerides, ratio of total cholesterol to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, and atherosclerogenic index were significantly lower in the CON group than those in the INT group (p < 0.05). The standard deviation of the NN intervals and root mean square of the successive differences were significantly higher, and low-frequency power was significantly lower in the INT group than that in the CON group (p < 0.05). No significant changes in blood pressure were noted in both groups, and nor were there any significant differences between groups. Long-term regular continuous and intermittent walking may comparably increase antioxidants, reduce oxidative stress, and be beneficial for improving important blood pressure-related outcomes, including metabolic profile or cardiac autonomic function in older adults with hypertension.

statements.text: In older adults with hypertension, long-term regular continuous walking and intermittent walking similarly increased antioxidant status and reduced oxidative stress without showing meaningful differences between walking patterns.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reported that 12 weeks of aerobic Nordic walking or resistance training in middle-aged men with impaired glucose regulation did not change measured oxidative stress markers or related cytokines, although Nordic walking improved some metabolic risk measures (PMID: 23623841). This abstract does not match the claim population or intervention well, because it studied middle-aged men with impaired glucose regulation rather than older adults with hypertension, and it did not compare continuous versus intermittent walking patterns. Thus, while it weakly contradicts the idea that walking improves oxidative stress, it is also a population/intervention mismatch and should be interpreted cautiously as indirect evidence.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear but leans weakly toward contradiction because the only cited study found no oxidative stress improvement with walking-related training. However, that study is indirect and does not address older hypertensive adults or compare continuous with intermittent walking, so it does not strongly refute the claim.

statements.text: In older adults with hypertension, continuous walking improved some metabolic risk markers more than intermittent walking.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Two exercise-comparison studies favor intermittent/interval walking or show no advantage for continuous walking, though neither directly matches the claim population of older adults with hypertension. In a randomized 24-week study of middle-aged adults, intermittent walking produced comparable or greater benefits than continuous walking, including a significant HDL increase in the intermittent group and larger gains in fitness measures (PMID: 16506860). In adults with obesity undergoing caloric restriction, interval versus continuous walking produced similar outcomes for most variables, but VLDL-C improved only in the interval group (PMID: 24149382). A key discrepancy is indirectness: these abstracts do not specifically study older hypertensive adults, so they contradict the direction of the claim more than they directly refute it in the target population.
statements.conclusion: The claim is somewhat more contradicted than supported because the available comparative studies do not show continuous walking improving metabolic markers more than intermittent walking, and some markers favored intermittent formats. However, the contradiction is limited by the fact that the cited studies are in different populations than older adults with hypertension.

statements.text: In older adults with hypertension, intermittent walking improved heart rate variability more than continuous walking, while neither walking pattern meaningfully changed blood pressure.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Most contradicting studies report that walking or aerobic exercise lowers blood pressure in older or hypertensive adults, opposing the claim that neither walking pattern meaningfully changed blood pressure. In hypertensive older women, aerobic exercise sessions produced significant postexercise hypotension with no HRV changes (PMID: 27936947), and multiple walking-training studies found significant BP reductions after weeks to months of walking in hypertensive or elderly participants (PMID: 19150992, PMID: 9797187, PMID: 29363988). Acute or intermittent walking-related protocols also reduced systolic and/or diastolic BP in very old hypertensive adults and in adults breaking up sitting with walking bouts (PMID: 25760278, PMID: 24875670), while accumulated short walking bouts in prehypertension lowered systolic BP but did not significantly change HRV (PMID: 18463671). A recurring theme is that these studies generally do not directly compare intermittent versus continuous walking for HRV, so they mainly contradict the blood-pressure portion of the claim rather than directly refuting the asserted HRV advantage; one cited study is also less directly relevant because it involved community older adults not specifically hypertensive (PMID: 16078955).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although the available studies do not directly test whether intermittent walking improves HRV more than continuous walking, they consistently indicate that walking/aerobic exercise often does reduce blood pressure in older or hypertensive adults, which conflicts with the claim that neither walking pattern meaningfully changed blood pressure.

source_article.title: Gut Microbiome Signatures of Risk and Prodromal Markers of Parkinson Disease.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: Alterations of the gut microbiome in Parkinson disease (PD) have been repeatedly demonstrated. However, little is known about whether such alterations precede disease onset and how they relate to risk and prodromal markers of PD. We investigated associations of these features with gut microbiome composition. METHODS: Established risk and prodromal markers of PD as well as factors related to diet/lifestyle, bowel function, and medication were studied in relation to bacterial α-/β-diversity, enterotypes, and differential abundance in stool samples of 666 elderly TREND (Tübingen Evaluation of Risk Factors for Early Detection of Neurodegeneration) study participants. RESULTS: Among risk and prodromal markers, physical activity, occupational solvent exposure, and constipation showed associations with α-diversity. Physical activity, sex, constipation, possible rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder (RBD), and smoking were associated with β-diversity. Subthreshold parkinsonism and physical activity showed an interaction effect. Among other factors, age and urate-lowering medication were associated with α- and β-diversity. Physical inactivity and constipation were highest in individuals with the Firmicutes-enriched enterotype. Constipation was lowest and subthreshold parkinsonism least frequent in individuals with the Prevotella-enriched enterotype. Differentially abundant taxa were linked to constipation, physical activity, possible RBD, smoking, and subthreshold parkinsonism. Substantia nigra hyperechogenicity, olfactory loss, depression, orthostatic hypotension, urinary/erectile dysfunction, PD family history, and the prodromal PD probability showed no significant microbiome associations. INTERPRETATION: Several risk and prodromal markers of PD are associated with gut microbiome composition. However, the impact of the gut microbiome on PD risk and potential microbiome-dependent subtypes in the prodrome of PD need further investigation based on prospective clinical and (multi)omics data in incident PD cases. ANN NEUROL 2020;88:320-331.

statements.text: In elderly participants without established Parkinson disease, physical activity, occupational solvent exposure, constipation, possible rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder, smoking, sex, age, and urate-lowering medication were associated with gut microbiome composition, including diversity measures and bacterial taxa abundance.
statements.support_summary: One study in 666 elderly participants from the TREND cohort without established Parkinson disease directly supports the claim, reporting that physical inactivity, occupational solvent exposure, and constipation were associated with alpha-diversity, while physical inactivity, sex, constipation, possible REM sleep behavior disorder, and smoking were associated with beta-diversity; age and urate-lowering medication were associated with both alpha- and beta-diversity (PMID: 34021620). The same study also found enterotype and differentially abundant taxa associations involving constipation, physical inactivity, possible RBD, smoking, and subthreshold parkinsonism, aligning with the claim's mention of bacterial taxa abundance (PMID: 34021620). Overall, the abstract closely matches the listed factors and outcomes in the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported because the available abstract directly reports associations between the specified Parkinson disease risk/prodromal markers and gut microbiome diversity and taxa in elderly individuals without established PD (PMID: 34021620). However, the evidence base presented here consists of a single observational study, so support is strong but not definitive.

statements.text: In elderly participants without established Parkinson disease, physical inactivity and constipation were more common in a Firmicutes-enriched gut enterotype, while constipation was less common and subthreshold parkinsonism was less frequent in a Prevotella-enriched gut enterotype.
statements.support_summary: One study in 666 elderly participants without established Parkinson disease reported that constipation was highest in the Firmicutes-enriched enterotype, while constipation was lowest and subthreshold parkinsonism least frequent in the Prevotella-enriched enterotype (PMID: 34021620). The same abstract found that physical inactivity was most frequent in the Bacteroides-enriched enterotype, not the Firmicutes-enriched enterotype, creating an internal discrepancy with the claim despite support for the constipation and Prevotella-related parts (PMID: 34021620). Overall, the available abstract supports the enterotype associations for constipation and subthreshold parkinsonism, but not the specific assertion that physical inactivity was more common in the Firmicutes-enriched enterotype.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided. However, within the single supporting study itself, the abstract states that physical inactivity was most frequent in the Bacteroides-enriched rather than Firmicutes-enriched enterotype, which conflicts with that portion of the claim (PMID: 34021620).
statements.conclusion: The statement is only partially supported overall. The available evidence supports that constipation was more common in the Firmicutes-enriched enterotype and that constipation and subthreshold parkinsonism were less common in the Prevotella-enriched enterotype, but it does not support—and in fact conflicts with—the claim that physical inactivity was more common in the Firmicutes-enriched enterotype.

statements.text: Several established risk and prodromal markers of Parkinson disease showed no significant association with gut microbiome composition, including substantia nigra hyperechogenicity, olfactory loss, depression, orthostatic hypotension, urinary or erectile dysfunction, PD family history, and overall prodromal Parkinson disease probability.
statements.support_summary: The strongest support comes from the directly relevant TREND cohort study, which analyzed stool microbiome features in 666 elderly participants and explicitly reported no significant associations for substantia nigra hyperechogenicity, olfactory loss, depression, orthostatic hypotension, urinary/erectile dysfunction, PD family history, or overall prodromal PD probability (PMID: 34021620). That same abstract contrasts these null findings with positive associations for other markers such as constipation, possible RBD, physical inactivity, smoking, and subthreshold parkinsonism, reinforcing that the listed markers were specifically tested but not associated (PMID: 34021620). A second study found orthostatic hypotension was not associated with future PD risk in the general population, but it did not assess gut microbiome composition, so it is only indirect and weakly relevant to the claim (PMID: 32965064).
statements.contradict_summary: Most contradicting items are only indirectly related or miscategorized because they do not test the claim's specific question of whether the listed markers are associated with gut microbiome composition. Reviews or observational studies discussing PD-related microbiome changes, depression as a prodromal feature, olfactory loss in IRBD, or speculative gut-PD links do not directly address the null microbiome associations for the specified markers (PMID: 30171304, PMID: 31422283, PMID: 28615430, PMID: 36921591). One more relevant paper reports that lower fecal butyrate and reduced butyrate-producing genera were related to depressive symptoms in PD patients, which conflicts with the claim regarding depression, although it studies symptomatic PD rather than a prodromal-risk cohort like the source statement (PMID: 35723531). Overall, the contradiction set contains substantial scope mismatch and likely categorization noise.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is driven by one directly on-point cohort study that explicitly tested and found no significant microbiome associations for all listed markers, whereas most opposing items do not actually examine the same microbiome-marker question and only one offers partial conflicting evidence for depression in established PD.

matched_article.title: Severe Neurological Toxicity of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors: Growing Spectrum.
matched_article.abstract: Expanding use of immune-checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) underscores the importance of accurate diagnosis and timely management of neurological immune-related adverse events (irAE-N). We evaluate the real-world frequency, phenotypes, co-occurring immune-related adverse events (irAEs), and long-term outcomes of severe, grade III to V irAE-N at a tertiary care center over 6 years. We analyze how our experience supports published literature and professional society guidelines. We also discuss these data with regard to common clinical scenarios, such as combination therapy, ICI rechallenge and risk of relapse of irAE-N, and corticosteroid taper, which are not specifically addressed by current guidelines and/or have limited data. Recommendations for management and future irAE-N reporting are outlined. ANN NEUROL 2020;87:659-669.

statements.text: Severe neurological immune-related adverse events from immune checkpoint inhibitors can occur with a broad and growing spectrum of clinical phenotypes.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently describes neurologic immune-related adverse events (n-irAEs) from immune checkpoint inhibitors as rare but potentially severe, sometimes fatal, and affecting both the central and peripheral nervous systems with heterogeneous presentations including myositis, myasthenia gravis, neuropathies, Guillain-Barre-like syndromes, meningitis, encephalitis, myelopathy, and overlap syndromes (PMID: 30482825, PMID: 32166812, PMID: 31393660, PMID: 31776691, PMID: 28938341, PMID: 29761297, PMID: 32941192). Several reviews explicitly frame this as a broad or expanding spectrum of phenotypes and note that increasing ICI use is making these complications a growing clinical problem, with CNS phenotypes and delayed presentations increasingly recognized (PMID: 35175441, PMID: 34972978, PMID: 37059646, PMID: 39317778, PMID: 37790035). Cohort and single-center data add concrete clinical variability, reporting diverse severe cases such as transverse myelopathy, demyelinating sensorimotor polyneuropathy, oculomotor nerve palsy, sensory neuropathy, and posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PMID: 29761297), as well as mixed CNS/PNS involvement in real-world cohorts (PMID: 32166812). Multiple papers also emphasize diagnostic difficulty because these syndromes can mimic other neurologic or oncologic conditions and may present with overlapping autoimmune manifestations (PMID: 30482825, PMID: 30867573, PMID: 32796758). A few listed support items are only indirect or nonspecific about neurologic phenotypes and mainly discuss irAEs broadly rather than directly substantiating the claim (for example PMID: 39040444, PMID: 31051213, PMID: 30464684).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The evidence includes multiple reviews and real-world clinical series showing that severe neurologic irAEs from checkpoint inhibitors do occur and encompass a wide, increasingly recognized range of CNS, PNS, and overlap phenotypes, with no direct contradictory evidence presented.

statements.text: Severe neurological immune-related adverse events from immune checkpoint inhibitors are often accompanied by other immune-related adverse events.
statements.support_summary: Several review articles state that neurologic immune-related adverse events (irAEs) from checkpoint inhibitors can occur as overlap syndromes, with multiple neurologic syndromes or involvement of other organ systems in the same patient; this is stated explicitly in the literature review by Cuzzubbo et al. (PMID: 30482825). A neuro-oncology review similarly notes that multiple neurologic irAEs such as myositis, myasthenia gravis, and myocarditis can arise in the same patient, supporting co-occurrence of severe neurologic toxicity with other irAEs (PMID: 35175441). However, some items in the support set are only indirect or weakly relevant: the single-center cohort of severe neurologic complications does not clearly report accompanying non-neurologic irAEs from the abstract (PMID: 29761297), and broader reviews of anticancer neurotoxicity or immunotherapy neurotoxicity discuss severity and overlap in general terms without specifically quantifying how often severe checkpoint inhibitor-related neurologic irAEs are accompanied by other irAEs (PMID: 37059646; PMID: 34972978). Overall, the main supportive theme is overlap/constellation syndromes rather than direct frequency estimates.
statements.contradict_summary: The lone contradicting review describes neurologic irAEs from immune checkpoint inhibitors as rare but potentially severe and focuses on their types and management, without stating that they are commonly isolated or usually unaccompanied by other irAEs (PMID: 28938341). This makes it more of a lack-of-support than a true contradiction, so its placement in the contradicting group is discrepant with the abstract content. No abstract here directly reports that severe neurologic irAEs are usually not accompanied by other immune-related toxicities.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest abstract-level evidence explicitly describes frequent overlap syndromes and co-occurrence with other organ toxicities, whereas the opposing evidence does not directly refute the claim and mainly reflects absence of discussion rather than contradiction.

statements.text: Severe neurological immune-related adverse events from immune checkpoint inhibitors can have important long-term outcomes that inform management, including decisions about combination therapy, rechallenge, and corticosteroid taper.
statements.support_summary: The evidence overall supports that neurologic immune-related adverse events (n-irAEs) from immune checkpoint inhibitors are rare but can be severe, with substantial morbidity, mortality, and sometimes lasting disability, making management decisions clinically consequential. A retrospective single-center cohort found variable outcomes ranging from complete resolution to death, noted that combination immunotherapy appeared associated with more severe neurologic irAEs and greater long-term morbidity/mortality, and reported real-world decisions to hold, rechallenge, or dose-reduce ICIs and use corticosteroids/IVIG (PMID: 29761297). Multiple reviews and narrative overviews consistently state that severe n-irAEs require prompt ICI interruption, corticosteroids or other immunomodulation, multidisciplinary management, and individualized consideration of rechallenge based on severity/outcomes; some explicitly discuss reintroduction safety and decision-making after prior n-irAEs (PMID: 35175441, PMID: 31393660, PMID: 31776691, PMID: 32852312). Additional reviews note that neurologic toxicities may be prolonged or delayed and can impair quality of life in the short or long term, reinforcing that long-term outcomes matter for management planning, though many abstracts do not specifically address corticosteroid taper or combination therapy in detail (PMID: 37790035, PMID: 39317778). A notable discrepancy is that several cited support items are broad reviews of irAEs or neurotoxicity and only indirectly address the specific claim elements of long-term neurologic outcomes, combination therapy, rechallenge, and steroid taper.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support comes from one directly relevant clinical series plus several reviews showing that severe neurologic irAEs can cause major lasting harm and that these outcomes influence decisions about stopping or restarting ICIs, use of combination therapy, and immunosuppressive management, although explicit abstract-level evidence for corticosteroid taper is limited.

source_article.title: The Accessibility of Educational Public Service Facilities with Two-Step Mobile Search Taking Hetao District of Ganzhou City as an Example.
source_article.abstract: By using the two-step mobile search method of time and distance cost combined with Internet map data, taking the old urban district of Ganzhou city as research object, the spatial accessibility of primary and secondary schools from residential sites to residential areas was studied. The research shows that the reachability of primary and secondary schools in the old urban district of Ganzhou city is low, and most of the population fails to enjoy better education resources and the spatial distribution of education resources is not balanced. The reachability of primary education resources is high in geospatial area. The gathering area is the intersection of Ganjiang Street and Jiefang Street. The low-value areas are mainly the southwestern part of Nanwai Street and the northwestern area of Dongwai Street. The reachability of education resources shows a decreasing trend from the center to the surrounding areas. The education resources are concentrated in Ganjiang Street. Compared with traditional methods, the two-step mobile search method using Internet map data can more effectively and accurately reflect the accessibility of residential areas to primary and secondary schools in real time and can obtain the real conditions of traffic and roads more conveniently and also more accurat data acquisition.

statements.text: In the old urban district of Ganzhou City, China, spatial accessibility to primary and secondary schools from residential areas is low, indicating that many residents do not have good access to educational resources and that the distribution of education resources is unbalanced.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In the old urban district of Ganzhou City, China, accessibility to primary education resources is relatively high in some central geospatial areas and decreases from the center toward surrounding areas, with education resources concentrated in Ganjiang Street.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Internet map data combined with a two-step mobile search approach can more effectively and accurately assess real-time accessibility of residential areas to primary and secondary schools than traditional methods.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: A Novel and Effective Brain Tumor Classification Model Using Deep Feature Fusion and Famous Machine Learning Classifiers.
matched_article.abstract: Brain tumors are difficult to treat and cause substantial fatalities worldwide. Medical professionals visually analyze the images and mark out the tumor regions to identify brain tumors, which is time-consuming and prone to error. Researchers have proposed automated methods in recent years to detect brain tumors early. These approaches, however, encounter difficulties due to their low accuracy and large false-positive values. An efficient tumor identification and classification approach is required to extract robust features and perform accurate disease classification. This paper proposes a novel multiclass brain tumor classification method based on deep feature fusion. The MR images are preprocessed using min-max normalization, and then extensive data augmentation is applied to MR images to overcome the lack of data problem. The deep CNN features obtained from transfer learned architectures such as AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet18 are fused to build a single feature vector and then loaded into Support Vector Machine (SVM) and K-nearest neighbor (KNN) to predict the final output. The novel feature vector contains more information than the independent vectors, boosting the proposed method's classification performance. The proposed framework is trained and evaluated on 15,320 Magnetic Resonance Images (MRIs). The study shows that the fused feature vector performs better than the individual vectors. Moreover, the proposed technique performed better than the existing systems and achieved accuracy of 99.7%; hence, it can be used in clinical setup to classify brain tumors from MRIs.

statements.text: In patients with magnetic resonance imaging scans of the brain, deep feature fusion from transfer-learned convolutional neural network architectures improved brain tumor classification performance compared with individual deep feature vectors.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a study that explicitly concatenated top-performing deep features from multiple pre-trained CNNs on brain MRI datasets and reported that this ensemble of deep features significantly improved classification performance over individual deep feature sets (PMID: 33810176). Additional studies report that feature fusion from transfer-learned CNNs improved brain tumor detection/classification metrics, including fusion of shallow and deep ResNet18 features (PMID: 37018527), fused/optimized ResNet101-derived features for tumor classification (PMID: 35965745), fused VGG16/VGG19-derived multimodal features with high BraTS accuracy (PMID: 32781795), and fusion of residual-network representations improving glioma classification performance (PMID: 34974232). However, several of these abstracts support fusion as beneficial without clearly stating a head-to-head comparison against individual deep feature vectors, so their support is indirect or partial rather than definitive. Two items appear somewhat miscategorized as support because they discuss transfer learning or CNN-based classification improvements generally, but not feature fusion versus individual feature vectors specifically (PMID: 36097024; PMID: 35503847). Overall, the recurring theme is that combining deep representations from transfer-learned architectures tends to enhance MRI-based brain tumor classification, with only one abstract clearly documenting the specific comparison in the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This judgement is driven by one directly relevant study showing ensemble deep features outperforming individual feature vectors (PMID: 33810176), plus several additional MRI brain tumor studies reporting improved performance with fused transfer-learned features, though many do not make the exact direct comparison stated.

statements.text: In patients with magnetic resonance imaging scans of the brain, the proposed deep feature fusion framework performed better than existing brain tumor classification systems.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently reports that MRI-based brain tumor classification frameworks using deep features, hybrid/ensemble fusion, transfer learning, or optimized classifiers often achieve very high accuracy and are described as outperforming prior or state-of-the-art methods on common datasets such as Figshare, Kaggle, CE-MRI, and BraTS (e.g., PMID: 35408340, PMID: 31279167, PMID: 31150950, PMID: 36292230, PMID: 34974232). Several studies specifically emphasize feature fusion or ensemble strategies as the source of gains over existing systems, including fused CNN+HOG or multimodel residual-network fusion approaches (PMID: 35408340, PMID: 34974232, PMID: 36042871, PMID: 34089524). Many other abstracts provide only weaker indirect support because they report high performance or superiority over selected baselines without clearly matching the exact framework or comparison standard in the claim, creating some scope/terminology discrepancy within the support set (e.g., PMID: 33810176, PMID: 37018527, PMID: 36797543, PMID: 35997926). Overall, the dominant theme is that proposed MRI-based deep/hybrid classification systems commonly perform as well as or better than prior brain tumor classifiers in benchmark comparisons.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Across many MRI-based brain tumor classification studies, especially those using feature fusion, transfer learning, or ensemble designs, abstracts frequently report superior performance versus prior or state-of-the-art systems, although some evidence is indirect because not every study matches the exact framework named in the claim.

statements.text: In patients with magnetic resonance imaging scans of the brain, the proposed deep feature fusion framework can support clinical brain tumor classification from magnetic resonance images.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently reports that deep-learning or hybrid deep-feature frameworks can classify brain tumors from MRI with high performance, often using transfer learning, feature fusion, and conventional classifiers such as SVM or kNN. Several studies specifically emphasize fused deep features or hybrid feature sets as improving classification performance, including ensembles of deep features (PMID: 33810176), shallow/deep feature fusion with optimized classifiers that could aid radiologists (PMID: 37018527), deep-plus-handcrafted feature fusion with deployment potential in radiology practice (PMID: 37430604), and residual-network fusion for glioma classification (PMID: 34974232). Many additional MRI-based CAD studies, even when not explicitly describing a "deep feature fusion framework," report strong multiclass tumor classification results and clinical-assistive intent, such as transfer-learning CNN feature extraction and classification approaches (PMID: 31279167, PMID: 31150950, PMID: 34324425, PMID: 37171681). A few SUPPORT-tagged articles are only indirectly aligned because they use handcrafted or non-deep features rather than true deep-feature fusion (eg, PMID: 26509188, PMID: 37537514, PMID: 37033909), which is a categorization discrepancy, but overall the abstract-level evidence strongly favors the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided. No abstract in the supplied CONTRADICT group argues that MRI-based deep feature fusion or related deep-feature frameworks fail to support brain tumor classification. Therefore, there is no direct contradictory evidence to summarize.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The evidence base is large and mostly consistent, with multiple MRI studies showing that deep-feature extraction, fusion, and hybrid CAD frameworks can accurately classify brain tumors and in several cases are described as assistive tools for clinicians or radiologists, although some cited papers are only indirectly relevant to the specific "deep feature fusion" wording.

source_article.title: Effects of TLR4 gene silencing on the proliferation and apotosis of hepatocarcinoma HEPG2 cells.
source_article.abstract: Toll-like receptors (TLRs) are key factors in the innate immune system and initiate an inflammatory response to foreign pathogens, such as bacteria, fungi and viruses. TLR4-mediated signaling has been implicated in tumor cell proliferation and apoptosis in numerous cancers. The present study aimed to investigate the biological effect of TLR4 on the proliferation and apoptosis of human liver cancer cells and the mechanisms responsible for the regulation of cellular responses following TLR4 gene knockdown. Three TLR4 small interfering (si)RNA constructs, consisting of TLR4-siRNA-1, TLR4-siRNA-2 and TLR4-siRNA-3, were transiently transfected into HepG2 cells using Lipofectamine 2000. TLR4 knockdown was confirmed using reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction and western blotting. The effect of the TLR4 siRNA on tumor cell proliferation was monitored by methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium assay and cell apoptosis was observed by flow cytometry. The expression of TLR4-associated proteins, consisting of myeloid differentiation primary response 88 (MyD88), Toll-interleukin-1R-domain-containing adapter-inducing interferon-β (TRIF), interferon regulatory factor-3 (IRF3), nuclear factor (NF)-κB, NF-κB inhibitor α (IκBα), phosphorylated IκBα (p-IκBα), extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK) and c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK), was detected by western blot analysis. TLR4-siRNA-1 had the strongest knockdown effect and inhibited TLR4 messenger RNA and protein expression. TLR4 knockdown with TLR4-siRNA-1 reduced cell proliferation and promoted cell apoptosis. MyD88, TRIF, IRF3, IκBα, JNK and ERK were markedly suppressed in the cells transfected with TLR4 siRNA. However, nuclear expression of NF-κB and p-IκBα increased in HepG2 cells with TLR4 gene knockdown. The present study revealed that TLR4-mediated signaling plays a key role in the proliferation and apoptosis of cultured hepatocarcinoma cells. Therefore, RNA interference-directed targeting of TLR4 may raise the potential of the application of TLR4 knockdown for liver cancer therapy.

statements.text: In cultured human hepatocarcinoma HepG2 cells, TLR4 gene knockdown reduces cell proliferation and promotes apoptosis compared with control cells.
statements.support_summary: One indirect study in HepG2 cells found that loss-of-function TLR4 polymorphisms (A896G/C1196T), compared with wild-type TLR4, reduced cell survival/proliferation and lowered the apoptotic threshold, along with reduced NF-kappaB, IL-6, and TGF-beta1 signaling, suggesting diminished TLR4 activity can suppress malignant phenotypes in these cells (PMID: 28945461). However, this study examined mutant TLR4 constructs rather than direct TLR4 gene knockdown, so it only partially matches the claim. No clear discrepancy is evident within the abstract, but the mechanistic intervention differs from the stated knockdown experiment.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall weakly supported. The only cited evidence is an indirect HepG2 study showing that reduced TLR4 function is associated with less proliferation and greater susceptibility to apoptosis, but it does not directly test TLR4 knockdown versus control cells.

statements.text: In cultured human hepatocarcinoma HepG2 cells, TLR4 gene knockdown suppresses MyD88, TRIF, IRF3, IκBα, JNK, and ERK signaling proteins and increases nuclear NF-κB and phosphorylated IκBα.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: TLR4-mediated signaling contributes to the growth and survival of hepatocarcinoma cells, making TLR4 a potential therapeutic target in liver cancer.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies support a pro-tumor role for TLR4 signaling in hepatocarcinoma/HCC cells. HSP70 was reported to promote proliferation of H22 hepatocarcinoma cells through TLR2/TLR4 signaling with NF-κB activation, linking TLR4 to growth and malignant invasion-related signaling (PMID: 23836405). In HCC models, activation of the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB pathway was described as contributing to tumor development and progression, and USP13-driven enhancement of this pathway increased proliferation, EMT, migration, invasion, tumor growth, and metastasis, while USP13 knockdown suppressed these phenotypes (PMID: 33195243). One listed supportive paper is somewhat discrepant because its title indicates TLR4 signaling under hypoxia led to anti-tumor effects rather than straightforward tumor promotion, although the truncated abstract still places TLR4 in proliferation/survival-related biology (PMID: 27446390).
statements.contradict_summary: The sole item in the contradiction set does not actually contradict the claim based on its abstract. In HepG2 cells, TLR4 SNPs that weakened TLR4-mediated signaling led to lower proliferation, migration, invasion, inflammatory signaling, and survival/apoptotic threshold compared with wild-type TLR4, implying intact TLR4 signaling promotes malignant behavior (PMID: 28945461). This is therefore a categorization discrepancy: the study is more consistent with support than contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Two abstracts directly link TLR4 signaling to hepatocarcinoma/HCC growth and progression, and the only purported contradictory study actually reports that reduced TLR4 signaling diminishes malignant phenotypes, although one supportive item appears mixed because it may describe anti-tumor effects under hypoxia.

matched_article.title: Low expression of PKCα and high expression of KRAS predict poor prognosis in patients with colorectal cancer.
matched_article.abstract: The current study aimed to determine the association between protein kinase Cα (PKCα) and Kirsten rat sarcoma viral oncogene homolog (KRAS) expression and the response to folinic acid, 5-fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (FOLFOX regimen) in patients with colorectal cancer (CRC). The protein levels of PKCα and KRAS were analyzed by immunohistochemistry in tissue samples from patients with CRC and in non-cancerous tissues, including 152 cases of colorectal adenocarcinoma, 30 cases of colorectal adenoma and 20 normal colonic mucosa samples. The association between PKCα and KRAS expression and clinicopathological features was analyzed. The rates of positive PKCα protein expression in patients with poorly, moderately and well-differentiated adenocarcinoma were 16.7% (6/36), 40.0% (24/60), and 57.1% (32/56), respectively (P<0.013). The rate of positive KRAS expression in CRC patients was significantly higher than in patients with colon adenoma and normal colon mucosa (P<0.001). Expression levels of KRAS were associated with the degree of differentiation of CRC (P<0.001). Expression of PKCα was negatively correlated with KRAS expression in CRC tissues. The mean progression-free survival (PFS) times in patients with high and low expression of PKCα were 43.9 and 38.8 months, respectively (P<0.001). The mean PFS times were 38.5 and 45.5 months in patients with high and low expression of KRAS, respectively (P=0.001). In conclusion, low PKCα and high KRAS expression predicted relatively poor prognosis in patients with CRC.

statements.text: In patients with colorectal cancer, low protein kinase Cα expression is associated with poorer prognosis and shorter progression-free survival than high protein kinase Cα expression.

statements.text: In patients with colorectal cancer, high KRAS expression is associated with poorer prognosis and shorter progression-free survival than low KRAS expression.
statements.support_summary: Most supporting studies actually evaluate KRAS mutation status rather than total KRAS expression, so their relevance to the specific claim about "high KRAS expression" is indirect. Several metastatic CRC cohorts reported worse progression-related outcomes for KRAS-mutant versus wild-type tumors, including shorter PFS and/or worse prognosis in patients treated with bevacizumab or FOLFOX (PMID: 24012456, PMID: 25870609, PMID: 23510802), and a review also summarizes KRAS mutation as being linked to more aggressive disease, shorter recurrence-free survival, and higher recurrence risk (PMID: 31597436). Additional studies found KRAS mutation associated with earlier lung-specific recurrence after pulmonary metastasectomy (PMID: 24281417) and poor prognosis in left-sided colon cancer but not right-sided disease, indicating heterogeneity by tumor location (PMID: 30917791). One evidence item is internally discrepant with the claim: although KRAS mutations were linked to poor overall survival, KRAS4A overexpression was associated with better overall survival, while KRAS4B overexpression tracked with larger tumor size (PMID: 19824059). Overall, the supporting set mainly supports poorer outcomes for KRAS-mutant disease, not clearly for high KRAS expression per se.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence also focuses largely on KRAS/RAS mutation status rather than expression. Two studies directly reported no significant prognostic association of RAS/KRAS mutations with PFS or OS in CRC cohorts, including fluoropyrimidine/oxaliplatin-treated advanced CRC with or without bevacizumab (PMID: 28451421) and a STROBE-compliant study reporting no consistent association between KRAS/BRAF mutations and prognosis (PMID: 30658510), although the latter abstract is truncated and therefore less informative. Other items are only partially contradictory or not directly on point: OPUS showed KRAS wild-type predicts benefit from cetuximab plus FOLFOX-4, which is more predictive than prognostic evidence (PMID: 21228335), and a review explicitly states KRAS prognostic value remains controversial (PMID: 24778007). Isoform-related studies complicate the claim by showing that loss or low relative expression of other pathway components/isoforms can associate with worse outcomes, such as low KRAS-4A expression in wild-type MSS CRC (PMID: 30121958), rather than high KRAS expression being uniformly adverse. Thus, the contradictory set suggests either no consistent prognostic effect or substantial context dependence, but only limited direct evidence against the exact expression-based claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly to moderately supported overall, but the evidence is unclear because most studies assess KRAS mutation status rather than high versus low KRAS expression. While several cohorts associate KRAS-mutant CRC with poorer prognosis and shorter progression-related outcomes, multiple studies report no consistent prognostic effect and isoform-specific findings directly complicate the idea that higher KRAS expression is uniformly adverse.

source_article.title: The effects of sodium nitroprusside-induced hypotension on splanchnic perfusion and hepatocellular integrity.
source_article.abstract: UNLABELLED: The purpose of our study was to investigate the effects of sodium nitroprusside-induced hypotension on splanchnic perfusion and hepatocellular integrity. Thirty patients undergoing radical prostatectomy were allocated randomly to a sodium nitroprusside (SNP) or control group (control). Regional pco2 was measured using gastric tonometry, and the regional to arterial difference in partial pressure of CO2 and intramucosal pH were calculated. The cytosolic liver enzyme alpha-glutathione S-transferase and standard liver enzyme markers (alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, and gamma-glutamyltransferase) were also measured. Mean arterial pressure in the SNP group was 50 mm Hg for 97 min during surgery. A significant increase from baseline in regional pco2 (from 40.0+/-4.2 mm Hg to 45.3+/-1.3 mm Hg) and regional to arterial difference in partial pressure of CO2 (from 4.1+/-1.1 mm Hg to 9.7+/-1.4 mm Hg) was seen at 90 min after skin incision only in the SNP group. Intramucosal pH decreased significantly from 7.40+/-0.02 to 7.35+/-0.03 during the same period in this group. Tonometric variables returned to baseline values within 2 h postoperatively. Alpha-glutathione S-transferase concentrations increased significantly in the SNP group from baseline to peak concentrations at the end of surgery (SNP: 9.93+/-4.94 microg/L; control: 5.85+/-1.86 microg/L). A return to baseline values was seen 24 h postoperatively. No significant changes in standard liver enzyme markers were seen throughout the study period. It is concluded, that splanchnic perfusion was transiently impaired during controlled hypotension. This is supported by significant changes in tonometric data. Increased serum levels of alpha-glutathione S-transferase may indicate a disturbance in hepatocellular integrity. IMPLICATIONS: We studied gastric mucosal tonometry and the cytosolic liver enzyme alpha-glutathione S-transferase to evaluate the effects of controlled hypotension induced by sodium nitroprusside on splanchnic perfusion and hepatocellular integrity. Splanchnic perfusion decreased and alpha-glutathione S-transferase increased during and after a hypotensive period, but returned to baseline values within the first postoperative day, indicating a transient impairment of splanchnic perfusion and hepatocellular integrity.

statements.text: In patients undergoing radical prostatectomy, sodium nitroprusside-induced controlled hypotension transiently impairs splanchnic perfusion compared with normotensive control conditions.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Three studies report that sodium nitroprusside (SNP) does not impair, and may increase, splanchnic or abdominal organ blood flow, although none directly studied radical prostatectomy patients. In postoperative cardiac surgery patients, SNP lowered blood pressure while increasing splanchnic blood flow and was concluded to have no adverse effect on splanchnic tissue oxygenation despite a small fall in gastric intramucosal pH (PMID: 8792893). In anesthetized dogs, SNP-induced hypotension abolished abdominal organ vasoconstriction and increased abdominal organ blood flow 2.5-fold during hypotension (PMID: 8102224). In halothane-anesthetized rats, SNP infusion increased splanchnic organ blood flow and these regional effects persisted after infusion stopped (PMID: 6466527).
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported because all available evidence items point toward preserved or increased splanchnic perfusion with SNP rather than transient impairment. However, the contradiction is only moderate because the cited studies are indirect in population and setting, with only one human study and none in radical prostatectomy patients.

matched_article.title: Diclofenac does not decrease renal blood flow or glomerular filtration in elderly patients undergoing orthopedic surgery.
matched_article.abstract: Nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) have become increasingly popular in the treatment of perioperative pain. Due to concerns that cyclooxygenase inhibition may adversely affect renal function, these drugs are often not used in geriatric surgical patients. However, the perioperative effect of NSAIDs on renal blood flow (RBF) and glomerular filtration rate (GFR) has not been assessed. Therefore, using a prospective, controlled, double-blinded study design, we evaluated the effect of diclofenac on RBF and GFR in 20 patients (>65 yr) undergoing open reduction and internal fixation of the femur. All patients were normovolemic before the study. A standardized general anesthetic was administered. On induction of anesthesia, patients in the diclofenac group received an IV bolus of diclofenac (0.7 mg/kg) followed by a constant infusion (0.15 mg x kg(-1) x h(-1)) until the end of surgery. In the saline group, an equal volume of saline was administered. During four time periods (equilibration, anesthesia, surgical, recovery), GFR and effective renal plasma flow (ERPF) were measured by inulin and paraaminohippurate clearance, respectively. After the induction of anesthesia and throughout the surgical period, ERPF and GFR were significantly decreased compared with preoperative baseline values. However, no difference was demonstrated between the groups. These results suggest that, in geriatric surgical patients, the adjuvant administration of NSAIDs does not adversely affect renal function. As determined by inulin and paraaminohippurate clearance, the intraoperative administration of diclofenac does not decrease glomerular filtration rate or effective renal plasma flow in normovolemic geriatric patients. Therefore, diclofenac may be administered during the perioperative period.

statements.text: In normovolemic elderly patients undergoing orthopedic surgery, perioperative diclofenac administration does not reduce glomerular filtration rate compared with saline placebo.
statements.support_summary: One small randomized, double-blind crossover study in healthy volunteers aged 60 years or older found that a single intravenous 75 mg dose of diclofenac did not change renal clearance measures and did not increase urinary markers of glomerular or tubular dysfunction, suggesting no acute renal impairment in this population (PMID: 9258981). However, this evidence is indirect for the claim because it was conducted in healthy elderly volunteers rather than normovolemic elderly orthopedic surgery patients, and it did not directly measure perioperative glomerular filtration rate versus saline placebo in the surgical setting.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is weakly supported overall because the only available evidence is indirect but consistent with no acute renal harm from diclofenac in older adults (PMID: 9258981). There is no direct contradictory evidence here, but the absence of studies in the specific orthopedic perioperative setting limits confidence.

statements.text: In normovolemic elderly patients undergoing orthopedic surgery, perioperative diclofenac administration does not reduce effective renal plasma flow compared with saline placebo.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One small physiologic study reported that diclofenac sodium caused a rapid, significant initial decline in renal plasma flow and glomerular filtration rate in eight patients with normal renal function, with renal plasma flow falling by about 35% before recovering over the next 2 hours (PMID: 2756361). The same study also found marked reductions in urine output and persistent reduction in free water clearance, indicating acute renal hemodynamic and tubular effects (PMID: 2756361). However, this evidence is only indirectly relevant because it was not conducted in normovolemic elderly orthopedic surgery patients and did not compare diclofenac with saline placebo, so its contradiction of the claim is limited.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly contradicted overall. The only available evidence suggests diclofenac can transiently reduce renal plasma flow, but it comes from a small non-surgical population without a placebo comparison, so its applicability to the specific claimed setting is limited.

statements.text: In geriatric surgical patients undergoing orthopedic surgery, adjuvant nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drug administration does not appear to adversely affect renal function during the perioperative period.
statements.support_summary: Directly relevant evidence in elderly orthopedic patients comes from a randomized placebo-controlled trial showing that IV parecoxib caused a brief fall in creatinine clearance, but overall postoperative glomerular/tubular changes were small, similar across groups, and judged not clinically relevant (PMID: 17056950). In minor orthopedic surgery, perioperative diclofenac produced transient renal effects consistent with prostaglandin inhibition, but no lasting adverse renal effects over the short follow-up, with caution advised in patients with pre-existing renal impairment (PMID: 7588670). Additional, less directly applicable studies found no renal harm from tenoxicam in healthy adults undergoing spinal surgery (PMID: 9972405) and no renal dysfunction after single-dose diclofenac in healthy older volunteers rather than surgical patients (PMID: 9258981). Overall, the supporting literature mostly suggests transient physiologic renal changes without clinically important perioperative deterioration, but several studies are in healthier or non-geriatric populations.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence largely comes from populations different from the claim: ibuprofen significantly reduced renal plasma flow and GFR in patients with glomerulonephritis, especially those with baseline renal impairment (PMID: 8569600), and ketoprofen/indomethacin caused acute falls in GFR and renal plasma flow in chronic renal insufficiency but not normal subjects (PMID: 3784306). A case report describes diclofenac-associated acute renal failure in a decompensated elderly patient, emphasizing risk with volume depletion and comorbidities (PMID: 23761717). More indirect evidence includes decreased creatinine clearance in healthy dogs given perioperative NSAIDs (PMID: 11138853), adverse renal hemodynamics from chronic ketorolac in aged obese rats (PMID: 7634547), and review/background articles warning that NSAIDs can reduce renal blood flow and GFR in volume-depleted or high-risk perioperative patients (PMID: 3057393; PMID: 17240605). Thus, the contradicting set mainly highlights vulnerability in renal impairment, dehydration, advanced age, or nonhuman models rather than directly refuting the claim in typical geriatric orthopedic surgical patients.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant human evidence in elderly orthopedic patients indicates only small or transient renal effects without clinically important perioperative harm, whereas most contradictory evidence comes from higher-risk renal populations, case reports, reviews, or nonhuman studies that mainly suggest caution in vulnerable patients rather than clear contradiction in the target setting.

source_article.title: Application of Intelligent Image Matching and Visual Communication in Brand Design.
source_article.abstract: In this paper, from the perspective of improving the visual communication of brand design, image texture intelligent matching processing is needed, proposing a brand design texture intelligent matching method based on visual communication, constructing a brand design texture intelligent information acquisition model under visual communication, using automatic image imaging technology for texture imaging and feature segmentation of brand design, and extracting typical brand design and ethnic design language of texture histogram, texture segmentation, and automatic matching under visual communication according to histogram distribution, brand design texture information enhancement and optimization detection by regularized feature fusion method, extraction of edge contour feature points of brand design, and texture matching with the extracted edge contour feature points of decorative patterns as input statistics. The adaptive performance of texture matching for a brand design using this method is better, and the texture discrimination ability is stronger, which improves the application research of better reflecting brand design in modern visual communication design and promotes the innovative combination of traditional cultural elements and modern design.

statements.text: Intelligent image matching and texture feature extraction can improve brand design image processing for visual communication-oriented brand design applications.
statements.support_summary: The supporting evidence is indirect but consistent with the technical part of the claim. A review of swarm intelligence methods reports that optimization algorithms are widely applied to image matching and image feature extraction, including image segmentation, classification, and edge detection, suggesting these intelligent methods can improve image-processing workflows, though it does not address brand design specifically (PMID: 37366829). Another study shows that improving shape-texture representations in CNNs yields more distinguishable features and substantially improves template matching, achieving state-of-the-art benchmark performance, which supports the idea that intelligent matching and texture-related feature extraction can enhance image processing tasks (PMID: 36081117). However, both studies are from general computer vision/image-processing contexts rather than visual communication-oriented brand design, so applicability to the exact claim is inferred rather than directly demonstrated.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only moderately. The available evidence supports the underlying technical proposition that intelligent matching and texture/feature extraction can improve image-processing performance, yet it does not directly test brand design or visual communication-specific applications.

matched_article.title: Disease Control and Prevention in Rare Plants Based on the Dominant Population Selection Method in Opportunistic Social Networks.
matched_article.abstract: The spread of seeds of rare and dangerous plants affects the regeneration, pattern, genetic structure, invasion, and settlement of plant populations. However, seed transmission is a relatively weak research link. The spread of plant seeds is not controlled by the communicator. Rather, this event results from the interaction between the host and the external environment determined by the mother. The way plants transmit and accept seeds is similar to how user nodes accept data transmission requests in social networks. Plants select the characteristics including seed size, maturity time, and gene matching, which are consistent with the size, delay, and keywords of the data received by the user. In this study, we selected rare and endangered Pterospermum heterophyllum as the research object and applied them to a social network. All plants were considered nodes and all seeds as transmitted data. This method avoids the influence of errors in actual sampling and statistical laws. By using historical information to record the reception of seeds, the Infection and Immunity Algorithm (IAIA) in opportunistic social networks was established. This method selects healthy plants through plant social populations and reduces the number of diseased plants. The experimental results show that the IAIA algorithm has a good effect in distinguishing dominant seedlings from seedlings with disease genes and realizes the selection of dominant plants in social networks.

source_article.title: MicroRNA-937 inhibits the malignant phenotypes of breast cancer by directly targeting and downregulating forkhead box Q1.
source_article.abstract: Purpose: Numerous microRNAs (miRNAs) are aberrantly expressed in breast cancer, and the dysregulation of miRNAs may affect the aggressiveness of this cancer. Aberrant expression of miRNA-937 (miR-937) in gastric and lung cancers has been reported, which plays tumor-suppressive or oncogenic roles in carcinogenesis including cancer progression. Our purpose was to investigate the involvement of miR-937 in breast cancer progression. Patients and methods: The expression profile of miR-937 in breast cancer was assessed by reverse-transcription quantitative PCR. Biological effects of miR-937 upregulation on the malignant characteristics of breast cancer cells were determined in a series of functional experiments. The direct target of miR-937 in breast cancer cells was also identified. Results: Herein, the expression levels of miR-937 were notably lower in breast cancer, and its underexpression was significantly correlated with lymph node metastasis and TNM stage. Patients with breast cancer underexpressing miR-937 showed shorter overall survival than did patients with breast cancer overexpressing miR-937. Proliferation, migration, and invasiveness of breast cancer cells were evidently suppressed by miR-937 upregulation. In addition, ectopic miR-937 expression hindered breast cancer tumor growth in vivo. Forkhead box Q1 (FOXQ1) mRNA was found to be a direct target of miR-937 in breast cancer. FOXQ1 turned out to be overexpressed in breast cancer tissues, and its overexpression negatively correlated with miR-937 expression. Moreover, silencing of FOXQ1 recapitulated the tumor-suppressive effects of miR-937 overexpression on breast cancer cells. Notably, FOXQ1 restoration abrogated the miR-937-mediated suppression of proliferation, migration, and invasiveness of breast cancer cells. Conclusion: These results collectively revealed that miR-937 acts as a tumor suppressor in breast cancer and restrains cancer progression by directly targeting FOXQ1 mRNA. These data suggest that targeting of the novel miR-937-FOXQ1 axis is an attractive therapeutic method against breast cancer.

statements.text: In breast cancer, low miR-937 expression is associated with more aggressive disease and poorer overall survival.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice rather than a primary study, and its abstract provides no substantive results about miR-937 expression, aggressiveness, or survival in breast cancer (PMID: 34675539). Although the title of the retracted paper implies miR-937 may suppress malignant breast cancer phenotypes, that inference comes from the title alone and is undermined by the retraction. This creates a discrepancy between the item’s categorization as contradiction and the actual evidentiary value available from the abstract, which is minimal.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear because there is no direct supporting evidence here, and the only contradicting item is a retraction notice without usable abstract-level findings. Overall, the claim is neither meaningfully supported nor convincingly contradicted by the provided evidence set.

statements.contradict_summary: One item is a retraction notice for a paper whose title matches the claim, indicating that the specific published evidence asserting that miR-937 suppresses breast cancer phenotypes by targeting FOXQ1 has been withdrawn (PMID: 34675539). The abstract does not provide new experimental data contradicting the biological claim directly; rather, it undermines confidence in the original reported support. Thus, this is best interpreted as invalidation of prior evidence rather than independent mechanistic evidence in the opposite direction, creating a discrepancy between the item's 'contradict' grouping and the nature of the abstract.
statements.conclusion: The claim is not supported by the provided evidence and is weakly contradicted overall. The only cited item is a retraction notice, which substantially reduces confidence in the claim's original supporting study but does not itself establish the opposite biological effect.

matched_article.title: MicroRNA-30a-3p inhibits the progression of lung cancer via the PI3K/AKT by targeting DNA methyltransferase 3a.
matched_article.abstract: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small non-coding RNAs, involved in pathological and physiological processes via regulating target genes expression. Abnormally expressed miR-30a-3p has been verified in several tumors, such as liver cancer, esophageal cancer and lung cancer. It was reported that DNA methylation plays a critical role in the tumorigenesis of lung cancer through regulated tumor suppressor genes silencing. Nevertheless, the potential mechanism of miR-30a-3p in restoring abnormal DNA methylation patterns is still unclear in lung cancer. Therefore, because the miR-30a-3p is complementary to the 3'-untranslated regions (3'-UTR) of DNA methyltransferase 3A (DNMT3A), we investigated whether miRNA-30a-3p could target DNMT3a to regulate the progression of lung cancer cell. qRT-PCR was used to evaluate miR-30a-3p and DNMT3a mRNA expression levels in A549 lung cancer cells and normal cell line BEAS-2B. MiR-30a-3p expression plasmid was transferred into A549 cells. The target of miR-30a-3p was detected by luciferase reporter assay. Western blot was used to measure related protein expression levels. MTT assay was used to measure the proliferation of cells in each group. The cycle and apoptosis of cells were detected by flow cytometry. We found down-regulation of miR-30a-3p mRNA expression and up-regulation of DNMT3a mRNA expression in A549 cells. Overexpression of miR-30a-3p downregulates DNMT3a or blocked DNMT3a by interference vector, significantly inhibited the proliferation and G1/S transition in A549 cells via regulating p38 MAPK pathway, and induced the apoptosis in A549 cells via regulating Bcl-2/Bax protein levels. Furthermore, we observed the opposite phenomenon in A549 cells transfected with both miR-30a-3p and DNMT3a vector. Our data show that miR-30a-3p suppressed the progression of lung cancer via regulating p38 MAPK pathway by targeting DNMT3A in A549 cells, indicating that miR-30a-3p might be a novel potential therapeutic strategy in the treatment of lung cancer.

statements.text: In A549 lung cancer cells, increased microRNA-30a-3p suppresses lung cancer cell progression by targeting DNA methyltransferase 3A and inhibiting the p38 MAPK pathway.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study in A549 lung adenocarcinoma cells found that miR-30a overexpression inhibited migration and invasion, but not proliferation or cell-cycle progression, and attributed this effect to direct targeting of EYA2 rather than DNMT3A or p38 MAPK, which conflicts with the specific mechanism in the claim (PMID: 26837415). Another A549 study reported that stable miR-30a overexpression did not significantly alter cell cycle, proliferation, colony formation, xenograft growth, or chemosensitivity, with only a modest increase in migration, directly arguing against a broad tumor-suppressive progression effect (PMID: 20169152). A separate paper reported that miR-30a-3p enhanced invasion, migration, angiogenesis, tumorigenicity, metastasis, and drug resistance through a CAGE/p53-related loop, the opposite direction of effect from the claim, although this study is less clearly tied to A549 cells specifically (PMID: 26912082). Overall, the contradicting literature mainly disputes either the claimed anti-progression phenotype or the proposed DNMT3A/p38 MAPK mechanism, and none of these abstracts support that exact pathway in A549 cells.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. There is no direct supporting evidence here, while multiple abstracts either report no suppressive effect of miR-30a in A549 cells, suggest alternative targets such as EYA2, or even describe pro-tumorigenic effects of miR-30a-3p.

statements.text: In A549 lung cancer cells, increased microRNA-30a-3p reduces cell proliferation and G1/S cell-cycle transition while promoting apoptosis.
statements.support_summary: Several NSCLC studies report tumor-suppressive effects of miR-30a in lung cancer cells, including A549. In particular, miR-30a overexpression inhibited growth of A549 and H460 cells, increased apoptosis, and induced cell-cycle arrest in NSCLC, supporting key parts of the claim though the abstract does not specify the 3p strand or explicitly name G1/S arrest in A549 (PMID: 29434747). Another NSCLC study in A549 cells found miR-30a overexpression inhibited proliferation and suppressed G1/S and S/G2 transition via IGF1R/PI3K/AKT signaling, directly supporting the proliferation and G1/S components but not reporting apoptosis in the abstract (PMID: 26025408). Additional indirect support comes from other cancer contexts where miR-30a-3p reduced proliferation and promoted apoptosis or cell-cycle arrest, including hepatocellular carcinoma and small-cell lung cancer, but these are not A549 models and therefore are only partially applicable (PMID: 24290372; PMID: 36345848).
