One listed support item concerns miR-30a-5p rather than miR-30a-3p, so it is a strand mismatch and only weakly relevant to the claim (PMID: 28158983).
statements.contradict_summary: Two A549-focused studies directly conflict with the claim by reporting that miR-30a overexpression did not significantly alter proliferation or cell-cycle progression in A549 cells, despite affecting migration/invasion or showing no phenotype overall (PMID: 26837415; PMID: 20169152). These are important contradictions because they use the same cell line, although the abstracts refer to miR-30a generally rather than specifically miR-30a-3p and do not discuss apoptosis. The remaining contradiction items are less directly relevant: one ESCC study actually found that increased miR-30a-3p/5p inhibited proliferation, which aligns with rather than contradicts the claim and appears miscategorized (PMID: 29259372). Another study suggests miR-30a-3p can promote aggressive behavior and drug resistance through a CAGE/p53 loop, but it is not clearly about A549 proliferation, G1/S transition, or apoptosis, so it is only indirect contradiction at best (PMID: 26912082).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest supporting evidence includes NSCLC/A549 studies showing reduced proliferation, cell-cycle arrest, and in one case increased apoptosis, but this is tempered by two direct A549 studies finding no effect of miR-30a overexpression on proliferation or cell cycle and by frequent ambiguity between miR-30a overall versus the specific 3p strand.

statements.text: In A549 lung cancer cells, restoring DNA methyltransferase 3A counteracts the antitumor effects of microRNA-30a-3p.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: Dissecting closely linked association signals in combination with the mammalian phenotype database can identify candidate genes in dairy cattle.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have been successfully implemented in cattle research and breeding. However, moving from the associations to identifying the causal variants and revealing underlying mechanisms have proven complicated. In dairy cattle populations, we face a challenge due to long-range linkage disequilibrium (LD) arising from close familial relationships in the studied individuals. Long range LD makes it difficult to distinguish if one or multiple quantitative trait loci (QTL) are segregating in a genomic region showing association with a phenotype. We had two objectives in this study: 1) to distinguish between multiple QTL segregating in a genomic region, and 2) use of external information to prioritize candidate genes for a QTL along with the candidate variant. RESULTS: We observed fixing the lead SNP as a covariate can help to distinguish additional close association signal(s). Thereafter, using the mammalian phenotype database, we successfully found candidate genes, in concordance with previous studies, demonstrating the power of this strategy. Secondly, we used variant annotation information to search for causative variants in our candidate genes. The variant information successfully identified known causal mutations and showed the potential to pinpoint the causative mutation(s) which are located in coding regions. CONCLUSIONS: Our approach can distinguish multiple QTL segregating on the same chromosome in a single analysis without manual input. Moreover, utilizing information from the mammalian phenotype database and variant effect predictor as post-GWAS analysis could benefit in candidate genes and causative mutations finding in cattle. Our study not only identified additional candidate genes for milk traits, but also can serve as a routine method for GWAS in dairy cattle.

statements.text: In dairy cattle populations, conditioning on a lead association signal can help distinguish additional closely linked quantitative trait loci segregating in the same genomic region or on the same chromosome.
statements.support_summary: Direct support comes from a dairy cattle GWAS methods study showing that fixing the lead SNP as a covariate helped reveal additional nearby association signals and distinguish multiple QTL segregating on the same chromosome (PMID: 30696404). Another dairy cattle GWAS reported that conditional testing on BTA6 indicated at least two closely located QTL for young stock survival and stillbirth, and also found two independent QTL on BTA23, consistent with conditioning helping separate linked signals (PMID: 37641238). Several other cited studies are only indirect or weakly relevant: one focuses on systems-biology post-GWAS network analysis rather than conditional lead-SNP analysis (PMID: 29965995), one fine-maps a calving QTL but emphasizes that extensive LD hampered pinpointing causality rather than demonstrating conditional dissection of multiple linked QTL (PMID: 26486911), and one confirms/fine-maps mastitis QTL without clearly using conditioning on a lead signal to separate close loci (PMID: 23647142). Overall, the strongest evidence specifically supports the claim, while some items in the support set appear miscategorized because their abstracts do not directly address conditional dissection of linked QTL.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. This is mainly because one study explicitly demonstrates that conditioning on the lead SNP can uncover additional close association signals in dairy cattle (PMID: 30696404), and a second study reports conditional analyses revealing multiple closely located QTL (PMID: 37641238), while no direct contradictory evidence is present.

statements.text: Using mammalian phenotype database information after genome-wide association analysis can improve prioritization of candidate genes for dairy cattle trait associations.
statements.support_summary: Several dairy-cattle post-GWAS studies directly report that adding mammalian phenotype database information helps refine candidate-gene lists after association mapping. A closely matched study states that combining GWAS follow-up with the mammalian phenotype database successfully identified candidate genes for milk traits and argues this can benefit candidate gene and causal mutation discovery in cattle (PMID: 30696404). Similar multi-source prioritization frameworks for female fertility and mastitis resistance explicitly used mammalian phenotype database information alongside LD structure, GO/KEGG, expression, and variant annotation to refine biologically relevant candidates after GWAS (PMID: 30935378; PMID: 30189836). Additional studies support the broader idea that integrating external biological priors or multi-omics data improves candidate/QTL prioritization in dairy cattle, though they do not specifically test mammalian phenotype database use and are therefore only indirect support (PMID: 26613780; PMID: 26920147; PMID: 27328759; PMID: 36759924).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Multiple directly relevant dairy-cattle studies show that using mammalian phenotype database information in post-GWAS analysis improves prioritization of biologically plausible candidate genes, and there is no presented evidence contradicting this claim.

statements.text: Variant annotation after genome-wide association analysis can help identify known causal mutations and potential causative coding variants in candidate genes for dairy cattle traits.
statements.support_summary: Multiple dairy cattle studies report that post-GWAS fine-mapping using sequence variants, functional annotation, and candidate-gene prioritization can recover known causal mutations and nominate plausible coding or functional variants. The strongest direct support comes from PMID: 30696404, which explicitly states that variant annotation after GWAS identified known causal mutations and highlighted potential causative coding variants in candidate genes, and from sequence-level association studies that recovered known missense causal variants such as DGAT1, ABCG2, and GHR for milk traits (PMID: 29121857, PMID: 27474979, PMID: 17584938, PMID: 22486504). Additional studies identified putative causal genes/variants after GWAS for survival, fertility, mastitis resistance, milk composition, and fatty-acid traits, including coding or frameshift candidates in genes such as LCORL and SCD (PMID: 37641238, PMID: 30935378, PMID: 30189836, PMID: 30219425, PMID: 26970560, PMID: 27468856). A recurring theme is that integrating variant annotation with other post-GWAS evidence—gene expression, pathways, phenotype databases, or multi-breed/meta-analysis—improves prioritization of candidate genes and variants (PMID: 30189836, PMID: 30935378, PMID: 31077144, PMID: 28923017). Some supporting papers are only indirect or weaker because they identify candidate regions/genes without explicitly demonstrating annotation-driven discovery of causal mutations, creating some heterogeneity within the support set.
statements.contradict_summary: One study on clinical mastitis found that even after sequence-level follow-up of GWAS hits across breeds, strong linkage disequilibrium prevented conclusive identification of the causal gene or polymorphism in any targeted region (PMID: 25151887). This contradicts a strong interpretation of the claim by showing that post-GWAS variant analysis does not always succeed in pinpointing causal mutations in dairy cattle. However, the study still identified biologically plausible candidate genes, so it challenges the consistency and resolution of the approach more than its general usefulness.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The evidence includes many dairy cattle studies—especially PMID: 30696404 and several sequence-level fine-mapping papers—that show post-GWAS variant annotation and related functional prioritization can identify known causal mutations and plausible causative coding variants, although success is limited in some regions by long-range linkage disequilibrium and imputation/annotation constraints.

matched_article.title: RNA-seq analysis of lncRNA-controlled developmental gene expression during puberty in goat & rat.
matched_article.abstract: Puberty is a pivotal stage in female animal development, and marks the onset of reproductive capability. However, little is known about the function of lncRNAs (long noncoding RNAs) in puberty. Therefore, RNA-seq analysis were performed between goats and rats to clarify the roles of lncRNAs and mRNAs in the onset of puberty. In the present study, the length of lncRNAs, the length of the open reading frame and the exon count were compared between the two species. Furthermore, functional annotation analysis based on Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) and Gene Ontology (GO) analysis of lncRNAs target genes and differentially expressed mRNA demonstrated the significantly enriched terms, such as AMPK signaling pathway, oxytocin signaling pathway, insulin secretion as well as pheromone receptor activity, and some other signaling pathways which were involved in the regulation of female puberty. Moreover, our results of siRNA interference in vitro showed the candidate lncRNA XLOC_446331 may play a crucial role in regulating female puberty. In conclusion, the RNA-seq analysis between goat and rat provide novel candidate regulators for genetic and molecular studies on female puberty.

statements.text: Comparative RNA-seq analysis of goat and rat identified long noncoding RNAs and messenger RNAs with expression patterns consistent with a role in regulating female puberty onset.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Functional analysis of long noncoding RNA target genes and differentially expressed messenger RNAs implicated signaling pathways related to female puberty regulation, including AMP-activated protein kinase signaling, oxytocin signaling, insulin secretion, and pheromone receptor activity.
statements.support_summary: One directly relevant goat hypothalamus puberty study reported differential lncRNAs and their target genes in pubertal versus prepubertal goats, with enrichment of oxytocin signaling and pheromone receptor activity among pathways related to puberty/reproduction, which partially matches the claim (PMID: 28196477). Additional goat reproductive transcriptome studies found lncRNA/mRNA-associated pathway enrichment including AMPK signaling in the oviduct and oxytocin signaling in hypothalamus, but these focused on fecundity/fertility rather than female puberty, so they are indirect support and a scope discrepancy relative to the claim (PMID: 35741792; PMID: 37056233). A rat PCOS ovary study also identified AMPK-related differential mRNA/lncRNA pathways, but it addresses disease pathophysiology rather than puberty regulation and does not cover the full claimed pathway set (PMID: 29505837). Across the supporting set, the recurring theme is that reproductive lncRNA/mRNA analyses can implicate AMPK or oxytocin-related pathways, while only the puberty-focused goat study clearly supports pheromone receptor activity; none of these abstracts clearly support insulin secretion in the specific puberty context.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall moderately supported. The strongest support comes from a directly relevant goat puberty transcriptomics study showing lncRNA target/pathway enrichment including oxytocin signaling and pheromone receptor activity, while the remaining evidence is more indirect because it concerns fertility, fecundity, or PCOS rather than female puberty and does not fully confirm all listed pathways, especially insulin secretion.

statements.text: Experimental suppression of candidate long noncoding RNA XLOC_446331 indicated that this long noncoding RNA may play an important role in regulating female puberty.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: Design of Faster R-CNN-Based Fault Detection Method for Subway Vehicles.
source_article.abstract: A substantial amount of maintenance and fault data is not properly utilized in the daily maintenance of pantographs in urban metro cars. Pantograph fault analysis can begin with three factors: the external environment, internal flaws, and joint behavior. Based on the analysis of pantograph fault types, corresponding measures are proposed in terms of pantograph fault handling and maintenance strategies, in order to provide safety guarantee for the safe and effective realization of rail transit vehicle speed-up and also provide reference for the maintenance and overhaul of pantographs. For the problem of planned maintenance no longer meeting current pantograph maintenance requirements, a defect diagnosis system based on a combination of faster R-CNN neural networks is presented. The pantograph image features are extracted by introducing an alternative to the original feature extraction module that can extract deep-level image features and achieve feature reuse, and the data transformation operations such as image rotation and enhancement are used to expand the sample set in the experiment to enhance the detection effect. The simulation results demonstrate that the diagnosis procedure is quick and accurate.

statements.text: A Faster R-CNN-based pantograph defect diagnosis system can detect subway vehicle pantograph faults quickly and accurately using image features extracted from deep neural network feature extraction and augmented training images.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a paper on a Faster R-CNN-based fault detection method for subway vehicles, which indicates the original article should not be relied upon as evidence (PMID: 37538476). However, the abstract does not report experimental results showing that the method is inaccurate or slow; it only states that the prior article was retracted. Thus, this is best interpreted as undermining confidence in related supporting evidence rather than directly proving the claim false.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear but leans away from support. There is no direct supporting evidence provided, and the sole available item is a retraction notice that weakens confidence in the claimed Faster R-CNN pantograph fault-diagnosis approach without directly demonstrating that the approach cannot work.

statements.text: Maintenance of urban metro car pantographs should use fault-handling and maintenance strategies tailored to pantograph fault types and fault analysis from external environment factors, internal flaws, and joint behavior to improve safe and effective railway vehicle operation.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Planned maintenance alone is no longer sufficient for pantograph maintenance requirements, and a data-driven defect diagnosis approach can better support pantograph maintenance and overhaul than conventional planned maintenance.
statements.support_summary: One study supports the claim indirectly by arguing that traditional pantograph slider inspection methods are inefficient and by proposing a deep-learning/computer-vision system to improve wear detection accuracy and segmentation performance (PMID: 39204830). The abstract emphasizes the need for regular condition monitoring because wear can progress to deep depressions and fracture risk, which is consistent with a shift toward condition- or data-driven maintenance rather than relying only on fixed planned maintenance intervals (PMID: 39204830). However, this evidence is limited because the abstract focuses on defect detection methodology and does not directly compare data-driven diagnosis against conventional planned maintenance outcomes. No obvious internal discrepancy is present, but the support is only partial.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall weakly supported. The available abstract suggests that data-driven defect detection can enhance pantograph condition monitoring beyond traditional approaches, but there is no direct evidence here showing that planned maintenance alone is insufficient or that data-driven diagnosis is superior for maintenance and overhaul outcomes.

matched_article.title: The Association of Sperm DNA Fragment and Assisted Reproductive Outcomes: A Meta-Analysis.
matched_article.abstract: To analyze the effect of sperm DNA fragmentation index (DFI) on the outcomes of in vitro fertilization (IVF) and intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI). Literature search was conducted on English databases PubMed, Cochrane, and Embase to obtain eligible studies. A total of 11 cohort studies were included and analyzed using the random effects model. The results illustrated that the IVF fertilization rate (RR = 0.94, 95% CI: 0.77-1.14, P = 0.61), pregnancy rate (RR = 0.83, 95% CI: 0.57-1.21, P = 0.32), and live birth rate (RR = 0.53, 95% CI: 0.16-1.80, P = 0.31) in the high DFI group were statistically insignificant with those in the low FI group. The correlations between DFI and ICSI fertilization rate (RR = 0.79, 95% CI: 0.52-1.18, P = 0.25), pregnancy rate (RR = 0.89, 95% CI: 0.74-1.06, P = 0.18), and live birth rate (RR = 0.89, 95% CI: 0.70-1.14, P = 0.36) were also not statistically significant. This study has observed no significant correlation between sperm DFI and assisted reproductive outcomes. Multicenter large-sample clinical trials are required to conclusively determine the impact of DNA damage on the clinical outcomes of assisted reproduction.

statements.text: In couples undergoing in vitro fertilization, higher sperm DNA fragmentation is not associated with lower fertilization, pregnancy, or live birth rates compared with lower sperm DNA fragmentation.
statements.support_summary: Multiple cohort studies and reviews reported no significant differences in fertilization, clinical pregnancy, implantation/ongoing pregnancy, or live birth outcomes between high- and low-sperm DNA fragmentation groups in IVF/ICSI, including large retrospective cohorts and prospective studies (PMID: 30122567, 17904130, 31755002, 31555559, 32359369, 35400579). The broadest synthesis, an umbrella review, found associations of sperm DNA fragmentation with live birth, pregnancy, implantation, blastulation, and fertilization to be weak or nonsignificant, while also judging most prior reviews to be low quality and high risk of bias (PMID: 38450853). Several ICSI-focused or selected-population studies likewise found no association with pregnancy or live birth, though some noted higher miscarriage or poorer embryo/blastocyst metrics despite similar pregnancy outcomes (PMID: 25227693, 30941554, 38172506, 35712248). Some studies in the support set are mixed rather than purely supportive, for example showing lower ICSI fertilization or implantation with high fragmentation but unchanged pregnancy/live birth, indicating internal inconsistency in this evidence group (PMID: 28322098, 30157358). Overall, the recurring theme is that sperm DNA fragmentation often correlates with poorer semen parameters, but not consistently with downstream IVF/ICSI success rates.
statements.contradict_summary: Many contradictory studies, including several meta-analyses and systematic reviews, reported that higher sperm DNA fragmentation is associated with lower clinical pregnancy and/or live birth rates, especially in conventional IVF rather than ICSI (PMID: 25530036, 25190048, 27345006, 33644978). Large cohort studies found significant reductions in IVF fertilization, good-quality embryo formation, implantation, or live birth/cumulative live birth with higher fragmentation, again often with little or no effect in ICSI (PMID: 26757265, 34376283, 21479459, 27416667). Several smaller observational studies also linked elevated fragmentation to lower fertilization, implantation, pregnancy, or delivery rates, or to thresholds above which no pregnancies occurred, although some abstracts report mixed findings such as unchanged fertilization but reduced pregnancy, or subgroup-specific effects by female age, ovarian reserve, or BMI (PMID: 23200202, 23290739, 35909570, 31753711, 25747135, 38602720). There are also discrepancies within this contradiction set: a few included papers found no IVF effect or only ICSI/subgroup effects, and one older study actually failed to show high-fragmentation thresholds for poor pregnancy outcome, suggesting misclassification/noisy labeling (PMID: 16084876, 20495207). The dominant theme, however, is that high sperm DNA fragmentation may impair IVF outcomes more clearly than ICSI outcomes, particularly for pregnancy and live birth endpoints.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall unclear but leans contradicted. Although many studies report no association, the contradictory evidence is larger and includes multiple meta-analyses and sizable IVF cohorts showing worse pregnancy or live birth outcomes with higher sperm DNA fragmentation, particularly in conventional IVF, while the literature repeatedly suggests any adverse effect is less consistent in ICSI.

statements.text: In couples undergoing intracytoplasmic sperm injection, higher sperm DNA fragmentation is not associated with lower fertilization, pregnancy, or live birth rates compared with lower sperm DNA fragmentation.
statements.support_summary: Multiple cohort studies specifically in ICSI found no significant differences between high- and low-sperm DNA fragmentation groups for fertilization and pregnancy-related outcomes, including studies reporting similar fertilization, implantation, clinical/ongoing pregnancy, and in some cases live birth outcomes (PMID: 30122567, PMID: 17904130, PMID: 31755002, PMID: 25227693, PMID: 31555559, PMID: 32359369, PMID: 18294305, PMID: 30941554, PMID: 35400579, PMID: 34376283, PMID: 22235675, PMID: 21479459, PMID: 17019633, PMID: 23290739). Large recent datasets also reported no effect of DFI on ICSI fertilization, pregnancy, stillbirth, or birth-defect outcomes, though some noted higher miscarriage or lower birth weight with higher DFI, which weakens full support for live-birth neutrality (PMID: 38172506, PMID: 32359369). An umbrella review concluded that across ART, including ICSI, associations of sperm DNA fragmentation with fertilization, pregnancy, and live birth were weak or nonsignificant, while also highlighting that the underlying reviews were mostly low quality and high risk of bias (PMID: 38450853). Some items in the support set are only partially relevant or indirect, such as evidence centered on high DNA stainability rather than fragmentation itself (PMID: 25897483). Overall, the supporting literature recurrently suggests that ICSI may attenuate or eliminate adverse associations of sperm DNA fragmentation with fertilization and pregnancy outcomes, with several studies also finding no reduction in live birth.
statements.contradict_summary: Several ICSI-focused primary studies reported worse outcomes with higher sperm DNA fragmentation, including lower fertilization and/or lower implantation, pregnancy, continuing pregnancy, or live birth, particularly above specific DFI thresholds (PMID: 17074327, PMID: 21973140, PMID: 20495207, PMID: 29778100, PMID: 33648046, PMID: 27231097, PMID: 28322098). Some observational studies comparing ejaculated versus testicular sperm in men with high ejaculated fragmentation found better pregnancy and live-birth outcomes with lower-fragmentation testicular sperm, indirectly suggesting clinically important harm from high sperm DNA fragmentation in ICSI (PMID: 26428305, PMID: 30734539). Meta-analyses and reviews often reported an overall adverse association of sperm DNA damage with ART pregnancy or live-birth outcomes, but several of these pooled IVF and ICSI together or explicitly found the pregnancy effect stronger in IVF than ICSI, making them only partially contradictory for this ICSI-specific claim (PMID: 27345006, PMID: 25190048, PMID: 27832085, PMID: 25530036, PMID: 16740220). There are notable discrepancies in this contradiction set: some articles are miscategorized because their abstracts actually state no significant association in ICSI or mainly concern IVF/other biomarkers rather than ICSI DFI effects (e.g., PMID: 23200202, PMID: 26757265, PMID: 31393936, PMID: 14556809, PMID: 37556219). A recurring theme among the truly contradictory evidence is that high fragmentation may have less effect on fertilization than on downstream outcomes such as implantation, miscarriage, and live birth, especially in selected subgroups or at high DFI cutoffs.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted for ICSI specifically. Although some ICSI studies and mixed-ART meta-analyses suggest worse downstream outcomes with high sperm DNA fragmentation, a larger body of directly relevant ICSI evidence—including large cohorts and an umbrella review—reports no clear reduction in fertilization, pregnancy, or live birth, and several contradictory items are only indirectly relevant or miscategorized.

statements.text: Overall, sperm DNA fragmentation does not appear to have a significant effect on assisted reproductive outcomes.
statements.support_summary: Multiple cohort studies across IVF, ICSI, and IUI reported no significant associations between sperm DNA fragmentation (DFI/SDF) and key assisted reproduction outcomes such as fertilization, embryo quality, implantation, clinical pregnancy, miscarriage, or live birth, although DFI often correlated with poorer semen parameters like motility and concentration (PMID: 30122567, PMID: 31755002, PMID: 35400579, PMID: 36120437, PMID: 18294305). Several studies also found no effect in specific contexts such as euploid blastocyst transfer, oocyte donation cycles, recurrent implantation failure, and neonatal/birth outcomes after ICSI or IVF/ICSI (PMID: 31755002, PMID: 30941554, PMID: 35671775, PMID: 32359369, PMID: 21950616). Higher-level evidence includes an umbrella review concluding that associations between SDF and live birth, pregnancy, miscarriage, implantation, blastulation, and fertilization were weak or nonsignificant, with included reviews generally low quality and high risk of bias (PMID: 38450853). Some “supporting” studies contain caveats, including higher pregnancy loss or abortion trends with higher DFI despite no effect on fertilization or pregnancy rates, indicating internal inconsistency with the broad claim (PMID: 25227693, PMID: 17904130, PMID: 31555559). Overall, the supporting evidence emphasizes limited predictive value of sperm DNA fragmentation testing for ART outcomes rather than proving complete absence of effect.
statements.contradict_summary: Many observational studies and several systematic reviews/meta-analyses reported that higher sperm DNA fragmentation is associated with poorer ART outcomes, especially in conventional IVF, including lower fertilization, blastocyst development, implantation, clinical pregnancy, live birth, or cumulative live birth, and higher miscarriage (PMID: 23200202, PMID: 25530036, PMID: 26757265, PMID: 34376283, PMID: 25190048, PMID: 27345006, PMID: 33644978). A recurring pattern is effect heterogeneity: detrimental associations are often stronger in IVF than ICSI, or appear only in subgroups such as women with reduced ovarian reserve, older female age, overweight subgroups, or frozen/fresh transfer settings (PMID: 31393936, PMID: 31753711, PMID: 25747135, PMID: 35909570, PMID: 33354950). Several studies suggest clinical improvement when high-DFI cases are managed differently, such as using ICSI instead of IVF or using testicular rather than ejaculated sperm, indirectly supporting a harmful role for sperm DNA damage (PMID: 29026719, PMID: 26428305, PMID: 30734539, PMID: 16921163). Some contradict items are weaker or discrepant with the claim framing because they focus on related biomarkers, sperm selection interventions, or only specific intermediate outcomes rather than overall ART success (e.g., mitochondrial membrane potential, TXNDC8, embryo quality only), and one item lacks usable abstract detail (PMID: 37556219, PMID: 32851881, PMID: 37215498). Overall, the contradictory evidence is larger and repeatedly points to clinically relevant negative effects in at least some ART settings, particularly IVF.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although many individual studies and one umbrella review report weak or nonsignificant overall associations, the contradictory literature is larger and includes multiple meta-analyses and sizable cohorts showing worse outcomes—especially for IVF and certain subgroups—suggesting sperm DNA fragmentation can meaningfully affect assisted reproductive outcomes rather than having no significant effect overall.

source_article.title: Regulation of beta-catenin structure and activity by tyrosine phosphorylation.
source_article.abstract: beta-Catenin plays a dual role as a key effector in the regulation of adherens junctions and as a transcriptional coactivator. Phosphorylation of Tyr-654, a residue placed in the last armadillo repeat of beta-catenin, decreases its binding to E-cadherin. We show here that phosphorylation of Tyr-654 also stimulates the association of beta-catenin to the basal transcription factor TATA-binding protein. The structural bases of these different affinities were investigated. Our results indicate that the beta-catenin C-terminal tail interacts with the armadillo repeat domain, hindering the association of the armadillo region to the TATA-binding protein or to E-cadherin. Phosphorylation of beta-catenin Tyr-654 decreases armadillo-C-terminal tail association, uncovering the last armadillo repeats. In a C-terminal-depleted beta-catenin, the presence of a negative charge at Tyr-654 does not affect the interaction of the TATA-binding protein to the armadillo domain. However, in the case of E-cadherin, the establishment of ion pairs dominates its association with beta-catenin, and its binding is greatly dependent on the absence of a negative charge at Tyr-654. Thus, phosphorylation of Tyr-654 blocks the Ecadherin-beta-catenin interaction, even though the steric hindrance of the C-tail is no longer present. These results explain how phosphorylation of beta-catenin in Tyr-654 modifies the tertiary structure of this protein and the interaction with its different partners.

statements.text: Tyrosine phosphorylation of beta-catenin at Tyr-654 decreases beta-catenin binding to E-cadherin in the adherens junction complex.
statements.support_summary: Two studies support the broader idea that tyrosine phosphorylation of beta-catenin weakens its association with E-cadherin. In Caco-2 cells, acetaldehyde increased beta-catenin phosphorylation at several sites including Tyr-654 and reduced beta-catenin/E-cadherin binding; purified-protein assays further showed that tyrosine phosphorylation of beta-catenin directly reduced binding to the E-cadherin cytoplasmic tail, although the abstract does not isolate Tyr-654 as the sole causal site (PMID: 17087658). In human keratinocytes, peroxovanadate-induced tyrosine phosphorylation of beta-catenin reversibly reduced binding to E-cadherin and decreased cell-cell adhesion, but no specific phosphorylation residue was identified, so this is indirect support for the Tyr-654-specific claim (PMID: 11710913). Overall, the supporting set consistently links beta-catenin tyrosine phosphorylation with reduced E-cadherin association, but only one abstract explicitly includes Tyr-654 and even that study implicates multiple phosphorylated tyrosines rather than Tyr-654 alone.
statements.contradict_summary: The contradicting set contains a major categorization discrepancy: one study actually strongly supports the claim rather than contradicting it, reporting that Src phosphorylation decreases beta-catenin/E-cadherin binding and that Tyr-654 is the relevant residue controlling this interaction in vitro and in vivo (PMID: 10593980). Another melanoma study also appears supportive from the abstract, as geldanamycin-induced beta-catenin dephosphorylation increased beta-catenin/E-cadherin association, and Tyr-654 mutants were used to model phosphorylated versus dephosphorylated states, implying phosphorylation at Tyr-654 weakens association rather than strengthens it (PMID: 11245482). Clearer contradiction comes from a cancer-cell study reporting that Tyr-654 phosphorylation correlated with dissociation of beta-catenin from alpha-catenin or c-erbB-2, but not with dissociation between beta-catenin and E-cadherin (PMID: 19639172). An older breast epithelial-cell study found beta-catenin tyrosine phosphorylation associated with loss of alpha-catenin from the complex while E-cadherin still coprecipitated with beta-catenin, but it did not test Tyr-654 specifically, making it at most indirect contradiction (PMID: 9233779).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Although one study specifically argues Tyr-654 phosphorylation is not linked to beta-catenin/E-cadherin dissociation (PMID: 19639172), the strongest site-specific abstract in the set indicates that phosphorylation of beta-catenin at Tyr-654 reduces E-cadherin binding (PMID: 10593980), and several other studies are broadly consistent with tyrosine phosphorylation weakening the adherens junction complex.

statements.text: Tyrosine phosphorylation of beta-catenin at Tyr-654 increases beta-catenin association with the basal transcription factor TATA-binding protein.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One abstract directly argues against the claim's framing in the accompanying explanation, but the abstract itself actually reports that blocking tyrosine phosphorylation opposes TBP binding rather than supporting a contradiction. Specifically, SHP-1 reduced β-catenin phosphorylation at Tyr-86 and Tyr-654, and both SHP-1 expression and Y86F/Y654F mutations significantly interfered with β-catenin association with TBP, implying that these tyrosine sites contribute positively to TBP association (PMID: 20840866). Thus, the evidence item appears miscategorized as contradicting, because its abstract is more consistent with support for the claim than refutation.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear from the provided set because there is no explicit supporting item and the lone contradicting item appears to be misclassified. Based on the abstract text, PMID: 20840866 is more consistent with tyrosine phosphorylation at Tyr-654 promoting β-catenin association with TBP than contradicting that statement.

statements.text: The beta-catenin C-terminal tail regulates access of the armadillo repeat domain to binding partners, and phosphorylation at Tyr-654 alters beta-catenin structure by weakening C-terminal tail association with the armadillo domain.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Expression studies on clustered trypanosomatid box C/D small nucleolar RNAs.
matched_article.abstract: We analyzed three chromosomal loci of the trypanosomatid Leptomonas collosoma encoding box C/D small nucleolar RNAs (snoRNAs). All the snoRNAs that were analyzed here carry two sequences complementary to rRNA target sites and obey the +5 rule for guide methylation. Studies on transgenic parasites carrying the snoRNA-2 gene in the episomal expression vector (pX-neo) indicated that no promoter activity was found immediately adjacent to this gene. Deleting the flanking sequences of snoRNA-2 affected the expression; in the absence of the 3'-flanking (but not 5'-flanking) sequence, the expression was almost completely abolished. The snoRNA genes are transcribed as polycistronic RNA. All snoRNAs can be folded into a common stem-loop structure, which may play a role in processing the polycistronic transcript. snoRNA B2, a member of a snoRNA cluster, was expressed when cloned into the episomal vector, suggesting that each gene within a cluster is individually processed. Studies with permeable cells indicated that snoRNA gene transcription was relatively sensitive to alpha-amanitin, thus supporting transcription by RNA polymerase II. We propose that snoRNA gene expression, similar to protein-coding genes in this family, is regulated at the processing level.

statements.text: Expression of clustered box C/D small nucleolar RNA genes in the trypanosomatid Leptomonas collosoma is regulated mainly at the RNA processing level rather than by a nearby promoter for individual snoRNA genes.
statements.support_summary: Two abstracts support the claim that clustered snoRNAs in Leptomonas collosoma are produced from polycistronic precursors and that regulation depends importantly on processing rather than on independent nearby promoters. PMID: 14645367 reports that snoRNA genes are clustered and transcribed polycistronically, and deletion/mutational analyses identified 10-nt flanking sequences essential for processing a tagged C/D snoRNA from its precursor; although a 700-bp upstream fragment enhanced expression in an orientation-dependent manner, the detection of continuous transcripts spanning this region suggests transcription can also originate from an upstream long polycistronic unit rather than a dedicated promoter for each snoRNA. PMID: 11483606 explicitly states, in reference to the same Leptomonas locus, that multiple C/D snoRNAs have expression regulated at the processing level, and further shows that an H/ACA snoRNA from that locus is likewise processed from a long polycistronic transcript carrying both C/D and H/ACA snoRNAs. A minor nuance is that PMID: 14645367 also suggests an upstream transcription-enhancing element may exist, so the evidence supports processing-centered regulation but does not fully exclude contributory transcriptional control.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The available abstracts consistently indicate that these clustered snoRNAs arise from long polycistronic transcripts and that key determinants of expression lie in RNA processing signals, while the only caveat is limited evidence for an upstream transcription-enhancing element rather than a clear individual snoRNA promoter.

statements.text: A 3'-flanking sequence is important for expression of the Leptomonas collosoma snoRNA-2 gene, whereas the 5'-flanking sequence is not essential.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Genes within a box C/D small nucleolar RNA cluster in Leptomonas collosoma can be individually processed and expressed as separate small nucleolar RNAs.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract states that in Leptomonas collosoma a genomic locus encodes multiple C/D snoRNAs whose expression is regulated at the processing level, and that an H/ACA RNA from the same locus is processed from a long polycistronic transcript carrying both C/D and H/ACA snoRNAs (PMID: 11483606). This indirectly supports the claim by indicating that clustered snoRNA genes can be processed out of a shared precursor into mature individual RNAs, although the abstract does not directly demonstrate separate expression of each C/D gene within the cluster.
statements.contradict_summary: The two 'contradicting' abstracts actually mostly describe polycistronic transcription followed by processing into mature snoRNAs rather than disproving individual processing. PMID: 14645367 reports that snoRNAs in Leptomonas collosoma are transcribed polycistronically and processed into mature RNAs, and identifies short flanking sequences essential for processing of a tagged C/D snoRNA from its precursor, which is more consistent with the claim than contradictory. PMID: 12808138 similarly states that clustered C/D and H/ACA snoRNAs are transcribed as polycistronic transcripts and then processed to mature snoRNAs; this addresses common-precursor transcription, not an inability to generate separate snoRNAs. Thus, there is a categorization discrepancy: these studies do not clearly contradict the claim and in part align with it.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Across the abstracts, the consistent theme is polycistronic transcription with subsequent processing into mature snoRNAs in Leptomonas collosoma, and none of the cited studies clearly refutes the possibility that clustered box C/D snoRNA genes can be individually processed into separate snoRNAs.

source_article.title: RETRACTED ARTICLE: Nicardipine inhibits amrinone-enhanced contractility in fatigued diaphragm.
source_article.abstract: The purpose of this study was to examine the effect of amrinone, a bipyridine derivative, with and without nicardipine, a calcium channel blocker, on the contractility of fatigued diaphragm in dogs. Twenty dogs were divided into two groups of ten each: amrinone group (group A) and combined amrinone and nicardipine group (group AN). Diaphragmatic fatigue was induced by intermittent supramaximal bilateral electrophrenic stimulation at a frequency of 20 Hz applied for 30 min. Diaphragmatic contractility was assessed from changes in transdiaphragmatic pressure (Pdi). In group A, after producing fatigue, amrinone (0.75 mg·kg(-1) loading dose plus 10 μg·kg(-1)·min(-1) maintenance dose) was administered iv. In group AN, nicardipine 5 μg·kg(-1)·min(-1) was infused iv simultaneously with amrinone during this period. After diaphragmatic fatigue, Pdi at low-frequency (10-30 Hz) stimulation decreased compared with the prefatigue values (P<0.05), whereas no change in Pdi was observed at high-frequency (50-100 Hz), stimulation. The Pdi at each stimulus were increased compared with the fatigued values (P<0.05) by administering amrinone, and returned to these values after this agent was discontinued. The Pdi values at any frequency of stimulation did not change when amrinone was administered with nicardipine. Our results suggest that amirinone may enhance contractility in fatigued diaphragm via its effect on transmembrane calcium movement.

statements.text: In fatigued diaphragms in dogs, amrinone increased diaphragmatic contractility compared with fatigued diaphragms without amrinone.
statements.support_summary: One related dog study reported that after induced diaphragmatic fatigue, inhaled milrinone at doses of 0.2 or 0.5 mg/ml increased transdiaphragmatic pressure from fatigued values, whereas vehicle and the lowest milrinone dose did not, indicating improved contractility in fatigued diaphragms (PMID: 14643172). However, this study tested milrinone rather than amrinone, so it is only indirect evidence for the specific claim and represents a drug-class/context match rather than direct confirmation.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The sole abstract shows improved contractility in fatigued dog diaphragms with milrinone, but because it does not study amrinone directly, the evidence for the specific statement remains limited.

statements.text: In fatigued diaphragms in dogs, concomitant nicardipine eliminated the contractility-enhancing effect of amrinone compared with amrinone alone.
statements.support_summary: The only cited supporting item is a retraction notice stating that the specific article "Nicardipine inhibits amrinone-enhanced contractility in fatigued diaphragm" was retracted because of overwhelming evidence of fabrication and lack of institutional confirmation of data integrity (PMID: 23666482). Although the notice names a study whose title is directionally consistent with the claim, the abstract does not provide usable experimental results and instead undermines the reliability of that study. This is therefore a major discrepancy: the item is categorized as support, but its content mainly weakens confidence in the claim rather than substantively supporting it.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear and effectively very weak. The only item mentions a study with a title consistent with the claim, but because it is a retraction for likely fabricated data (PMID: 23666482), it cannot be considered reliable support.

statements.text: The contractility-enhancing effect of amrinone in fatigued diaphragms is consistent with a mechanism involving transmembrane calcium movement.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Studies of the mechanism of nephrotoxicity of compound A in rats.
matched_article.abstract: CO2 absorbents acting on sevoflurane produce compound A [CF2=C(CF3)OCH2F]. Rats breathing 25-50 ppm of compound A for 3-12 h demonstrate corticomedullary renal injury. Several halogenated alkenes also produce a well described corticomedullary lesion by conversion of glutathione conjugates of these alkenes to cysteine s-conjugates and subsequent metabolism by renal cysteine conjugate β-lyase to nephrotoxic halothionoacetyl halides. We tested whether a similar mechanism explained the nephrotoxicity of compound A or whether an oxidative metabolism of compound A by cytochrome P-450 was required for the induction of nephrotoxicity. A closed rebreathing system was used and male Wistar rats were exposed for 1 h to: (1) oxygen alone; (2) 800 ppm compound A; (3) 800 ppm compound A after pretreatment with intraperitoneal aminooxyacetic acid (AOAA), 0.5 mmoles/kg, an inhibitor of renal cysteine conjugate β-lyase; (4) 600 ppm compound A; (5) 600 ppm compound A after pretreatment with intraperitoneal AOAA, 0.50 mmoles/kg plus acivicin (AT-125), 0.25 mmoles/kg, an inhibitor of gamma glutamyl transpeptidase; (6) 600 ppm compound A after pretreatment with 1600 mg/kg piperonyl butoxide (PB) subcutaneously, and (7) 600 ppm compound A after pretreatment with 100 mg/kg 1-aminobenzotriazole (ABT) by intraperitoneal injection (both PB and ABT inhibit cytochrome P-450s). All rats were killed 24 h following exposure to compound A or oxygen, or to pretreatments without compound A, and the kidneys were collected for histological analysis. Pretreatments given without compound A did not cause renal injury. Necrosis was found in 20.9±16.7% (mean±SD) of corticomedullary tubule cells following exposure of Wistar rats to 600 ppm compound A. Pretreatment with AOAA plus AT-125 increased necrosis to 57.9±32.6%, (P<0.005). PB or ABT given prior to compound A increased corticomedullary injury to 39.0±31.4% (P<0.02) and 51.2±31.8% (P<0.025), respectively. In rats exposed to 800 ppm compound A, pretreatment with AOAA increased necrosis from 63.8±30.1% to 81.2±27.7% (P<0.1). Unlike many other halogenated alkenes, compound A does not appear to produce renal injury by conversion of a cysteine S-conjugate to a toxic thiol, nor does injury require metabolism mediaited by cytochrome P-450. Injury may result from direct toxicity of compound A or by an undetermined metabolic pathway.

statements.text: Compound A caused corticomedullary renal necrosis in rats exposed to compound A by inhalation.
statements.support_summary: Multiple rat inhalation studies directly report that Compound A causes renal necrosis centered at the corticomedullary junction/outer stripe of the outer medulla. Dose-response experiments found corticomedullary tubular necrosis after 3-hour exposures at 50 ppm and above (PMID: 8141452), after 6-12 hour exposures at 25-50 ppm and above depending on duration (PMID: 7908177), and after 1-hour exposures at 200 ppm and above (PMID: 7653823). Mechanistic and pathway studies also describe Compound A as causing corticomedullary junction necrosis or proximal tubular necrosis after inhalation in rats, while exploring whether glutathione/cysteine conjugate and beta-lyase pathways modify injury severity rather than disputing that injury occurs (PMID: 8615496, PMID: 9637657). One additional study notes that compound A can produce renal corticomedullary necrosis but also suggests other sevoflurane degradation products may augment injury, so it supports nephrotoxicity while introducing some attribution complexity rather than true contradiction (PMID: 12620947); a comparative toxicity paper likewise supports renal injury from inhaled Compound A but is less specific on the exact lesion location (PMID: 9356119).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Several independent rat inhalation studies consistently show Compound A produces corticomedullary/outer medullary renal necrosis, with clear exposure-duration and concentration relationships, and no direct contradictory evidence was provided.

statements.text: Inhibition of renal cysteine conjugate beta-lyase did not reduce compound A–induced renal injury in rats and instead increased injury, arguing against a cysteine S-conjugate to toxic thiol mechanism.
statements.support_summary: Two rat studies directly support the claim that blocking the cysteine S-conjugate/beta-lyase pathway did not protect against Compound A nephrotoxicity and may worsen it. In inhalation experiments, depletion of glutathione, inhibition of cysteine S-conjugate formation, and inhibition of renal cysteine conjugate beta-lyase with aminooxyacetic acid produced either no change or increased renal injury after Compound A exposure, leading the authors to conclude this pathway more likely mediates detoxification than toxicity (PMID: 8615496). A follow-up study found that aminooxyacetic acid did not inhibit toxicity of Compound A mercapturates, acivicin potentiated toxicity, and no renal protein adducts of the putative thionoacyl halide metabolite were detected, arguing against beta-lyase–mediated bioactivation as the main mechanism (PMID: 9952153). Together these abstracts consistently argue against a cysteine S-conjugate to toxic thiol mechanism in male Wistar rats.
statements.contradict_summary: Several earlier studies support at least partial involvement of renal uptake and beta-lyase metabolism in Compound A nephrotoxicity, though the evidence is mixed. Intraperitoneal rat studies reported that aminooxyacetic acid reduced biochemical injury markers such as proteinuria, glucosuria, diuresis, and alpha-GST excretion, and one abstract interprets this as partial protection, while probenecid was more strongly protective; however, even that study notes aminooxyacetic acid did not significantly alter the histologic pattern of injury, creating some internal tension with the stronger mechanistic conclusion (PMID: 9009951; PMID: 7755627). In inhalation studies, aminooxyacetic acid diminished proteinuria and glucosuria but did not reduce tubular necrosis, which contradicts the claim only partially because it does not show increased injury from beta-lyase inhibition (PMID: 9637657). Additional indirect mechanistic papers show Compound A/FDVE is metabolized through glutathione/cysteine conjugates and that beta-lyase inhibition decreases formation of a beta-lyase-derived metabolite, but these do not directly demonstrate reduced renal injury from inhibition in the same way as the claim addresses (PMID: 8831812; PMID: 3288445).
statements.conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans slightly toward the claim because the most directly relevant later studies using inhaled Compound A and immunochemical assessment found no protection from beta-lyase inhibition and in some cases worsening of injury, arguing against the toxic thiol mechanism. Earlier studies do suggest partial biochemical protection and pathway involvement, but their histologic results were weaker or inconsistent, so overall the claim is somewhat more supported than contradicted.

statements.text: Inhibition of cytochrome P-450 did not prevent compound A–induced renal injury in rats, indicating that cytochrome P-450–mediated metabolism is not required for nephrotoxicity.
statements.support_summary: One rat inhalation study found that aminobenzotriazole, a cytochrome P450 inhibitor, had no consistent protective effect against compound A nephrotoxicity, while interventions targeting renal uptake of S-conjugates or the beta-lyase pathway were more clearly influential, supporting the idea that P450 inhibition does not prevent injury (PMID: 9637657). The same abstract reports dose-related proximal tubular necrosis and urinary injury markers despite P450 inhibition, whereas probenecid prevented necrosis and aminooxyacetic acid only partly reduced injury markers, emphasizing non-P450 pathways. Overall, this abstract supports the claim that cytochrome P450-mediated metabolism is not necessary for compound A nephrotoxicity under those conditions.
statements.contradict_summary: A later rat study directly identified in vivo formation of compound A (FDVE) mercapturic acid sulfoxides and showed that modulating CYP3A activity changed both sulfoxide formation and nephrotoxicity: dexamethasone/phenobarbital increased, and the CYP3A inhibitor troleandomycin decreased, urinary sulfoxides and renal injury (PMID: 15377151). This abstract concludes that P4503A-catalyzed sulfoxidation contributes to nephrotoxicity, indicating that at least one cytochrome P450-dependent bioactivation pathway is involved. This creates a clear discrepancy with the earlier study, suggesting that broad P450 inhibition may have shown no consistent protection in one experiment even though CYP3A-dependent metabolism can still contribute under other conditions.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although one abstract reports no consistent protection from a general P450 inhibitor, stronger mechanistic evidence indicates that CYP3A-mediated sulfoxidation of compound A conjugates contributes to rat nephrotoxicity, so cytochrome P450-mediated metabolism cannot be excluded as nonrequired.

source_article.title: RETRACTED: The use of oral nomegestrol acetate/estradiol in rapid and random start preparation of endometrium before office hysteroscopic polypectomies: A multicenter, prospective, randomized controlled trial.
source_article.abstract: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (https://www.elsevier.com/about/policies/article-withdrawal). The Editor-in-Chief has decided to retract this article. A reader alerted the Editors to similarities between this trial and a different trial reported in the journal Climacteric : Etrusco A, D'Amato A, Agrifoglio V, Chiantera V, Russo G, D'Augè TG, Monti M, Riemma G, Laganà AS, Giannini A. Rapid and random-start endometrial preparation before outpatient hysteroscopic polypectomy in patients of perimenopausal age. Climacteric. 2024 Dec;27(6):561-567. doi: 10.1080/13697137.2024.2404574. Epub 2024 Sep 27. PMID: 39329272. The two studies recruited over the same time period in the same centre, and there is evidence of data copying between them. The corresponding author cooperated with the investigation by supplying original data from both trials. Both sets of data showed evidence of fabrication. The EJOG Editors are therefore retracting this paper. The authors disagree with the retraction and dispute the grounds for it. The Editor of Climacteric has been informed.

matched_article.title: Uterine wound healing after caesarean section: A systematic review.
matched_article.abstract: The rate of caesarean section (CS) is increasing worldwide. Defects in uterine healing have a major gynaecological and obstetric impact (uterine rupture, caesarean scar defect, caesarean scar pregnancy, placenta accreta spectrum). The complex process of cellular uterine healing after surgery, and specifically after CS, remains poorly understood in contrast to skin wound healing. This literature review on uterine wound healing was mainly based on histological observations, particularly after CS. The primary objective of the review was to examine the effects of CS on uterine tissue at the cellular level, based on histological observations. The secondary objectives were to describe the biomechanical characteristics and the therapies used to improve scar tissue after CS. This review was performed using PRISMA criteria, and PubMed was the data source. The study included all clinical and animal model studies with CS and histological analysis of the uterine scar area (macroscopic, microscopic, immunohistochemical and biomechanical). Twenty studies were included: 10 human and 10 animal models. In total, 533 female humans and 511 female animals were included. Review articles, meeting abstracts, case series, case reports, and abstracts without access to full-text were excluded. The search was limited to studies published in English. No correlation was found between cutaneous and uterine healing. The histology of uterine scars is characterized by disorganized smooth muscle, fibrosis with collagen fibres and fewer endometrial glands. As for skin healing, the initial inflammation phase and mediation of some growth factors (particularly connective tissue growth factor, vascular endothelial growth factor, platelet-derived growth factor, tumour necrosis factor α and tumour necrosis factor β) seem to be essential. This initial phase has an impact on the subsequent phases of proliferation and maturation. Collagen appears to play a key role in the initial granulation tissue to replace the loss of substance. Subsequent maturation of the scar tissue is essential, with a decrease in collagen and smooth muscle restoration. Unlike skin, the glandular structure of uterine tissue could be responsible for the relatively high incidence of healing defects. Uterine scar defects after CS are characterized by an atrophic disorganized endometrium with atypia and a fibroblastic highly collagenic stromal reaction. Concerning immunohistochemistry, one study found a decrease in tumour necrosis factor β in uterine scar defects. No correlation was found between biomechanical characteristics (particularly uterine strength) and the presence of a collagenous scar after CS. Based on the findings of this review, an illustration of current understanding about uterine healing is provided. There is currently no validated prevention of caesarean scar defects. Various treatments to improve uterine healing after CS have been tested, and appeared to have good efficacy in animal studies: alpha lipoic acid, growth factors, collagen scaffolds and mesenchymal stem cells. Further prospective studies are needed.

statements.text: After caesarean section, uterine scar healing is characterized by disorganized smooth muscle, fibrosis with collagen deposition, and fewer endometrial glands compared with normal uterine tissue.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies support that cesarean scar tissue is more fibrotic than normal uterine tissue, with significantly increased collagen content or deposition reported in human scar samples and scarred lower uterine segments (PMID: 37955528, PMID: 16458657, PMID: 30340551). Human histology also shows reduced muscle fiber density and ultrastructural destruction of the myometrium in scarred areas, which is consistent with abnormal/disorganized smooth muscle architecture rather than normal myometrial organization (PMID: 30340551, PMID: 37955528). An animal uterine scar model similarly describes collagen deposition, thin myometrium, and endometrial injury after full-thickness uterine injury, supporting the broader pattern of fibrotic scar healing with muscular loss (PMID: 33762002). However, direct abstract-level evidence for fewer endometrial glands specifically is limited here; the available studies more clearly support fibrosis/collagen accumulation and myometrial disruption than gland counts.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The evidence consistently shows cesarean scar healing involves increased collagen/fibrosis and abnormal or reduced myometrial muscle structure, but direct abstract-level evidence for fewer endometrial glands is weaker and less explicitly documented in the provided studies.

statements.text: There is no clear association between cutaneous wound healing and uterine wound healing after caesarean section.
statements.support_summary: One prospective cohort study found no meaningful relationship between cutaneous cesarean scar appearance and uterine healing as measured by residual myometrial thickness ratio. In 247 women, Manchester scar scale scores were essentially uncorrelated with residual myometrial thickness ratio (rho = -0.01, p = 0.8), and the authors concluded that scar appearance does not reflect myometrial healing after cesarean section, recommending separate sonographic uterine assessment (PMID: 33415438). No clear discrepancy is apparent between the abstract and its support classification.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, because the available evidence directly addresses the question and reports no association between cutaneous scar appearance and uterine wound healing after cesarean section. However, support is limited by reliance on a single study, so certainty remains moderate rather than strong.

statements.text: No validated preventive strategy for caesarean scar defects after caesarean section has been established, although several interventions have shown benefit in animal studies.
statements.support_summary: The supporting items mainly indicate that preventive approaches for cesarean scar defects remain unestablished or insufficiently validated, while mechanistic hypotheses and exploratory work continue. A review on niche development emphasizes suspected contributors such as low cervical incision, suturing technique, adhesions, and impaired healing, but frames these as hypotheses and a need for future preventive strategies rather than validated solutions (PMID: 26409016). A review of scar integrity after cesarean likewise notes that no prospectively validated, clinically useful model exists for estimating scar-related risk, indirectly supporting the broader idea that validated strategies are lacking, though it addresses prediction more than prevention (PMID: 23103207). One animal study found that human amniotic epithelial cell transplantation improved uterine scar morphology and pregnancy outcomes in a rat model, which supports the claim's statement that some interventions show benefit in animals, although this was a therapeutic scar-repair model rather than a validated preventive human strategy (PMID: 33762002).
statements.contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence includes human interventional studies suggesting that preventive or healing-enhancing strategies may already exist. A systematic review concluded that double-layer continuous hysterotomy closure is superior to single-layer closure and described this as a suitable option to prevent cesarean scar defect formation (PMID: 36724750). A randomized prospective trial reported that full-thickness suturing significantly reduced incomplete uterine incision healing compared with excluding the endometrial layer (PMID: 16023780), and a triple-blind placebo-controlled randomized trial found alpha lipoic acid reduced niche formation after primary cesarean section (PMID: 30564928). However, there is a discrepancy between showing benefit in trials and proving that a strategy is broadly validated and established in practice, so these studies contradict the claim more on efficacy than on universal validation/establishment.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is somewhat more contradicted than supported. Although the supporting evidence reasonably backs the idea that validated preventive strategies are still uncertain and animal studies show benefit, multiple human studies and a systematic review report preventive effects of specific closure techniques or adjunctive therapy, weakening the claim's absolute wording that no such strategy has been established.

source_article.title: RETRACTED: Thermal Analysis of a Metal-Organic Framework ZnxCo1-X-ZIF-8 for Recent Applications.
source_article.abstract: Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) are interesting materials for use in several aspects: energy storage material, gas sensing, and photocatalysis. The thermal stability and pyrolysis process are crucial in determining the active phase of the material. A deep understanding of the pyrolysis mechanism is in demand. Therefore, the thermodynamics and combustion process with different heating rates was examined, and the kinetic parameters were computed employing thermogravimetric tests. Based on the TG analysis of combustion, pyrolysis moves to the high-temperature region with an increase in heating rate. The decomposition process can be separated into the dehydration (300-503 K) and the pyrolysis reaction (703-1100 K). Three points of the decomposition process are performed by dynamical analysis owing to shifts of slopes, but the combustion process has only one stage. The Zeolitic imidazolate framework's structure properties were examined using TDDFT-DFT/DMOl3 simulation techniques. Dynamical parameters, for instance, the possible mechanism, the pre-exponential factor, and the apparent activation energy are obtained through comparison using the Kissinger formula. The thermodynamics analysis of the Zn1-xCox-ZIF-8 materials is an effective way to explore the temperature influence on the process of pyrolysis, which can benefit several environment purifications, photocatalyst, and recent applications.

statements.text: Thermal analysis of a zinc-cobalt zeolitic imidazolate framework showed that increasing the heating rate shifted pyrolysis to higher temperatures.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Decomposition of the zinc-cobalt zeolitic imidazolate framework occurred in distinct dehydration and pyrolysis phases, while combustion occurred in a single stage.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is not directly about zinc-cobalt zeolitic imidazolate frameworks, but about thermal oxidative decomposition of oil-plant residues, where combustion proceeded in three stages: dehydration, volatile release/combustion, and char oxidation (PMID: 28651137). This differs from the claim's assertion that combustion occurred in a single stage, but the material system is unrelated, so its relevance is limited. Thus, this is better viewed as weak, indirect contradiction rather than strong direct refutation.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear and insufficient. There is no direct supporting evidence here, and the single contradicting study concerns a different class of materials, so it provides only weak indirect contradiction to the specific claim about zinc-cobalt zeolitic imidazolate framework decomposition.

statements.text: Thermodynamic and kinetic analysis indicated that the thermal behavior of the zinc-cobalt zeolitic imidazolate framework can be used to understand temperature effects on pyrolysis and related applications.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Recovery of Carbon Fibre from Waste Prepreg via Microwave Pyrolysis.
matched_article.abstract: Management of waste from carbon fibre composites has become a significant societal issue as the application of composite grows across many industries. In this study, carbon fibres (CF) were successfully recovered from cured carbon fibre/epoxy (CF/EP) prepreg under microwave pyrolysis at 450, 550 and 650 °C followed by oxidation of any residual char. The recovered fibres were investigated for their tensile properties, surface morphologies and the elements/functional groups presented on the surface. The chemical compositions of gaseous and oil pyrolysis products were also analysed. The microwave pyrolysis effectively pyrolyzed the epoxy (EP) resin. Char residue remained on the fibre surface and the amount of char reduced as the pyrolysis temperature increased. Compared to virgin fibres, the recovered fibre suffered from a strength reduction by less than 20%, and this reduction could be mitigated by reducing the pyrolysis temperature. The surface of recovered fibre remained clean and smooth, while the profile of elements and functional groups at the surface were similar to those of virgin fibres. The main gaseous products were CO, H2, CO2 and CH4, whilst the liquid product stream included phenolic and aromatic compounds.

statements.text: Microwave pyrolysis can recover carbon fibres from waste cured carbon fibre/epoxy prepreg while largely preserving fibre surface cleanliness and chemical surface characteristics after removal of residual char.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One abstract reports that, compared with two-step pyrolysis, microwave-assisted thermolysis caused significant surface changes in recovered carbon fibers, reduced tensile strength by up to 60%, and altered graphitic structure/crystallinity, which argues against preservation of original surface and structural characteristics (PMID: 37086657). However, this study evaluates microwave-assisted thermolysis rather than explicitly the specific microwave pyrolysis process and post-char-removal surface cleanliness described in the claim, so the contradiction is relevant but not perfectly matched.
statements.conclusion: The statement is somewhat more contradicted than supported because the only cited evidence reports substantial surface and structural deterioration after microwave-based recovery (PMID: 37086657). Still, the evidence is limited to a single study and the process studied is not an exact match to the claim, so confidence is only moderate.

statements.text: Recovered carbon fibres from waste cured carbon fibre/epoxy prepreg retain most of their tensile strength relative to virgin carbon fibres, and lower pyrolysis temperatures better preserve fibre strength.
statements.support_summary: Multiple experimental studies report that recovered carbon fibres can retain a large fraction of virgin-fibre tensile performance: ~96.2% after pyrolysis/oxidation under optimal conditions (PMID: 37778068), ~99.4% after microwave pyrolysis plus oxidation at 500 °C (PMID: 35559185), ~90% after super-heated steam treatment (PMID: 28506669), and in one supercritical-water process the reclaimed fibres reportedly retained original tensile strength (PMID: 22629638). Additional studies describe pyrolysis-based recovery with <5% loss of original mechanical properties or little visible fibre degradation, though some of these are less direct about single-fibre tensile strength and/or compare composites rather than fibres themselves (PMID: 37086657; PMID: 38255591). One study on reused recovered fabrics found retention of tensile modulus rather than tensile strength, so it is only indirect support for the specific claim (PMID: 34641223). Across this group, the clearest recurring theme is that carefully optimized thermal recovery followed by mild oxidation/cleaning can preserve most fibre strength, but direct abstract-level evidence that lower pyrolysis temperatures are superior is limited and often only implied rather than explicitly compared.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is weaker and more indirect overall. One experimental study found that composites made from microwave-recovered carbon fibre had lower tensile, bending, and impact strength than composites made from original material, including a 15.94% tensile-strength drop, but this reflects composite-level performance after reuse rather than direct single-fibre tensile retention (PMID: 38794577). A review states broadly that recycled carbon fibre properties are expected to be worse than virgin fibre, without presenting primary comparative data in the abstract (PMID: 35888458). Another article emphasizes that oxidative thermal treatment used to remove char can damage fibre tensile strength, highlighting a real mechanism of degradation, but it does not quantify losses or directly compare lower versus higher pyrolysis temperatures in the abstract (PMID: 30761950).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Several primary studies directly report high retention of fibre tensile properties after recycling, often around 90-99% of virgin values, whereas the contradicting evidence is mostly indirect, review-based, or focused on composite-level performance; however, the specific claim that lower pyrolysis temperatures better preserve strength is only modestly supported from these abstracts.

statements.text: Microwave pyrolysis of cured carbon fibre/epoxy prepreg effectively breaks down epoxy resin and produces gaseous products dominated by carbon monoxide, hydrogen, carbon dioxide, and methane, with liquid products containing phenolic and aromatic compounds.
