source_article.title: Nucleus Pulposus Cells Induce M2 Polarization of RAW264.7 via CX3CL1/CX3CR1 Pathway and M2 Macrophages Promote Proliferation and Anabolism of Nucleus Pulposus Cells.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: The mechanisms underlying M2 macrophage polarization induced by nucleus pulposus (NP) cells are unclear. The effects that M2-polarized macrophages have on NP cells are also controversial. METHODS: Transcriptome sequencing was performed to detect the gene change profiles between NP cells from ruptured intervertebral disc (IVD) and normal IVD. The main difference on biological activities between the two cell groups were analyzed by GO analysis and KEGG analysis. Virus transduction, flow cytometry, immunofluorescence, RT-PCR, western blot, CCK-8, TUNEL staining, and AO/EB staining were performed to explore the interactions between NP cells and RAW264.7 macrophages. Statistics were performed using SPSS26. RESULTS: 801 upregulated and 276 downregulated genes were identified in NP cells from ruptured IVD in mouse models. According to GO and KEGG analysis, we found that the differentially expressed genes (DEG) were dominantly enriched in inflammatory response, extracellular matrix degradation, blood vessel morphogenesis, immune effector process, ossification, chemokine signaling pathway, macrophage activation, etc. CX3CL1 was one of the top 20% DEG, and we confirmed that both NP tissue and cells expressed remarkably higher level of CX3CL1 in mouse models (p < 0.001∗). Besides, we further revealed that both the recombinant CX3CL1 and NP cells remarkably induced M2 polarization of RAW264.7 (p < 0.001∗), respectively, while this effect was significantly reversed by si-CX3CL1 or JMS-17-2 (p < 0.001∗). Furthermore, we found that M2 macrophages significantly decreased the apoptosis rate (p < 0.001∗) and the catabolic gene levels (p < 0.001∗) of NP cells, while increased the viability, proliferation as well as the anabolic gene levels of NP cells (p < 0.01∗). CONCLUSIONS: Via regulating CX3CL1/CX3CR1 pathway, NP cells can induce the M2 macrophage polarization. M2 polarized macrophages can further promote NP cell viability, proliferation, and anabolism, while inhibit NP cell apoptosis and catabolism.

statements.text: Nucleus pulposus cells can induce M2 macrophage polarization through the CX3CL1/CX3CR1 signaling pathway.
statements.support_summary: One study directly supports the claim in the intervertebral disc context: human nucleus pulposus cell-derived CX3CL1 was reported to bind CX3CR1 and promote M2 macrophage polarization via JAK2/STAT3 signaling, with associated anti-inflammatory effects and reduced apoptosis of nucleus pulposus cells in IDD models and tissues (PMID: 37324510). The other listed supportive item is only a retraction notice and provides no usable abstract-level experimental evidence, so it should not be counted as substantive support despite its title (PMID: 38298230). Overall, the main support comes from a single mechanistically aligned study in the relevant cell/tissue setting.
statements.contradict_summary: The contradicting study reports that in RAW264.7 macrophages under hypoxia/reoxygenation injury, CX3CL1/CX3CR1 signaling was associated with increased M1 markers and decreased M2 markers, and CX3CR1 knockdown attenuated these pro-inflammatory changes (PMID: 34786544). However, this study was performed in a renal ischemia/reperfusion-related model rather than nucleus pulposus cells or intervertebral disc degeneration, so it challenges the pathway's general polarization direction but is only indirectly relevant to the specific claim. Thus, it represents contextual contradiction rather than direct refutation in the same biological system.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is because there is one directly relevant mechanistic study in nucleus pulposus cells supporting CX3CL1/CX3CR1-mediated M2 polarization, whereas the opposing evidence comes from a different injury model and tissue context, and the second supportive citation is retracted and should not materially strengthen the case.

statements.text: M2-polarized macrophages can increase nucleus pulposus cell viability and proliferation and enhance nucleus pulposus cell anabolism.
statements.support_summary: Most supporting items suggest that M2-polarized macrophages have protective effects on nucleus pulposus cells, mainly by reducing inflammation and apoptosis rather than directly proving proliferation and anabolism. PMID: 37324510 reports that the CX3CL1/CX3CR1 axis promotes M2 polarization and reduces apoptosis of human nucleus pulposus cells, implying improved viability, while PMID: 37802465 shows CD206+ M2-like macrophage accumulation is associated with reduced apoptosis and protection against experimental disc degeneration. PMID: 37122738, a review, states that M2 macrophages are involved in anti-inflammatory responses and extracellular matrix remodeling/repair in the nucleus pulposus, which is broadly consistent with enhanced anabolic repair. PMID: 36059551 appears highly relevant from its title, indicating macrophage polarization modulates nucleus pulposus cell proliferation and extracellular matrix metabolism, but the provided abstract is truncated, so its direction for M2 specifically cannot be fully verified. A major discrepancy is PMID: 38298230, which is a retraction notice rather than usable supporting evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence indicates that not all macrophage-related signals are beneficial for nucleus pulposus cells, and some may instead drive degeneration or matrix imbalance. PMID: 34168643 specifically implicates M2a macrophage-secreted CHI3L1 in promoting extracellular matrix metabolic imbalances, which directly conflicts with the claim that M2 macrophages enhance nucleus pulposus anabolism. PMID: 37638033 shows that immune cells including macrophages and T cells promote ossification and degeneration in degenerative nucleus pulposus, but this study does not distinguish M2-polarized macrophages, so it is only an indirect contradiction to the specific claim. Overall, the recurring theme on this side is that macrophage infiltration/signaling can contribute to degenerative remodeling rather than repair.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly to moderately supported overall, but the evidence is mixed and not definitive. Supporting studies more consistently show reduced apoptosis and protective/repair-associated effects of M2 macrophages, whereas direct evidence for increased proliferation and anabolism is limited, and one specific study suggests an M2a subtype can worsen matrix metabolism.

statements.text: M2-polarized macrophages can reduce nucleus pulposus cell apoptosis and catabolism.
statements.support_summary: Direct experimental support comes from studies showing that M2-polarized macrophages protect nucleus pulposus cells (NPCs) from degeneration-related injury. CX3CL1/CX3CR1 signaling promoted M2 polarization and reduced human NPC apoptosis, with M2 macrophage-derived CCL17 implicated in this anti-apoptotic effect (PMID: 37324510). In mice and NPC cultures, increasing CD206+ M2-like macrophages decreased apoptosis and inhibited Rspo2-driven extracellular matrix catabolism, supporting both anti-apoptotic and anti-catabolic effects (PMID: 37802465). Additional support is indirect: a review describes M2 macrophages as anti-inflammatory and involved in ECM remodeling/repair rather than matrix degradation (PMID: 37122738), and an in vivo distribution study found M2-dominant macrophage regions associated with lower IL-1β and higher IL-4 but did not directly test NPC apoptosis/catabolism (PMID: 34545043). One listed supportive item is actually a retraction notice rather than usable primary evidence, so it should not be counted as substantive support (PMID: 38298230).
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited but includes one study whose title and partial abstract indicate that M2a macrophage-secreted CHI3L1 promotes extracellular matrix metabolic imbalance, which conflicts with the claim's anti-catabolic component (PMID: 34168643). Another single-cell RNA-seq study found that immune cells including macrophages were increased in degenerative nucleus pulposus and promoted stromal-cell ossification/degeneration via inflammatory factors, but it did not distinguish M2 macrophages specifically, so this is only indirect contradiction to the claim (PMID: 37638033). Overall, the contradiction mainly challenges a uniformly protective role for macrophages in IDD, with more specific opposition to reduced catabolism than to reduced apoptosis.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest abstract-level evidence directly shows M2 macrophages reducing NPC apoptosis and suppressing catabolic changes (PMID: 37324510, PMID: 37802465), whereas the contradictory studies are either indirect or subtype-specific and do not squarely refute the broader claim.

matched_article.title: The Effect of Mesenchymal Stem Cell-Derived Exosomes and miR17-5p Inhibitor on Multicellular Liver Fibrosis Microtissues.
matched_article.abstract: Although several studies have been conducted on modeling human liver disease, it is still challenging to mimic nonalcoholic fatty liver disease in vitro. Here, we aimed to develop a fibrotic liver microtissue composed of hepatocytes, hepatic stellate, and endothelial cells. In addition, the therapeutic effects of umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes (UC-MSC-EXO) and anti-miR17-5p as new antifibrotic drugs were investigated. To create an effective preclinical fibrosis model, multicellular liver microtissues (MLMs) consisting of HepG2, LX2, and HUVECs were cultured and supplemented with a mixture of palmitic acid and oleic acid for 96 hr. Then, MLMs were exposed to UC-MSC-EXO and anti-miR17-5p in different groups. The results of cell viability, reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, liver enzyme levels, inflammation, and histopathology were analyzed to assess the treatment efficacy. Furthermore, the expression of collagen I (COL I) and α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA) as critical matrix components, transforming growth factor beta (TGF-β), and miR-17-5p were measured. Free fatty acid supplementation causes fibrosis in MLMs. Our results demonstrated that UC-MSC-EXO and anti-miR17-5p attenuated TGF-β1, interleukin-1β, and interleukin-6 in all experimental groups. According to the suppression of the TGF-β1 pathway, LX2 activation was inhibited, reducing extracellular matrix proteins, including COL I and α-SMA. Also, miR-17-5p expression was elevated in fibrosis conditions. Furthermore, we showed that our treatments decreased alanine aminotransferase and aspartate aminotransferase, and increased albumin levels in the culture supernatant. We also found that both MSC-EXO and MSC-EXO + anti-miR17-5p treatments could reduce ROS production. Our findings indicated that anti-miR17-5p and MSC-EXO might be promising therapeutic options for treating liver fibrosis. Furthermore, EXO + anti-miR had the best effects on boosting the fibrotic markers. Therefore, we propose this novel MLM model to understand fibrosis mechanisms better and develop new drugs.

statements.text: In a multicellular human liver fibrosis microtissue model exposed to free fatty acids, umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes and anti-miR17-5p reduced fibrotic and inflammatory activity compared with untreated fibrotic microtissues.

statements.text: In a multicellular human liver fibrosis microtissue model exposed to free fatty acids, umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes and anti-miR17-5p suppressed hepatic stellate cell activation and decreased extracellular matrix protein accumulation compared with untreated fibrotic microtissues.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study reports that human umbilical cord MSC-derived exosomes attenuated thioacetamide-induced liver fibrosis in mice, with effects similar to the parent MSCs, and that these exosomes inhibited hepatic stellate cell activation while reducing fibrotic injury via Hedgehog/SMO pathway suppression (PMID: 39138757). This aligns with the claim’s general direction that UC-MSC-derived exosomes can suppress stellate-cell-driven fibrogenesis. However, the abstract does not address the specific multicellular human liver fibrosis microtissue model, free-fatty-acid exposure, anti-miR17-5p, or direct extracellular matrix readouts claimed, so support is indirect rather than direct.
statements.contradict_summary: The cited contradicting study shows that serum-derived exosomes enriched in miR-574-5p were associated with liver fibrosis and promoted hepatic stellate cell activation, including increased α-SMA and COL1A1 expression in LX-2 cells (PMID: 34843038). This indicates that some exosome populations can be pro-fibrotic rather than anti-fibrotic. However, this is not a close contradiction to the claim because it involves circulating serum exosomes carrying a different miRNA, not umbilical cord MSC-derived exosomes or anti-miR17-5p; thus the contradiction grouping appears somewhat discrepant with the specific claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only weakly and indirectly. The main supporting evidence is a mechanistically relevant UC-MSC-exosome fibrosis study, whereas the opposing evidence concerns a different exosome source and miRNA and therefore does not directly refute the claimed microtissue-model findings.

statements.text: In a multicellular human liver fibrosis microtissue model exposed to free fatty acids, the combination of umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes and anti-miR17-5p produced the strongest antifibrotic effect compared with exosomes alone or anti-miR17-5p alone.

source_article.title: Respiratory Care of Big Data Communication to Prevent Respiratory Tract Infection Nursing Analysis of Patients with Heart Failure.
source_article.abstract: Heart failure is the final stage of the development of heart disease, with a high mortality and disability rate. It poses a serious threat to human health and brings tremendous pressure to human society. Preventing respiratory infections in patients with heart failure is also the first priority of care. This article is aimed at studying the nursing analysis of respiratory tract care based on big data exchanges to prevent respiratory tract infections in patients with heart failure. This article uses benchmark and sample collection. Studies have shown that for Pseudomonas aeruginosa, its resistance to ampicillin, amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, cefazolin, cefuroxime, ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, and cefoxitin has reached more than 80%. It is also suitable for piperacillin, ticarcillin/clavulanic acid, piperacillin/tazobactam, cefepime, aztreonam, gentamicin, tobramycin, ciprofloxacin, and levofloxacin. The resistance rate of stars is within 10%-30%. These antibiotics are effective and can be used for clinical treatment. The drug resistance rates of ceftazidime, imipenem, meropenem, and amikacin were all lower than 10%, and the drug resistance rates of ceftazidime and imipenem were much lower than those reported in the 2016 literature. These antibiotics have become the most effective drugs for the treatment of Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections. Basically, good communication of respiratory care data is realized, thereby preventing respiratory care analysis of patients with heart failure.

statements.text: Big data-based respiratory care communication for patients with heart failure was associated with prevention of respiratory tract infection in patients with heart failure.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.

statements.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa showed high resistance to several commonly used beta-lactam antibiotics, indicating that these agents were poor treatment options for Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection.
statements.support_summary: Many supporting studies report substantial resistance of Pseudomonas aeruginosa to multiple beta-lactams, especially ceftazidime, cefepime, piperacillin/tazobactam, aztreonam, and sometimes carbapenems, often in ICU, burn, or nosocomial settings; examples include very high or rising resistance in pediatric ICU, burn wards, and ceftazidime-resistant hospital isolates (PMID: 23158814, 27647068, 24684983, 26167816). Several abstracts describe multidrug-resistant populations with low susceptibility to key antipseudomonal beta-lactams, such as only 41-55% susceptibility to cefepime, ceftazidime, and piperacillin/tazobactam in nosocomial isolates (PMID: 17001842), resistance rates exceeding 25% before treatment with further MIC increases during therapy (PMID: 20926262), and 84.6% resistance to cefepime/ceftazidime in burn isolates (PMID: 37699096). Mechanistic or selected-resistant cohorts also support the claim, including GES- or ESBL-associated beta-lactam resistance and strains resistant to many antipseudomonal drugs (PMID: 17293906, 16775953, 18827351). However, some items in the support set are weaker or context-limited because they involve animals, mixed-species summaries, incomplete abstracts, or report only moderate resistance or preserved activity for some beta-lactams rather than uniformly poor options (e.g., PMID: 24629901, 9572029, 35697891, 24025503). Overall, the dominant theme is that in many high-risk hospital settings P. aeruginosa frequently shows clinically important resistance to several commonly used beta-lactams, narrowing treatment options.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting studies often report that many P. aeruginosa isolates remained susceptible to at least some commonly used beta-lactams, particularly piperacillin/tazobactam, ceftazidime, cefepime, imipenem, or meropenem, rather than showing uniformly high resistance; for example, >90% susceptibility to piperacillin-tazobactam and 80-90% susceptibility to cefepime, ceftazidime, imipenem, and meropenem were reported in US surveillance (PMID: 12709340). Other studies found most isolates sensitive to tested beta-lactams in respiratory or nosocomial infections, including 96.5-97.1% gram-negative susceptibility to piperacillin or piperacillin/tazobactam (PMID: 11589485), 75-83% susceptibility among small nosocomial P. aeruginosa series (PMID: 17446961), and resistance rates below 50% with >60% sensitivity to ceftazidime, cefepime, meropenem, and imipenem in a nursing hospital cohort (PMID: 32490228). Some reports specifically identify ceftazidime and imipenem as among the most active beta-lactams with resistance around 15-25%, indicating these were not broadly poor treatment choices in those settings (PMID: 19875241), and older multicenter data also showed around 81-82% susceptibility to imipenem, ceftazidime, or piperacillin/tazobactam (PMID: 8958855). A notable discrepancy is that several contradicting articles evaluate newer beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations rather than the older commonly used agents in the claim, so they only partially address the statement (PMID: 40089153, 36738849). Overall, the contradicting evidence emphasizes strong geographic and temporal variability, with preserved activity of some beta-lactams in several surveillance datasets.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, because numerous studies—especially in ICU, burn, and resistant-hospital cohorts—show substantial or rising resistance of P. aeruginosa to several commonly used beta-lactam antibiotics. However, the evidence is not uniform across settings, and several surveillance studies report good susceptibility to some beta-lactams, indicating that the claim is context-dependent rather than universally true.

statements.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa remained more susceptible to piperacillin/tazobactam, cefepime, aztreonam, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, ceftazidime, imipenem, meropenem, and amikacin, making these agents more effective treatment options for Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection.
statements.support_summary: Multiple surveillance and susceptibility studies report that Pseudomonas aeruginosa often retained comparatively good in vitro susceptibility to key antipseudomonal agents, especially piperacillin/tazobactam, cefepime, ceftazidime, carbapenems, amikacin and other aminoglycosides, with several large datasets showing ~80–90% susceptibility for some drugs and lower resistance to amikacin and piperacillin/tazobactam (PMID: 12106958, PMID: 12709340, PMID: 10103217, PMID: 8958855). Additional hospital-based studies found moderate-to-high susceptibility to cefepime, ceftazidime, meropenem/imipenem, and amikacin, and one recent study reported relatively higher sensitivity to ceftazidime/cefepime and 100% sensitivity to amikacin despite notable carbapenem resistance (PMID: 32490228, PMID: 29920041, PMID: 40855472). Some narrower or non-human datasets also support activity of imipenem/amikacin or broad susceptibility across several listed agents, though these are less directly applicable to human clinical treatment (PMID: 7498617, PMID: 1365010, PMID: 24629901). There is also mechanistic/time-kill evidence that imipenem, cefepime, ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, amikacin, and ciprofloxacin can retain bactericidal or synergistic activity, especially in combinations (PMID: 9296099). Overall, the support mostly concerns in vitro susceptibility rather than direct proof that these drugs are clinically the most effective treatment options.
statements.contradict_summary: A substantial body of evidence shows that susceptibility is highly context-dependent and can be poor in high-risk settings or resistant subpopulations: ICU, burn, bacteremia, cystic fibrosis, ESBL-producing, carbapenem-resistant, and multidrug-resistant P. aeruginosa often had marked resistance to cefepime, ceftazidime, aztreonam, piperacillin/tazobactam, carbapenems, fluoroquinolones, and sometimes aminoglycosides (PMID: 20926262, PMID: 16775953, PMID: 17001842, PMID: 26167816, PMID: 38676583). Several longitudinal studies specifically reported declining susceptibility over time or increasing resistance to key agents such as meropenem, cefepime, ceftazidime, piperacillin/tazobactam, amikacin, and imipenem, especially in ICU or burn-unit isolates (PMID: 12820914, PMID: 24684983, PMID: 23116904, PMID: 15737511, PMID: 30697478). Clinical-source studies also found nontrivial resistance rates in bacteremia and severe burns, undermining a broad claim that these agents generally 'remained more susceptible' across settings (PMID: 19223699, PMID: 27647068). Some contradict items appear misclassified or only partially contradictory because they still report relatively good susceptibility for certain drugs, such as low resistance to piperacillin/tazobactam, amikacin, or ceftazidime in some cohorts (PMID: 12654234, PMID: 35697891, PMID: 18395369). Overall, the contradicting evidence emphasizes heterogeneity and resistance emergence rather than uniformly favorable susceptibility.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only partially supported and overall too broad; the evidence is mixed but leans toward contradiction because although many studies show reasonable susceptibility of P. aeruginosa to several listed antipseudomonal agents, numerous studies in resistant or high-risk clinical settings document substantial and rising resistance. Thus, these drugs can be effective options in some settings, but the claim overgeneralizes by implying consistently higher susceptibility across contexts.

matched_article.title: Neutrophil Extracellular Traps Facilitate A549 Cell Invasion and Migration in a Macrophage-Maintained Inflammatory Microenvironment.
matched_article.abstract: The biological functions of neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) in tumorigenesis have drawn an increasing amount of attention. This study explored the relationship between NETs and the inflammatory microenvironment in lung cancer cell invasion and metastasis. NETs were quantified using myeloperoxidase (MPO-DNA) and immunofluorescence staining. Cytokine levels were measured using ELISA kits. THP-1 and A549 cells were used for in vitro experiments. Transwell and Matrigel assays were used to assess the invasion and migration abilities of the cells. Neutrophil infiltration and NET formation were observed in the lung cancer tissues. Compared with healthy controls, the level of MPO-DNA complexes in lung cancer patients increased remarkably and was positively correlated with peripheral blood neutrophil counts, smoking status, and poor prognosis. Increased circulating NET levels were also positively correlated with the levels of inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1β, IL-6, IL-18, and TNF-α. Neutrophils isolated from patients with lung cancer are more prone to NET release. NETs can promote the invasion and migration ability of THP-1 and A549 cell in coculture systems, while pretreatment with NET inhibitors can effectively reduce NET-induced invasion and metastasis. The ability of NETs to promote invasion and metastasis is partly dependent on macrophages. Taken together, our study demonstrated that NETs facilitate A549 cell invasion and migration in a macrophage-maintained inflammatory microenvironment.

statements.text: In lung cancer, neutrophil extracellular traps are associated with a pro-inflammatory microenvironment and poor prognosis.
statements.support_summary: Supportive evidence is mostly indirect but converges on NETs as markers/effectors of a tumor-promoting inflammatory milieu and adverse outcomes. In lung cancer patients, circulating NETs or NETosis markers were elevated, and lung-cancer-related models linked NET formation to metastasis or dissemination, including studies showing elevated NETs in advanced lung cancer and reduced metastasis after NET inhibition (PMID: 31343990), C5a-driven PMN-MDSC NETosis correlating with higher MPO-DNA complexes in lung cancer patients and promoting metastatic burden (PMID: 34971753), and pan-cancer analysis associating higher NET scores with inflammatory responses, EMT, poor immunotherapy response, and shorter survival in some cancers including lung cancer contexts (PMID: 35432310). Several additional mechanistic or review papers, though not specific to primary lung cancer, consistently describe NETs as induced by inflammatory cues and as promoting metastasis, immune remodeling, EMT, chemoresistance, and tumor-friendly microenvironments in the lung or across cancers (PMID: 34885240, PMID: 36859358, PMID: 36942262, PMID: 37037615). However, many supporting items concern breast/liver/esophageal or metastatic-to-lung models rather than lung cancer itself, so direct evidence for prognosis in lung cancer is limited.
statements.contradict_summary: There is little direct contradicting evidence. The lone item reports that in breast cancer pulmonary metastasis, tumor-released autophagosomes induce PD-L1-decorated NETs that suppress T-cell function and create an immunosuppressive premetastatic niche (PMID: 38926151), which could be seen as differing from a purely pro-inflammatory framing. However, this study is not in lung cancer, and immunosuppression does not necessarily exclude concurrent inflammatory signaling, so it is better viewed as a nuance/discrepancy than a strong contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence directly tied to lung cancer indicates elevated NETosis and links NETs to metastatic progression and inflammatory/EMT-related programs, but much of the literature is indirect or from other tumor types, so support for poor prognosis specifically in lung cancer is moderate rather than definitive.

statements.text: Neutrophil extracellular traps promote lung cancer cell invasion and migration in vitro.
statements.support_summary: Direct in vitro support comes from a recent study in EGFR-driven lung cancer cells showing that NET-coated surfaces increased adhesion, prolonged NET exposure activated Notch1-mediated EMT, and NET-treated cells had significantly increased migration, consistent with enhanced invasive behavior (PMID: 39430758). A computational/modeling study tied to Boyden invasion assay experiments in lung cancer also reports that tumor-associated neutrophils and NET-associated mechanisms reproduced major observations of NET-mediated cancer invasion, although this is less direct than a purely experimental cell study (PMID: 33596197). Additional support is mostly indirect or broader-than-lung-cancer evidence: reviews and non-lung cancer studies repeatedly state that NETs enhance tumor cell migration/invasion, EMT, metastatic seeding, and related prometastatic signaling, including studies in breast, pancreatic, and other cancers (PMID: 27798263, PMID: 31034495, PMID: 34503307, PMID: 36618417). Some items are reviews or animal/metastasis-focused papers rather than direct in vitro lung cancer invasion assays, so their relevance is supportive but limited (e.g., PMID: 33042107, PMID: 36050539, PMID: 36942262). No major discrepancy within the support set overturns the central theme, but several papers are only indirectly applicable to the specific claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence is a direct lung cancer cell study demonstrating NET-induced EMT and increased migration in vitro (PMID: 39430758), with additional lung-related and broader cancer literature consistently aligning with a prometastatic, pro-invasive role for NETs, though much of that evidence is indirect for the exact claim.

statements.text: The pro-invasive and pro-migratory effects of neutrophil extracellular traps on lung cancer cells are partly dependent on macrophages and can be reduced by neutrophil extracellular trap inhibition.
statements.support_summary: One preclinical study reports that NETs promote cancer metastasis and that targeting the NET-sensing receptor CCDC25 with an oncolytic bacteria-delivered shCCDC25 reduced NET formation, blocked prometastatic signaling, and inhibited lung metastasis in B16F10 and 4T1 models (PMID: 38369519). This supports the narrower part of the claim that NET inhibition can reduce NET-driven prometastatic effects. However, the abstract does not directly assess lung cancer cell invasion/migration assays or show that these effects are partly dependent on macrophages; although macrophage recruitment is mentioned, it is not presented as a mechanistic dependency (PMID: 38369519). Thus, the support is indirect and only partial, with a discrepancy between the claim's macrophage-specific mechanism and the evidence available from the abstract.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly to partially supported overall. The available evidence supports that inhibiting NET-related signaling can reduce metastasis, but it does not directly establish that NET-induced pro-invasive/pro-migratory effects in lung cancer cells are partly macrophage-dependent.

source_article.title: Compensated Fuzzy Neural Network-Based Music Teaching Ability Assessment Model.
source_article.abstract: College is the main place to carry out music teaching, and it is important to assess the music teaching ability in college effectively. Based on this, this paper firstly analyzes the necessity of music teaching ability assessment and briefly summarizes the application of neural network and deep learning technology in music teaching ability assessment and secondly designs an assessment model based on compensated fuzzy neural network algorithm and analyzes the accuracy of the model, finds out the causes of forming abnormal output by analysing the general dimensional conditions of the algorithm of the model, and proposes corresponding correction. Finally, the reliability and feasibility of the music teaching ability assessment model were experimentally verified by combining with teaching practice. The research results confirm the feasibility of the compensated fuzzy neural network algorithm in music teaching ability assessment, which has important reference significance for improving the quality of music teaching in colleges and universities.

statements.text: A compensated fuzzy neural network algorithm was feasible for assessing music teaching ability in college and university settings.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One contradicting item indicates that the paper titled "Compensated Fuzzy Neural Network-Based Music Teaching Ability Assessment Model" was retracted (PMID: 37416550). From the abstract alone, the only firm conclusion is that the original article's findings are no longer considered reliable, which undermines confidence in the claim of feasibility. This is not direct experimental evidence against feasibility, but it is a major reliability concern and creates a clear discrepancy with any implied validity of the source claim.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported because there is no supporting evidence provided, and the only related evidence is a retraction of the directly relevant article (PMID: 37416550). However, the evidence is limited in that the retraction notice does not itself provide substantive data showing the algorithm is infeasible; it mainly shows that the original evidence base is unreliable.

statements.text: A compensated fuzzy neural network-based assessment model could reliably support music teaching ability evaluation in teaching practice.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only available evidence is a retraction notice for the article titled "Compensated Fuzzy Neural Network-Based Music Teaching Ability Assessment Model," which withdraws the underlying study rather than presenting new empirical data against the model itself (PMID: 37416550). This weakens confidence in the claim because the main directly relevant publication has been judged unreliable, but it is a credibility problem rather than a substantive experimental refutation. No abstract-level details are available about why the model would fail in teaching practice.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the claim is more contradicted than supported because there is no supporting evidence and the directly relevant study has been retracted (PMID: 37416550). However, the evidence is limited, since a retraction undermines reliability of prior support rather than providing direct empirical disproof of the model's effectiveness.

statements.text: Using a compensated fuzzy neural network approach may help improve the quality of music teaching in colleges and universities.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study describes a self-organizing fuzzy neural network that learns and reproduces multiple sequential tasks and reports better performance than several comparison methods, including in an application involving piano-related sequence learning (PMID: 32356769). However, the abstract is about technical sequence modeling rather than music education, colleges, universities, or teaching quality, so its relevance to the claim is indirect and limited.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a paper on a compensated fuzzy neural network-based music teaching ability assessment model (PMID: 37416550). The abstract provides no experimental findings beyond the fact of retraction, so it undermines the reliability of that specific prior article rather than directly showing that compensated fuzzy neural networks do not improve music teaching quality. This is therefore best interpreted as weakening direct support rather than providing strong empirical contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear and weak overall. There is only indirect technical support from a fuzzy neural network study not focused on education, while the main directly relevant item is a retraction notice that reduces confidence in prior positive claims without itself proving the approach is ineffective.

matched_article.title: Denoising of 3D Brain MR Images with Parallel Residual Learning of Convolutional Neural Network Using Global and Local Feature Extraction.
matched_article.abstract: Magnetic resonance (MR) images often suffer from random noise pollution during image acquisition and transmission, which impairs disease diagnosis by doctors or automated systems. In recent years, many noise removal algorithms with impressive performances have been proposed. In this work, inspired by the idea of deep learning, we propose a denoising method named 3D-Parallel-RicianNet, which will combine global and local information to remove noise in MR images. Specifically, we introduce a powerful dilated convolution residual (DCR) module to expand the receptive field of the network and to avoid the loss of global features. Then, to extract more local information and reduce the computational complexity, we design the depthwise separable convolution residual (DSCR) module to learn the channel and position information in the image, which not only reduces parameters dramatically but also improves the local denoising performance. In addition, a parallel network is constructed by fusing the features extracted from each DCR module and DSCR module to improve the efficiency and reduce the complexity for training a denoising model. Finally, a reconstruction (REC) module aims to construct the clean image through the obtained noise deviation and the given noisy image. Due to the lack of ground-truth images in the real MR dataset, the performance of the proposed model was tested qualitatively and quantitatively on one simulated T1-weighted MR image dataset and then expanded to four real datasets. The experimental results show that the proposed 3D-Parallel-RicianNet network achieves performance superior to that of several state-of-the-art methods in terms of the peak signal-to-noise ratio, structural similarity index, and entropy metric. In particular, our method demonstrates powerful abilities in both noise suppression and structure preservation.

statements.text: A 3D parallel residual convolutional neural network for brain magnetic resonance image denoising improves noise suppression and preserves anatomical structure better than several state-of-the-art denoising methods.
statements.support_summary: Several related MRI denoising studies support the general claim that deep residual/3D CNN-based methods can outperform comparator denoising approaches in both noise suppression and structure preservation, although they do not test the exact same architecture as the source paper. A multi-channel residual CNN for 3D MR denoising achieved the best PSNR and SSIM versus four state-of-the-art methods across multiple datasets, supporting improved denoising and structural fidelity claims (PMID: 29982919). A 3D residual encoder-decoder WGAN also reported superior performance to several state-of-the-art methods on simulated and real clinical MRI data, with strong noise suppression and structure preservation (PMID: 31085444). Additional indirect support comes from deep learning-based reconstruction showing higher PSNR/SSIM and better qualitative brain MRI image quality than DnCNN and SCNN (PMID: 31484849), and a deep residual multifeature extraction method reporting better denoising than comparison algorithms, especially at high noise levels (PMID: 33204301). A key discrepancy is that these studies involve related but different denoising architectures rather than the exact 3D parallel residual CNN in the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The available evidence consistently shows that related 3D/residual deep-learning MRI denoising models can improve noise suppression and often preserve structural detail better than comparator methods, but direct support is somewhat limited because most studies evaluate architectures similar to, not exactly the same as, the claimed 3D parallel residual CNN.

statements.text: Combining global feature extraction with local feature extraction in a brain magnetic resonance image denoising network improves denoising performance compared with methods lacking this parallel feature-learning strategy.
statements.support_summary: Several MRI denoising studies report better performance when architectures or models combine broader-context/global information with local, multiscale, or hierarchical features, although most provide indirect rather than claim-matched evidence. A direct denoising example is a multifeature deep residual MR denoising method using multiple kernel sizes for fused feature extraction that outperformed comparison algorithms, especially at high noise levels (PMID: 33204301); another MRI denoising model explicitly states that jointly using global image priors and local image features yielded superior quantitative and qualitative results versus state-of-the-art methods (PMID: 24746774). Deep-learning denoising papers also describe gains from capturing long-range plus hierarchical information or global attention, with state-of-the-art denoising performance reported for 3D MRI denoising networks (PMID: 34101607) and a generative MRI denoiser with global feature attention in parallel blocks (PMID: 36474088). More indirect support comes from related MRI/CEST imaging tasks where parallel global/local feature learning improved reconstruction or denoising metrics (PMID: 37332198, PMID: 38039897). One included paper is somewhat mismatched to the claim because it focuses on tumor segmentation rather than denoising, despite mentioning local-global feature connection and a denoising mechanism (PMID: 36190611).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because multiple abstracts describe improved MRI denoising or closely related imaging performance when models combine global/long-range and local/multiscale feature information, and no direct contradictory evidence is present. However, support is moderate rather than definitive because several studies are indirect, non-brain-specific, or focused on related tasks such as reconstruction or segmentation rather than the exact parallel feature-learning denoising network claimed.

statements.text: A convolutional neural network denoising approach for brain magnetic resonance images can achieve superior image quality, including higher similarity to clean images and better entropy-based image quality, than competing methods.
statements.support_summary: Multiple brain/MR denoising studies report that CNN-based methods outperform comparator methods on similarity-to-reference metrics such as PSNR and SSIM, often with better qualitative ratings and preserved structure; for example, dDLR was superior to DnCNN and SCNN in brain MR imaging in both phantom/volunteer and clinical evaluations (PMID: 31484849), and MCDnCNN robustly outperformed four other denoising methods on 3D MR datasets (PMID: 29982919). Additional brain-relevant studies found CNN denoisers produced images closer to noise-free or low-noise references than alternatives, including DWI/DTI applications where 1D-CNN, SDnDTI, and deep-learning denoisers outperformed MP-PCA, BM4D, AONLM, or related methods (PMID: 36108272; PMID: 35240299; PMID: 32147643). Several other MR denoising papers similarly report superiority versus state-of-the-art methods on quantitative and qualitative measures, including diffusion MRI, fMRI, CEST, and artifact/noise reduction settings (PMID: 33251531; PMID: 31012226; PMID: 37332198; PMID: 34101607). However, a notable portion of the support set concerns MRI reconstruction, super-resolution, synthesis, or non-brain/knee applications rather than direct brain MRI denoising, so those items are only indirectly supportive and create scope discrepancies with the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: One contradicting study examined deep-learning denoising for single MR spectroscopy rather than conventional brain MRI images and found that although outputs looked visually improved, denoising was inhomogeneous and produced substantially biased quantitative estimates (PMID: 37332185). The abstract suggests DL denoising may help display quality but not unbiased quantitative evaluation, so it challenges broad claims of superiority. Because this study is on MRS spectra rather than brain MRI image denoising, it is only a partial contradiction to the specific claim.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The evidence base includes numerous denoising studies—several directly in brain MRI or brain diffusion MRI—showing CNN-based approaches achieve better PSNR/SSIM and often better visual quality than comparator methods, while the lone contradictory item addresses MRS and mainly raises concerns about quantitative bias rather than overturning image-quality gains.

source_article.title: Inhibition of microRNA-200a Upregulates the Expression of Striatal Dopamine Receptor D2 to Repress Apoptosis of Striatum via the cAMP/PKA Signaling Pathway in Rats with Parkinson's Disease.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND/AIMS: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative movement disease with a high annual incidence. Accumulating evidence demonstrates that microRNAs play important roles in the pathogenesis of multiple neurological disorders, including PD. This study aims to investigate how microRNA-200a (miR-200a) regulates striatal dopamine receptor D2 (DRD2) to affect apoptosis of striatum in rats with PD and to explore the associated mechanism. METHODS: After successfully establishing a PD model by 6-hydroxydopamine injections, PD rats were mainly treated with miR-200a mimics, inhibitors, Forskolin or a combination of miR-200a inhibitors and Forskolin. High-performance liquid chromatography-electrochemical detection (HPLC-ECD) was employed to detect the levels of dopamine, 3, 4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC) and homovanillic acid (HVA), and chemistry colorimetric methods were applied to detect the levels of malondialdehyde (MDA), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px). A TUNEL assay and immunocytochemical staining were performed to observe apoptosis and tyrosine hydroxylase (TH)-positive cells in the striatum. The expression of miR-200a, DRD2, Bad, Bax, Bcl-2, cAMP and PKA was determined by reverse transcription quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) and western blot assays. RESULTS: In the cellular experiments, after transfection with the inhibitor of miR-200a, decreased levels of Bax, GSH-Px, SOD, dopamine, DOPAC and HVA but increased levels of MDA and Bcl-2 were found along with a reduced apoptosis rate and increased TH-positive cell number. In addition, downregulating miR-200a resulted in lower expression of AKT, cAMP and PKA but higher expression of DRD2 and CREB, indicating that the downregulation of miR-200a increases DRD2 expression, which blocks the cAMP/PKA signaling pathway. CONCLUSION: This study provides evidence that the inhibition of miR-200a can repress apoptosis in the striatum via inhibition of the cAMP/PKA signaling pathway by upregulating DRD2 expression in PD rats.

statements.text: In Parkinson's disease rats, inhibition of microRNA-200a reduced striatal apoptosis and improved striatal dopaminergic cell survival.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In Parkinson's disease rats, inhibition of microRNA-200a increased dopamine receptor D2 expression and suppressed striatal apoptosis through inhibition of the cAMP/PKA signaling pathway.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: High Glucose-Mediated Cytokine Regulation in Gingival Fibroblasts and THP-1 Macrophage: a Possible Mechanism of Severe Periodontitis with Diabetes.
matched_article.abstract: Diabetic patients are susceptible to severe periodontitis, but the precise mechanism is not fully understood. Aim of this study was to explore the biological pathogenesis of severe periodontitis in diabetic patients focusing on the crosstalk of human gingival fibroblasts (HGFs) and macrophages. A total of 70 periodontitis patients with or without diabetes mellitus (DM) were enrolled, and the statistical relationships of diabetic conditions to the periodontal inflammatory parameters were examined by cross-sectional study. In in vitro study, HGFs cell line CRL-2014® (ATCC) and differentiated THP-1 macrophages were cultured with normal glucose (NG: 5.5 mM) or high glucose (HG: 25 mM) condition, and treated with indicated inflammatory factors such as calprotectin (CPT), interleukin (IL)-1β and IL-6. To examine the effects of HG on soluble IL-6 receptor (sIL-6R) production in THP-1 macrophages, the supernatants were collected and the sIL-6R levels were measured by ELISA. To examine the effects of HG on IL-1β or IL-6-induced matrix metalloproteinase (MMPs) production in HGFs, the supernatants were collected. Levels of MMP-1 and tissue inhibitor of MMP-1 (TIMP-1) were measured by ELISA. Finally, after conditioned medium (CM) from THP-1 macrophages cultured with NG or HG conditions was collected, HGFs were treated with the CM. The supernatants were collected 24 hours later and the levels of MMP-1 and TIMP-1 were measured. To examine the specific effects of IL-1β contained in CM on MMP-1 and TIMP-1 production in HGFs, IL-1 receptor antagonist (IL-1ra) was used. There were statistical correlation between IL-1β and sIL-6R levels in gingival crevicular fluid (GCF) and HbA1c in periodontitis patients with DM (IL-1β: P=0.035, sIL-6R: P=0.040). HG and CPT significantly induced sIL-6R production in THP-1 macrophages. HG significantly enhanced IL-1β or IL-6/sIL-6R-induced MMP-1 production in HGFs. The increase of MMP-1 by both IL-1β and IL-6/sIL-6R was significantly inhibited by specific ERK or IκB inhibitors. Corresponding to the regulation of MMP-1 production, HG condition increased the phosphorylation of p44/42 MAPK and IκBα in HGFs treated with IL-1β or IL-6/sIL-6R. Finally, MMP-1 production in HGFs cultured with HG increased significantly by CM from THP-1 macrophages cultured with HG. The induction of MMP-1 by the CM from THP-1 macrophages cultured with HG was significantly inhibited by dose dependent of IL-1ra in HGFs cultured with HG. Diabetic conditions such as HG induce IL-1β and sIL-6R production from macrophages in inflammatory periodontal tissues and may exacerbate the periodontitis synergistically via MMP-1 production from HGFs.

statements.text: In periodontitis patients with diabetes mellitus, worse glycemic control is associated with higher inflammatory cytokine levels in gingival crevicular fluid, including interleukin-1β and soluble interleukin-6 receptor.
statements.support_summary: Direct clinical support comes mainly from a study of 45 patients with type 2 diabetes and chronic periodontitis showing that worse glycemic control (higher HbA1c and random glucose) was independently associated with higher gingival crevicular fluid IL-1β, and patients with HbA1c >8% had significantly higher mean GCF IL-1β than those with better control (PMID: 15515334). Another human study found that as diabetic control worsened, GCF IL-1β showed an increasing trend and periodontal inflammation measures were elevated, though this abstract reports a trend rather than a definitive increase and measured IL-6 in gingival tissue rather than GCF soluble IL-6 receptor (PMID: 10588494). Several mechanistic or indirect studies support the broader concept that hyperglycemia/AGEs amplify periodontal inflammatory signaling, including increased IL-6 or IL-1β under high-glucose conditions in gingival fibroblasts or macrophages (PMID: 29193068, PMID: 39415131, PMID: 39319591), but these do not directly test glycemic control against GCF cytokines in patients. Other cited items are reviews or studies of serum/plasma markers rather than GCF and therefore only indirectly relevant (PMID: 21780542, PMID: 36688675, PMID: 38920850). A notable discrepancy is that none of the provided abstracts directly document soluble IL-6 receptor in GCF, so support is strong for the IL-1β part of the claim but weak for the specific sIL-6R component.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, primarily because one direct clinical study strongly links poorer glycemic control with higher GCF IL-1β in periodontitis patients with diabetes (PMID: 15515334), with limited additional supportive human and mechanistic evidence. However, the evidence provided does not directly establish the soluble IL-6 receptor portion of the claim, so support is incomplete rather than definitive.

statements.text: High glucose conditions in macrophages increase soluble interleukin-6 receptor production, and high glucose conditions in gingival fibroblasts amplify interleukin-1β- and interleukin-6/sIL-6R-induced matrix metalloproteinase-1 production.
statements.support_summary: One study using a transwell coculture of human gingival fibroblasts and U937 macrophages found that coculture increased macrophage MMP-1 expression and that high glucose further enhanced this effect; fibroblast-derived IL-6 was essential for the augmentation, and high glucose, IL-6, and LPS acted synergistically on MMP-1 expression (PMID: 19307187). This provides partial support for the broader idea that high glucose amplifies IL-6-related MMP-1 responses in fibroblast-macrophage interactions relevant to periodontal tissue. However, the abstract does not report that high glucose increases soluble IL-6 receptor production in macrophages, nor does it show that high glucose in gingival fibroblasts specifically amplifies IL-1β- or IL-6/sIL-6R-induced MMP-1 production, so this is only indirect/partial support.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly to partially supported overall. The sole cited abstract supports high-glucose enhancement of IL-6-dependent MMP-1 signaling in fibroblast-macrophage coculture, but it does not directly address macrophage sIL-6R production or fibroblast amplification of IL-1β- and IL-6/sIL-6R-induced MMP-1, which are central parts of the statement.

statements.text: Macrophage-conditioned medium generated under high glucose conditions increases matrix metalloproteinase-1 production in gingival fibroblasts through an interleukin-1β-dependent mechanism, suggesting a pathway by which diabetes may worsen periodontitis.
statements.support_summary: One in vitro study shows that IL-1β directly increases MMP-1 production in human gingival fibroblasts, supporting the plausibility of an IL-1β-dependent increase in fibroblast MMP-1, although it does not test macrophage-conditioned medium or high-glucose conditions (PMID: 12206592). A review on diabetes and periodontal disease notes that poorly controlled diabetes is associated with higher IL-1β levels and greater periodontal inflammation, which is broadly consistent with a diabetes-linked inflammatory mechanism but does not directly address fibroblast MMP-1 induction by macrophage-conditioned medium (PMID: 21780542). Overall, the support is indirect and mainly rests on IL-1β being capable of stimulating gingival fibroblast MMP-1 and diabetes being associated with elevated inflammatory cytokines.
statements.contradict_summary: A directly relevant coculture study under high-glucose conditions reports that cross-talk between gingival fibroblasts and U937 macrophages augments MMP-1 expression in macrophages, not fibroblasts, and identifies fibroblast-derived IL-6—not IL-1β—as essential for this effect (PMID: 19307187). This study therefore conflicts with the claim on both the responding cell type and the proposed cytokine mechanism. It is also more mechanistically aligned with the claim's diabetes/high-glucose context than the supporting papers, making the discrepancy especially important.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported. The supporting evidence is indirect, whereas the most directly relevant high-glucose macrophage–fibroblast interaction study points to an IL-6-dependent mechanism affecting macrophage MMP-1 expression rather than an IL-1β-dependent increase of MMP-1 in gingival fibroblasts.

source_article.title: Construction and Validation of a Risk Prediction Model for Postoperative Urinary Retention in Lung Cancer Patients.
source_article.abstract: Indwelling catheter is a routine procedure in surgical patients. Studies have shown that prolonged indwelling urinary catheterization increases the risk of postoperative urinary tract infection. Although early removal of the urinary catheter after operation can reduce the risk of postoperative urinary symptoms and tract infections, it may lead to postoperative anesthetic dysuria. Therefore, this study investigates the urinary retention and related risk factors in patients after thoracoscopic lobectomy under general anesthesia. The clinical data of 214 patients who underwent thoracoscopic lobectomy in the Department of Thoracic Surgery of a tertiary class A cancer hospital in Beijing from July 2020 to April 2021 were collected. A risk prediction model was established by logistic regression analysis, and the prediction effect was determined using the area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. The incidence of indwelling catheter after thoracoscopic lobectomy was 44.8% (96/214). Sex (OR = 21.102, 95% CI: 2.906-153.239, P=0.003), perception of shame (OR = 74.256, 95% CI: 6.171-893.475, P=0.001), age (OR = 1.095, 95% CI: 1.014-1.182, P=0.021), and bed rest time (OR = 1.598, 95% CI: 1.263-2.023, P < 0.021) were the factors influencing urinary retention after thoracoscopic lobectomy. This model can effectively predict the occurrence of postoperative urinary retention in patients with lung cancer and help medical staff to intervene effectively before the onset of urinary retention, which provides reference for preventive treatment and nursing intervention.

statements.text: In patients with lung cancer undergoing thoracoscopic lobectomy under general anesthesia, postoperative urinary retention is common.
statements.support_summary: One retrospective study of 292 thoracic surgery patients without routine urinary catheterization under general anesthesia reported postoperative urinary retention (defined as post-void residual >200 ml) in 11.6% overall, with lung resection being an independent risk factor along with older age, male sex, and diabetes (PMID: 25564576). This provides indirect support that urinary retention is not rare after thoracic procedures involving lung resection. However, the abstract does not specifically study patients with lung cancer, thoracoscopic lobectomy, or quantify incidence within that exact subgroup, so applicability to the claim is limited.
statements.contradict_summary: No direct contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The only available study suggests postoperative urinary retention occurs in a meaningful minority of thoracic surgery patients and is more likely after lung resection, but it does not directly address the specific population of lung cancer patients undergoing thoracoscopic lobectomy.

statements.text: In patients with lung cancer undergoing thoracoscopic lobectomy under general anesthesia, female sex, greater perception of shame, older age, and longer bed rest time are associated with a higher risk of postoperative urinary retention.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One thoracic surgery study found postoperative urinary retention was independently associated with age >40 years, male sex, diabetes mellitus, and lung resection, with male rather than female sex identified as a risk factor (PMID: 25564576). This partially contradicts the claim because it conflicts on sex while aligning on older age; the abstract does not address perception of shame or longer bed rest time, so it cannot evaluate those factors (PMID: 25564576). The study population was minor thoracic surgery patients rather than specifically lung cancer patients undergoing thoracoscopic lobectomy, which limits direct comparability (PMID: 25564576).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported, but the evidence is limited. The only cited study directly conflicts with the claim on sex and supports only the age component, while providing no abstract-level evidence on shame or bed rest time and using a somewhat different surgical population.

statements.text: A risk prediction model based on clinical factors can effectively predict postoperative urinary retention in patients with lung cancer undergoing thoracoscopic lobectomy under general anesthesia.
statements.support_summary: One retrospective thoracic-surgery study developed a clinical scoring system for postoperative urinary retention (POUR) in 292 patients undergoing thoracic procedures without routine catheterization under general anesthesia, identifying age >40, male sex, diabetes, and lung resection as independent predictors (PMID: 25564576). The model showed good apparent discrimination (C-index 0.88) with 73% sensitivity and 90% specificity at a cutoff of 5 points, supporting the broader idea that clinical-factor-based models can predict POUR after thoracic surgery (PMID: 25564576). However, this evidence is indirect for the claim because the abstract addresses minor thoracic surgery broadly rather than specifically lung cancer patients undergoing thoracoscopic lobectomy.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only moderately. The available evidence suggests that a clinical-factor-based model can predict postoperative urinary retention in thoracic surgery patients, yet direct evidence for the narrower population of lung cancer patients undergoing thoracoscopic lobectomy is limited.

matched_article.title: Comparison of PLIF and TLIF in the Treatment of LDH Complicated with Spinal Stenosis.
matched_article.abstract: The purpose was to compare the clinical effects of posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF) and transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) in the treatment of lumbar disc herniation (LDH) complicated with spinal stenosis. 96 LDH patients complicated with spinal stenosis treated in our hospital (April 2018-April 2020) were chosen as the subjects, and split into the PLIF group and the TLIF group according to different surgical approaches, with 48 cases in each group. The clinical effects of the two groups were compared. There was no significant difference in hospitalization time between the two groups (P > 0.05). Compared with the PLIF group, the TLIF group had obviously shorter operation time and greatly lesser intraoperative blood loss (P < 0.05). The Numerical Rating Scale (NRS) scores of lower limb pain and low back pain in the two groups at 3 months after surgery were significantly lower than those before surgery (P < 0.001). The Japanese Orthopaedic Association (JOA) scores of the two groups at 3 months after surgery were significantly higher than those before surgery (P < 0.001). The Spitzer Quality of Life Index (SQLI) scores of the two groups at 3 months after surgery were significantly higher than those before surgery (P < 0.001). The two surgical approaches have similar efficacy in treating LDH complicated with spinal stenosis. However, PLIF is better than TLIF in terms of operation time and intraoperative blood loss, which should be adopted as the preferred surgical scheme.

statements.text: Posterior lumbar interbody fusion and transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion provide similar overall clinical efficacy for patients with lumbar disc herniation complicated by spinal stenosis.
statements.support_summary: Several comparative studies and one meta-analysis suggest PLIF and TLIF achieve broadly similar clinical efficacy, though most are in related degenerative lumbar conditions rather than exactly lumbar disc herniation with stenosis. A retrospective study in recurrent lumbar disc herniation found both procedures significantly improved JOA scores with no significant between-group differences in postoperative score, recovery rate, or overall surgical outcome, although TLIF had fewer dural tears and shorter operative time (PMID: 27811052). In three-level degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis, PLIF and MIS-TLIF showed no significant differences at 12 months in VAS, ODI, SF-36, fusion, or complications (PMID: 27747244). A systematic review/meta-analysis of 16 studies reported no significant difference in fusion rate or clinical satisfaction, while TLIF had less blood loss, shorter operative time, and fewer nerve root injuries/dural tears (PMID: 29367001). Additional retrospective studies also found similar pain/disability improvement or fusion outcomes despite some perioperative advantages for TLIF or MIS-TLIF (PMID: 32891847, PMID: 20546682).
statements.contradict_summary: The listed contradicting study does not directly compare PLIF with TLIF and therefore does not meaningfully contradict the claim. It compared lumbar total joint replacement with TLIF and found better 1-year patient-reported outcomes for the replacement procedure than TLIF in selected degenerative conditions (PMID: 33346156). Because PLIF was not studied, this item is better viewed as indirectly questioning TLIF's relative performance rather than contradicting similarity between PLIF and TLIF; thus its placement in the contradicting group is a discrepancy.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The main body of evidence, including a meta-analysis and multiple retrospective comparisons, consistently indicates similar clinical outcomes between PLIF and TLIF, although much of it is indirect because populations often include broader degenerative lumbar disease rather than the exact subgroup of disc herniation complicated by stenosis.

statements.text: Transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion results in shorter operation time and less intraoperative blood loss than posterior lumbar interbody fusion for patients with lumbar disc herniation complicated by spinal stenosis.
statements.support_summary: Multiple comparative studies and meta-analyses report that TLIF is associated with shorter operative time and less intraoperative blood loss than PLIF, although most are in broader lumbar degenerative conditions rather than the exact population of lumbar disc herniation with stenosis. A systematic review/meta-analysis of 16 studies found PLIF had significantly longer operation time and greater blood loss than TLIF in lumbar degenerative diseases (PMID: 29367001), and a meta-analysis in lumbar spondylolisthesis similarly found shorter surgery and lower blood loss with TLIF (PMID: 28647584). Large retrospective/prospective cohort studies in degenerative spondylolisthesis also consistently reported lower blood loss and shorter operative time with TLIF versus PLIF (PMID: 26126415, PMID: 34463112). A retrospective study in recurrent lumbar disc herniation stated TLIF may be preferred because of shorter operative time and fewer complications, though the abstract does not clearly report blood-loss data (PMID: 27811052). One study compared MIS-TLIF with open PLIF and found significantly lower blood loss with MIS-TLIF, but this design mixes minimally invasive versus open approaches and does not clearly support the operation-time part of the claim (PMID: 31068542).
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item does not directly test standard TLIF versus PLIF; instead it compares full-endoscopic PLIF with MIS-TLIF and found FE-PLIF had longer operation time but less blood loss than MIS-TLIF (PMID: 32891847). This creates only partial contradiction because its PLIF technique is a distinct endoscopic variant and therefore is not fully comparable to conventional PLIF in the claim. Thus, the contradiction is limited in both relevance and generalizability to the stated comparison.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from multiple meta-analyses and cohort studies consistently showing TLIF has shorter operative time and lower blood loss than PLIF, but most evidence is indirect because it involves broader degenerative lumbar populations rather than specifically lumbar disc herniation complicated by spinal stenosis.

statements.text: Postoperative pain, functional status, and quality of life improve after either posterior lumbar interbody fusion or transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion in patients with lumbar disc herniation complicated by spinal stenosis.
statements.support_summary: Most included studies report that both PLIF and/or TLIF are associated with significant postoperative improvement in pain and disability/functional measures such as VAS, ODI, JOA, or recovery rates, although many are in broader degenerative lumbar disease populations rather than the exact population of lumbar disc herniation with spinal stenosis. Direct comparative evidence suggests both PLIF and TLIF yield similar clinical improvement overall, with no major between-group differences in satisfaction or functional outcomes, while TLIF often shows perioperative advantages such as less blood loss, shorter operative time, and fewer dural tears or nerve injuries (PMID: 27811052, PMID: 29367001, PMID: 20546682, PMID: 34463112). More condition-relevant reports show improvement after TLIF for foraminal stenosis or spinal stenosis/instability, including marked reductions in back/leg pain and ODI/JOA improvement (PMID: 20887148, PMID: 22333447, PMID: 23290169), and PLIF also showed pain improvement in stenosis-related cohorts (PMID: 24172588, PMID: 26122941). One retrospective series explicitly reported significant decreases in pain-related disability and pain scores after both PLIF and TLIF, concluding both provide quality-of-life and functional benefit, though this was for localized segment degeneration rather than the exact claim population (PMID: 21341184). Overall, the evidence consistently supports postoperative improvement after either fusion approach, but there is an important indirectness/discrepancy because many studies do not specifically enroll patients with lumbar disc herniation complicated by spinal stenosis.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence across multiple comparative studies, cohort studies, and one meta-analysis consistently shows improved pain and functional outcomes after both PLIF and TLIF, but support is moderated by the fact that much of the literature involves broader degenerative lumbar conditions rather than the exact subgroup of lumbar disc herniation complicated by spinal stenosis.

source_article.title: A Multiscenario Intelligent QoS Routing Algorithm for Vehicle Network.
source_article.abstract: Vehicular ad hoc network (VANET) is a key part of intelligent transportation system. VANET technology is very important for realizing vehicle-to-vehicle communication, remote control of unmanned vehicles, and early warning reception of road condition information ahead of time when external networks such as the Internet are limited. Aiming at the problems of uncertainty in vehicle mobility, uneven distribution of traffic density in road sections, and uncertainty in the road scene where the vehicle is located in VANET, a multiscenario intelligent QoS routing algorithm (MISR) for vehicle network is proposed. The algorithm analyzes a variety of vehicle network scenarios and discusses the routing methods used in scenarios with/without roadside auxiliary units and vehicle uniform acceleration limited/unrestricted, so that the vehicle network can ensure that the communication link is not interrupted as much as possible. At the same time, QoS performance criteria such as data transmission rate, bit error rate, and delay time are considered. For complex scenes with variable vehicle speeds, this paper introduces a deep reinforcement learning method to intelligently select routing nodes for vehicle networks.

statements.text: A multiscenario intelligent QoS routing algorithm for vehicular ad hoc networks improves communication link continuity across different vehicle-network scenarios with and without roadside auxiliary units and under different vehicle acceleration constraints.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a different VANET routing paper, not an abstract reporting experimental results about the claimed multiscenario intelligent QoS routing algorithm, so it does not directly test or refute the specific claim (PMID: 33809402). This appears to be a categorization discrepancy: a retraction of another article weakens confidence in that retracted work but is not substantive evidence against the stated performance claim here.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear. There is no direct supporting evidence, and the lone contradicting item is only an unrelated retraction notice rather than empirical evidence refuting the claim.

statements.text: A multiscenario intelligent QoS routing algorithm for vehicular ad hoc networks considers quality-of-service goals including higher data transmission performance, lower bit error rate, and reduced delay in routing decisions.
statements.support_summary: Across multiple VANET and related ad hoc routing studies, routing decisions explicitly target QoS-relevant metrics such as packet delivery/data transmission performance and reduced delay, though most do not mention bit error rate directly. A multiscenario optimization framework for VANETs evaluated different urban scenarios and improved packet delivery ratio, packet loss, and end-to-end delay, which is close to the claim's multiscenario QoS emphasis (PMID: 29462884). Several intelligent or adaptive routing approaches also report better delivery ratio/throughput and lower delay, including intelligent junction selection (PMID: 27433485), mobility/node-density-based routing satisfying QoS considerations (PMID: 27855165), Q-learning/trajectory-based routing with higher packet delivery ratio and lower end-to-end delay (PMID: 36365920), SDN/cloud-assisted hybrid routing with higher PDR and lower EED (PMID: 38400202), and time-delay or multipath approaches prioritizing prompt delivery and improved transmission performance (PMID: 34833777; PMID: 37420744). One listed study concerns unmanned ground vehicles rather than standard VANET packet-routing performance and is therefore only loosely relevant to the claim (PMID: 36772471). Overall, the support is strongest for consideration of transmission performance and delay in routing decisions, but weaker for the specific QoS goal of lower bit error rate.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because several studies in VANET or closely related vehicular ad hoc routing optimize routing using QoS-like objectives, especially improved delivery/performance and reduced delay. However, the evidence is only moderate rather than definitive because the exact combination of multiscenario intelligent QoS routing and explicit bit-error-rate minimization is not consistently stated in the abstracts.

statements.text: A deep reinforcement learning approach can be used to intelligently select routing nodes in vehicular ad hoc networks with variable vehicle speeds and complex road scenes.
statements.support_summary: Several recent VANET studies support using reinforcement learning, including deep reinforcement learning, to select relay/next-hop routing nodes under dynamic and complex conditions. An environment-aware adaptive reinforcement routing protocol used beacon-derived metrics such as vehicle speed, bandwidth, and signal strength to choose relay vehicles and improved packet delivery, throughput, and latency in urban/interference-prone scenarios, closely matching the claim (PMID: 38202902). Other studies similarly report RL-based route selection using geo-positioning, signal strength, and obstacle/path-loss information in city maps (PMID: 35808228), DQN-based adaptive forwarding over a complex state space of candidate-node attributes and message type (PMID: 36772244), and improved DRL routing under dynamic vehicle density with better latency and delivery performance (PMID: 38066104). A related Q-learning/trajectory-based system also selected best next-hop neighbors in real time using direction, position, and trajectory data from real urban traffic traces, though it is not explicitly deep RL, so it is only indirect support and a category discrepancy relative to the claim's 'deep' RL wording (PMID: 36365920).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The evidence includes multiple simulation-based VANET studies showing that RL/DRL methods can intelligently choose routing or forwarding nodes in dynamic vehicular settings, with the strongest match explicitly incorporating vehicle speed and complex urban environments (PMID: 38202902), although some supporting papers address the concept more generally or use non-deep RL.

matched_article.title: A Hybrid Model for Leaf Diseases Classification Based on the Modified Deep Transfer Learning and Ensemble Approach for Agricultural AIoT-Based Monitoring.
matched_article.abstract: As possible diseases develop on plant leaves, classification is constantly hampered by obstacles such as overfitting and low accuracy. To distinguish healthy products from defective ones, the agricultural industry requires precise and error-free analysis. Deep convolutional neural networks are an efficient model of autonomous feature extraction that has been shown to be fairly effective for detection and classification tasks. However, deep convolutional neural networks often require a large amount of training data, cannot be translated, and need a number of parameters to be specified and tweaked. This paper proposes a highly effective structure that can be applied to classifying multiple leaf diseases of plants and fruits during the feature extraction step. It uses a deep transfer learning model that has been modified to serve this purpose. In summary, we use model engineering (ME) to extract features.
