Multiple support vector machine (SVM) models are employed to enhance feature discrimination and processing speed. The kernel parameters of the radial basis function (RBF) are determined based on the selected model in the training step. PlantVillage and UCI datasets were used to analyze six leaf image sets containing healthy and diseased leaves of apple, corn, cotton, grape, pepper, and rice. The classification process resulted in approximately 90,000 images. During the experimental implementation phase, the results show the potential of a powerful model in classification operations, which will be beneficial for a variety of future leaf disease diagnostic applications for the agricultural industry.

statements.text: A modified deep transfer learning model combined with an ensemble of support vector machines can classify healthy and diseased plant leaves effectively across multiple crop species.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study reports that an EResNet-SVM model improved feature extraction, reduced overfitting, and achieved high recognition accuracy for plant diseases and pests, including 99.30% test accuracy across eight conditions consisting of seven plant diseases and one normal class (PMID: 38483173). The abstract indicates that combining a deep learning architecture with an SVM-based classifier can effectively distinguish normal versus diseased plant phenotypes. However, this evidence does not clearly describe a deep transfer learning model, an ensemble of SVMs, or broad validation across multiple crop species, so it only partially matches the claim. Overall, the available support is directionally consistent but narrower than the full statement.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only weakly to moderately. The sole available study is consistent with effective plant disease classification using a deep model combined with SVM (PMID: 38483173), yet it does not clearly establish the specific elements of modified deep transfer learning, an ensemble of SVMs, and performance across multiple crop species.

statements.text: The proposed hybrid model reduces overfitting and improves classification accuracy for leaf disease detection compared with relying on deep convolutional neural networks alone.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies report that hybrid or modified architectures for plant leaf disease recognition can outperform single CNN baselines in accuracy and sometimes explicitly address overfitting. Stronger direct support comes from EResNet-SVM, which explicitly states that the SVM-fused architecture alleviated overfitting and improved test accuracy versus ResNet18 and several other CNNs (PMID: 38483173), and from an EfficientNetB0+SVM hybrid that achieved 97.29% accuracy, outperforming several standalone deep models including CNN, ResNet50, Xception, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNetB0 (PMID: 39194972). Additional studies describe hybrid/ensemble or feature-fusion approaches that improved recognition versus single models or conventional CNNs, such as stacking CNNs with SVM for rice leaf disease identification (PMID: 37346133), ROI extraction plus DenseNet for rice disease classification (PMID: 36330248), weighted ensemble learning for crop leaf disease recognition (PMID: 38264019), and transfer learning/ensemble systems reported as more robust or generalizable than constituent CNNs (PMID: 38408925). However, much of the supporting set is indirect because many abstracts emphasize higher accuracy, parameter reduction, or generalization without explicitly measuring overfitting or directly comparing against “deep CNN alone”; some items are really improved CNNs rather than clearly hybrid models, which is a discrepancy relative to the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted because several abstracts, especially PMID: 38483173 and PMID: 39194972, directly or near-directly report that hybrid CNN-plus-classifier approaches improve performance over standalone deep models, with one explicitly citing reduced overfitting. Still, the evidence is not uniformly strong because many studies only imply better generalization or accuracy without directly testing overfitting against deep CNN-only baselines.

statements.text: The hybrid feature-extraction and ensemble classification approach is suitable for agricultural leaf disease diagnostic applications in plant and fruit monitoring.
statements.support_summary: Multiple recent studies report that hybrid pipelines combining feature extraction/selection with machine-learning or ensemble classifiers perform well for agricultural leaf disease diagnosis across tomato, rice, grape, sugarcane, fig, brinjal, apple, cucumber, and other crops. Stronger examples include CNN-plus-stacking or multimodel ensemble systems that achieved high accuracy and were explicitly framed as practical for agricultural monitoring, such as tomato disease classification with a CNN-stacking hybrid on PlantVillage and unseen noisy images (PMID: 39277634), transfer-learning ensembles with logistic-regression fusion for plant disease detection (PMID: 38408925), stacking CNNs plus SVM for rice leaf disease identification (PMID: 37346133), and weighted ensemble learning across multiple crop datasets (PMID: 38264019). Several additional studies support suitability of hybrid feature-extraction/classification workflows, including wavelet/CNN or ROI/CNN systems, transformer-CNN hybrids, and optimized feature-fusion models with strong reported accuracy and in some cases real-time or deployable performance (PMID: 36552243, PMID: 37896622, PMID: 36330248, PMID: 38914605, PMID: 37663670). Some items are only indirect support because they use hybrid feature extraction and classification but not true ensemble classification, or they are dataset/resource papers rather than performance demonstrations, creating a terminology mismatch with the exact claim (e.g., PMID: 37018344, PMID: 38027230, PMID: 35062534, PMID: 38317727). Overall, the abstracts consistently indicate that hybrid and/or ensemble approaches are suitable and often advantageous for leaf disease diagnostic applications in plant and fruit monitoring.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The evidence includes numerous recent abstract-level studies across diverse crops showing high-performing hybrid feature-extraction and ensemble or ensemble-like classification systems for leaf disease diagnosis, with no direct contradictory evidence provided.

source_article.title: Fibronectin enhances tumor metastasis through B7-H3 in clear cell renal cell carcinoma.
source_article.abstract: B7 homolog 3 (B7-H3) plays an important role in tumor biology, but the molecular mechanism underlying the role of B7-H3 in tumor metastasis remains unclear. In this article, our analysis of The Cancer Genome Atlas database suggested that B7-H3 expression is associated with poor prognosis of patients with clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). B7-H3 knockdown affected the expression of metastasis-related genes and significantly suppressed the metastasis of ccRCC cells, but it had no significant effect on the proliferation of ccRCC cells. Database analysis revealed a strong positive correlation between B7-H3 and fibronectin (FN) in ccRCC cells, and further study also confirmed that FN interacts with B7-H3. Silencing FN expression inhibited the migration and invasion of ccRCC cells, whereas exogenous FN promoted the migration and invasion of ccRCC cells, which was accompanied by activation of kinases [namely, phosphorylated (p)-phosphoinositide 3-kinase, p-protein kinase B, p-p38 and p-extracellular regulated protein kinase]. B7-H3 knockdown abolished the prometastatic effect of FN. In conclusion, our data suggest that B7-H3 binds to exogenous FN and promotes the metastasis of ccRCC cells.

statements.text: In clear cell renal cell carcinoma cells, B7-H3 promotes metastasis without affecting cell proliferation.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Two abstracts contradict the claim that B7-H3 promotes metastasis without affecting proliferation in ccRCC. In PMID: 23916610, miR-187 directly targets B7-H3, and B7-H3 knockdown phenocopied miR-187 overexpression, which suppressed in vitro proliferation, inhibited in vivo tumor growth, and decreased motility, indicating B7-H3 contributes to both growth and migration. In PMID: 39621555, high B7-H3 expression correlated with aggressive clinicopathologic features including synchronous metastasis and lymph node metastasis, but B7-H3 knockdown by siRNA/CRISPR also reduced 2D/3D proliferation and viability, again opposing the 'without affecting cell proliferation' part of the claim. A minor discrepancy is that PMID: 39621555 simultaneously supports a metastasis association while contradicting the no-proliferation-effect portion, so it is best viewed as partial contradiction overall.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although one study links B7-H3 with metastatic/aggressive disease, both available abstracts indicate that B7-H3 also promotes ccRCC cell proliferation or tumor growth, directly opposing the claim that metastasis is promoted without any effect on proliferation.

statements.text: In clear cell renal cell carcinoma cells, fibronectin promotes migration and invasion through activation of signaling pathways and requires B7-H3 to exert its prometastatic effect.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In clear cell renal cell carcinoma, higher B7-H3 expression is associated with poorer patient prognosis.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies support an association between higher B7-H3 expression and worse outcomes in clear cell renal cell carcinoma. A large nephrectomy cohort found that tumor cell or diffuse tumor vasculature B7-H3 expression was linked to adverse clinicopathologic features and independently predicted increased risk of death from RCC after multivariable adjustment (PMID: 18694993). Other immunohistochemistry studies reported that higher B7-H3 expression correlated with higher grade, TNM stage, lymph node invasion, tumor necrosis, and poorer disease-specific or disease-free survival, including specifically higher cytoplasmic B7-H3 in ccRCC (PMID: 32143173, PMID: 29180874, PMID: 39621555). One mechanistic study is only indirect support: low miR-187 was associated with worse survival and B7-H3 was identified as a miR-187 target, but the abstract does not directly test B7-H3 expression versus prognosis in patients, so this item is weaker/more inferential than the others (PMID: 23916610).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall strongly supported. The evidence includes a large multivariable prognostic study and several additional ccRCC cohorts showing that higher B7-H3 expression tracks with aggressive tumor features and poorer survival-related outcomes, with no direct contradictory evidence presented.

matched_article.title: Development and validation of a four-lipid metabolism gene signature for diagnosis of pancreatic cancer.
matched_article.abstract: Abnormal lipid metabolism is closely related to the malignant biological behavior of tumor cells. Such abnormal lipid metabolism provides energy for rapid proliferation, and certain genes related to lipid metabolism encode important components of tumor signaling pathways. In this study, we analyzed pancreatic cancer datasets from The Cancer Genome Atlas and searched for prognostic genes related to lipid metabolism in the Molecular Signature Database. A risk score model was built and verified using the GSE57495 dataset and International Cancer Genome Consortium dataset. Four molecular subtypes and 4249 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified. The DEGs obtained by Weighted Gene Coexpression Network Construction analysis were intersected with 4249 DEGs to obtain a total of 1340 DEGs. The final prognosis model included CA8, CEP55, GNB3 and SGSM2, and these had a significant effect on overall survival. The area under the curve at 1, 3 and 5 years was 0.72, 0.79 and 0.87, respectively. These same results were obtained using the validation cohort. Survival analysis data showed that the model could stratify the prognosis of patients with different clinical characteristics, and the model has clinical independence. Functional analysis indicated that the model is associated with multiple cancer-related pathways. Compared with published models, our model has a higher C-index and greater risk value. In summary, this four-gene signature is an independent risk factor for pancreatic cancer survival and may be an effective prognostic indicator.

statements.text: A four-gene lipid metabolism signature composed of CA8, CEP55, GNB3, and SGSM2 is an independent prognostic risk factor for pancreatic cancer survival.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: The four-gene signature stratifies pancreatic cancer patients into groups with different overall survival outcomes across clinical subgroups.
statements.support_summary: Several abstracts support the general idea that four-marker signatures can stratify pancreatic cancer patients by prognosis, but the evidence is uneven for the specific claim about overall survival differences across clinical subgroups. The most directly relevant study reports a four-gene autophagy-related model (ATG16L2, GNAI3, APOL1, PTK6) that classified pancreatic cancer patients into high- and low-risk groups and that survival/ROC analyses indicated prognostic accuracy (PMID: 34587439), though the abstract does not explicitly mention subgroup-specific survival analyses. Additional partial support comes from a four-miRNA signature that stratified early-stage PDAC patients into high- and low-risk groups (PMID: 30483731), and from other pancreatic cancer studies describing four-gene prognostic models with predictive value for prognosis or chemotherapy response (PMID: 33901011; PMID: 34654352; PMID: 31706267). However, these latter studies concern different signatures or biomarkers, and some are not strictly four-gene signatures or do not state analysis across clinical subgroups, so they are supportive only by analogy rather than direct confirmation.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted, because no opposing evidence is provided and one study directly shows a four-gene model separating pancreatic cancer patients into prognostic risk groups (PMID: 34587439). However, support is only moderate because the available abstracts generally do not explicitly demonstrate different overall survival outcomes across clinical subgroups, which is a key part of the claim.

statements.text: The four-gene signature shows better prognostic performance than previously published pancreatic cancer models and is associated with multiple cancer-related pathways.
statements.support_summary: Several pancreatic cancer studies describe four-feature prognostic signatures with pathway associations, but most only partially align with the specific claim. A four-miRNA PDAC signature was linked to prognosis and enriched cancer-related pathways including TGF-β, Ras, cell cycle, FoxO, Hippo, and PI3K-Akt signaling, supporting the pathway-association part of the claim but not a direct comparison against prior models (PMID: 30483731). Other studies reported four-gene signatures with good prognostic or predictive performance in pancreatic cancer, including autophagy-related and immune-related models, and noted enrichment in biologic pathways or immune microenvironment associations, but again did not clearly show superiority over previously published pancreatic cancer models in the abstracts (PMID: 34587439, PMID: 34654352, PMID: 33901011, PMID: 31706267). Thus, the main recurring theme is that four-gene/miRNA signatures are prognostic and biologically connected to cancer pathways, while explicit evidence of outperforming prior published models is largely absent in these abstracts.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, mainly because multiple studies show that four-marker pancreatic cancer signatures have prognostic relevance and are associated with cancer-related pathways. However, support is only moderate since the provided abstracts generally do not directly demonstrate better prognostic performance than previously published pancreatic cancer models.

source_article.title: Site-specific deoxynucleotide substitutions in yeast U6 snRNA block splicing of pre-mRNA in vitro.
source_article.abstract: We have identified 2'-hydroxyl groups of the U6 phosphate-ribose backbone which are required for reconstitution of splicing activity in U6-depleted yeast extract. To screen the 2'-hydroxyls of yeast U6 at nucleotides 39-88, spanning the conserved central domain, synthetic U6 RNAs were constructed with deoxyribonucleotides incorporated site specifically. Only four individual deoxynucleotide substitutions blocked splicing activity: dA51 (in the ACAGAG sequence), dA62 (next to the AGC triad), and dU70 and dC72 (both in the loop of the 3' intramolecular stem-loop). Native gel analysis revealed that these deoxy-substituted U6 RNAs were competent for assembly of spliceosomes. Interestingly, a 2'-O-methyl substituent at A51, A62, U70 or C72 did not inhibit splicing activity, indicating that the essential 2'-OH groups at these positions in U6 act as hydrogen bond acceptors or neutral coordinated ligands. The requisite 2'-hydroxyls at A62, U70 and C72 show both similarities and differences relative to the positions of essential 2'-hydroxyls of catalytic domain V of group II ribozymes. The identification of the essential 2'-hydroxyls at positions 62, 70 and 72 corroborates that the 3' intramolecular stem-loop in U6 plays an important role in pre-mRNA splicing.

statements.text: Specific 2'-hydroxyl groups in yeast U6 snRNA at positions corresponding to A51, A62, U70, and C72 are required for pre-mRNA splicing activity in yeast extract, whereas other tested 2'-hydroxyl groups in the central domain are not essential.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Yeast U6 snRNA molecules with deoxynucleotide substitutions at positions corresponding to A51, A62, U70, or C72 can still assemble into spliceosomes but fail to support splicing of pre-mRNA in vitro.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: The 3' intramolecular stem-loop of yeast U6 snRNA has an important functional role in pre-mRNA splicing because essential 2'-hydroxyl groups are located in this region.
statements.support_summary: Two abstracts support a functional role for the U6 intramolecular stem-loop in splicing, though neither directly demonstrates that essential 2'-hydroxyl groups in the yeast 3' stem-loop are responsible. Phosphorothioate interference mapping identified a backbone pro-R oxygen within the U6 intramolecular stem-loop as critical for the first catalytic step, consistent with functional importance of this region in splicing (PMID: 7489488). A separate in vivo study showed that the U6/U6atac stem-loop is required for splicing, that yeast U6 stem-loop sequences can functionally replace the U6atac stem-loop, and that disruption of stem regions abolishes activity unless compensatory pairing is restored, supporting a conserved functional role for the stem-loop structure (PMID: 11214185). However, these studies emphasize backbone oxygens or general structural/functional groups rather than specifically identifying essential 2'-hydroxyls in the 3' yeast U6 stem-loop, so support for the precise claim is indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a review that questions whether the important 3' internal stem-loop is actually present in free U6 snRNP and discusses a new secondary-structure model lacking this element, thereby challenging assumptions about its universal structural importance (PMID: 20659011). However, the abstract frames this as an evaluation of alternative models for free U6 and spliceosome assembly rather than direct experimental evidence that the 3' stem-loop lacks function in catalysis or that essential 2'-hydroxyl groups are absent. Thus, this is a partial and indirect contradiction, and its review nature should be noted.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only at a moderate and indirect level. The supporting abstracts consistently indicate that the U6 intramolecular stem-loop is functionally important for splicing, whereas the contradiction mainly questions structural models of free U6 and does not directly refute the specific claim about essential 2'-hydroxyl groups.

matched_article.title: FBP WW domains and the Abl SH3 domain bind to a specific class of proline-rich ligands.
matched_article.abstract: WW domains are conserved protein motifs of 38-40 amino acids found in a broad spectrum of proteins. They mediate protein-protein interactions by binding proline-rich modules in ligands. A 10 amino acid proline-rich portion of the morphogenic protein, formin, is bound in vitro by both the WW domain of the formin-binding protein 11 (FBP11) and the SH3 domain of Abl. To explore whether the FBP11 WW domain and Abl SH3 domain bind to similar ligands, we screened a mouse limb bud expression library for putative ligands of the FBP11 WW domain. In so doing, we identified eight ligands (WBP3 through WBP10), each of which contains a proline-rich region or regions. Peptide sequence comparisons of the ligands revealed a conserved motif of 10 amino acids that acts as a modular sequence binding the FBP11 WW domain, but not the WW domain of the putative signal transducing factor, hYAP65. Interestingly, the consensus ligand for the FBP11 WW domain contains residues that are also required for binding by the Abl SH3 domain. These findings support the notion that the FBP11 WW domain and the Abl SH3 domain can compete for the same proline-rich ligands and suggest that at least two subclasses of WW domains exist, namely those that bind a PPLP motif, and those that bind a PPXY motif.

statements.text: The FBP11 WW domain binds a specific class of proline-rich ligands containing a conserved motif, while the hYAP65 WW domain does not bind that ligand class.
statements.support_summary: Two structural studies support that FBP11 WW1 recognizes a specific subset of proline-rich ligands containing a conserved PPLP-related motif. One reports that FBP11/FBP11WW1 specifically recognizes proteins containing PPLPp motifs and that leucine-side-chain recognition is a key determinant of class II WW-domain specificity (PMID: 15811376). Another solved an FBP11 WW1 complex with a Pro-Pro-Leu-Pro ligand and showed how aromatic grooves and Loop I mediate selective binding by group II/III WW domains (PMID: 17065151). However, these studies do not directly test hYAP65, so support is limited to the FBP11-specific part of the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: Three YAP65 studies show that the hYAP65/hu-YAP65 WW domain does bind proline-rich peptides, especially PY-motif ligands, contradicting any broad interpretation that YAP65 lacks binding to proline-rich ligands. Early functional and mutational work identified YAP ligands with a PPPPY/PY motif and showed relatively high-affinity, sequence-specific binding with an XPPXY consensus (PMID: 7644498). Structural studies then characterized hYAP65 WW-domain complexes with PPxY-containing peptides such as GTPPPPYTVG and other related ligands, confirming direct binding and defining a minimal binding epitope (PMID: 8757138; PMID: 11743730). A key discrepancy is that these papers address PY/PPxY-type ligands, whereas the claim appears to concern the distinct FBP11-recognized PPLP class; thus they strongly contradict a nonspecific reading of the claim but do not directly prove YAP65 binds the same FBP11 ligand class.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is mixed but overall leans slightly toward support for the specific claim as written: FBP11 clearly binds a distinct PPLP/Pro-Pro-Leu-Pro-like proline-rich motif, while the contradicting studies mainly show that hYAP65 binds a different proline-rich class centered on PY/PPxY motifs. Thus, the strongest evidence establishes differential ligand specificity between FBP11 and hYAP65, although the provided abstracts do not directly test hYAP65 against the exact FBP11 ligand class.

statements.text: The FBP11 WW domain and the Abl SH3 domain recognize overlapping proline-rich ligand determinants and can compete for the same ligands.
statements.support_summary: Direct support comes from the discovery paper showing that WW/WP domains bind proline-rich sequences similar or sometimes identical to SH3 ligands, and specifically that a WW domain can compete with the Abl SH3 domain for binding to a formin-derived proline-rich peptide, supporting overlapping ligand recognition and competition (PMID: 8605874). Additional indirect support comes from structural/biophysical work on yeast Prp40 WW domains reporting binding to proline-rich peptides that are also recognized by Abl-SH3 and by the mammalian Prp40 orthologue FBP11 WW domain, consistent with shared ligand determinants, though competition was not directly tested there (PMID: 12460579). A broader review/modeling study further supports the general concept that WW and SH3 domains can show convergent recognition of proline-rich ligands, but it does not specifically test FBP11 or Abl and is therefore only weakly supportive (PMID: 11911877). Overall, the main support is one direct experimental abstract plus two more indirect studies/themes showing WW–SH3 overlap in proline-rich ligand recognition.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence is a direct abstract reporting WW-domain binding to SH3-like proline-rich motifs and competition with Abl SH3 for a shared ligand (PMID: 8605874), while the remaining studies provide consistent but more indirect support for overlapping WW/SH3 ligand recognition.

statements.text: WW domains include at least two ligand-binding subclasses, one that binds PPLP-containing motifs and another that binds PPXY-containing motifs.
statements.support_summary: Multiple abstracts directly support the existence of distinct WW-domain ligand-binding classes, with especially explicit statements that PPXY/PPxY and PPLP are recognized by different WW-domain classes or groups (PMID: 10744724, PMID: 12592019, PMID: 19003214). Foundational and structural studies separately document WW domains that bind PPxY motifs, such as YAP, WWOX, and Prp40 family domains (PMID: 8757138, PMID: 7644498, PMID: 10837147, PMID: 12460579, PMID: 15064722, PMID: 22634283), while other studies describe class II or related WW domains that specifically recognize PPLP/PPLP-like motifs in FE65 and FBP11/formin systems (PMID: 9407065, PMID: 15811376, PMID: 17915251). Several papers further frame this as a broader specificity-based classification of WW domains into multiple groups, where PPxY-recognizing and PPLP-recognizing domains are recurring subclasses (PMID: 12592019, PMID: 10744724). Many additional supporting items only document one side of the claim—usually PPxY recognition—so they are partial rather than complete support, but they are consistent with the overall subclass model.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The evidence includes several abstracts that explicitly classify WW domains into distinct ligand-specific subclasses recognizing PPXY/PPxY versus PPLP motifs, reinforced by multiple structural and binding studies identifying representatives of each class.

source_article.title: Effects of microRNA-136 on melanoma cell proliferation, apoptosis, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition by targetting PMEL through the Wnt signaling pathway.
source_article.abstract: The study aims to evaluate the effects of miR-136 on the proliferation, apoptosis, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) of melanoma cells by targetting premelanosome protein (PMEL) through the Wnt signaling pathway. After establishment of melanoma mouse models, melanoma (model group) and normal tissues (normal group) were collected. Immunohistochemistry was performed to determine PMEL protein concentration. Mouse melanoma cells were assigned into control, blank, negative control (NC), miR-136 mimics, miR-136 inhibitors, siRNA-PMEL, and miR-136 inhibitors + siRNA-PMEL, LiC1 (Wnt signaling pathway activator), and siRNA-PMEL+ LiCl groups. MTT, Scratch test, Transwell assay, and flow cytometry were performed to measure cell proliferation, migration, invasion, and apoptosis. Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) and Western blotting were performed to evaluate miR-136, PMEL, β-catenin, Wnt3a, Bcl-2, Bax, Caspase, E-cadherin, and N-cadherin expressions. PMEL is highly expressed in melanoma tissues. MiR-136, Bax, Caspase, and E-cadherin expressions decreased in the model group, whereas PMEL, β-catenin, Bcl-2, Wnt3a, and N-cadherin expressions increased. Bax, Caspase, and E-cadherin expressions increased in the miR-136 mimics and siRNA-PMEL groups, whereas the expressions decreased in the miR-136 inhibitors group and LiC1 group. PMEL, β-catenin, Bcl-2, Wnt3a, and N-cadherin expressions, cell proliferation, migration, and invasion decreased, and the apoptosis rate inceased in the miR-136 mimics and siRNA-PMEL groups; whereas the tendencies were opposite to those in the miR-136 inhibitors group and LiC1 group. In the siRNA-PMEL+ LiCl group, PMEL expression decreased. These findings indicated that overexpression of miR-136 inhibits melanoma cell EMT, proliferation, migration, invasion, and promotes apoptosis by targetting PMEL through down-regulation of the Wnt signaling pathway.

matched_article.title: A simple toolset to identify endogenous post-translational modifications for a target protein: a snapshot of the EGFR signaling pathway.
matched_article.abstract: Identification of a novel post-translational modification (PTM) for a target protein, defining its physiologic role, and studying its potential crosstalk with other PTMs is a challenging process. A set of highly sensitive tools termed Signal-Seeker kits was developed, which enables rapid and simple detection of post-translational modifications on any target protein. The methodology for these tools utilizes affinity purification of modified proteins from a cell or tissue lysate and immunoblot analysis. These tools utilize a single lysis system that is effective at identifying endogenous, dynamic PTM changes, as well as the potential crosstalk between PTMs. As a proof-of-concept experiment, the acetylation, tyrosine phosphorylation, SUMOylation 2/3, and ubiquitination profiles of the EGFR - Ras - c-Fos axis were examined in response to EGF stimulation. All 10 previously identified PTMs of this signaling axis were confirmed using these tools, and it also identified acetylation as a novel modification of c-Fos. This axis in the EGF/EGFR signaling pathway was chosen because it is a well-established signaling pathway with proteins localized in the membrane, cytoplasmic, and nuclear compartments that ranged in abundance from 4.18x108 (EGFR) to 1.35x104 (c-Fos) molecules per A431 cell. These tools enabled the identification of low abundance PTMs, such as c-Fos Ac, at 17 molecules per cell. These studies highlight how pervasive PTMs are, and how stimulants like EGF induce multiple PTM changes on downstream signaling axis. Identification of endogenous changes and potential crosstalk between multiple PTMs for a target protein or signaling axis will provide regulatory mechanistic insight to investigators.

statements.text: Signal-Seeker kits enabled rapid detection of endogenous post-translational modifications on target proteins from cell or tissue lysates and can identify dynamic modification changes and potential crosstalk between post-translational modifications.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study describes use of Signal-Seeker PTM detection tools to assess endogenous acetylation changes in mitochondrial proteins after H2O2 treatment and to examine endogenous acetylation, ubiquitination, SUMO2/3, and tyrosine phosphorylation on selected targets, concluding that the toolkit is useful for investigating potential PTM crosstalk (PMID: 29786648). The abstract specifically reports dynamic PTM changes in response to stress and distinct PTM profiles across proteins, which is consistent with detecting endogenous modifications and possible crosstalk. However, the abstract emphasizes a proof-of-concept application and does not directly quantify "rapid" detection, so that aspect of the claim is only indirectly supported.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available abstract directly describes Signal-Seeker kits being used to detect endogenous PTMs, monitor modification changes after treatment, and explore potential PTM crosstalk (PMID: 29786648). The evidence base is limited to a single proof-of-concept study, so support is moderate rather than definitive.

statements.text: In the epidermal growth factor receptor signaling axis, epidermal growth factor stimulation induced multiple post-translational modification changes across EGFR, Ras, and c-Fos, including confirmation of previously known modifications.
statements.support_summary: Two method-focused studies provide limited indirect support that EGF/ligand stimulation can induce multiple PTMs in the EGFR axis, but they do not directly test the full claim across EGFR, Ras, and c-Fos. A HiBiT-BRET immunoassay study showed detection of phosphorylation changes in endogenous EGFR and other signaling proteins, demonstrating that PTM dynamics in this pathway can be monitored, but it did not mention Ras, c-Fos, or confirmation of known sites (PMID: 38272933). An eSiMBlot study reported direct visualization of novel combinatorial phospho-codes on ligand-induced EGFR, supporting the idea that EGFR undergoes multiple PTM changes after stimulation, though again without addressing Ras, c-Fos, or explicit confirmation of previously known modifications (PMID: 29806008). Overall, the support is partial and largely limited to EGFR-focused methodological evidence rather than direct pathway-wide evidence matching the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is weakly to moderately supported based on the provided evidence. The available studies are relevant to stimulus-induced EGFR post-translational modification dynamics, but they do not directly establish multiple PTM changes across EGFR, Ras, and c-Fos together or clearly document confirmation of previously known modifications.

statements.text: In the epidermal growth factor receptor signaling axis, the toolset identified acetylation as a novel post-translational modification of c-Fos and was able to detect low-abundance modified proteins.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: COVID-19 mRNA Vaccines: Lessons Learned from the Registrational Trials and Global Vaccination Campaign.
source_article.abstract: Our understanding of COVID-19 vaccinations and their impact on health and mortality has evolved substantially since the first vaccine rollouts. Published reports from the original randomized phase 3 trials concluded that the COVID-19 mRNA vaccines could greatly reduce COVID-19 symptoms. In the interim, problems with the methods, execution, and reporting of these pivotal trials have emerged. Re-analysis of the Pfizer trial data identified statistically significant increases in serious adverse events (SAEs) in the vaccine group. Numerous SAEs were identified following the Emergency Use Authorization (EUA), including death, cancer, cardiac events, and various autoimmune, hematological, reproductive, and neurological disorders. Furthermore, these products never underwent adequate safety and toxicological testing in accordance with previously established scientific standards. Among the other major topics addressed in this narrative review are the published analyses of serious harms to humans, quality control issues and process-related impurities, mechanisms underlying adverse events (AEs), the immunologic basis for vaccine inefficacy, and concerning mortality trends based on the registrational trial data. The risk-benefit imbalance substantiated by the evidence to date contraindicates further booster injections and suggests that, at a minimum, the mRNA injections should be removed from the childhood immunization program until proper safety and toxicological studies are conducted. Federal agency approval of the COVID-19 mRNA vaccines on a blanket-coverage population-wide basis had no support from an honest assessment of all relevant registrational data and commensurate consideration of risks versus benefits. Given the extensive, well-documented SAEs and unacceptably high harm-to-reward ratio, we urge governments to endorse a global moratorium on the modified mRNA products until all relevant questions pertaining to causality, residual DNA, and aberrant protein production are answered.

statements.text: COVID-19 mRNA vaccination is presented as having an unfavorable risk-benefit profile because serious adverse events and other harms are described as outweighing the benefits of preventing COVID-19 symptoms and severe outcomes.
statements.support_summary: Two observational pharmacovigilance analyses using passive reporting systems raise safety concerns but do not directly demonstrate that harms outweigh benefits. A VAERS-based myocarditis study reported substantially increased myocarditis reports after COVID-19 vaccination, especially in young males, with many cases involving emergency care or hospitalization, and concluded there was a serious safety signal (PMID: 38293564). Another VAERS comparison found higher reported serious adverse event rates for COVID-19 vaccines than for influenza, HPV, or monkeypox vaccines in adolescents and young adults, and called for more accurate harm-benefit analyses, especially for male adolescents (PMID: 37377545). However, both studies rely on spontaneous report data and mainly describe adverse-event reporting patterns rather than a formal net risk-benefit analysis, so they only partially support the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: A large body of evidence from randomized trials, systematic reviews/meta-analyses, surveillance studies, and policy reviews consistently states that mRNA COVID-19 vaccines reduce symptomatic and severe COVID-19 while serious adverse events are rare or not meaningfully increased, directly contradicting the claim of an unfavorable risk-benefit profile. A Cochrane review of 41 RCTs found high-certainty evidence that BNT162b2 and mRNA-1273 reduce symptomatic and severe COVID-19, with serious adverse events showing little or no difference versus placebo for mRNA-1273 and uncertain but not clearly increased risk for BNT162b2 (PMID: 36473651). Multiple reviews and official surveillance summaries explicitly conclude that benefits outweigh risks, including ACIP/CDC assessments in adults and adolescents and reviews of neurologic and other adverse events (PMID: 34383735, PMID: 34351881, PMID: 34715347, PMID: 35986451, PMID: 37223456). Additional evidence in children, pregnancy, boosters, and newer mRNA candidates generally reports favorable safety profiles, rare vaccine-related serious adverse events, and protection against hospitalization or severe outcomes (PMID: 37084750, PMID: 36689319, PMID: 39009928, PMID: 35420661, PMID: 35489985, PMID: 38462432, PMID: 35420661). Some contradicting items are less directly about formal risk-benefit balance and instead emphasize safety/effectiveness alone, but the overall direction across abstracts is highly consistent.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. The supporting evidence consists of two passive-reporting analyses that identify potential harms but do not establish that these harms outweigh vaccine benefits, whereas numerous higher-level reviews, trials, and surveillance studies explicitly conclude that mRNA COVID-19 vaccines have favorable benefit-risk profiles and substantially reduce severe COVID-19 outcomes.

statements.text: COVID-19 mRNA vaccination is presented as not having adequate evidence of safety or toxicologic evaluation to support broad population-wide use, including use in children and use as booster injections.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: A large body of contradicting evidence indicates that COVID-19 mRNA vaccines underwent clinical and post-authorization safety evaluation and generally showed acceptable safety profiles, including randomized trials, systematic reviews, and surveillance studies. A Cochrane review of 41 RCTs found high-certainty evidence that several vaccines reduce symptomatic and severe COVID-19 and that serious adverse events were generally similar to placebo or uncertain but not clearly increased for mRNA vaccines (PMID: 36473651). Multiple pediatric studies and reviews reported that in children and adolescents, mRNA vaccines were effective and severe adverse events were rare or not increased, including systematic reviews/meta-analyses in ages 5-11 years and surveillance data in adolescents and children (PMID: 37084750, PMID: 36689319, PMID: 34968370, PMID: 34351881), while pediatric booster/variant-containing studies also reported no new safety concerns (PMID: 36634021, PMID: 38518789, PMID: 35980875). Booster-specific adult evidence likewise reported low rates of severe adverse events after third or fourth mRNA doses and acceptable safety in heterologous or concomitant use settings (PMID: 35420661, PMID: 38761778, PMID: 35114141), and several pharmacovigilance/review papers concluded that serious adverse events are rare and benefits outweigh risks despite acknowledged rare myocarditis or other uncommon events (PMID: 34383735, PMID: 37223456, PMID: 34709664, PMID: 36274082); a few included items are only protocols, recommendations, or partially truncated abstracts and therefore speak more to ongoing evaluation/authorization than direct safety results, which is a minor evidence-quality discrepancy (PMID: 35710572, PMID: 36355612, PMID: 36327162, PMID: 34237049, PMID: 35298454).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall much more contradicted than supported. Across many RCTs, systematic reviews/meta-analyses, and large real-world surveillance studies, the abstracts indicate substantial safety evaluation of COVID-19 mRNA vaccines, including in children and for booster doses, while acknowledging that rare adverse events exist and continued monitoring remains important.

statements.text: COVID-19 mRNA vaccination is presented as being associated with serious harms, including deaths, cancers, cardiac events, and autoimmune, hematologic, reproductive, and neurologic disorders.
statements.support_summary: A secondary analysis of the Pfizer and Moderna phase III randomized trials reported an excess of serious adverse events of special interest with mRNA vaccines versus placebo, with a combined excess risk of 12.5 per 10,000 and risk ratio 1.43, although the abstract does not enumerate all harm categories listed in the claim (PMID: 36055877). A VAERS-based myocarditis study reported markedly increased reporting of myocarditis after COVID-19 vaccination, especially in young males, with many cases requiring emergency care/hospitalization and some deaths, supporting at least the cardiac-event and death components of the claim, though this relies on passive surveillance rather than controlled incidence estimates (PMID: 38293564). Another VAERS analysis in adolescents and young adults found higher reported rates of serious adverse events, including deaths, hospitalizations, and life-threatening illness, for COVID-19 vaccines than for influenza, HPV, or monkeypox vaccines (PMID: 37377545). Overall, the supporting evidence mainly points to excess serious adverse-event signals and myocarditis rather than directly substantiating the full range of cancers, autoimmune, hematologic, reproductive, and neurologic harms named in the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: Most contradicting evidence consists of systematic reviews, randomized trials, and surveillance studies concluding that mRNA COVID-19 vaccines have acceptable safety profiles and that serious adverse events are rare or show little/no increase versus placebo; notably, a Cochrane review of 41 RCTs found little or no difference in serious adverse events for most vaccines and uncertainty rather than clear excess for BNT162b2 (PMID: 36473651). Multiple phase 1-3 trials and booster studies reported mainly mild-to-moderate reactogenicity and no vaccine-related serious adverse events or no new safety concerns in adults, children, and older adults (PMID: 39422261, PMID: 38761778, PMID: 37898410, PMID: 33707061, PMID: 34826381, PMID: 38518789, PMID: 35114141). Large reviews and observational studies in children and pregnancy generally found no concerning safety patterns, no clear increases in serious maternal/fetal outcomes, and rare myocarditis or other special-interest events, while male reproductive safety reviews found little evidence of fertility harm (PMID: 37084750, PMID: 36689319, PMID: 39009928, PMID: 35489985, PMID: 38462432, PMID: 37976132). Some papers acknowledge rare myocarditis, neurologic, hematologic, or cardiovascular events but still conclude they are uncommon and that benefits outweigh risks, creating a discrepancy with any blanket claim of broad serious harms across many organ systems (PMID: 34715347, PMID: 34351881, PMID: 37223456, PMID: 35986451, PMID: 36795118, PMID: 34709664).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. While a few analyses report serious adverse-event signals—especially myocarditis and some excess SAE findings—the much larger body of higher-level evidence from systematic reviews, randomized trials, and broad safety surveillance does not support a broad presentation of mRNA vaccination as being associated with serious harms across deaths, cancers, cardiac, autoimmune, hematologic, reproductive, and neurologic disorders.

matched_article.title: Can DALL-E 3 Reliably Generate 12-Lead ECGs and Teaching Illustrations?
matched_article.abstract: The recent integration of the latest image generation model DALL-E 3 into ChatGPT allows text prompts to easily generate the corresponding images, enabling multimodal output from ChatGPT. We explored the feasibility of DALL-E 3 for drawing a 12-lead ECG and found that it can draw rudimentary 12-lead electrocardiograms (ECG) displaying some of the parameters, although the details are not completely accurate. We also explored DALL-E 3's capacity to create vivid illustrations for teaching resuscitation-related medical knowledge. DALL-E 3 produced accurate CPR illustrations emphasizing proper hand placement and technique. For ECG principles, it produced creative heart-shaped waveforms tying ECGs to the heart. With further training, DALL-E 3 shows promise to expand easy-to-understand visual medical teaching materials and ECG simulations for different disease states. In conclusion, DALL-E 3 has the potential to generate realistic 12-lead ECGs and teaching schematics, but expert validation is still needed.

statements.text: DALL-E 3 can generate rudimentary 12-lead electrocardiograms that include some ECG features, but the rendered ECG details are not fully accurate.

statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.

statements.text: DALL-E 3 has potential to generate useful medical teaching schematics and ECG simulations, but expert validation remains necessary before relying on the generated images for accurate medical use.
statements.support_summary: One study directly evaluating DALL·E 3 for congenital heart disease illustrations found substantial current limitations but still speaks to the educational-use context: among 110 generated images assessed by 33 healthcare professionals, most were judged anatomically incorrect, mislabeled, and unusable for medical education, leading the authors to recommend caution and thorough validation before use (PMID: 38780839). This strongly supports the claim's warning that expert validation is necessary, although it more clearly demonstrates current unreliability than practical usefulness, and it does not address ECG simulations specifically (PMID: 38780839). A second, more indirect study reported that DALL·E-powered image generation may help create realistic pathology-related facial images for medical education and could augment datasets while improving diversity, supporting the broader idea of educational potential (PMID: 38510393). Overall, the supporting evidence mainly backs the combination of possible educational value plus the need for expert review, but there is a scope mismatch because the cited abstracts do not specifically test ECG simulations.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The available evidence suggests AI image generators including DALL·E have potential educational uses, while the strongest directly relevant study emphasizes that expert validation is essential before medical reliance; however, direct evidence for ECG simulation use is limited.

source_article.title: Analysis of the Diagnostic Effect of EUS-RTE on Giant Cystic Tumours of the Oesophagus Based on Cluster Analysis.
source_article.abstract: This paper presents an in-depth analysis and study of the diagnostic effectiveness of EUS-RTE in giant cystic tumours of the oesophagus utilizing cluster analysis. A new form of interval data expression was designed based on the cluster analysis algorithm, as well as a new way of updating the cluster radius and cluster centre. Feature triads are defined, eliminating the need to access all historical data at the time of update. It also prevents the case of overfusion of clusters and outputting only one cluster. If there exist a very low number of clusters, the newly merged clusters are reclustered according to the density clustering method for the internal data objects based on the cluster segmentation so that the data objects in the same cluster have a high similarity as possible. All accumulated electronic files of oesophageal cancer cases were collected and comprehensively organized, and all clinical data of 129 eligible cases with a total of 356 consultations were screened in strict accordance with inclusion and exclusion criteria. A database of oesophageal cancer cases was established using Visual FoxPro software, and frequency distribution, cluster analysis, association rule, and chi-square test were used to focus on mining the association between symptoms, disease mechanisms, prescriptions, and medications. The results were analysed and summarized. Overall, the therapeutic efficacy and safety of the three groups of treatment modalities for gastric mesenchymal tumours were positive, and the preoperative endoscopic treatment modalities should be selected based on the EUS-RTE characteristics of the tumour, the site, and the operator's skill level in a comprehensive manner.

statements.text: Endoscopic ultrasound real-time elastography was diagnostically effective for giant cystic tumours of the oesophagus.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a study about the diagnostic effect of EUS-RTE on giant cystic tumours of the oesophagus, indicating that the original article's findings should not be relied upon (PMID: 38094803). However, the abstract does not present new data showing ineffectiveness; it only states that the prior article was retracted. This creates a discrepancy: the item weakens confidence in prior support for the claim, but it is not direct empirical contradiction from an abstract.
statements.conclusion: The claim is not supported by the provided evidence, but it is also not directly disproven by strong empirical contradictory data. Overall, the evidence is unclear, with confidence reduced mainly because the only related item is a retraction notice rather than a substantive diagnostic study.

statements.text: The therapeutic efficacy and safety of the three treatment modalities for gastric mesenchymal tumours were favorable.
statements.support_summary: One prospective single-center study suggests favorable outcomes for one of the treatment modalities, endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD), in upper gastrointestinal submucosal tumors, including many gastric mesenchymal tumors such as GISTs and leiomyomas (PMID: 26823060). In 224 patients, en bloc resection was achieved in 92.9%, severe complications occurred in 1.8%, residual tumors were seen in 1.3% at 3 months, and no recurrence was reported during 12-month follow-up, supporting ESD as safe and effective (PMID: 26823060). However, this evidence does not directly compare or evaluate all three treatment modalities mentioned in the claim, so it only partially supports the broader statement.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall only weakly supported. The available evidence supports favorable efficacy and safety for ESD specifically, but there is no direct evidence here addressing all three treatment modalities for gastric mesenchymal tumors.

statements.text: Selection of a preoperative endoscopic treatment modality for gastric mesenchymal tumours should be individualized according to tumour characteristics, tumour location, and operator skill level.

statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Computed Tomography Image Processing Analysis in COVID-19 Patient Follow-Up Assessment.
matched_article.abstract: The rapid worldwide spread of the COVID-19 pandemic has infected patients around the world in a short space of time. Chest computed tomography (CT) images of patients who are infected with COVID-19 can offer early diagnosis and efficient forecast monitoring at a low cost. The diagnosis of COVID-19 on CT in an automated way can speed up many tasks and the application of medical treatments. This can help complement reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) diagnosis. The aim of this work is to develop a system that automatically identifies ground-glass opacity (GGO) and pulmonary infiltrates (PIs) on CT images from patients with COVID-19. The purpose is to assess the disease progression during the patient's follow-up assessment and evaluation. We propose an efficient methodology that incorporates oversegmentation mean shift followed by superpixel-SLIC (simple linear iterative clustering) algorithm on CT images with COVID-19 for pulmonary parenchyma segmentation. To identify the pulmonary parenchyma, we described each superpixel cluster according to its position, grey intensity, second-order texture, and spatial-context-saliency features to classify by a tree random forest (TRF). Second, by applying the watershed segmentation to the mean-shift clusters, only pulmonary parenchyma segmentation-identified zones showed GGO and PI based on the description of each watershed cluster of its position, grey intensity, gradient entropy, second-order texture, Euclidean position to the border region of the PI zone, and global saliency features, after using TRF. Our classification results for pulmonary parenchyma identification on CT images with COVID-19 had a precision of over 92% and recall of over 92% on twofold cross validation. For GGO, the PI identification showed 96% precision and 96% recall on twofold cross validation.

statements.text: Automated analysis of chest CT images in patients with COVID-19 can identify ground-glass opacity and pulmonary infiltrates to support follow-up assessment of disease progression.
statements.support_summary: Supporting evidence consistently shows that automated or AI-based chest CT analysis can detect, segment, and quantify COVID-19 lung abnormalities including ground-glass opacities, consolidations, and infected regions, often with good agreement to radiologists or manual annotation (e.g., PMID: 32789756, PMID: 33757431, PMID: 34364263, PMID: 32617690, PMID: 32666395). Several studies go beyond detection and specifically assess longitudinal change or follow-up utility, reporting automated quantification of progression on serial CTs, heatmaps of interval change, opacity/infection volume trends, and severity/prognostic stratification during the disease course (PMID: 32789756, PMID: 32683550, PMID: 34581706, PMID: 32524223). Additional studies support quantification of GGO/consolidation volumes or lesion subtypes as objective markers of severity or outcome, though some are more focused on diagnosis/prognosis than follow-up progression per se (PMID: 33206301, PMID: 33047295, PMID: 33197638, PMID: 34865622). A few items in the support set are only indirectly relevant or not COVID-specific, such as general CT feature descriptions without automation or older non-COVID GGO segmentation work, which weakens directness despite thematic relevance (PMID: 32538893, PMID: 22089834). Overall, the dominant theme is that automated CT analysis can identify GGO/pulmonary infiltrative abnormalities and can support monitoring of disease evolution in COVID-19.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Multiple COVID-19 chest CT studies directly report automated identification and quantification of GGO/infectious opacities and show usefulness for serial assessment of severity or progression, while no direct contradictory evidence was provided.

statements.text: Computed tomography image processing can achieve high accuracy for identifying pulmonary parenchyma involvement and for detecting ground-glass opacity and pulmonary infiltrates in patients with COVID-19.
statements.support_summary: Most supporting studies report that CT-based image processing, especially deep learning segmentation/classification methods, can identify COVID-19 lung involvement with high performance, including strong detection of ground-glass opacities (GGO), consolidations, and infected parenchyma; reported metrics are often high, such as MinIP reconstruction accuracy for GGO of 99.1% (PMID: 33360269), lesion/lung segmentation Dice >0.85 with accuracies >0.95 (PMID: 33046116), and lesion segmentation accuracies around 97–99% in some AI systems (PMID: 34364263). Several studies also found automated CT analysis comparable to radiologists or useful for severity/progression quantification, including superior or similar diagnostic performance to less experienced readers (PMID: 32740817, PMID: 32617690, PMID: 33757431). Recurring themes are automated segmentation of infected lung, quantification of GGO/consolidation volumes, and accurate classification of COVID-19 versus other pneumonias using CT-derived features (PMID: 33705321, PMID: 32666395, PMID: 33225476). However, many supportive items are indirect because they emphasize COVID-19 classification or severity assessment rather than explicitly validating accuracy for GGO/pulmonary infiltrate detection, and some are reviews, feasibility studies, or small datasets rather than robust external validations. Overall, the abstracts largely support that CT image processing can accurately identify pulmonary parenchymal involvement and common COVID-19 lesion patterns, especially GGO.
statements.contradict_summary: One contradictory study found substantial inconsistency across three commercial CT quantification software tools for COVID-19 pneumonia, with segmentation failures in 7.4% to 37.7% of cases and poor-to-moderate agreement for residual healthy lung, GGO, and consolidation measurements (ICC 0.17-0.51), suggesting that accuracy is not uniformly high across platforms (PMID: 32971756). The same study concluded that although computer-aided quantification is feasible for severity stratification, major variability among tools must be considered, and InferRead showed low correlation with radiological severity (PMID: 32971756). This mainly contradicts the generalizability and consistency of the claim rather than proving CT image processing is broadly inaccurate.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. There are many abstracts reporting high-performing CT processing methods for detecting COVID-19 lung abnormalities, including GGO and infected parenchyma, whereas the main opposing evidence points to variability between commercial software tools rather than a consistent failure of CT image processing overall.

source_article.title: RETRACTED: Maternal nutrient restriction predisposes ventricular remodeling in adult sheep offspring.
source_article.abstract: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (https://www.elsevier.com/about/our-business/policies/article-withdrawal). This article has been retracted at the request of the Editor-in-Chief. The Journal of Nutritional Biochemistry and Editor have been informed by the University of Wyoming's Research Integrity Officer that the University conducted an examination of selected publications of Dr. Ren's under the direction of the HHS Office of Research Integrity. Based on the findings of this examination, the University of Wyoming recommended retraction of this paper, due to concerns regarding data irregularities inconsistent with published conclusions. Specifically, the University found evidence of data irregularities and image reuse in Figures 3, 5, and 6 that significantly affect the results and conclusions reported in the manuscript.

matched_article.title: Effects of ALA, EPA and DHA in high-carbohydrate, high-fat diet-induced metabolic syndrome in rats.
matched_article.abstract: We compared the cardiovascular, hepatic and metabolic responses to individual dietary n-3 fatty acids (α-linolenic acid, ALA; eicosapentaenoic acid, EPA; and docosahexaenoic acid, DHA) in a high-carbohydrate, high-fat diet-induced model of metabolic syndrome in rats. Additionally, we measured fatty acid composition of plasma, adipose tissue, liver, heart and skeletal muscle in these rats. The same dosages of ALA and EPA/DHA produced different physiological responses to decrease the risk factors for metabolic syndrome. ALA did not reduce total body fat but induced lipid redistribution away from the abdominal area and favorably improved glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, dyslipidemia, hypertension and left ventricular dimensions, contractility, volumes and stiffness. EPA and DHA increased sympathetic activation, reduced the abdominal adiposity and total body fat and attenuated insulin sensitivity, dyslipidemia, hypertension and left ventricular stiffness but not glucose tolerance. However, ALA, EPA and DHA all reduced inflammation in both the heart and the liver, cardiac fibrosis and hepatic steatosis. These effects were associated with complete suppression of stearoyl-CoA desaturase 1 activity. Since the physiological responses to EPA and DHA were similar, it is likely that the effects are mediated by DHA with EPA serving as a precursor. Also, ALA supplementation increased DHA concentrations but induced different physiological responses to EPA and DHA. This result strongly suggests that ALA has independent effects in metabolic syndrome, not relying on its metabolism to DHA.

statements.text: In rats with high-carbohydrate, high-fat diet-induced metabolic syndrome, dietary alpha-linolenic acid improved glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and left ventricular structure and function while redistributing body fat away from the abdomen without reducing total body fat.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a closely matched rat study showing that α-linolenic acid-rich flaxseed oil in high-carbohydrate, high-fat diet-induced metabolic syndrome improved glucose tolerance, systolic blood pressure, and left ventricular structure/function and caused lipid redistribution away from the abdomen; the abstract also notes reduced plasma triglycerides and nonesterified fatty acids, while only oleic acid increased whole-body adiposity, which is broadly consistent with redistribution without overall fat loss (PMID: 23333092). Additional rat and mouse studies support parts of the claim by reporting improved insulin sensitivity or glucose tolerance with ALA in high-fat/high-fructose settings, though they do not cover the full phenotype, especially cardiac remodeling and blood pressure (PMID: 26743332, PMID: 18806101, PMID: 30379219, PMID: 21281526). Reviews also describe n-3 PUFA/ALA as improving insulin sensitivity and metabolic syndrome mechanisms, but these are indirect and not specific to this exact rat model or all claimed outcomes (PMID: 25841249, PMID: 22332072, PMID: 34553434). Overall, support is driven mainly by one highly relevant primary study with several partially aligned mechanistic or related-model studies.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly comparative rather than directly refuting benefit versus no benefit. One study found ALA less effective than EPA/DHA for improving insulin sensitivity in rats, but this does not show that ALA itself was ineffective in the specific high-carbohydrate, high-fat metabolic syndrome model of the claim (PMID: 18562792). A chia-seed study in a very similar rat model actually agreed on improved glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, and visceral fat redistribution, but it reported no change in plasma lipids or blood pressure, creating a partial discrepancy with the claim's dyslipidemia and hypertension components (PMID: 21429727). Another high-fat rat study concluded linseed oil had no isolated effects while EPA/DHA were more effective, but this involved a different diet/model and shorter treatment context, so its contradiction is indirect (PMID: 35091082).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is because one directly relevant primary rat study closely matches the claim and reports most of the stated benefits, while the contradicting studies mainly show weaker or absent effects in different models or only indicate that ALA is less effective than EPA/DHA rather than disproving benefit altogether.

statements.text: In rats with high-carbohydrate, high-fat diet-induced metabolic syndrome, dietary eicosapentaenoic acid and docosahexaenoic acid reduced abdominal adiposity and total body fat but increased sympathetic activation and did not improve glucose tolerance, although they improved some cardiovascular and metabolic risk factors.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Several animal studies report that EPA and/or DHA improve glycemic control or insulin sensitivity, which conflicts with the claim that they did not improve glucose tolerance: this is seen in high-fat-diet mice for EPA/DHA with rosiglitazone (PMID: 19277604), EPA alone improving glucose tolerance while DHA did not in a Western-diet model (PMID: 27307576), obese mice with restored insulin sensitivity after EPA/DHA (PMID: 23349501), db/db mice with attenuated hyperglycemia and insulin resistance after either fatty acid (PMID: 34507608), and rats with improved insulin sensitivity and lower glucose/insulin after EPA or DHA versus ALA (PMID: 18562792). Other contradictory studies challenge the body-fat component of the claim, finding no effect on body weight or fat depots despite metabolic benefits, including no body-weight change in obese/diabetic mice (PMID: 32298529; PMID: 34507608), no change in perigonadal fat with omega-3s alone in rats (PMID: 27418399), and no clear metabolic improvement in obese male rats (PMID: 34605386). A notable discrepancy within the contradictory set is that some evidence is mixed rather than purely contradictory: one study found EPA but not DHA limited fat mass and improved glucose tolerance (PMID: 27307576), which only partially opposes a combined EPA/DHA claim. None of these abstracts address the claim's sympathetic activation finding directly, so contradiction mainly concerns glucose tolerance/insulin sensitivity and adiposity outcomes rather than autonomic effects.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more contradicted than supported because multiple animal abstracts report improved insulin sensitivity, hyperglycemia, or glucose tolerance with EPA and/or DHA, and several also fail to confirm reduced adiposity. However, the contradiction is not complete because many studies are in mice rather than the specific rat model, and the sympathetic activation component is not directly addressed.

statements.text: In rats with high-carbohydrate, high-fat diet-induced metabolic syndrome, alpha-linolenic acid, eicosapentaenoic acid, and docosahexaenoic acid all reduced inflammation, cardiac fibrosis, and hepatic steatosis, with these benefits associated with suppression of stearoyl-CoA desaturase 1 activity.
statements.support_summary: Direct support for the claim comes mainly from rat diet-induced metabolic syndrome studies using ALA-rich chia seed, which reduced hepatic steatosis as well as cardiac and hepatic inflammation/fibrosis, and was accompanied by depletion of stearoyl-CoA desaturase-1 products consistent with suppressed SCD1 activity (PMID: 21429727). A longer-term rat study in the same high-carbohydrate, high-fat model also found chia seed normalized chronic low-grade inflammation and attenuated liver/cardiovascular changes, although SCD1 was not directly reported in that abstract (PMID: 22436699). For EPA/DHA, several rodent studies reported reduced fatty liver, inflammatory markers, and/or fibrosis: EPA/DHA lowered hepatic triglycerides and delayed fatty liver in mice (PMID: 26193881), reduced serum TNF-α, liver fat, microsteatosis, and hepatic collagen deposition in high-fat-fed rats (PMID: 35091082), and in high-carbohydrate, high-fat rats reduced cardiac inflammatory-cell infiltration/collagen deposition and hepatic lipid accumulation/inflammation (PMID: 29794984). EPA alone also attenuated cardiac fibrosis and inflammation in a rat metabolic syndrome model (PMID: 27708849), while ALA alone reduced hepatic steatosis and inflammatory cytokines in mice (PMID: 30379219); however, most of these studies are indirect because they do not test all three fatty acids together in the exact rat model or directly link benefits to SCD1 suppression.
statements.contradict_summary: One mouse study directly conflicts with the anti-inflammatory portion of the claim for EPA, finding that although EPA normalized hepatic SCD1 mRNA and reduced cholesteryl ester accumulation, it worsened liver injury, inflammatory gene responses, and inflammatory-cell exudation in a cholate-supplemented high-fat diet model (PMID: 23665937). Another mouse study argues against ALA being independently sufficient to prevent hepatic steatosis, concluding that EPA/DHA rather than ALA itself were critical for lowering liver triglycerides, with flax/ALA showing weaker or intermediate effects despite some lipogenic gene changes including Scd1 suppression (PMID: 39303984). Both contradictory studies are in mice and use dietary contexts different from the specific high-carbohydrate, high-fat rat metabolic syndrome model, so their contradiction is partial rather than decisive for the exact claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence is the ALA-rich chia rat study in the matching high-carbohydrate, high-fat model showing reduced inflammation, fibrosis, steatosis, and changes consistent with SCD1 suppression (PMID: 21429727), while EPA/DHA benefits are supported by several related rodent studies; however, contradiction remains because some mouse data show EPA can worsen hepatic inflammation and that ALA may be less effective than EPA/DHA for steatosis prevention.

source_article.title: Clinical Analysis of 85 Cases of External Auditory Canal Cholesteatoma Surgery under Specialized Endoscopy.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: To investigate the clinical characteristics, surgical experience, and surgical outcomes of external auditory canal cholesteatoma (EACC) surgery under endoscopic otolaryngoscopy. METHODS: A retrospective analysis of 85 EACC cases admitted to the Department of Otolaryngology, Renji Hospital, Shanghai Jiaotong University School of Medicine, from January 2016 to February 2021 was performed, followed by retrospective analysis of clinical data to explore the feasibility and clinical characteristics of all-oral endoscopic EACC surgery. A total of 85 EACC patients (90 ears) with a mean age of 49.93 ± 14.87 years were included in the study. According to Udayabhanu staging, 43 ears (47.78%) were stage I, 40 ears (44.44%) were stage II, and 7 ears (7.78%) were stage III. All patients underwent transendoscopic surgery. RESULTS: 79 ears (87.78%) underwent endoscopic EACC resection alone (+external auditory canal tumor resection/tympanostomy tube insertion), 9 ears (10%) underwent endoscopic EACC resection+tympanostomy+tympanoplasty, 1 ear (1.11%) underwent endoscopic EACC resection+tympanoplasty, and 2 ears (2.22%) underwent EACC resection+otolaryngotomy+tympanoplasty+auditory chain reconstruction endoscopically. Of these, 7 ears (7.78%) underwent auricular cartilage-chondroplasty and 2 ears (2.22%) underwent auricular cartilage membrane repair. All patients were reviewed at 1 week, 2 weeks, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year postoperatively. One patient with stage II external auditory atresia had a recurrence after 6 months and underwent endoscopic ear surgery (ESS) again. One patient with stage 2 atresia recurred after 1 year and again underwent endoscopic ear surgery. The rest of the patients recovered well after the surgery, and the grafts healed well. CONCLUSION: EACC surgery through the external ear canal under a dedicated endoscope is a safe, reliable, and effective method. Patients with stage I and II external auditory canal cholesteatoma surgery under endoscopy have a rapid postoperative recovery with significant hearing improvement, and stage IIIA patients can also achieve good results under strict evaluation of indications.

statements.text: Endoscopic surgery through the external ear canal for external auditory canal cholesteatoma is safe, reliable, and effective.
statements.support_summary: Direct evidence for external auditory canal cholesteatoma (EACC) comes from retrospective case series showing favorable outcomes with transcanal endoscopic surgery: all nine stage III EACC cases were successfully treated with shorter median healing time than historical open approaches in one study (PMID: 28346290), and 96% of 23 advanced stage III-IV cases achieved a dry, self-cleaning canal after a mean 15-month follow-up in another, which explicitly concluded TEES was feasible and safe (PMID: 34766954). A related series using transcanal underwater endoscopic bone resection for cholesteatoma extending to the antrum/mastoid reported no severe intra/postoperative complications except one secondary labyrinthitis and only two residual cases on follow-up, supporting safety and efficiency for extensive disease, though this was not limited specifically to EACC (PMID: 32947491). Additional weaker support includes a case report of combined underwater endoscopic/microscopic surgery suggesting good visualization and successful removal without canalplasty for EACC (PMID: 34875889). Several other SUPPORT items concern middle ear or congenital cholesteatoma rather than EACC specifically (PMID: 28543174, PMID: 28688558, PMID: 36825840, PMID: 31377903, PMID: 27346682), and one older surgical series on EACC does not clearly describe endoscopic surgery (PMID: 19444659), so these are only indirect or discrepant support for the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided. No abstract in the supplied set directly reports that transcanal endoscopic surgery for EACC is unsafe, unreliable, or ineffective.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest evidence consists of two EACC-specific retrospective series with good healing and disease-control outcomes and explicit claims of feasibility/safety, although the overall evidence base is still limited by small, non-comparative studies and several only indirectly relevant cholesteatoma papers.

statements.text: Patients with stage I and stage II external auditory canal cholesteatoma treated with endoscopic surgery have rapid postoperative recovery and significant hearing improvement.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Patients with stage IIIA external auditory canal cholesteatoma can also achieve good outcomes with endoscopic surgery when indications are carefully selected.
