statements.support_summary: One retrospective case series reported that nine patients with unilateral Naim stage III external auditory canal cholesteatoma were successfully treated using solely transcanal endoscopic ear surgery, with a median healing time of 23.8 days and no failures described in the abstract (PMID: 28346290). The authors concluded that transcanal endoscopic surgery is a valid treatment option for EACC up to Naim stage III and suggested this stage is an ideal target for endoscopic management (PMID: 28346290). This supports the claim indirectly because stage IIIA is not explicitly isolated in the abstract, so applicability to stage IIIA specifically depends on whether it is considered within the broader stage III group.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because the available abstract describes favorable outcomes for endoscopic surgery in stage III EACC and explicitly endorses its use up to that stage (PMID: 28346290). However, the evidence is limited to a small retrospective case series and does not specifically analyze stage IIIA patients, so support is moderate rather than strong.

matched_article.title: Application Values of 2D and 3D Radiomics Models Based on CT Plain Scan in Differentiating Benign from Malignant Ovarian Tumors.
matched_article.abstract: Accurate identification of ovarian tumors as benign or malignant is highly crucial. Radiomics is a new branch of imaging that has emerged in recent years to replace the traditional naked eye qualitative diagnosis. This study is aimed at exploring the difference in the application potential of two- (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) radiomics models based on CT plain scan in differentiating benign from malignant ovarian tumors. A retrospective analysis was performed on 140 patients with ovarian tumors confirmed by surgery and pathology in our hospital from July 2017 to August 2020. These 140 patients were divided into benign group and malignant group according to the pathological results. The ITK-SNAP software was used to outline the regions-of-interest (ROI) of 2D or 3D tumors on the CT plain scan image of each patient; the texture features were extracted through analysis kit (AK), and the cases were randomly divided into training groups (n = 99) and validation group (n = 41) in a ratio of 7 : 3. The least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) algorithm was used to perform dimensionality reduction, followed by the construction of the radiomics nomogram model using the logistic regression method. The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve was drawn, and the calibration curve and decision curve analysis (DCA) were used to evaluate and verify the results of the radiomics nomogram and compare the differences between 2D and 3D diagnostic performance. There were 396 quantitative radiomics feature parameters extracted from 2D group and the 3D group, respectively. The area under the curve (AUC) of the radiomics nomogram of the 2D training group and the validation group were 0.96 and 0.97, respectively. The accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity of the training set were 92.9%, 88.9%, and 96.3%, respectively, and those of the validation set were 90.2%, 82.6%, and 100.0%, respectively. The AUCs of the radiomics nomogram of the 3D training group and validation group were 0.96% and 0.99%, respectively. The accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity of the training set were 92.9%, 96.3%, and 88.9%, respectively, and those of the validation set were 97.6%, 95.7%, and 100.0%, respectively. DeLong's test indicated that there was no statistical significance between the two sets (P > 0.05). For the differential diagnosis of benign and malignant ovarian tumors, the 2D and 3D radiomics nomogram models exhibited comparable diagnostic performance. Considering that the 2D model was cost-effective and time-efficient, it was more recommended to use 2D features in future research.

statements.text: In patients with ovarian tumors evaluated on CT plain scans, both 2D and 3D radiomics nomogram models can differentiate benign from malignant ovarian tumors with high diagnostic performance.
statements.support_summary: Several CT-based radiomics studies support that ovarian tumors can be differentiated as benign versus malignant with good diagnostic performance, especially on plain CT. A 2D plain-CT radiomics study reported strong discrimination and a nomogram with AUCs of 0.95-0.96 in training/test sets plus external validation AUC 0.95, directly aligning with the claim for 2D nomogram performance (PMID: 33888749). Additional plain-CT/CT-based studies found radiomics nomograms or models to be reliable for distinguishing benign from early-stage malignant tumors, with nomograms outperforming traditional models in some cohorts (PMID: 38115095), and 3D CT radiomics with machine learning also showed effective external-test discrimination across benign, borderline, and early malignant tumors, though not specifically a nomogram and not limited to benign vs malignant only (PMID: 38343232). Other supportive but less direct studies used CT radiomics/texture or AI features and also found useful discrimination between benign and malignant ovarian tumors, though some involved contrast-enhanced CT, texture analysis rather than explicit radiomics nomograms, or broader class distinctions, making them indirect support or a scope mismatch rather than exact confirmation of both 2D and 3D nomogram models (PMID: 37093321, PMID: 36688472, PMID: 38746030).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest direct evidence is for a 2D plain-CT radiomics nomogram with high diagnostic performance (PMID: 33888749), while evidence for 3D CT radiomics is supportive but somewhat less exact because some studies used machine-learning models rather than explicit nomograms or addressed broader tumor classification settings.

statements.text: In patients with ovarian tumors evaluated on CT plain scans, 2D radiomics nomogram performance is comparable to 3D radiomics nomogram performance for distinguishing benign from malignant ovarian tumors.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: For differentiating benign from malignant ovarian tumors on CT plain scans, a 2D radiomics model may be preferable because it is more cost-effective and time-efficient than a 3D radiomics model.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: CircPSMC3 suppresses the proliferation and metastasis of gastric cancer by acting as a competitive endogenous RNA through sponging miR-296-5p.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: Circular RNAs (circRNAs) are a class of non-coding RNAs with a loop structure, but its functions remain largely unknown. Growing evidence has revealed that circRNAs play a striking role as functional RNAs in the progression of malignant disease. However, the precise role of circRNAs in gastric cancer (GC) remains unclear. METHODS: CircRNAs were determined by human circRNA array analysis and quantitative reverse transcription polymerase reaction. Luciferase reporter, RNA pull down, and fluorescence in situ hybridization assays were employed to test the interaction between circPSMC3 and miR-296-5p. Ectopic over-expression and siRNA-mediated knockdown of circPSMC3, proliferation, migration and invasion in vitro, and in vivo experiment of metastasis were used to evaluate the function of circPSMC3. RESULTS: CircPSMC3 rather than liner PSMC3 mRNA was down-regulated in GC tissues, corresponding plasmas from GC patients as well as GC cell lines compared to normal controls. Lower circPSMC3 expression in GC patients was correlated with higher TNM stage and shorter overall survival. Over-expression of circPSMC3 and miR-296-5p inhibitor could inhibit the tumorigenesis of gastric cancer cells in vivo and vitro whereas co-transfection of circPSMC3 and miRNA-296-5p could counteract this effect. Importantly, we demonstrated that circPSMC3 could act as a sponge of miR-296-5p to regulate the expression of Phosphatase and Tensin Homolog (PTEN), and further suppress the tumorigenesis of gastric cancer cells. CONCLUSION: Our study reveals that circPSMC3 can serve as a novel potential circulating biomarker for detection of GC. CircPSMC3 participates in progression of gastric cancer by sponging miRNA-296-5p with PTEN, providing a new insight into the treatment of gastric cancer.

statements.text: In gastric cancer, higher circPSMC3 expression suppresses cancer cell proliferation, migration, invasion, and metastasis, whereas lower circPSMC3 expression is associated with more aggressive disease and poorer survival.
statements.support_summary: The only supporting item is a correction notice rather than a substantive abstract, so it provides minimal direct evidence from the abstract itself; however, its title states that circPSMC3 suppresses gastric cancer proliferation and metastasis by acting as a competing endogenous RNA that sponges miR-296-5p (PMID: 32907590). This is consistent with the claim that higher circPSMC3 expression inhibits malignant behaviors in gastric cancer. The abstract does not provide direct details on migration, invasion, clinicopathologic aggressiveness, or survival, so support for those parts of the claim is indirect and limited.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but the evidence provided here is weak because it consists only of a correction notice with little abstract-level detail. The title supports a tumor-suppressive role for circPSMC3 in gastric cancer, but the survival and aggressive-disease aspects are not directly documented in the supplied evidence.

statements.text: In gastric cancer cells, circPSMC3 suppresses tumor progression by acting as a competing endogenous RNA that sponges miR-296-5p and increases PTEN expression.
statements.support_summary: The only provided supporting item is a correction notice to the original CircPSMC3 paper rather than a study abstract with results, so the abstract itself does not present direct experimental evidence (PMID: 32907590). Based on the title, it is consistent with circPSMC3 suppressing gastric cancer proliferation and metastasis by acting as a competitive endogenous RNA that sponges miR-296-5p (PMID: 32907590). However, the abstract does not mention PTEN, so this evidence does not substantiate the claim's PTEN upregulation component. Overall, the support is limited and indirect because it relies mainly on the title of a correction notice rather than reported findings in the abstract.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The available evidence is directionally consistent with circPSMC3 suppressing gastric cancer progression via miR-296-5p sponging, but the only cited item is a correction notice and it does not provide abstract-level evidence for increased PTEN expression.

statements.text: In gastric cancer patients, circPSMC3 has potential value as a circulating biomarker for disease detection.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: METTL3 facilitates tumor progression via an m6A-IGF2BP2-dependent mechanism in colorectal carcinoma.
matched_article.abstract: Colorectal carcinoma (CRC) is one of the most common malignant tumors, and its main cause of death is tumor metastasis. RNA N6-methyladenosine (m6A) is an emerging regulatory mechanism for gene expression and methyltransferase-like 3 (METTL3) participates in tumor progression in several cancer types. However, its role in CRC remains unexplored. Western blot, quantitative real-time PCR (RT-qPCR) and immunohistochemical (IHC) were used to detect METTL3 expression in cell lines and patient tissues. Methylated RNA immunoprecipitation sequencing (MeRIP-seq) and transcriptomic RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) were used to screen the target genes of METTL3. The biological functions of METTL3 were investigated in vitro and in vivo. RNA pull-down and RNA immunoprecipitation assays were conducted to explore the specific binding of target genes. RNA stability assay was used to detect the half-lives of the downstream genes of METTL3. Using TCGA database, higher METTL3 expression was found in CRC metastatic tissues and was associated with a poor prognosis. MeRIP-seq revealed that SRY (sex determining region Y)-box 2 (SOX2) was the downstream gene of METTL3. METTL3 knockdown in CRC cells drastically inhibited cell self-renewal, stem cell frequency and migration in vitro and suppressed CRC tumorigenesis and metastasis in both cell-based models and PDX models. Mechanistically, methylated SOX2 transcripts, specifically the coding sequence (CDS) regions, were subsequently recognized by the specific m6A "reader", insulin-like growth factor 2 mRNA binding protein 2 (IGF2BP2), to prevent SOX2 mRNA degradation. Further, SOX2 expression positively correlated with METTL3 and IGF2BP2 in CRC tissues. The combined IHC panel, including "writer", "reader", and "target", exhibited a better prognostic value for CRC patients than any of these components individually. Overall, our study revealed that METTL3, acting as an oncogene, maintained SOX2 expression through an m6A-IGF2BP2-dependent mechanism in CRC cells, and indicated a potential biomarker panel for prognostic prediction in CRC.

statements.text: In colorectal carcinoma, higher METTL3 expression is associated with metastatic disease and poorer prognosis.
statements.support_summary: Multiple CRC-focused studies report that METTL3 is upregulated in tumor tissue and linked to aggressive clinicopathologic features, including distant metastasis/advanced stage and worse survival. The strongest direct clinical evidence is an IHC study of 180 CRC patients showing higher METTL3 in stage III/IV and metastatic disease, with high METTL3 as an independent adverse prognostic factor for overall survival (PMID: 37713887). Other studies found high METTL3 predicted poor prognosis or shorter overall survival and promoted migration, invasion, angiogenesis, EMT, and metastasis in vitro/in vivo, including explicit associations with tumor metastasis or poor survival (PMID: 33411363, PMID: 31492150, PMID: 35567945, PMID: 35012593, PMID: 36348020, PMID: 38605400). Several additional papers support a pro-tumor/metastatic role mechanistically but are less direct on prognosis, and one item appears non-CRC or ambiguously described (PMID: 36710350), so it should be interpreted cautiously. Overall, the dominant theme is that elevated METTL3 in CRC associates with metastatic behavior and poorer outcomes.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and largely indirect for this CRC-specific claim. One study found that myeloid-cell Mettl3 ablation increased tumor growth and metastasis via macrophage reprogramming, implying a context-dependent protective role of METTL3 in immune cells rather than tumor-cell METTL3 expression in CRC patients (PMID: 33654093). Another study reported that predominantly cytoplasmic METTL3 inversely correlated with nodal metastasis across human cancers and examined acetylation-dependent localization, but it was not clearly CRC-specific from the abstract and may not contradict total tumor METTL3 upregulation in colorectal carcinoma (PMID: 36289222). Thus, the main discrepancy is compartment- and cell-type-specific biology rather than direct evidence that high METTL3 in colorectal carcinoma predicts better prognosis.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The evidence includes several CRC-specific studies, with direct clinical associations between higher METTL3 and distant metastasis/shorter survival plus multiple functional studies showing METTL3 promotes invasion and metastasis, whereas the contradictory studies are fewer and reflect different cellular contexts or broader cancer settings rather than direct CRC patient-level refutation.

statements.text: In colorectal carcinoma, METTL3 promotes tumor cell self-renewal, stemness, migration, tumor growth, and metastasis by maintaining SOX2 expression through an m6A-dependent IGF2BP2-mediated mechanism.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support an oncogenic role for METTL3 in colorectal cancer, showing that METTL3 is upregulated and that its depletion suppresses CRC growth-related phenotypes. In particular, METTL3 was identified as an essential factor for CRC progression in CRISPR-based screening and was reported to promote CRC through m6A-dependent pathways (PMID: 33217448); METTL3 also promoted tumorigenesis and metastasis via BMI1-related signaling (PMID: 32621798). However, from the provided abstracts, these studies generally support METTL3-driven proliferation, tumorigenesis, migration, and metastasis rather than specifically confirming the full claim about self-renewal/stemness being mediated by maintenance of SOX2 via IGF2BP2. Many additional listed support items have missing or truncated abstracts, so their relevance to the precise SOX2-IGF2BP2 mechanism cannot be verified from the text provided. Overall, the support side shows a recurring theme that METTL3 acts as a CRC promoter through m6A-dependent post-transcriptional regulation, but direct abstract-level confirmation of the exact SOX2/IGF2BP2 axis is limited.
statements.contradict_summary: Most of the so-called contradicting evidence does not truly refute the overall oncogenic role of METTL3 in CRC; instead, it often supports METTL3-driven malignancy but via different downstream targets. For example, METTL3/YTHDF2 was reported to accelerate CRC tumor formation and metastasis by suppressing YPEL5, with knockdown repressing proliferation and migration and overexpression promoting tumor growth/metastasis (PMID: 33411363), and METTL3 was shown to maintain colon cancer tumorigenicity by suppressing SOCS2, with METTL3 knockout inhibiting proliferation (PMID: 32705223). Another study found that METTL3 promotes 5-FU resistance and that silencing METTL3 suppresses proliferation and migration in CRC cells, again consistent with an oncogenic role rather than contradiction of it (PMID: 35856434). The main genuinely discordant item is a report that METTL3 promotes cellular senescence in CRC through CDKN2B-related regulation (PMID: 38361047), which points to a potentially context-dependent, anti-proliferative role; thus the contradiction is mainly about mechanism and context, not a broad reversal of the phenotype across the literature.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The available abstracts consistently portray METTL3 as promoting CRC progression, migration, tumorigenesis, and metastasis, but the exact claim that this occurs specifically by maintaining SOX2 through an m6A-dependent IGF2BP2 mechanism is only weakly established by the evidence provided here because most studies support alternative downstream mechanisms.

statements.text: In colorectal carcinoma, a prognostic panel combining METTL3, IGF2BP2, and SOX2 provides better prognostic value than any single component alone.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: Risk of urge and stress urinary incontinence at long-term follow-up after vaginal hysterectomy.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: The purpose of this study was to evaluate the prevalence of urinary symptoms at long-term follow-up after vaginal hysterectomy. STUDY DESIGN: One hundred seventeen patients who had a vaginal hysterectomy for menorrhagia from January 1991 to December 2001 answered a self-report questionnaire about de novo urinary symptoms. The control group was a population of 116 patients who had a conservative treatment for dysfunctional uterine bleeding by endometrial thermocoagulation from January 1994 to December 2001. RESULTS: Patient characteristics (mean age, mean parity, menopausal status, smoking status, drink habits) were similar in the 2 groups. Mean follow-up was 4.6+/-2.2 years (range, 1.5-11 years) after vaginal hysterectomy and 4+/-1.8 years (range, 1.5-7 years) after conservative treatment. The prevalence of urinary symptoms, which included urge and stress incontinence, was statistically similar in the 2 groups. CONCLUSION: This study reveals no risk of urge or stress urinary incontinence at long-term follow-up after vaginal hysterectomy, compared with conservative treatment.

statements.text: In women treated for menorrhagia, vaginal hysterectomy was not associated with a higher long-term risk of urge urinary incontinence compared with conservative treatment for dysfunctional uterine bleeding.
statements.support_summary: One directly relevant long-term comparative study found that women who underwent vaginal hysterectomy for menorrhagia had urinary symptom rates, including urge and stress incontinence, that were statistically similar to women treated conservatively for dysfunctional uterine bleeding by endometrial thermocoagulation after about 4-5 years of follow-up (PMID: 16876308). The abstract specifically concludes there was no increased long-term risk of urge or stress urinary incontinence after vaginal hysterectomy versus conservative treatment (PMID: 16876308).
statements.contradict_summary: A large nationwide population-based Taiwanese study reported that hysterectomy was associated with increased risk of de novo lower urinary tract symptoms overall, with the highest risk seen after vaginal hysterectomy, including outcomes such as incontinence and urgency (PMID: 30357471). However, this study compared hysterectomy patients with women who had not undergone hysterectomy rather than with women receiving conservative treatment for dysfunctional uterine bleeding, so its relevance to the specific claim is indirect and represents a population/comparator mismatch (PMID: 30357471).
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The supporting study directly matches the population, intervention, comparator, and long-term urinary outcome in question, whereas the contradictory evidence is broader and less directly comparable because it uses a different control group and outcome framework.

statements.text: In women treated for menorrhagia, vaginal hysterectomy was not associated with a higher long-term risk of stress urinary incontinence compared with conservative treatment for dysfunctional uterine bleeding.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct evidence comes from a comparative long-term follow-up study in women treated for menorrhagia, which found statistically similar prevalence of stress and urge incontinence after vaginal hysterectomy versus conservative treatment by endometrial thermocoagulation, concluding there was no excess long-term risk with vaginal hysterectomy (PMID: 16876308). A Swedish national register analysis also found de novo stress incontinence rates did not differ between hysterectomy approaches, including vaginal hysterectomy, and endometrial destruction controls, although this was broader and less specific to the exact claim (PMID: 16752243). Additional cohort studies reported no significant worsening or de novo urinary incontinence after vaginal hysterectomy or hysterectomy overall, including at 6-12 months and at 10-year follow-up, but these are indirect because they do not specifically compare vaginal hysterectomy with conservative treatment for dysfunctional uterine bleeding (PMID: 14677004, PMID: 28633096, PMID: 28756528). Overall, the support is driven by one directly on-point comparative study and several more general studies suggesting hysterectomy is not a major independent risk factor for stress urinary incontinence.
statements.contradict_summary: One prospective observational study found that among women undergoing hysterectomy for benign conditions, vaginal hysterectomy was an independent prognostic factor for developing de novo stress urinary incontinence at 3 years compared with abdominal hysterectomy (OR 2.3, 95% CI 1.0-5.2) (PMID: 21484363). However, this study does not directly compare hysterectomy with conservative treatment for dysfunctional uterine bleeding, so it contradicts the claim only indirectly. Its findings suggest possible higher stress incontinence risk by surgical route within hysterectomy patients rather than against non-hysterectomy management.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The key reason is that a directly relevant comparative study in women treated for menorrhagia found no higher long-term stress urinary incontinence risk after vaginal hysterectomy versus conservative treatment (PMID: 16876308), whereas the contradictory study compares vaginal with abdominal hysterectomy rather than with conservative treatment.

matched_article.title: The effect of number of throws on knot security with nonidentical sliding knots.
matched_article.abstract: The study was undertaken to test the integrity of nonidentical sliding knots made with 3 throws compared with those made with 6 throws with monofilament and braided absorbable suture. The 3 throw nonidentical sliding knot was compared with the 6 throw nonidentical sliding knot in 4 different suture groups. The groups were 0-0 polydioxanone, 2-0 polydioxanone, 0-0 polyglactin 910, and 2-0 polyglactin 910. Knots were tested to failure with a tensiometer. The proportion of 3 throw knots becoming untied was compared with the 6 throw knot within each group. Ultimate load required to break tied knots within each suture group was also evaluated. The 3 throw knots had very high rates of knot failure and untied significantly more often than the 6 throw knots. The 6 throw nonidentical sliding knot demonstrates superior knot integrity compared with the 3 throw knot with both monofilament and braided absorbable suture.

statements.text: Among nonidentical sliding knots tied with monofilament and braided absorbable sutures, knots made with fewer throws were less secure and untied more often than knots made with more throws.
statements.support_summary: Several in vitro studies consistently report that increasing throw count improves knot security and reduces untying/slippage, with specific evidence that nonidentical or related sliding knots with 5 throws outperform 3-throw versions in absorbable multifilament sutures (PMID: 23144642), and that 4-throw knots untie more often than 5- or 6-throw knots across multiple suture types (PMID: 21338970). A study directly involving sliding knots in monofilament and multifilament materials found 5-throw knots generally stronger than 3-throw knots, with the added throws significantly beneficial in monofilament sutures (PMID: 3016627). Broader knot-security literature also repeatedly shows that more throws improve holding capacity, lower slippage/unraveling, or are required to achieve secure knots, including in laparoscopic and monofilament settings (PMID: 12085155, PMID: 22495855, PMID: 37835063). One item placed in SUPPORT is especially close to the claim despite misgrouping: nonidentical monofilament sliding knots with 6 throws untied frequently whereas 10-throw versions did not differ significantly from parallel knots, explicitly indicating improved security with more throws (PMID: 14749640). Overall, the support is strongest for the general throw-count effect and reasonably direct for nonidentical sliding knots in monofilament and braided/multifilament absorbable sutures, though many studies examine square or other knot types rather than the exact claim configuration.
statements.contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is mixed and partly off-target. Some studies indicate that specific materials or techniques can achieve knot security with fewer throws, such as a monofilament absorbable suture requiring fewer throws than braided Vicryl (PMID: 10167360), coated polyglycolic acid requiring one less throw than coated polyglactin 910 (PMID: 6317984), or heat-welded coated polyglactin achieving security with fewer throws (PMID: 3898890); however, these do not directly show that, within the same nonidentical sliding-knot setup, fewer throws are equally secure. A rat microvascular study reported better patency with two-throw reef knots than traditional three-throw knots (PMID: 38174453), but this concerns reef knots and clinical vessel patency rather than nonidentical sliding knots or untying frequency. One finger-trap catheter study found no performance difference among variants differing in number of throws (PMID: 25671283), but this is a specialized fixation pattern, not the knot type in the claim. Notably, PMID: 14749640 is miscategorized under CONTRADICT because its abstract actually supports the claim by stating that adding throws improved security of nonidentical sliding monofilament knots.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most relevant abstracts show that nonidentical/sliding knots with fewer throws are less reliable and more prone to untying or slippage, especially in monofilament and multifilament absorbable sutures, whereas most contradictory items involve different knot types, special techniques, or comparisons across different suture materials rather than the same knot with fewer versus more throws.

statements.text: Among nonidentical sliding knots tied with monofilament and braided absorbable sutures, knots made with more throws had superior knot integrity than knots made with fewer throws.
statements.support_summary: Directly relevant evidence supports the claim for nonidentical sliding knots: with monofilament suture, adding throws improved nonidentical sliding-knot security, with 6-throw nonidentical knots untying frequently but 10-throw versions not showing this deficit (PMID: 14749640). In synthetic resorbable multifilament/braided materials, adding two throws to 3-throw nonidentical or parallel sliding knots significantly improved reliability, and 5-throw nonidentical sliding knots had superior integrity to 3-throw knots (PMID: 23144642). A broader older sliding-knot study found 5-throw knots were generally stronger than 3-throw knots, although the significant benefit of extra throws was reported mainly for monofilament materials and nonidentical versus parallel knots differed little overall (PMID: 3016627). Many additional, less directly matched studies and reviews across knot types also report that increasing throw count improves knot security up to a threshold, especially for sliding knots and monofilament sutures (e.g., PMID: 12085155, PMID: 21338970, PMID: 37835063), though these are indirect rather than claim-specific. Overall, the main theme is that throw count is an important determinant of knot integrity, with the strongest abstract-level support coming from the monofilament and braided sliding-knot studies.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is mixed and often indirect or not claim-specific. One clinically oriented swine study of braided absorbable surgeon's knots found extra throws did not improve holding capacity once a secure 3-throw knot was achieved and instead increased suture extrusion, especially with 5 throws (PMID: 15301668), but this addresses tissue reaction and surgeon's knots rather than nonidentical sliding knots. Several studies in other knot types reported no added strength beyond a threshold or even favorable outcomes with fewer throws, such as two-throw versus three-throw reef knots in microvascular anastomosis (PMID: 38174453; PMID: 31769498), no performance difference among finger-trap variants with different surgeon's throws (PMID: 25671283), and no significant increase in knot-breaking strength from a fourth throw in Vicryl surgeon's knots (PMID: 28989101). A sliding-knot study in oral/maxillofacial surgery still found sliding knots generally needed 5 throws, but noted no further security gain after 5 throws (PMID: 26979258), which is only a partial contradiction because it does not refute superiority of more versus fewer throws below that threshold. Overall, the contradictory theme is not that fewer throws are usually better for the specific claim, but that benefits of additional throws plateau and depend strongly on knot type, context, and outcome measured.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most claim-relevant abstracts directly show that nonidentical sliding knots in monofilament and braided/resorbable multifilament sutures become more reliable with additional throws (PMID: 14749640; PMID: 23144642), whereas most contradictory studies involve other knot types or show a plateau effect rather than a true reversal.

source_article.title: MiR-181c-5p Mitigates Tumorigenesis in Cervical Squamous Cell Carcinoma via Targeting Glycogen Synthase Kinase 3β Interaction Protein (GSKIP).
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: Cervical cancer (CC) is a highly prevalent cancer and one of the main causes of death among women worldwide. The miR-181 family has turned out to be associated with tumorigenesis in a variety of tumors by regulating the expression of tumor-related genes. However, the mechanisms and biological function of miR-181c-5p in cervical squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) have not been well elucidated. MATERIALS AND METHODS: SiHa cell lines with specific gene overexpression vectors were constructed. Targetscan was used to predict the binding site of miR-181c-5p and GSKIP. MTT assay was used to detect the clone formation rate. Flow cytometry was used to detect the apoptosis rate and to separate the cell markers. The Transwell test was used to detect cell invasion. Immunohistochemistry was used to detect protein expression in tumor tissues. Western Blotting was used to detect the expression levels of related proteins. RESULTS: GSKIP was predicted to be the target gene of miR-181c-5p in cervical SCC. MiR-181c-5p overexpression suppressed SiHa cells proliferation and promoted apoptosis; the protein expressions of Ki67 and PCNA were decreased, but the expressions of Caspase-3 and Bax/Bcl-2 were increased. The overexpression of miR-181c-5p inhibited the stem-like properties of SiHa cells; the expressions of SOX2, OCT4 and CD44 were decreased. Furthermore, miR-181c-5p upregulation limited the invasion of SiHa cells; the expression of E-cadherin was higher, but the expressions of N-cadherin and Vimentin were lower. MiR-181c-5p overexpression inhibited tumorigenesis in cervical SCC tissues; the expressions of Ki67, Caspase-3, CD44 and Vimentin in vivo were consistent with those in vitro. CONCLUSION: Taken together, miR-181c-5p was able to mitigate the cancer cell characteristic and invasive properties of cervical SCC through targeting GSKIP gene.

statements.text: In cervical squamous cell carcinoma, increased miR-181c-5p expression suppresses cancer cell proliferation and promotes apoptosis.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only cited contradicting study is in epithelial ovarian cancer, not cervical squamous cell carcinoma, so its relevance to the claim is indirect and represents a cross-cancer discrepancy rather than direct contradiction. In that study, miR-181c-5p packaged in hypoxic tumor-derived extracellular vesicles promoted M2 macrophage polarization and was associated with increased ovarian cancer cell proliferation, migration, invasion, tumor growth, and metastasis, which is directionally opposite to the claim that higher miR-181c-5p suppresses proliferation (PMID: 36027869). However, the abstract does not evaluate apoptosis in cervical squamous cell carcinoma specifically, limiting how strongly it contradicts the stated claim.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear but leans against the claim because there is no direct supporting evidence provided, and the only available study shows a pro-tumor association for miR-181c-5p in a different cancer type (PMID: 36027869). Since that study is not in cervical squamous cell carcinoma, it weakens confidence in drawing a direct contradiction.

statements.text: In cervical squamous cell carcinoma, increased miR-181c-5p expression reduces stem-like properties and invasive capacity of cancer cells.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that extracellular vesicle miR-181c-5p from hypoxic epithelial ovarian cancer cells promotes M2 macrophage polarization and, in turn, enhances proliferation, migration, invasion, growth, and metastasis of ovarian cancer cells in vitro and in vivo (PMID: 36027869). However, this is an ovarian cancer/microenvironment study rather than a cervical squamous cell carcinoma study, so it is only an indirect contradiction to the claim. The main discrepancy is disease context: the abstract suggests a pro-tumor, pro-invasive role for miR-181c-5p, but it does not directly test stem-like properties or invasive capacity in cervical squamous cell carcinoma.
statements.conclusion: The claim is not supported by the provided evidence and is only weakly contradicted overall. The sole available study points in the opposite biological direction for miR-181c-5p, but because it is in epithelial ovarian cancer rather than cervical squamous cell carcinoma, the evidence against the claim is limited and indirect.

statements.text: In cervical squamous cell carcinoma, miR-181c-5p mitigates tumorigenic behavior by targeting GSKIP.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a paper with the same claim in its title, indicating that the underlying article's findings should no longer be considered reliable (PMID: 39140083). The abstract provides no experimental data beyond stating that it retracts the original article, so this is evidence about reliability rather than direct biological falsification. This creates a discrepancy in that the item does not independently test the claim, but it substantially undermines confidence in the original reported association.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported because there is no supporting evidence here and the only available record is a retraction notice for the article asserting the claim (PMID: 39140083). However, the evidence is limited, since the retraction does not itself provide direct experimental refutation.

matched_article.title: Down-Regulation of USP8 Suppresses HER-3 Positive Gastric Cancer Cells Proliferation.
matched_article.abstract: Ubiquitin specific peptidase 8 (USP8) has been reported to induce the degradation of several receptor tyrosine kinases such as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), among which human epidermal growth factor receptor-3 (HER-3) is one of them. However, the role and functional mechanisms of USP8 and HER-3 in gastric cancer (GC) remain unknown. To explore the function and mechanism of USP8 and HER-3 in the progression of GC. Eighty-eight patients with histologically confirmed GC were recruited for this study. Tumor samples and GC cell lines were used to detect USP8 and HER-3 levels. MGC803 (HER-3 negative GC cell) was selected as the control group and NCI-N87, MKN-45 and AGS (HER-3 positive GC cells) as the experimental group. USP8i and si-RNA were then used to down-regulate USP8 in each group. Apoptosis and cell-cycle experiments were performed to detect the effects of USP8 on GC cells. Cytotoxicity Assay Kit (MTT) and colony formation assays were used to analyze cell proliferation. Cell migration and invasion ability were examined by wound healing. The expression of related mRNA and protein was detected by Western blot and quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR). In vivo experiments were used to examine the effect of USP8 and HER-3. Patients with high expression of USP8 or HER-3 tumors alone died earlier than those with low expression and the patients with both USP8 and HER-3 high expression had a shorter overall survival than those with the opposite pattern (both USP8 and HER-3 low expression). Down-regulation of USP8 inhibited cell proliferation and cell metastasis and also reduced the HER-3 expression. We also observed that down-regulation of USP8 inhibited the proliferation of GC cells which highly expressed HER-3. Moreover, down-regulation of USP8 could promote the apoptosis of HER3-positive GC cells and inhibit the proliferation of them by affecting the cell-cycle. In vivo studies demonstrated that down-regulation of USP8 inhibited HER-3 positive tumors growth. Down-regulation of USP8 inhibits HER-3 positive GC cells proliferation in vivo and in vitro, which indicate that USP8 represents a feasible choice as a therapeutic target for HER-3 positive GC cells.

statements.text: In gastric cancer, down-regulation of USP8 inhibits proliferation, migration, and invasion of HER-3–positive cancer cells and reduces HER-3 expression.
statements.support_summary: The only provided supporting item is a corrigendum/erratum rather than a study abstract, and it contains no experimental results beyond indicating a correction to the original article on USP8 down-regulation in HER-3-positive gastric cancer cells (PMID: 34848972). Because the abstract does not report data, it does not directly substantiate the claim that USP8 down-regulation inhibits proliferation, migration, invasion, or reduces HER-3 expression. This is a notable evidence-category discrepancy: despite being listed as supportive, the abstract itself offers essentially no usable evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear based on the provided items. Although the claim may relate to the corrected article, the only cited abstract is an erratum with no substantive results, so there is essentially no direct abstract-level support and no direct contradiction.

statements.text: In gastric cancer, down-regulation of USP8 promotes apoptosis and cell-cycle arrest in HER-3–positive cancer cells.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In gastric cancer xenograft models, down-regulation of USP8 suppresses growth of HER-3–positive tumors.
statements.support_summary: The only cited supporting item is a corrigendum whose abstract contains no experimental results, only that it corrects a prior article; therefore, from the abstract alone it does not provide direct evidence about xenograft tumor growth suppression by USP8 down-regulation (PMID: 34848972). Its title suggests the underlying original study reported that down-regulation of USP8 suppresses proliferation of HER-3–positive gastric cancer cells, which is directionally consistent with the claim, but this is indirect and appears focused on cells rather than clearly on xenograft models (PMID: 34848972). This creates a discrepancy between the item's placement as supporting evidence and the limited abstract-level evidence actually available.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The sole item is an erratum/corrigendum with no abstracted data, and while its title is consistent with an antitumor effect of USP8 down-regulation in HER-3–positive gastric cancer, it does not directly substantiate the specific xenograft-model statement.

source_article.title: Systemic circulatory adjustments to acute hypoxia and reoxygenation in unanesthetized sheep. Role of renin, angiotensin II, and catecholamine interactions.
source_article.abstract: The hemodynamic consequences of the hypoxic inhibition of angiotensin-converting enzyme activity were studied in chronically instrumented unanesthetized sheep (n = 8) breathing a hypoxic gas mixture for 60 min (PaO2 = 31 mm Hg) followed by reoxygenation with room air. Changes in cardiac output, vascular pressures, blood flow distribution, arterial pH, PaCO2, PaO2, and arterial levels of plasma renin activity, angiotensin II, bradykinin, and catecholamines were measured at selected time points. Seven additional sheep underwent the same protocol but received saralasin, an angiotensin II receptor blocker beginning at 55 min of hypoxia and extending into the reoxygenation period. During hypoxia, both groups developed identical hemodynamic patterns including a rise in cardiac output (25%), blood pressure (15%), and preferential blood flow distribution to the heart, brain, adrenals, diaphragm, and skeletal muscle, as well as a decrease in the fraction of cardiac output to the kidneys and most of the gut. This was associated with a decrease in angiotensin II concentrations (from 35 to 17 pg/ml) in spite of a doubling in plasma renin activity and catecholamines. Bradykinin levels did not change. Upon reoxygenation, bolus production of angiotensin II (from 17 to 1,819 pg/ml) occurred in spite of a constant level of plasma renin activity. Concurrently, different hemodynamic patterns between control and saralasin groups emerged upon reoxygenation, including an elevation from base line in blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance in the control group. Cardiac work (heart-rate systolic pressure product) in the control group remained elevated upon reoxygenation while coronary blood flow returned to base-line values. Saralasin reduced cardiac work upon reoxygenation and restored the match between coronary blood flow and work. We conclude that plasma renin activity and oxygen tension together govern angiotensin II levels for an optimal level of systemic vasomotor tone during hypoxia. However, upon reoxygenation, bolus production of angiotensin II may result in pathophysiologic circulatory patterns, such as impairment in oxygen delivery to the myocardium proportional to persistently elevated cardiac work in the immediate postresuscitation period.

statements.text: During acute hypoxia in unanesthetized sheep, decreased oxygen tension together with increased plasma renin activity was associated with reduced angiotensin II levels and a circulatory response characterized by increased cardiac output, increased blood pressure, and redistributed blood flow toward the heart, brain, adrenals, diaphragm, and skeletal muscle.
statements.support_summary: Several studies in conscious/unanesthetized sheep or lambs report that acute hypoxia increases cardiac output and/or arterial pressure and redistributes flow toward oxygen-sensitive organs, especially the heart, brain, adrenals, diaphragm, and skeletal/respiratory muscle (PMID: 3084446, PMID: 6635378, PMID: 6441818, PMID: 3970169). In conscious sheep, severe acute hypoxemia increased cardiac output, systemic arterial pressure, and flow to coronary, cerebral, adrenal, respiratory, and nonrespiratory muscle beds while renal/abdominal flows were unchanged or relatively less favored, closely matching the circulatory part of the claim (PMID: 6635378). Related sheep studies under acute or longer hypoxic exposure also found increased coronary/cerebral/diaphragmatic flow and, in some cases, elevated cardiac output, supporting a recurring theme of flow redistribution favoring heart and brain during hypoxia (PMID: 3084446, PMID: 3983486, PMID: 6484323). However, these supportive studies largely do not address plasma renin activity or angiotensin II, so they mainly support the hemodynamic redistribution component rather than the full hormonal statement. Some support items are indirect because they involve newborn lambs, fetal sheep, or prolonged rather than acute hypoxia, reducing directness (PMID: 6441818, PMID: 2801974, PMID: 3983486, PMID: 6484323).
statements.contradict_summary: The main contradictory theme concerns the renin-angiotensin component: in fetal sheep, hypoxia or hypoxia plus hypercapnia increased plasma renin activity and angiotensin II rather than showing reduced angiotensin II, directly opposing the claim's hormonal pattern (PMID: 2118838, PMID: 4375185). Other studies found no evidence that acute hypoxia inhibits angiotensin II generation or found no change in plasma renin during prolonged hypoxia, again conflicting with the claim's specific endocrine response (PMID: 2988837, PMID: 3044147). Several hemodynamic studies in lambs, rabbits, or sheep reported unchanged rather than increased cardiac output or blood pressure during hypoxia, although some still showed redistribution of blood flow toward heart/brain; thus they partially contradict the claim's circulatory profile while agreeing on redistribution (PMID: 6483512, PMID: 3281, PMID: 12486694). One apparent discrepancy is that a study labeled as contradictory actually found decreases in both PRA and angiotensin II during acute hypoxia in conscious dogs, which conflicts with the claim's increased PRA but is directionally consistent with reduced angiotensin II (PMID: 10368356). Another contradictory item is not a pure hypoxia model but a laryngeal reflex with secondary hypoxemia, limiting relevance despite reporting decreased cardiac output and increased blood pressure/redistribution (PMID: 7183712).
statements.conclusion: The claim is only partially supported overall. The circulatory response in conscious sheep during acute hypoxia—higher cardiac output/pressure and redistribution toward heart, brain, adrenals, diaphragm, and muscle—is supported fairly well, but the specific association of increased plasma renin activity with reduced angiotensin II is challenged by several endocrine studies, including sheep-related evidence showing angiotensin II may increase or renin may remain unchanged.

statements.text: During reoxygenation after acute hypoxia in unanesthetized sheep, a surge in angiotensin II occurred despite unchanged plasma renin activity and was associated with adverse circulatory changes, including increased blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance in untreated sheep.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study in conscious dogs reported that during acute hypoxia, both plasma renin activity and plasma angiotensin II decreased, while mean arterial pressure and systemic vascular resistance did not change; losartan also did not affect hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (PMID: 10368356). This differs from the claim’s setting of reoxygenation after hypoxia in unanesthetized sheep, so it is only indirect contradiction rather than a direct test of the claimed post-hypoxic angiotensin II surge and adverse systemic circulatory changes. The abstract does not address reoxygenation, making this a cross-species, different-phase discrepancy.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear but leans weakly against the claim because the only cited study shows suppression rather than activation of the renin-angiotensin system and no systemic pressor response; however, it examined hypoxia in dogs rather than reoxygenation in sheep, so it does not directly refute the specific claim.

statements.text: Angiotensin II receptor blockade during reoxygenation after acute hypoxia in unanesthetized sheep reduced cardiac work and restored the match between coronary blood flow and myocardial work, indicating that angiotensin II contributes to impaired myocardial oxygen delivery after reoxygenation.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Surfactant and pulmonary blood flow distributions following treatment of premature lambs with natural surfactant.
matched_article.abstract: Prematurely delivered lambs were treated with radiolabeled natural surfactant by either tracheal instillation at birth and before the onset of mechanical ventilation, or after 23 +/- 1 (+/- SE) min of mechanical ventilation. Right ventricular blood flow distributions, left ventricular outputs, and left-to-right ductal shunts were measured with radiolabeled microspheres. After sacrifice, the lungs of lambs receiving surfactant at birth inflated uniformly with constant distending pressure while the lungs of lambs treated after a period of ventilation had aerated, partially aerated, and atelectatic areas. All lungs were divided into pieces which were weighed and catalogued as to location. The amount of radiolabeled surfactant and microsphere-associated radioactivity in each piece of lung was quantified. Surfactant was relatively homogenously distributed to pieces of lung from lambs that were treated with surfactant at birth; 48% of lung pieces received amounts of surfactant within +/- 25% of the mean value. Surfactant was preferentially recovered from the aerated pieces of lungs of lambs treated after a period of mechanical ventilation, and the distribution of surfactant to these lungs was very nonhomogeneous. Right ventricular blood flow distributions to the lungs were quite homogeneous in both groups of lambs. However, in 8 of 12 lambs, pulmonary blood flow was preferentially directed away from those pieces of lung that received relatively large amounts of surfactant and toward pieces of lung that received less surfactant. This acute redirection of pulmonary blood flow distribution may result from the local changes in compliances within the lung following surfactant instillation.

statements.text: In prematurely delivered lambs treated with natural surfactant at birth before mechanical ventilation, surfactant was distributed relatively homogeneously throughout the lungs.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a study of premature lambs given radiolabeled natural sheep surfactant at birth before ventilation, which found the surfactant distribution was more homogeneous than when treatment was delayed until respiratory distress syndrome was established, and this was independent of CMV versus HFOV ventilation (PMID: 3431958). Additional lamb studies suggest that delivery technique matters: bolus instillation produced uniform distribution across many lung pieces and lobes, whereas infusion was very nonuniform, indirectly supporting the plausibility of relatively homogeneous distribution under appropriate instillation conditions (PMID: 8175544). Some studies are only indirect or only partially aligned with the claim, reporting improved ventilation homogeneity or more uniform spatial ventilation after early surfactant, rather than directly measuring homogeneous surfactant distribution itself (PMID: 1480463, PMID: 24356523). Other supportive items involve different surfactants or compare surfactant formulations with similar or more uniform intrapulmonary distribution, but are less specific to natural surfactant given at birth before mechanical ventilation (PMID: 22465908, PMID: 20531255). One cited rabbit study concerns uniform expansion rather than direct surfactant distribution and is species-mismatched, so it is only weakly relevant (PMID: 576337).
statements.contradict_summary: Several contradicting items are only partially applicable because they examine different scenarios than the claim, such as less-invasive surfactant in spontaneously breathing lambs, rescue treatment after ventilation, nebulized delivery, or synthetic surfactants (PMID: 24796373, PMID: 8724656, PMID: 8756808, PMID: 31382955). Notably, one study actually states that surfactant given before ventilation in a fluid-filled lung is likely deposited more uniformly than after ventilation in an air-filled lung, which aligns with rather than contradicts the claim, so its placement in the contradiction set is discrepant (PMID: 8848608). Another supposedly contradicting study infers inadequate alveolar spread from poorer oxygenation with single-dose treatment plus manual ventilation, but does not directly measure lungwide surfactant homogeneity and used a different ventilation context (PMID: 34000686). The LISA study found reduced deposition in the right upper lobe, indicating nonuniformity, but it involved spontaneously breathing/CPAP-supported lambs rather than prophylactic intubated treatment before mechanical ventilation (PMID: 24796373). Overall, most contradiction is indirect, context-shifted, or miscategorized relative to the precise claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The key direct evidence specifically matching premature lambs treated with natural surfactant at birth before ventilation shows relatively more homogeneous lung distribution (PMID: 3431958), whereas most contradictory studies involve different administration methods, delayed/rescue treatment, different surfactants, or appear misclassified.

statements.text: In prematurely delivered lambs treated with natural surfactant after a period of mechanical ventilation, surfactant was distributed nonhomogeneously and was recovered preferentially from aerated lung regions.
statements.support_summary: Directly relevant lamb evidence shows that surfactant distribution depends on delivery method: in preterm lambs, bolus instillation produced relatively uniform distribution, whereas infusion produced very nonuniform distribution with localization to certain regions, and ventilation increased in lung pieces receiving more surfactant (PMID: 8175544). Indirectly related lamb studies report that aerated regions recover more aerosol than atelectatic regions and that ventilation/perfusion are highly region-dependent, which is consistent with preferential deposition or recovery in aerated lung (PMID: 2717241), while other lamb studies note inadequate alveolar spread or less homogeneous distribution under some surfactant administration approaches such as manual ventilation or nebulization (PMID: 34000686; PMID: 8756808). A non-lamb rabbit study also found endotracheally administered surfactant to be nonhomogeneously distributed (PMID: 8233717). However, several of these supporting items are indirect, use different species or delivery methods, and PMID: 8175544 also contains internal nuance because bolus dosing was actually fairly uniform.
statements.contradict_summary: Several studies in premature lambs report more homogeneous or improved distribution-related outcomes after surfactant, especially when given early or by optimized methods: prophylactic surfactant at birth gave more homogeneous radiolabeled surfactant distribution than rescue treatment after RDS (PMID: 3431958), and surfactant rapidly improved ventilation homogeneity within minutes (PMID: 1480463). Additional lamb studies found more uniform ventilation when surfactant was given before first inflation (PMID: 24356523), more uniform intrapulmonary distribution with larger-volume surfactant or similar distribution profiles across formulations (PMID: 20531255; PMID: 22465908). Some contradiction items are less directly relevant because they involve piglets or rabbits rather than premature lambs, though they still report relatively symmetric or more uniform surfactant distribution under certain conditions (PMID: 1603621; PMID: 9407243). Notably, even the strongest lamb contradiction (PMID: 3431958) does not fully negate the claim because it compares timing of treatment and implies rescue treatment is less homogeneous than prophylaxis, which partly aligns with the claim's context of treatment after ventilation.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat more contradicted than supported. The most directly relevant evidence is mixed, but multiple lamb studies describe homogeneous or improved distribution under specific surfactant timing/administration conditions, while support for preferential recovery from aerated regions is mostly indirect and method-dependent rather than consistently demonstrated.

statements.text: In prematurely delivered lambs treated with natural surfactant, pulmonary blood flow was often redirected away from lung regions receiving more surfactant and toward regions receiving less surfactant.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One abstract directly trends against the claim. In surfactant-treated preterm lambs, bolus administration produced relatively uniform surfactant distribution, and blood flow increased proportionately to surfactant content rather than being diverted away from better-treated regions (PMID: 8175544). The same study reported that with infusion, lung regions receiving large amounts of surfactant had decreased blood flow and increased ventilation, suggesting localized overinflation, which partly resembles the claim; this creates an internal discrepancy with its placement solely as contradicting evidence (PMID: 8175544). Overall, the abstract supports contradiction for bolus treatment but indicates a more mixed pattern depending on administration technique.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported, but the evidence is limited and somewhat mixed. The only cited abstract indicates that blood flow generally tracked with greater surfactant content after bolus dosing, though infusion produced an opposite-like pattern in some regions, so the broad statement that flow was often redirected away from more surfactant-treated regions is not well established.

source_article.title: Retraction. Fenofibrate improves the impaired endothelial progenitor cell function through inhibiting eNOS uncoupling in diabetic mice.
source_article.abstract: UNLABELLED: The above article, published online on 24th December 2014 as an Accepted Article in Wiley Online Library (http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bph.13054/abstract), has been retracted by agreement between the authors, the journal Editor in Chief, Professor J. McGrath and John Wiley & Sons Limited. The retraction has been agreed owing to duplication of some images. In this paper, there is a figure duplication in Figure 8A (‘Control’ is from an identical tissue slice as ‘STZ+FF’). There are also partially overlapping figures in supplemental figures S3 and S5. The authors explain this as follows: ‘Our technician took fluorescence images and saved them in one folder using the Arabic numerals. Then we use[d] Photoshop to arrange the images. We chose the STZ+FF group image from the Control group by mistake.’. REFERENCE: Zhao Ting, Deng Ya‐Ping, Ni Min, Chen Ji‐Kuai, Li Zhang‐Peng, Li Dong‐Jie and Shen Fu‐Ming (2014), Fenofibrate improves the impaired endothelial progenitor cell function through inhibiting eNOS uncoupling in diabetic mice. Br J Pharmacol, doi: 10.1111/bph.13054

matched_article.title: Regulation of μ and δ opioid receptor functions: involvement of cyclin-dependent kinase 5.
matched_article.abstract: Phosphorylation of δ opioid receptors (DOP receptors) by cyclin-dependent kinase 5 (CDK5) was shown to regulate the trafficking of this receptor. Therefore, we aimed to determine the role of CDK5 in regulating DOP receptors in rats treated with morphine or with complete Freund's adjuvant (CFA). As μ (MOP) and DOP receptors are known to be co-regulated, we also sought to determine if CDK5-mediated regulation of DOP receptors also affects MOP receptor functions. The role of CDK5 in regulating opioid receptors in CFA- and morphine-treated rats was studied using roscovitine as a CDK inhibitor and a cell-penetrant peptide mimicking the second intracellular loop of DOP receptors (C11-DOPri2). Opioid receptor functions were assessed in vivo in a series of behavioural experiments and correlated by measuring ERK1/2 activity in dorsal root ganglia homogenates. Chronic roscovitine treatment reduced the antinociceptive and antihyperalgesic effects of deltorphin II (Dlt II) in morphine- and CFA-treated rats respectively. Repeated administrations of C11-DOPri2 also robustly decreased Dlt II-induced analgesia. Interestingly, DAMGO-induced analgesia was significantly increased by roscovitine and C11-DOPri2. Concomitantly, in roscovitine-treated rats the Dlt II-induced ERK1/2 activation was decreased, whereas the DAMGO-induced ERK1/2 activation was increased. An acute roscovitine treatment had no effect on Dlt II- or DAMGO-induced analgesia. Together, our results demonstrate that CDK5 is a key player in the regulation of DOP receptors in morphine- and CFA-treated rats and that the regulation of DOP receptors by CDK5 is sufficient to modulate MOP receptor functions through an indirect process.

statements.text: In rats treated with morphine or complete Freund's adjuvant, cyclin-dependent kinase 5 activity contributes to regulation of δ opioid receptor-mediated analgesia and antihyperalgesia.
statements.support_summary: Two rat studies support a role for Cdk5 in regulating delta-opioid receptor (DOR) function in morphine and inflammatory pain contexts. One study showed that Cdk5 directly phosphorylates DOR at Thr-161 in DRG neurons, that this phosphorylation supports DOR surface expression and DOR-MOR heterodimer formation, and that disrupting this site attenuated morphine antinociceptive tolerance in rats, supporting a morphine-related regulatory role though not directly testing antihyperalgesia (PMID: 19295160). A second study in CFA-treated rats found increased total and surface phosphorylated DOR after CFA, and reported that disrupting the DOR intracellular loop increased inflammatory hypersensitivity, inhibited DOR-mediated spinal analgesia, and delayed morphine tolerance, which directly supports regulation of both analgesia and antihyperalgesia by Cdk5-dependent DOR phosphorylation in inflammatory pain (PMID: 22466129). A minor discrepancy is that the first study is more focused on morphine tolerance than on DOR-mediated analgesia/antihyperalgesia per se, so its support is indirect for part of the claim. Overall, the recurring theme is that Cdk5-dependent phosphorylation of DOR, especially at Thr-161, modulates receptor function and pain-related behavioral responses in rats.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The available evidence consistently indicates that Cdk5-dependent phosphorylation of DOR contributes to regulation of DOR-related analgesic and antihyperalgesic processes in CFA-treated rats and to morphine-related antinociceptive tolerance, although the morphine-specific evidence is somewhat more indirect regarding antihyperalgesia.

statements.text: In rats treated with morphine or complete Freund's adjuvant, inhibition of cyclin-dependent kinase 5 reduces δ opioid receptor function but enhances μ opioid receptor-mediated analgesia.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study in CFA-treated rats reported that interfering with Cdk5-dependent phosphorylation of the δ-opioid receptor at Thr-161 increased inflammatory hypersensitivity and inhibited δ-opioid receptor-mediated spinal analgesia, but did not inhibit μ-opioid receptor-mediated spinal analgesia (PMID: 22466129). The same study found that this intervention postponed morphine antinociceptive tolerance in inflammatory pain states, suggesting effects on morphine tolerance rather than clear enhancement of acute μ-opioid receptor analgesia (PMID: 22466129). Thus, the abstract supports reduced δ-opioid receptor function after disrupting this Cdk5-related pathway, but does not support the claim that μ-opioid receptor-mediated analgesia is enhanced; this is a key discrepancy.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported based on the available evidence. The only cited abstract supports reduced δ-opioid receptor-mediated analgesia after disrupting a Cdk5-related mechanism, but it does not show enhanced μ-opioid receptor-mediated analgesia, instead only indicating delayed morphine tolerance.

statements.text: Cyclin-dependent kinase 5-dependent regulation of δ opioid receptors is sufficient to indirectly modulate μ opioid receptor function in rats treated with morphine or complete Freund's adjuvant.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One abstract provides partial contradictory evidence. In CFA-treated rats, disrupting Cdk5-related phosphorylation of δ-opioid receptor (DOR) at Thr-161 altered inflammatory hypersensitivity and inhibited DOR-mediated, but not μ-opioid receptor-mediated, spinal analgesia, which argues against DOR phosphorylation being sufficient to indirectly modulate μ-opioid receptor function under these conditions (PMID: 22466129). However, the same study also reports that disrupting this DOR phosphorylation postponed morphine antinociceptive tolerance, suggesting some relationship between Cdk5-dependent DOR regulation and morphine responses; this creates a nuance/discrepancy relative to its categorization as purely contradictory (PMID: 22466129).
statements.conclusion: Overall, the claim is more contradicted than supported because there is no direct supporting evidence here, and the only cited abstract indicates that interfering with Cdk5-dependent DOR phosphorylation affects DOR but not μ-opioid receptor-mediated analgesia in CFA-treated rats. Still, because that same study links DOR phosphorylation to morphine tolerance, the contradiction is only partial rather than strong.

source_article.title: Oligomeric interaction of hepatitis C virus NS5B is critical for catalytic activity of RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.
source_article.abstract: HCV NS5B is an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRP), a central catalytic enzyme for HCV replication, which has the "palm and fingers" substructure. We recently identified five novel residues critical for RdRP activity (Qin, W., Yamashita, T., Shirota, Y., Lin, Y., Wei, W., and Murakami, S. (2001) Hepatology 33, 728-737). Among them, GLU-18 and His-502, far from the catalytic center, may be involved in conformational change(s) for RdRP activity as addressed in some palm and fingers enzymes. We examined the possibility that NS5B is oligomerized, and we could detect the interaction between two different tagged NS5B proteins in vitro and transiently expressed in mammalian cells. By scanning 27 clustered and then point alanine substitutions in vivo and in vitro, Glu-18 and His-502 were found to be critical for the homomeric interaction in vivo and in vitro, strongly suggesting a close relationship between the oligomerization and RdRP activity of NS5B. All mutants with substitutions at these two residues failed to bind wild type NS5B, however E18H interacted with H502E in vitro and in vivo. Interestingly, the NS5B protein with E18H or H502E did not exhibit RdRP activity, but a mixture of the two mutant proteins did. These results clearly indicate that two residues of HCV NS5B are critical for the oligomerization that is prerequisite to RdRP activity.

statements.text: Hepatitis C virus NS5B requires homomeric oligomerization for RNA-dependent RNA polymerase catalytic activity.
statements.support_summary: Several studies report that HCV NS5B self-associates and that this association is linked to polymerase function. Biochemical and structural work showed NS5B oligomerization by gel filtration, cross-linking, and two-hybrid assays, along with cooperative RNA synthesis and dominant-negative inhibition by a catalytically inactive NS5B, supporting a functional oligomer model (PMID: 11907226). Later studies state that mutations disrupting NS5B-NS5B interactions are lethal or severely impair polymerase activity, with FRET-based assays and mutational analyses connecting oligomerization interfaces to loss of de novo initiation and elongation activity (PMID: 20430057; PMID: 24815023). Additional evidence links a specific NS5B region needed for nucleolin binding to a site reported as essential for NS5B oligomerization and required for RdRP activity, and genotype-comparative work found differences in oligomerization that track with differences in polymerase cooperativity/activity (PMID: 12427757; PMID: 21490973). One crystallography paper only proposes disruption of oligomerization as a possible inhibitor mechanism rather than directly demonstrating a requirement, so its support is indirect (PMID: 12805457).
statements.contradict_summary: One study provides direct contradictory evidence by reporting that both wild-type and mutant NS5B were monomeric in solution, while still showing polymerase activity, with higher-order species appearing only in the presence of RNA template; this argues against oligomerization being strictly required for catalytic activity per se (PMID: 12145289). Another cited contradictory paper demonstrates robust de novo RNA synthesis by purified full-length and truncated NS5B but does not assess oligomerization, so from the abstract it is better viewed as neutral or only weakly contradictory rather than a true refutation (PMID: 10623748). Overall, the contradiction rests mainly on the monomer-in-solution observation, with some discrepancy because RNA-dependent higher-order assembly in the active state was also noted in that same study (PMID: 12145289).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple abstracts directly link NS5B self-interaction/oligomerization to cooperative or impaired RdRP function when interfaces are disrupted, whereas the main contradictory evidence is a single study showing monomeric NS5B in solution despite activity, which does not exclude RNA-induced oligomerization during catalysis.

statements.text: A pair of residues in hepatitis C virus NS5B are critical for NS5B-NS5B interaction, and disruption of these residues abolishes polymerase activity.
statements.support_summary: Three studies support the broader premise that NS5B-NS5B contacts are important for HCV polymerase function, though most do not directly verify one exact residue pair. A FRET-based study states that NS5B forms oligomers and that mutations disrupting these interactions are lethal for polymerase function, supporting the link between self-interaction and activity loss, but without specifying the exact residues in the claim (PMID: 20430057). Cross-genotype analysis identified the αF and αT helices and specific candidate residues including Glu128, Asp129, and Glu248 as likely contributors to NS5B-NS5B interactions, providing indirect structural support for critical interface residues (PMID: 21490973). The strongest abstract-level support comes from a study showing that mutations in residues predicted to mediate the NS5B contact interface caused severe defects in enzymatic activity, de novo initiation, elongation, and replicon replication, consistent with the claim that disrupting key interface residues can abolish polymerase function, although the abstract does not clearly isolate a single residue pair (PMID: 24815023).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The available abstracts consistently indicate that NS5B-NS5B interaction surfaces are functionally important and that disrupting key interface residues severely impairs or abolishes polymerase activity, although the evidence here is stronger for the general interaction-function relationship than for one precisely defined residue pair.

statements.text: Complementary NS5B mutants that restore NS5B-NS5B interaction can also restore RNA-dependent RNA polymerase activity when combined.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study reports that hepatitis C virus NS5B de novo initiation is enhanced by increasing enzyme concentration, consistent with a positive role for NS5B oligomeric contacts, and that initiation-defective mutants can increase de novo initiation by wild-type polymerase (PMID: 20375156). The abstract supports the broader idea that intermolecular NS5B-NS5B interactions can modulate RNA-dependent RNA polymerase function, but it does not explicitly test complementary mutants that restore mutant-mutant interaction or directly show restored polymerase activity upon combining such mutants (PMID: 20375156). Thus, the support is indirect rather than a direct demonstration of the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The only cited evidence is consistent with NS5B oligomerization influencing polymerase activity, but it does not directly demonstrate that complementary NS5B mutants restore both NS5B-NS5B interaction and RNA-dependent RNA polymerase activity when combined.

matched_article.title: MD-2 and TLR4 N-linked glycosylations are important for a functional lipopolysaccharide receptor.
matched_article.abstract: The lipopolysaccharide (LPS) receptor is a multi-protein complex that consists of at least three proteins, CD14, TLR4, and MD-2. Because each of these proteins is glycosylated, we have examined the functional role of N-linked carbohydrates of both MD-2 and TLR4. We demonstrate that MD-2 contains 2 N-glycosylated sites at positions Asn(26) and Asn(114), whereas the amino-terminal ectodomain of human TLR4 contains 9 N-linked glycosylation sites. Site-directed mutagenesis studies showed that cell surface expression of MD-2 did not depend on the presence of either N-linked site, whereas in contrast, TLR4 mutants carrying substitutions in Asn(526) or Asn(575) failed to be transported to the cell surface. Using a UV-activated derivative of Re595 LPS (ASD-Re595 LPS) in cross-linking assays, we demonstrated a critical role of MD-2 and TLR4 carbohydrates in LPS cross-linking to the LPS receptor. The ability of the various glycosylation mutants to support cell activation was also evaluated in transiently transfected HeLa cells. The double mutant of MD-2 failed to support LPS-induced activation of an interleukin-8 (IL-8) promoter-driven luciferase reporter to induce IL-8 secretion or to activate amino-terminal c-Jun kinase (JNK). Similar results were observed with TLR4 mutants lacking three or more N-linked glycosylation sites. Surprisingly, the reduction in activation resulting from expression of the Asn mutants of MD-2 and TLR4 can be partially reversed by co-expression with CD14.
