This suggests that the functional integrity of the LPS receptor depends both on the surface expression of at least three proteins, CD14, MD-2, and TLR4, and that N-linked sites of both MD-2 and TLR4 are essential in maintaining the functional integrity of this receptor.

statements.text: N-linked glycosylation of MD-2 is required for MD-2 to support lipopolysaccharide-induced activation of interleukin-8 signaling and c-Jun N-terminal kinase activation in cells expressing the lipopolysaccharide receptor complex.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Two abstracts directly argue that MD-2 glycosylation is not required for LPS signaling. A structural/functional study reports that bacterially expressed MD-2, which lacks glycosylation, still conferred LPS responsiveness to TLR4-expressing cells, contradicting a strict requirement for N-linked glycosylation (PMID: 15111623). A mutational study states explicitly that glycosylation had no effect on MD-2 function as a membrane receptor for LPS in CHO cells co-transfected with TLR4 and MD-2 mutants (PMID: 16467306). Both studies instead emphasize specific amino acid regions/basic clusters as important determinants of MD-2 activity rather than glycosylation status.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported. Although the evidence set is small, both available abstracts directly indicate that non-glycosylated or glycosylation-deficient MD-2 can still support LPS/TLR4 signaling, which conflicts with the statement that N-linked glycosylation is required.

statements.text: N-linked glycosylation of TLR4 is required for TLR4 transport to the cell surface and for TLR4 to support lipopolysaccharide-induced cellular activation in cells expressing the lipopolysaccharide receptor complex.
statements.support_summary: One abstract provides partial support that specific N-linked glycosylation of TLR4 is necessary for its cell-surface expression. When TLR4 was coexpressed with MD-2, a higher-molecular-weight glycosylated form appeared, and only this form was detected at the cell surface; mutation of TLR4 Asn526 or Asn575 abolished this form and prevented surface expression without disrupting MD-2 association (PMID: 12738639). This directly supports the trafficking/surface transport part of the claim, although the abstract does not directly test whether these glycosylation events are required for LPS-induced cellular activation.
statements.contradict_summary: The cited contradicting abstract does not directly test N-linked glycosylation of TLR4, but instead reports that bacterially expressed, non-glycosylated MD-2 can still confer LPS responsiveness to TLR4-expressing cells (PMID: 15111623). This could be seen as indirectly arguing that glycosylation is not universally required within the receptor complex, but it does not specifically contradict the claim about TLR4 glycosylation, transport, or TLR4-dependent activation. Thus, this item is better viewed as weak/indirect contradiction or a categorization discrepancy rather than strong opposing evidence.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The main direct evidence supports a requirement for specific N-linked glycosylation in TLR4 surface transport, whereas the opposing evidence concerns MD-2 glycosylation rather than TLR4 itself and does not directly refute the claim's central mechanism.

statements.text: CD14 can partially compensate for reduced lipopolysaccharide receptor function caused by loss of N-linked glycosylation in MD-2 or TLR4, but intact N-linked glycosylation of both proteins is important for full receptor function.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One abstract reports that glycosylation had no effect on MD-2 function as a membrane LPS receptor in CHO cells co-transfected with TLR4 and mutant MD-2, which conflicts with the claim that intact N-linked glycosylation of MD-2 is important for full receptor function (PMID: 16467306). However, this study appears limited to MD-2 mutational analysis and does not address TLR4 glycosylation or the claim's specific point that CD14 can partially compensate for glycosylation-related loss of function. Thus, it contradicts only part of the claim rather than fully refuting it.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is overall weakly contradicted. The only cited abstract challenges the importance of MD-2 glycosylation for receptor function, but it does not test CD14 compensation or TLR4 glycosylation, so the contradiction is limited in scope.

source_article.title: Synergistic effects of eukaryotic co-expression plasmid-based STAT3-specific siRNA and LKB1 on ovarian cancer in vitro and in vivo.
source_article.abstract: The signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 (STAT3) are ideal targets for ovarian cancer. Previous studies showed that downregulation of STAT3 using specific short hairpin RNAs (shRNA) can significantly reduce ovarian tumor growth. However, RNA interference does not fully ablate target gene expression due to idiosyncrasies associated with shRNAs and their targets. To enhance the therapeutic efficacy of STAT3-specific shRNA, we employed a combinatorial expression of STAT3-specific shRNA and liver kinase B1 (LKB1), a tumor suppressor. Thus, the LKB1 coding sequences and STAT3-specific shRNAs were constructed in a eukaryotic co-expression plasmid pCDNA3.1, and then transfected into ovarian cancer cells to evaluate the synergistic effects of this combination on anticancer activity and explore the relevant molecular mechanisms. Co-expression of STAT3‑specific siRNA and LKB1 (pSi-STAT3-LKB1) synergistically inhibited ovarian cancer cell growth, invasion and migration, induced cell apoptosis and arrested the cell cycle in vitro when compared with monotherapy. The results showed that the co-expression of plasmid pSi-STAT3-LKB1 inserted subcutaneously into ovarian tumor xenograft resulted in more significant inhibition of tumor growth. Further study showed that the synergistic anti-ovarian cancer effects of the co-expression of STAT3-specific siRNA and LKB1 may be associated with the upregulation of p-p53, p21 and downregulation of survivin, BCL-2 and cyclin D1. Results of the present study suggested that combined therapy with eukaryotic co-expression of the plasmid‑carrying STAT3-specific siRNA and LKB1 is a novel and efficient treatment strategy for human ovarian cancer.

statements.text: In ovarian cancer cells, co-expression of STAT3-specific siRNA and LKB1 inhibited cell growth, invasion, and migration more effectively than STAT3-targeting monotherapy or LKB1 monotherapy, and it increased apoptosis while causing cell-cycle arrest.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In ovarian cancer xenografts, co-expression of STAT3-specific siRNA and LKB1 suppressed tumor growth more strongly than monotherapy.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: The anti-ovarian cancer effects of co-expression of STAT3-specific siRNA and LKB1 were associated with activation of p53 and p21 signaling and suppression of survivin, BCL-2, and cyclin D1.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: RRM1, TUBB3, TOP2A, CYP19A1, CYP2D6: Difference between mRNA and protein expression in predicting prognosis of breast cancer patients.
matched_article.abstract: The study investigated the clinical significance of RRM1 (ribonucleoside reductase subunit M1), TUBB3 (tubulin-β-III), TOP2A (DNA topoisomerase II), CYP19A1 (cytochrome P450, family 19, subfamily A, polypeptide 1) and CYP2D6 (cytochrome P450, family 2, subfamily D, polypeptide 6) for the diagnosis and possible predictive roles in breast cancer. Tissue microarray detected the expression of RRM1, tubulin-β-III, Topo IIα, CYP19A1 and CYP2D6 protein in breast cancer tissue and tissue adjacent to tumors (TATs). In addition, a publically available tool, was used to assess the prognostic value of their gene expression in breast cancer (http://kmplot.com). Analysis for relapse-free survival (RFS), disease-free survival (DFS) and overall survival (OS) was performed. Cytoplasmic RRM1, tubulin-β-III, CYP19A1 and Topo IIα staining were significantly higher in breast cancer tissues compared with TATs (P<0.050). Significant correlation occurred between RRM1 expression with pathological classification (P=0.018), lymph node involvement (P=0.035) and ER status (P=0.003). Tubulin-β-III and CYP2D6 expression correlated significantly with tumor grade (P=0.021 for tubulin-β-III and P=0.029 for CYP2D6, respectively). Cox analysis showed that the protein expression of CYP2D6, CYP19A1, RRM1, Topo IIα or tubulin-β-III was not an independent prognostic factor. A significant association occurred between RFS and TUBB3, TOP2A, CYP19A1, and CYP2D6 mRNA expression. With CYP19A1 (P<0.001) and CYP2D6 (P<0.001), a high expression was associated with good clinical outcome. Conversely, a low expression of TUBB3 (P<0.001) and TOP2A (P<0.001) was associated with good clinical outcome. TUBB3 (P=0.0004) and TOP2A (P<0.001) were significant prognostic factors in predicting the patient OS. The expression of RRM1, tubulin-β-III, Topo IIα and CYP19A1 in tumor tissues was significantly higher than that in TATs. TUBB3, TOP2A, CYP19A1 and CYP2D6 gene expression, but not protein expression, was associated with patient survival.

statements.text: In breast cancer, higher mRNA expression of CYP19A1 and CYP2D6 is associated with better patient survival, whereas higher mRNA expression of TUBB3 and TOP2A is associated with worse patient survival.
statements.support_summary: Supporting studies are dominated by evidence that higher TUBB3 and especially TOP2A expression track with more aggressive breast cancer and poorer outcomes. Direct breast-cancer survival associations include high TUBB3 mRNA predicting worse DFS and OS in early breast cancer (PMID: 21390496), and multiple studies showing high TOP2A RNA/mRNA or protein associated with shorter recurrence-free, disease-free, metastasis-free, or overall survival, including large breast cancer cohorts and subtype-specific analyses (PMID: 18340528, PMID: 22706628, PMID: 22240029, PMID: 35297009, PMID: 33144457). Several additional breast cancer papers support this indirectly by linking high TUBB3/TOP2A to high grade, Ki-67, HER2 positivity, triple-negative phenotype, or other aggressive features (PMID: 33832110, PMID: 26998111, PMID: 21435434). However, most supporting items address only TUBB3/TOP2A, not CYP19A1 or CYP2D6, and a number are from non-breast cancers or treatment-response settings, which weakens support for the full 4-gene claim. I do not see direct supporting evidence among these abstracts for the claim that higher CYP19A1 and CYP2D6 mRNA are associated with better survival.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence mainly concerns TOP2A and CYP19A1. Several breast cancer studies in anthracycline-treated or HER2-amplified subsets report that TOP2A amplification/positivity is associated with better response or better DFS/OS, or at least not worse prognosis (PMID: 17260090, PMID: 16682728, PMID: 21681601, PMID: 16234514), although these often evaluate gene amplification or protein expression rather than mRNA and may reflect predictive treatment effects rather than pure prognosis. Other breast cancer studies report no clear prognostic value for TOP2A alterations/expression (PMID: 16502015, PMID: 25846735, PMID: 28079792), creating heterogeneity rather than a simple contradiction. Importantly, one breast cancer study directly contradicts the CYP19A1 portion of the claim, finding higher CYP19/aromatase mRNA correlated with poor survival and disease progression, not better survival (PMID: 16541304). One abstract in this set says TOP2A RNA level is associated with metastasis-free interval/favorable anthracycline response (PMID: 20371687), but the direction of the prognostic association is not fully explicit in the abstract and should be interpreted cautiously.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only partially supported and overall the evidence is mixed. There is fairly strong support that higher TUBB3 and TOP2A expression are associated with worse breast cancer outcomes, but the CYP19A1 "better survival" component is directly contradicted, CYP2D6 is not supported here, and TOP2A findings vary across prognostic versus anthracycline-predictive study settings.

statements.text: In breast cancer, protein expression of CYP2D6, CYP19A1, RRM1, TOP2A, and TUBB3 is not an independent prognostic factor for patient survival.
statements.support_summary: Supportive evidence is limited and mostly concerns TOP2A and TUBB3 rather than the full protein panel. In a neoadjuvant anthracycline-treated cohort, TOP2A expression predicted pathologic complete response but was not related to relapse-free or overall survival after follow-up, supporting lack of prognostic value for survival in that setting (PMID: 21734419). A Taiwanese series found TOP2A amplification, not protein expression, had no prognostic value, so this only indirectly supports the claim and is a modality mismatch (PMID: 28079792). In a very large IHC study, TUBB3 overexpression was associated with adverse clinicopathologic features and worse survival in univariate analysis, but not in multivariate analysis, and the authors concluded it lacked clinically relevant prognostic value (PMID: 26998111). No supportive abstracts here address CYP2D6, CYP19A1, or RRM1 directly, creating an important gap relative to the full claim.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is much larger and is dominated by studies reporting that TOP2A is prognostic, often independently, in breast cancer or specific subtypes. Multiple abstracts report TOP2A protein overexpression as independently associated with worse DFS/OS or metastasis outcomes, including early-stage disease, triple-negative disease, luminal disease, and older primary breast cancer cohorts (PMID: 35297009; PMID: 15987433; PMID: 27799773; PMID: 29587760; PMID: 29928479). Additional studies show TOP2A expression correlates with shorter survival or recurrence, though several assess RNA or gene amplification/dosage rather than protein expression, which is a discrepancy with the claim's wording but still argues against a blanket non-prognostic interpretation (PMID: 18340528; PMID: 22706628; PMID: 22240029; PMID: 22871798; PMID: 22426810; PMID: 18465341). For TUBB3, one study found high TUBB3 mRNA to be an adverse independent prognostic factor for DFS and OS, but again this is transcriptional rather than protein evidence (PMID: 21390496). Some contradicting items are weaker for this claim because they focus on predictive treatment effects or non-protein measures, yet the overall recurring theme is that TOP2A-related tumor expression/alteration frequently carries adverse prognostic information.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more contradicted than supported. Although a few studies suggest TOP2A or TUBB3 lack independent prognostic value in certain cohorts, the much larger body of evidence—especially for TOP2A—reports independent associations with poorer survival outcomes, while evidence for CYP2D6, CYP19A1, and RRM1 is largely absent in the provided abstracts.

statements.text: In breast cancer tumor tissue compared with adjacent non-tumor tissue, RRM1, TUBB3, CYP19A1, and TOP2A protein expression is higher.
statements.support_summary: All three supporting studies address TOP2A, not the full four-protein panel in the claim, so support is only partial. Two immunohistochemical studies reported higher or detectable TOP2A/Topo IIα protein expression in breast cancer tissue versus nonmalignant/normal breast tissue, with one finding TOP2A undetectable in nonmalignant epithelium (PMID: 8669491) and another showing higher positive expression in cancer than peripheral normal tissues (PMID: 31258582). A bioinformatic/in vitro study also reported TOP2A is highly overexpressed in breast cancer tissues, but the abstract does not clearly specify comparison with adjacent non-tumor tissue (PMID: 35440335). No supporting abstract here provides evidence for increased RRM1, TUBB3, or CYP19A1 protein expression, creating a scope discrepancy between the claim and the available evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only partially supported overall. The available evidence consistently supports higher TOP2A protein expression in breast cancer versus normal/nonmalignant tissue, but there is no provided abstract-level evidence here for RRM1, TUBB3, or CYP19A1, so the full multi-marker claim is not established.

source_article.title: Effectiveness of complementary pain treatment for women with deep endometriosis through Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation (TENS): randomized controlled trial.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: Evaluate TENS effectiveness as a complementary treatment of chronic pelvic pain and deep dyspareunia in women with deep endometriosis. STUDY DESIGN: This randomized controlled trial was performed in a tertiary health care center, including twenty-two women with deep endometriosis undergoing hormone therapy with persistent pelvic pain and/or deep dyspareunia. This study was registered in the Brazilian Record of Clinical Trials (ReBEC), under n RBR-3rndh6. TENS application for 8 weeks followed a randomized allocation into two groups: Group 1 - acupuncture-like TENS (Frequency: 8Hz, pulse duration: 250μs) - VIF (n=11) and Group 2 - self-applied TENS (Frequency: 85Hz, pulse duration: 75μs) (n=11). The intensity applied was "strong, but comfortable". We evaluated patients before and after treatment by the use of the Visual Analogue Scale, Deep Dyspareunia Scale and Endometriosis Quality of Life Questionnaire. We used the Wilcoxon and Mann-Whitney tests to compare before and after treatment conditions. RESULTS: Despite the use of hormone therapy for 1.65±2.08 years, the 22 women with deep endometriosis sustained pelvic pain complaints (VAS=5.95±2.13 and 2.45±2.42, p<.001) and/or deep dyspareunia (DDS=2.29±0.46 and 1.20±1.01, p=.001). We observed significant improvement for chronic pelvic pain, deep dyspareunia and quality of life by the use of TENS. Both application types of TENS were effective for improving the evaluated types of pain. CONCLUSIONS: Both resources (acupuncture-like TENS and self-applied TENS) demonstrated effectiveness as a complementary treatment of pelvic pain and deep dyspareunia, improving quality of life in women with deep endometriosis regardless of the device used for treatment.

statements.text: In women with deep endometriosis and persistent chronic pelvic pain and deep dyspareunia despite hormone therapy, transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation improved pelvic pain, deep dyspareunia, and quality of life as a complementary treatment.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct evidence is a multicenter randomized clinical trial in women with deep infiltrative endometriosis receiving hormonal therapy, where adding self-applied electrotherapy/TENS reduced chronic pelvic pain, improved deep dyspareunia, and improved quality of life and sexual function over 8 weeks, with the abstract concluding it is a useful complementary option (PMID: 33129015). Broader indirect evidence from a systematic review of neuromodulation for chronic pelvic pain found that TENS improved pain and that quality of life generally improved, although many included studies had high risk of bias and were not specific to deep endometriosis (PMID: 31636030). A scoping review also states that TENS has proven effective for pelvic pain as part of a multimodal rehabilitation approach, but it is not specific to hormone-refractory deep endometriosis (PMID: 36701176). Additional indirect support comes from a systematic review/meta-analysis in dyspareunia showing electrotherapy-based interventions improved pain and quality of life, though not clearly sexual function, and from narrative/review articles mentioning TENS as a potentially helpful complementary treatment in endometriosis or pelvic pain management (PMID: 37482613, PMID: 28496368, PMID: 12742182). Overall, most support beyond PMID: 33129015 is indirect and less specific to the exact claim population and treatment context.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported, primarily because a randomized clinical trial directly in women with deep infiltrative endometriosis on hormonal therapy found that adding TENS/electrotherapy improved chronic pelvic pain, deep dyspareunia, and quality of life (PMID: 33129015). The remaining evidence is mostly indirect or review-based, so support is substantial but not unequivocally strong across multiple direct trials.

statements.text: In women with deep endometriosis and persistent chronic pelvic pain and deep dyspareunia despite hormone therapy, acupuncture-like transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation and self-applied transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation were both effective, with no meaningful difference in overall benefit between the two device types.
statements.support_summary: One randomized clinical trial found that adding self-applied transcutaneous electrical stimulation to hormonal therapy in women with deep infiltrative endometriosis improved chronic pelvic pain, with deep dyspareunia and quality-of-life measures also improving, supporting the general effectiveness of TENS-based electrotherapy but not directly comparing acupuncture-like versus self-applied device types (PMID: 33129015). Two review articles report that complementary therapies for endometriosis show limited or inconclusive evidence overall, with acupuncture showing the clearest pain benefit; these findings are only indirectly relevant to the claim because they do not test the specific head-to-head comparison between acupuncture-like TENS and self-applied TENS in the target population (PMID: 29944729; PMID: 34933385). A key discrepancy is that the reviews emphasize acupuncture rather than TENS-device comparisons, so they may be misclassified as direct support for this specific statement. Overall, the available support mainly backs electrotherapy/acupuncture-related pain benefit in endometriosis rather than the precise claim of equal effectiveness between the two TENS modalities.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The strongest relevant study supports benefit from electrotherapy in deep endometriosis, but the evidence set does not directly establish that acupuncture-like TENS and self-applied TENS have no meaningful difference in overall benefit, and the review articles are indirect rather than specific to the claimed comparison.

matched_article.title: Cesarean analgesia using levobupivacaine continuous wound infiltration: a randomized trial.
matched_article.abstract: Neuraxial morphine is considered as a "gold standard" for pain relief after cesarean section, however it causes bothersome side effects. Alternative analgesia including nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs (NSAID) has been proposed. We aimed to assess the morphine sparing effect of continuous wound infiltration with a local anesthetic, when added to multimodal systemic analgesia including NSAID without subarachnoid morphine. Sixty-eight women scheduled for elective cesarean section under spinal anesthesia were included in a randomized controlled open-label trial. Patients received bupivacaine spinal anesthesia without intrathecal morphine. Postoperative analgesia consisted for all patients in multimodal systemic analgesia with acetaminophen, nefopam, celecoxib, and patient-controlled intravenous morphine for 24h. The intervention group also received subfascial levobupivacaine infiltration through a multi-holed catheter, at 6.25mg/h for 48h. The primary endpoint was total morphine consumption at 24h postoperatively; and secondary endpoints were pain scores, side effects, breastfeeding comfort, maternal satisfaction, and nurse workload. Student t test, Mann-Whitney test or χ(2) test were used when appropriate. The intervention group had 6.7mg less morphine consumption (95%CI -1.3mg; -12mg, P=0.02), and 0.8 pain point less at rest on the numerical rating scale 0-10 (95%CI -0.3; -1.3, P=0.002). The intervention was associated with significantly better breastfeeding comfort (+1.7 at numerical rating scale score 0-10, 95%CI +0; +3.3, P=0.0498). Wound dressing changes were required in a significantly higher proportion of intervention-group women (12/34 vs. 1/34, P=0.002). Adding continuous levobupivacaine infiltration to multimodal analgesia after cesarean section without subarachnoid morphine decreased postoperative morphine consumption and pain, facilitated breastfeeding initial comfort, and slightly increased nurse workload.

statements.text: In women undergoing elective cesarean section under spinal anesthesia without intrathecal morphine, adding continuous subfascial levobupivacaine wound infiltration to multimodal systemic analgesia reduced postoperative morphine consumption and pain at rest compared with multimodal systemic analgesia alone.
statements.support_summary: Several reviews and meta-analyses consistently report that local anesthetic wound infiltration after cesarean delivery reduces postoperative opioid consumption, with some evidence of small reductions in pain scores, especially when the catheter is placed below the fascia and when intrathecal morphine is not used (PMID: 19588413, PMID: 20460027, PMID: 27259092, PMID: 26309720, PMID: 31262444). A later randomized triple-blind placebo-controlled trial found that continuous subfascial levobupivacaine infusion after cesarean delivery significantly lowered 24-hour morphine consumption and reduced hyperalgesia, supporting the general mechanism and intervention class, although that study included intrathecal fentanyl rather than exactly matching the claim context (PMID: 35086112). Older cesarean RCTs with wound catheters using bupivacaine beneath the rectus sheath also showed reduced morphine use and at least some lower pain scores versus placebo after spinal anesthesia (PMID: 8717568). Additional narrative/systematic reviews describe continuous subfascial wound catheter techniques as effective and particularly relevant within multimodal regimens, but these are indirect support rather than exact replications of the indexed claim (PMID: 21797083, PMID: 34682777). One listed support item is clearly only indirectly relevant because it studied hysterectomy rather than cesarean delivery, so it should be treated as a discrepancy in the support set (PMID: 9150773).
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting study did not compare continuous wound infusion with multimodal systemic analgesia alone; instead, it compared levobupivacaine continuous wound infusion with TAP block in women having elective cesarean delivery under spinal anesthesia without intrathecal morphine (PMID: 25093663). In that head-to-head trial, pain at rest, pain on mobilization, and morphine consumption were similar between the two active analgesic techniques, so it does not directly refute benefit versus no wound infusion control (PMID: 25093663). Because the study lacks a systemic-analgesia-only arm, its placement in the contradiction set appears to be a misclassification/discrepancy rather than true contradictory evidence.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support comes from multiple cesarean-specific reviews/meta-analyses and related randomized trials showing reduced opioid use, with some evidence for reduced pain at rest particularly for subfascial catheters without intrathecal morphine, whereas the lone contradicting item is not a true contradiction because it compared continuous wound infusion against another active regional technique rather than against multimodal systemic analgesia alone.

statements.text: In women undergoing elective cesarean section under spinal anesthesia without intrathecal morphine, adding continuous subfascial levobupivacaine wound infiltration to multimodal systemic analgesia improved early breastfeeding comfort compared with multimodal systemic analgesia alone.

source_article.title: RETRACTED: Vascularised human tissue models: a new approach for the refinement of biomedical research.
source_article.abstract: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (http://www.elsevier.com/locate/withdrawalpolicy). This article has been retracted at the request of the Author(s). (a) The authors have duplicated at least one figure from a paper that had already appeared in: • Engineered Liver-Like Tissue on a Capillarized Matrix for Applied Research. TISSUE ENGINEERING; Volume 13, Number 11, 2007 Engineered Liver-Like Tissue on a Capillarized Matrix for Applied Research. KIRSTIN LINKE, JOHANNA SCHANZ, JAN HANSMANN, THORSTEN WALLES, HERWIG BRUNNER, & HEIKE MERTSCHING Apparently, no permission was obtained to re-publish the image, as the authors did not provide us with a copy of a release issued by Mary Ann Liebert Inc. with an authorization to re-publish the figure initially published in Linke et al. (2007) in J. Biotechnology. In this case, this infringes on the copyright of Mary Ann Liebert Inc. The authors stated on Feb. 11 2017 in an email to the Editor in Chief: “Between 2007 and 2010 we modified the culture conditions in out (Chief Editor comment: this means “our”) tissue models. These changes did not influence the morphology and 3 D arrangement of the cells. However, they changed the long term function of out (Chief Editor comment: this means “our”) tissue models.” Therefore, they implied that the figures shown in the 2010 paper demonstrate that there were no changes in the “morphology and 3 D arrangement of the cells” when comparing the culture conditions. It would, in our opinion, be impossible to demonstrate the similarities in the two different culture conditions by using the same figures as in the 2007 article, as both instances only show the result of the original culture condition. (b) The authors have also (self)plagiarized significant text sections from: • Genes Nutr. 2009 September; 4(3): 165-172 Penza, Jeremic, Montani, Unkila, Caimi, Mazzoleni, Diego Di Lorenzo PMCID: PMC2745740 DOI: 10.1007/s12263-009-0214-7 Springer-Verlag 2009 • Van den Belt K, Berckmans P, Vangenechten C, Verheyen R, Witters H (2004) Comparative study on the in vitro/in vivo estrogenic potencies of 17beta-estradiol, estrone, 17alpha-ethynylestradiol and nonylphenol. Aquat Toxicol 66(2):183–195 • Generation and Transplantation of an Autologous Vascularized Bioartificial Human Tissue. (2009) Clinical and Translational Research Transplantation; 27 July 2009 - Volume 88 - Issue 2 - pp 203-210 Heike Mertsching, Johanna Schanz, Volker Steger, Markus Schandar, Martin Schenk, Jan Hansmann, Iris Dally, Godehard Friedel, & Thorsten Walles One of the conditions of submission of a paper for publication is that authors declare explicitly that their work is original and has not appeared in a publication elsewhere. Re-use of any data and text should be appropriately cited. As such this article represents a severe abuse of the scientific publishing system. The scientific community takes a very strong view on this matter and apologies are offered to readers of the journal that this was not detected during the submission process

matched_article.title: Hydrolase-catalyzed fast Henry reaction of nitroalkanes and aldehydes in organic media.
matched_article.abstract: Nitroalkanes underwent fast additions to a variety of structurally diverse aldehydes under the catalysis of d-aminoacylase in DMSO. The influences of reaction conditions including solvents, temperature, enzyme concentration and molar ratio of substrates were systematically investigated. Seventeen products were obtained in short time with moderate to high yields. It is the first report on hydrolase-catalyzed fast Henry reaction in organic solvent.

statements.text: d-Aminoacylase catalyzes a fast Henry reaction between nitroalkanes and a variety of structurally diverse aldehydes in organic media.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: The hydrolase-catalyzed Henry reaction between nitroalkanes and aldehydes can produce a broad range of products in moderate to high yield.
statements.support_summary: Two studies provide only indirect support for the claim because neither actually examines a hydrolase catalyst. Gelatin and collagen proteins were reported to catalyze Henry reactions between aldehydes and nitroalkanes under mild conditions, giving nitroaldol products in aqueous and organic media and achieving good yield on 1 g scale for 4-nitrobenzaldehyde with nitromethane, but the abstract does not clearly establish a broad product scope or identify these catalysts as hydrolases (PMID: 23843902). A second study showed phosphonium ionic liquids efficiently catalyze Henry additions of different aldehydes and ketones to nitroalkanes, including high-yield access to nitroaldols and dinitromethyl derivatives, which supports broad scope/yield for the Henry reaction generally but not for hydrolase-catalyzed chemistry (PMID: 22260372). Thus, the main discrepancy is that the available supporting items address non-hydrolase or ambiguously biological catalysts rather than hydrolase-catalyzed Henry reactions specifically.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The cited abstracts suggest that Henry reactions can access multiple products in good yields, but they do not specifically demonstrate hydrolase-catalyzed reactions with broad scope, so direct support for the exact claim is limited.

statements.text: Hydrolase catalysis can be used to promote Henry reactions in organic solvent rather than only in aqueous conditions.
statements.support_summary: Direct support comes from studies showing hydrolase-family enzymes can catalyze Henry reactions in non-aqueous or mixed organic media. An R-selective hydroxynitrile lyase with an α/β-hydrolase fold catalyzed formation of (R)-β-nitro alcohols from aromatic aldehydes and nitromethane in an aqueous-organic biphasic system, with n-butyl acetate identified as the best organic solvent (PMID: 21439333). A lipase from Aspergillus niger catalyzed a Henry reaction in choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvent/water mixtures, giving much higher yield than in pure water and explicitly presenting DES as the reaction medium (PMID: 26437947). Additional items are indirect rather than claim-specific: hydrolases and papain were shown to catalyze other noncanonical reactions in organic media (PMID: 16099065, PMID: 18600639), and a review describes hydrolase promiscuity in unconventional bond-forming reactions but does not by itself prove Henry chemistry in organic solvent (PMID: 26147872).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence is direct and shows hydrolase-related catalysts promoting Henry reactions in aqueous-organic biphasic media and deep eutectic solvent-containing systems rather than only in pure aqueous conditions, while the remaining studies provide broader indirect support for hydrolase activity in organic media.

source_article.title: Effect of Virtual Reality on Functional Ankle Instability Rehabilitation: A Systematic Review.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: To systematically evaluate the effectiveness of virtual reality (VR) in the rehabilitation of patients with functional ankle instability (FAI). METHODS: Nine databases were researched, including PubMed, Cochrane Library, Web of Science, Embase, OVID, CNKI, VIP, WanFang, SinoMed, ResearchGate, and WorldWildScience. The publication date deadline was May 22, 2021. To analyze the effect of VR rehabilitation of FAI, we systematically reviewed the literature using the RevMan 5.4 software. Main Results. Five randomized controlled trials (RCTs) were included in the analysis, consisting of 137 patients with FAI; 68 of them were in the experimental group, 69 were in the control group, and all were university students. A comparison study was conducted between the two groups in terms of balance function, muscle performance, and proprioception. VR rehabilitation in the treatment of FAI was found to be significantly more effective using a 30-second single-leg standing test than conventional rehabilitation. The angular offset index of VR rehabilitation training was significantly lower than that of conventional balance training (0.66 ± 0.18 vs. 0.95 ± 0.21; P = 0.005). CONCLUSION: VR rehabilitation is effective at treating FAI. However, RCTs with higher homogeneity are needed to provide a more reliable evidence-based foundation for clinical rehabilitation.

statements.text: Virtual reality rehabilitation for patients with functional ankle instability improves balance function more than conventional rehabilitation.
statements.support_summary: Two systematic reviews/meta-analyses report that virtual reality (VR) rehabilitation improves balance outcomes in ankle injury/instability populations relative to comparison interventions, including conventional physiotherapy. A review of 10 randomized trials found a significant benefit of VR on overall balance versus conventional physiotherapy, though study quality was mostly poor-to-fair (PMID: 36891135). A later meta-analysis of 12 RCTs in patients with ankle instability found significant improvements in static and dynamic balance, functional status, and some strength outcomes with VR-based interventions versus comparators, but the abstract does not clearly specify that all comparators were conventional rehabilitation and not all participants necessarily had functional ankle instability specifically (PMID: 40856380). Overall, the supportive evidence points to balance benefits from VR, but applicability is somewhat broadened beyond the exact claim population/intervention comparator.
statements.contradict_summary: Direct trial evidence in functional/chronic ankle instability is mixed and often does not show clear superiority of VR over conventional rehabilitation. A pilot RCT in functional ankle instability concluded VR-assisted training was not comparable to traditional rehabilitation for muscle strengthening and suggested VR only as an optional adjunct, although balance was not the main reported outcome in the abstract, creating some mismatch with the claim (PMID: 29385840). Another RCT reported results showing static and some dynamic balance measures were significantly lower with VR than with conventional exercise, despite an internally inconsistent abstract conclusion claiming VR was more effective; this discrepancy makes the study difficult to interpret but raises doubt about VR superiority (PMID: 30958334). A trial in soccer players with chronic ankle instability found both VR games and balance training improved Y-balance and perceived instability, with neither clearly superior and some neuromuscular measures favoring balance training (PMID: 39733187). In basketball players with functional ankle instability, both VR and traditional training improved balance and instability, with the VR group only slightly better and with a very small effect size, suggesting limited clinical superiority (PMID: 37312892).
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear to mixed, with a slight lean toward support for VR improving balance, but not strong proof that it improves balance more than conventional rehabilitation specifically in functional ankle instability. Meta-analyses suggest overall balance benefits of VR, whereas several direct RCTs in functional/chronic ankle instability show equivalence, minimal advantage, or conflicting results rather than clear superiority.

statements.text: Virtual reality rehabilitation for patients with functional ankle instability improves postural control more than conventional balance training.
statements.support_summary: Two systematic reviews/meta-analyses suggest that virtual reality (VR) improves balance outcomes in ankle injury/instability rehabilitation, with one reporting a significant advantage for overall balance versus conventional physiotherapy across 10 RCTs, though study quality was mostly poor to fair and the population was broader than functional ankle instability (PMID: 36891135). A more specific meta-analysis in ankle instability found large pooled improvements in static and dynamic balance, ankle function, and some strength outcomes with VR-based interventions versus comparator treatments across 12 RCTs, but the abstract does not clearly specify that the comparator was conventional balance training alone (PMID: 40856380). Overall, the supportive evidence points to VR being effective for balance/postural outcomes, but it is partly indirect for the exact claim because the target population and control intervention are not always an exact match.
statements.contradict_summary: Direct head-to-head trials in functional/chronic ankle instability provide mixed to negative evidence for VR superiority over conventional training. A pilot RCT found VR-assisted rehabilitation was not comparable to traditional rehabilitation for muscle strength and suggested VR may be only an optional adjunct rather than a replacement, but this study did not directly focus on postural control as the main outcome (PMID: 29385840). Another RCT specifically comparing VR versus conventional exercise for balance in functional ankle instability reported abstract inconsistencies: the results state VR had significantly lower static and dynamic balance than conventional exercise, whereas the conclusion claims VR was more effective in some directions, so this study should be treated as conflicted/noisy evidence rather than clean contradiction (PMID: 30958334). A recent RCT in soccer players with chronic ankle instability found both VR games and balance training improved Y-balance and perceived instability, with neither showing clear superiority and balance training achieving somewhat greater neuromuscular improvements (PMID: 39733187). Another matched RCT in basketball players with functional ankle instability found both protocols improved balance and instability, with only very small between-group advantages for VR (effect size <0.2), which weakens the claim of meaningful superiority (PMID: 37312892).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although meta-analyses indicate VR is beneficial for balance in ankle instability/injury, the more directly relevant comparative trials in functional/chronic ankle instability generally show no clear superiority over conventional balance training, only trivial advantages, or conflicting results.

statements.text: Virtual reality rehabilitation for patients with functional ankle instability is effective for treating functional ankle instability.
statements.support_summary: A recent systematic review/meta-analysis of 12 RCTs in 555 participants with ankle instability found VR-based interventions significantly improved ankle function, static and dynamic balance, and plantar-flexion/eversion strength, concluding VR is a safe and effective therapy (PMID: 40856380). A broader systematic review/meta-analysis of orthopedic ankle injuries also reported a significant positive effect of VR on overall balance versus conventional physiotherapy, though it emphasized that study quality was mostly poor to fair and the population was not limited to functional ankle instability (PMID: 36891135). Several RCTs in functional/chronic ankle instability reported that both VR and comparison training improved perceived instability and balance, with VR showing small advantages or comparable benefit: improved CAIT and SEBT outcomes in basketball players with FAI (PMID: 37312892), and reduced perceived instability plus better Y-balance performance in soccer players with chronic ankle instability without clear superiority over balance training (PMID: 39733187). One small RCT comparing VR with conventional exercise has internal wording inconsistencies in the abstract, but the stated conclusion says VR was more effective for some static and medial-lateral dynamic balance outcomes in FAI (PMID: 30958334). Overall, the supporting literature consistently suggests VR rehabilitation can improve balance, perceived instability, and functional status, although superiority over standard exercise is not consistently shown.
statements.contradict_summary: A pilot RCT in FAI found VR-assisted intervention produced less muscle-strength improvement across ankle motions than traditional rehabilitation, and concluded VR was not comparable to conventional training for strengthening, though it might still serve as an optional adjunct (PMID: 29385840). A systematic review of orthopedic rehabilitation reported that, for ankle instability, VR and exercise had similar effects rather than clear superiority of VR, framing the evidence as promising but not demonstrating added benefit over exercise (PMID: 31343702). These studies do not clearly show VR is ineffective in absolute terms; rather, they mainly challenge claims of VR being better than conventional rehabilitation. Thus, the contradicting evidence is more about limited comparative advantage and mixed outcome domains than a direct refutation of effectiveness.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence is a recent meta-analysis and multiple RCTs showing VR rehabilitation improves function, balance, and perceived instability in ankle instability, while the opposing evidence mainly indicates VR is not clearly superior to conventional exercise rather than ineffective.

matched_article.title: Ultrasound Images Guided under Deep Learning in the Anesthesia Effect of the Regional Nerve Block on Scapular Fracture Surgery.
matched_article.abstract: In order to discuss the clinical characteristics of patients with scapular fracture, deep learning model was adopted in ultrasound images of patients to locate the anesthesia point of patients during scapular fracture surgery treated with the regional nerve block. 100 patients with scapular fracture who were hospitalized for emergency treatment in the hospital were recruited. Patients in the algorithm group used ultrasound-guided regional nerve block puncture, and patients in the control group used traditional body surface anatomy for anesthesia positioning. The ultrasound images of the scapula of the contrast group were used for the identification of the deep learning model and analysis of anesthesia acupuncture sites. The ultrasound images of the scapula anatomy of the patients in the contrast group were extracted, and the convolutional neural network model was employed for training and test. Moreover, the model performance was evaluated. It was found that the adoption of deep learning greatly improved the accuracy of the image. It took an average of 7.5 ± 2.07 minutes from the time the puncture needle touched the skin to the completion of the injection in the algorithm group (treated with artificial intelligence ultrasound positioning). The operation time of the control group (anatomical positioning) averaged 10.2 ± 2.62 min. Moreover, there was a significant difference between the two groups (p < 0.05). The method adopted in the contrast group had high positioning accuracy and good anesthesia effect, and the patients had reduced postoperative complications of patients (all P < 0.005). The deep learning model can effectively improve the accuracy of ultrasound images and measure and assist the treatment of future clinical cases of scapular fractures. While improving medical efficiency, it can also accurately identify patient fractures, which has great adoption potential in improving the effect of surgical anesthesia.

statements.text: In patients undergoing scapular fracture surgery with regional nerve block, ultrasound-guided anesthesia positioning assisted by a deep learning model improved puncture-site localization accuracy and anesthesia effectiveness compared with traditional body-surface anatomic positioning.

source_article.title: Molecular characterization of Legionella pneumophila-induced interleukin-8 expression in T cells.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: Legionella pneumophila is the causative agent of human Legionnaire's disease. During infection, the bacterium invades macrophages and lung epithelial cells, and replicates intracellularly. However, little is known about its interaction with T cells. We investigated the ability of L. pneumophila to infect and stimulate the production of interleukin-8 (IL-8) in T cells. The objective of this study was to assess whether L. pneumophila interferes with the immune system by interacting and infecting T cells. RESULTS: Wild-type L. pneumophila and flagellin-deficient Legionella, but not L. pneumophila lacking a functional type IV secretion system Dot/Icm, replicated in T cells. On the other hand, wild-type L. pneumophila and Dot/Icm-deficient Legionella, but not flagellin-deficient Legionella or heat-killed Legionella induced IL-8 expression. L. pneumophila activated an IL-8 promoter through the NF-kappaB and AP-1 binding regions. Wild-type L. pneumophila but not flagellin-deficient Legionella activated NF-kappaB, p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK), and transforming growth factor beta-associated kinase 1 (TAK1). Transfection of dominant negative mutants of IkappaBalpha, IkappaB kinase, NF-kappaB-inducing kinase, TAK1, MyD88, and p38 MAPK inhibited L. pneumophila-induced IL-8 activation. Inhibitors of NF-kappaB, p38 MAPK, and JNK blocked L. pneumophila-induced IL-8 expression. In addition, c-Jun, JunD, cyclic AMP response element binding protein, and activating transcription factor 1, which are substrates of p38 MAPK and JNK, bound to the AP-1 site of the IL-8 promoter. CONCLUSIONS: Taken together, L. pneumophila induced a flagellin-dependent activation of TAK1, p38 MAPK, and JNK, as well as NF-kappaB and AP-1, which resulted in IL-8 production in human T cells, presumably contributing to the immune response in Legionnaire's disease.

statements.text: In human T cells, wild-type Legionella pneumophila and flagellin-deficient Legionella pneumophila can replicate, whereas Legionella pneumophila lacking a functional Dot/Icm type IV secretion system cannot replicate.

matched_article.title: Elucidation of the outer membrane proteome of Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium utilising a lipid-based protein immobilization technique.
matched_article.abstract: Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium (S. Typhimurium) is a major cause of human gastroenteritis worldwide. The outer membrane proteins expressed by S. Typhimurium mediate the process of adhesion and internalisation within the intestinal epithelium of the host thus influencing the progression of disease. Since the outer membrane proteins are surface-exposed, they provide attractive targets for the development of improved antimicrobial agents and vaccines. Various techniques have been developed for their characterisation, but issues such as carryover of cytosolic proteins still remain a problem. In this study we attempted to characterise the surface proteome of S. Typhimurium using Lipid-based Protein Immobilisation technology in the form of LPI FlowCells. No detergents are required and no sample clean up is needed prior to downstream analysis. The immobilised proteins can be digested with proteases in multiple steps to increase sequence coverage, and the peptides eluted can be characterised directly by liquid chromatography - tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) and identified from mass spectral database searches. In this study, 54 outer membrane proteins, were identified with two or more peptide hits using a multi-step digest approach. Out of these 28 were lipoproteins, nine were involved in transport and three with enzyme activity These included the transporters BtuB which is responsible for the uptake of vitamin B12, LamB which is involved in the uptake of maltose and maltodextrins and LolB which is involved in the incorporation of lipoproteins in the outer membrane. Other proteins identified included the enzymes MltC which may play a role in cell elongation and division and NlpD which is involved in catabolic processes in cell wall formation as well as proteins involved in virulence such as Lpp1, Lpp2 and OmpX. Using a multi-step digest approach the LPI technique enables the incorporation of a multi-step protease work flow ensuring enough sequence coverage of membrane proteins subsequently leading to the identification of more membrane proteins with higher confidence. Compared to current sub-cellular fractionation procedures and previous published work, the LPI technique currently provides the widest coverage of outer membrane proteins identified as demonstrated here for Salmonella Typhimurium.

source_article.title: Brønsted Acid Catalysis-Controlling the Competition between Monomeric versus Dimeric Reaction Pathways Enhances Stereoselectivities.
source_article.abstract: Chiral phosphoric acids (CPA) have become a privileged catalyst type in organocatalysis, but the selection of the optimum catalyst is still challenging. So far hidden competing reaction pathways may limit the maximum stereoselectivities and the potential of prediction models. In CPA-catalyzed transfer hydrogenation of imines, we identified for many systems two reaction pathways with inverse stereoselectivity, featuring as active catalyst either one CPA or a hydrogen bond bridged dimer. NMR measurements and DFT calculations revealed the dimeric intermediate and a stronger substrate activation via cooperativity. Both pathways are separable: Low temperatures and high catalysts loadings favor the dimeric pathway (ee up to -98 %), while low temperatures with reduced catalyst loading favor the monomeric pathway and give significantly enhanced ee (92-99 % ee; prior 68-86 % at higher temperatures). Thus, a broad impact is expected on CPA catalysis regarding reaction optimization and prediction.

statements.text: In chiral phosphoric acid-catalyzed transfer hydrogenation of imines, a monomeric catalyst pathway and a hydrogen-bond-bridged dimeric catalyst pathway can lead to opposite stereochemical outcomes.
statements.support_summary: One mechanistic study reports that chiral phosphoric acid catalysts can operate through both monomeric and dimeric pathways in asymmetric transfer hydrogenation, with strong concentration dependence for acyclic phosphoric acids and direct evidence of catalyst aggregation from NMR, experiments, and DFT (PMID: 34122895). The abstract further states that intermolecular acid-acid interactions drastically influence both reaction rate and stereoselectivity, which is consistent with the claim that distinct monomeric versus hydrogen-bonded dimeric pathways can produce different stereochemical outcomes (PMID: 34122895). However, the abstract does not explicitly mention imines or explicitly state that the stereochemical outcomes are opposite, so support is indirect rather than definitive.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but the support is moderate and indirect. The available abstract supports the existence of both monomeric and dimeric chiral phosphoric acid pathways affecting stereoselectivity in transfer hydrogenation, yet it does not explicitly confirm opposite stereochemical outcomes for imine reduction.

statements.text: For chiral phosphoric acid-catalyzed transfer hydrogenation of imines, lower catalyst loading and low temperature favor the monomeric pathway and substantially improve enantioselectivity compared with conditions that favor the dimeric pathway.

matched_article.title: Silver-Catalyzed Dearomative [2π+2σ] Cycloadditions of Indoles with Bicyclobutanes: Access to Indoline Fused Bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes.
matched_article.abstract: Bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (BCHs) are becoming ever more important in drug design and development as bridged scaffolds that provide underexplored chemical space, but are difficult to access. Here a silver-catalyzed dearomative [2π+2σ] cycloaddition strategy for the synthesis of indoline fused BCHs from N-unprotected indoles and bicyclobutane precursors is described. The strain-release dearomative cycloaddition operates under mild conditions, tolerating a wide range of functional groups. It is capable of forming BCHs with up to four contiguous quaternary carbon centers, achieving yields of up to 99 %. In addition, a scale-up experiment and the synthetic transformations of the cycloadducts further highlighted the synthetic utility.

statements.text: Silver-catalyzed dearomative [2π+2σ] cycloaddition of N-unprotected indoles with bicyclobutanes provides access to indoline fused bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes under mild conditions with broad functional group tolerance.

statements.text: The resulting indoline fused bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane cycloadducts are synthetically useful for scale-up and further chemical transformations.
statements.support_summary: Indirect support comes from studies showing that related strained or fused bicyclic/indoline products have practical synthetic value, though not the exact indoline-fused bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane cycloadducts in the claim. A photocycloaddition study reported diverse heterobicyclo[2.1.1]hexane products formed under catalyst-free conditions with high functional-group tolerance, explicitly emphasizing synthetic utility, but it did not discuss indoline-fused systems, scale-up, or downstream derivatization specifically (PMID: 38350439). A dearomative indole annulation study provided highly functionalized fused indoline scaffolds and demonstrated synthetic potential through concise syntheses of natural-product core structures, again supporting general transformability of related indoline products rather than the precise claim about bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane cycloadducts or scale-up (PMID: 24739089). Overall, the support is thematic and indirect, and there is a discrepancy between the claim specificity and what these abstracts explicitly report.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The available abstracts suggest that related bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane and fused indoline products can be synthetically useful, but they do not directly demonstrate scale-up and further chemical transformations for the specific indoline-fused bicyclo[2.1.1]hexane cycloadducts named in the claim.

source_article.title: Assessment of Acupoint Therapy of Traditional Chinese Medicine on Cough Variant Asthma: A Meta-analysis.
source_article.abstract: Acupoint application has been used in China to treat various illnesses for ages. In cough variant asthma (CVA), the main clinical sign is episodic night cough. Acupoint application therapy of traditional Chinese medicine is an effective procedure to treat cough variant asthma. The current study is designed to systematically assess the effectiveness of acupoint application therapy in traditional medicine for patients with cough variant asthma. The comprehensive computer retrieval related to comparison between acupoint application and nonacupoint application therapy for cough variant asthma was carried out in various databases (n = 8) from database establishment until July 4, 2021. Both English and Chinese articles about original investigations in humans were searched. Two independent authors extracted the data, and disagreements were resolved by discussion. ReviewManager 5.3 software provided by Cochrane did a meta-analysis of selected randomized controlled trials (RCTs). Quality of experimentation and risk bias were analyzed by the Cochrane Handbook tool. A total of thirteen randomized controlled clinical articles along with 1237 patients were included in the study. Findings of meta-analysis showed that compared with nonacupoint application treatment, the total effective rate of acupoint application treatment is more effective (RD = 0.13, 95% CI (0.09, 0.17), Z = 6.70, P < 0.00001). Besides, acupoint application can improve patients' lung function, the lung function index FVC (mean difference = 0.55, 95% confidence interval (0.42, 0.68), Z = 8.40, P < 0.00001), FEV1 (MD = 0.35, 95% CI (0.23, 0.47), Z = 5.86, P < 0.00001), FEV1/FVC (%) (MD = 12.68, 95% CI (4.32, 21.03), Z = 2.97, P = 0.003), FEV1 (%) (MD = 8.63, 95% CI (8.01, 9.25), Z = 27.44, P < 0.00001), and PEF (day) (MD = 0.62, 95% CI (0.52, 0.71), Z = 12.40, P < 0.00001) of patients treated by acupoint application therapy were increased. Moreover, acupoint application might lower the level of immunoglobulin E (MD = -54.58, 95% CI (-63.54, -45.61), Z = 11.93, P < 0.00001) and EOS (MD = -0.21, 95% CI (-0.35, -0.06), Z = 2.77, P = 0.006). The LCQ (Leicester cough questionnaire) total score of CVA patients was also increased (MD = 2.30, 95% CI (1.55, 3.06), Z = 5.98, P < 0.00001). Acupoint application therapy is effective in controlling symptoms of CVA. It also has a positive effect in improving lung function and life quality of patients. It can reduce the eosinophil levels and peripheral blood IgE levels of patients as well.

statements.text: In patients with cough variant asthma, acupoint application therapy is more effective than nonacupoint application therapy for overall clinical improvement and symptom control.
statements.support_summary: The most direct evidence is a meta-analysis of 13 RCTs in childhood cough variant asthma showing acupoint sticking therapy outperformed non-acupoint sticking therapy for total effective rate, reduced 2-year recurrence, and shortened cough duration, with some improvement in inflammatory markers, although the authors note that better-quality trials are needed (PMID: 32186073). Several additional studies/meta-analyses in CVA or asthma report that acupoint-based interventions improve symptoms, lung function, recurrence, or overall response versus standard treatments or other controls, including acupuncture for CVA (PMID: 33868443), moxibustion for CVA (PMID: 38467499), micro-invasive embedding plus montelukast in children with CVA (PMID: 29231431), and auricular acupressure-based integrative care in pediatric CVA (PMID: 41398784). Broader asthma evidence also suggests acupoint application may improve pulmonary function and clinical response compared with other therapies (PMID: 37740212), and acupoint injection outperformed intramuscular injection in bronchial asthma (PMID: 25845224). However, most of these supporting items are only indirectly relevant because they do not specifically compare acupoint application with nonacupoint application in cough variant asthma, so there is a relevance discrepancy within the support set. Overall, the direct support for the exact claim mainly comes from PMID: 32186073, while the rest provide contextual but less specific support.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because there is one directly relevant meta-analysis showing acupoint sticking is superior to non-acupoint sticking in childhood cough variant asthma (PMID: 32186073), and several additional studies suggest benefit of acupoint-based therapies in CVA/asthma. However, confidence is tempered because much of the cited support is indirect, involving other acupoint modalities, comparators, or broader asthma populations rather than the exact acupoint-versus-nonacupoint application comparison.

statements.text: In patients with cough variant asthma, acupoint application therapy improves lung function more than nonacupoint application therapy.
statements.support_summary: Directly relevant evidence comes mainly from a meta-analysis in childhood cough variant asthma comparing acupoint sticking/stimulation with non-acupoint sticking, which found better overall efficacy, lower recurrence, and shorter cough duration, but the abstract does not report lung-function superiority, so support for the specific lung-function claim is only indirect (PMID: 32186073). Several broader asthma studies report that acupoint application or related acupoint-based interventions improved pulmonary function indices such as FEV1, FVC, FEV1/FVC, and PEF versus conventional or non-acupoint/non-acupoint-like comparators, including an adult asthma meta-analysis (PMID: 26978427), a bronchial asthma network meta-analysis where acupoint application ranked best for pulmonary function (PMID: 37740212), and an acupoint-injection trial superior to intramuscular injection for FEV1/FVC and PEF% (PMID: 25845224). In CVA specifically, a meta-analysis of acupuncture found improved lung-function-related indicators versus control, but it did not specifically test acupoint application against nonacupoint application (PMID: 33868443), and an observational pediatric CVA study reported better FEV1/FVC with auricular acupressure plus nutritional/ferulic acid support versus standard care, though this is not a direct nonacupoint-application comparison and includes cointerventions (PMID: 41398784). Another pediatric CVA RCT of micro-invasive embedding plus montelukast versus montelukast alone suggested improved pulmonary/small-airway indices, but this is not a direct acupoint-versus-nonacupoint application comparison (PMID: 29231431). Overall, much of the SUPPORT set is only partially aligned with the claim because many studies involve asthma rather than CVA, or compare acupoint therapy with usual care rather than with nonacupoint application.
statements.contradict_summary: One multicenter randomized double-blind trial in bronchial asthma found that medication acupoint sticking improved symptoms and quality of life versus placebo sticking, but showed no significant between-group difference in FEV1 or FEV1/FVC, directly weakening the claim that acupoint application improves lung function more than control application (PMID: 19947260). However, this study was in bronchial asthma rather than cough variant asthma, so its contradiction is relevant but indirect for the exact population in the claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but the evidence is not strong for the exact comparison. The most relevant CVA evidence suggests acupoint-based therapy outperforms non-acupoint sticking in children, yet lung-function superiority is not clearly reported in that abstract, while broader asthma literature more consistently supports pulmonary-function benefit and one randomized asthma trial reports no lung-function advantage.

statements.text: In patients with cough variant asthma, acupoint application therapy improves quality of life and is associated with lower eosinophil and immunoglobulin E levels than nonacupoint application therapy.
statements.support_summary: Evidence directly relevant to cough variant asthma comes mainly from a pediatric meta-analysis showing acupoint sticking versus non-acupoint sticking improved overall effectiveness, reduced recurrence and cough duration, and lowered IgE, while eosinophil reduction was not statistically significant (PMID: 32186073). Additional indirect support comes from asthma/CVA studies reporting that acupoint-based interventions improve symptoms, lung function, and some inflammatory or immune markers, including lower IgE in adult asthma meta-analysis and improved immune-inflammatory indicators in CVA acupuncture meta-analysis (PMID: 26978427; PMID: 33868443). One RCT in CVA found moxibustion improved symptoms and pulmonary function with changes in eosinophils and cytokines, but it compared against inhaled medication rather than nonacupoint application, so it is only indirect for the specific claim (PMID: 38467499). Another pediatric RCT of micro-invasive embedding plus montelukast suggested better clinical outcomes than montelukast alone, but the abstract does not clearly provide the claimed quality-of-life or nonacupoint-comparator evidence (PMID: 29231431). Overall, the best abstract-level support for the specific statement is limited and only one item directly addresses nonacupoint comparison; notably, none of the abstracts clearly reports quality-of-life outcomes.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. There is, however, an internal limitation within the supporting set: the most directly relevant meta-analysis did not find a statistically significant reduction in eosinophils despite lower IgE and better clinical outcomes (PMID: 32186073), which weakens that portion of the claim rather than serving as direct contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but only partially. Support is driven mainly by one pediatric meta-analysis directly comparing acupoint with nonacupoint application and showing better clinical outcomes plus lower IgE (PMID: 32186073), while evidence for improved quality of life is not clearly reported and evidence for lower eosinophils is inconsistent or indirect.

matched_article.title: The Effect of an Educational Intervention on Oral Health Literacy, Knowledge, and Behavior in Iranian Adolescents: A Theory-Based Randomized Controlled Trial.
matched_article.abstract: Oral health is one of the most important issues in public health. Most educational interventions, as the primary prevention strategy, are focused on increasing information and knowledge and are not usually effective. Therefore, the present study is aimed at determining the effect of theory-based education on oral health behavior and its psychological determinants including dental health literacy. This randomized controlled educational trial was conducted in two girls' high schools that were selected by multistage cluster sampling and were divided into an intervention and a control group. Literacy, knowledge, oral health behavior, dental plaque index, and constructs of the protection motivation theory were evaluated before and one month after four training sessions. Finally, the data were entered into the SPSS 19 software and were analyzed using the chi-square test, independent t-test, and Mann-Whitney test at the significant level of 0.05. Before the intervention, there was no significant difference between the intervention and control groups regarding the mean scores of knowledge, behavior, and oral health literacy; plaque index; and protection motivation theory constructs. After the educational intervention, however, the means of these variables were significantly improved in the intervention group compared to the controls (p < 0.05). The study findings were in favor of the effectiveness of the theory-based educational intervention in improving the knowledge, literacy, and behavior related to oral health. Yet, further research is suggested to determine the effectiveness of such an intervention in male students as well as in populations with different socioeconomic and cultural statuses.

statements.text: A theory-based educational intervention for adolescent girls in high school improved oral health literacy, oral health knowledge, oral health behavior, and dental plaque compared with no educational intervention.
statements.support_summary: Five studies broadly support that school-based oral health education can improve adolescent oral health behaviors and clinical hygiene outcomes versus control conditions, though most do not match the claim exactly on population or outcomes. A randomized trial in Nepal found large gains in oral hygiene knowledge, attitude, and practices plus improved plaque and gingival indices after repeated education, with no improvement in controls (PMID: 34649553). Theory-informed interventions in adolescents also improved brushing/flossing and plaque or periodontal outcomes, including planning/implementation-intention interventions in Iran (PMID: 26791023) and a peer-led Health Belief Model/Social Cognitive Theory intervention in Hong Kong that improved brushing, flossing, plaque index, and quality of life (PMID: 34521576). More population-specific but less directly aligned evidence includes an intervention in adolescent girls in Iranian schools that improved self-efficacy/expectancy-related determinants and promoted oral health behavior (PMID: 33224998), and a Tehran school-based trial showing better oral cleanliness and gingival health after leaflet or videotape education (PMID: 19320914). A key discrepancy is that these abstracts rarely report oral health literacy explicitly, and several are not limited to high-school girls or to a no-intervention control, so support is indirect/partial rather than exact.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted because multiple controlled school-based studies in adolescents show improved oral health knowledge/behavior and better plaque or gingival outcomes after educational interventions. However, the evidence is not fully exact for all elements of the claim, especially oral health literacy, the exclusive focus on high-school girls, and the specific intervention format/control condition.

statements.text: A theory-based educational intervention for adolescent girls in high school improved psychological determinants of oral health behavior based on protection motivation theory compared with no educational intervention.
statements.support_summary: One directly relevant school-based intervention study in adolescent girls reported that oral-health education improved psychological determinants related to oral health behavior: after four sessions, the intervention group showed increased self-efficacy, outcome expectation, and outcome expectancy compared with controls, supporting the general claim that theory-based education can improve determinants of oral health behavior (PMID: 33224998). A second study in adolescent girls found that a web-based, theory-driven program improved attitude, subjective norms, intention, and reduced hookah smoking versus control, which is only indirect support because it targeted tobacco prevention rather than oral health and used the theory of planned behavior rather than protection motivation theory (PMID: 35178198). Overall, the support side is driven mainly by one oral-health intervention study, while the other item is a thematic but not claim-specific analogue. No clear discrepancy is evident within the support set, aside from the mismatch in topic/theory for PMID: 35178198.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because there is one reasonably relevant controlled school-based oral health intervention study showing improved psychological determinants in adolescent girls (PMID: 33224998), and no direct contradictory evidence. However, support is only moderate because the provided evidence does not clearly establish protection motivation theory specifically, and one supporting study is only indirectly related (PMID: 35178198).

source_article.title: X-Ray Film under Artificial Intelligence Algorithm in the Evaluation for Nursing Effect of Gamma Nail Internal Fixation in Elderly Patients with Intertrochanteric Fracture of Femur.
source_article.abstract: The aim of this work was to explore the effects of Gamma nail internal fixation for intertrochanteric fracture of femur by X-ray film classification and recognition method based on artificial intelligence algorithm. The study subjects were 100 elderly patients with intertrochanteric fracture of femur admitted to hospital. The cases were diagnosed as elderly (over 60 years old) femoral intertrochanteric fractures by X-ray or CT. They were divided into two groups, with 50 persons in each group: one group used the X-ray film evaluation image guidance based on the artificial intelligence algorithm (research group), and the other group did not use algorithmic guidance (control group). The results showed that the segmentation effect of the proposed algorithm was similar to the gold standard segmentation result, indicating that the algorithm was effective and feasible in the segmentation of fractures and bones. The global level set algorithm was set as the control. The ultimate measurement accuracy (UMA) value of the algorithm group was (1.77 ± 0.22), and the UMA value of the global level set algorithm group was (3.42 ± 0.36), indicating that the image processed by the algorithm group had obvious numerical effect, high accuracy, and good retention of details. The operation time, intraoperative blood loss, incision length, hospital stay, weight-bearing time, and fracture healing time of the two groups were all better than those of the control group. One month after surgery, the Harris score of the algorithm group was 67, and that of the control group was 51, with a 16-point difference between the two groups (p < 0.05). The patient had less pain and fast recovery speed, indicating that it was a good way to treat elderly intertrochanteric fractures with the nursing effect of X-ray Gamma nail internal fixation based on an artificial intelligence algorithm. The artificial intelligence algorithm not only can be applied to the Gamma nail internal fixation of elderly patients with intertrochanteric fractures but also can be applied to the X-ray image processing of other fractures and other surgical methods to provide effective treatment for fracture patients.

statements.text: In elderly patients with intertrochanteric femur fracture undergoing Gamma nail internal fixation, use of artificial intelligence-guided X-ray image evaluation improved postoperative recovery and functional outcome compared with conventional X-ray evaluation without algorithmic guidance.
