matched_article.title: A Hybrid Method to Predict Postoperative Survival of Lung Cancer Using Improved SMOTE and Adaptive SVM.
matched_article.abstract: Predicting postoperative survival of lung cancer patients (LCPs) is an important problem of medical decision-making. However, the imbalanced distribution of patient survival in the dataset increases the difficulty of prediction. Although the synthetic minority oversampling technique (SMOTE) can be used to deal with imbalanced data, it cannot identify data noise. On the other hand, many studies use a support vector machine (SVM) combined with resampling technology to deal with imbalanced data. However, most studies require manual setting of SVM parameters, which makes it difficult to obtain the best performance. In this paper, a hybrid improved SMOTE and adaptive SVM method is proposed for imbalance data to predict the postoperative survival of LCPs. The proposed method is divided into two stages: in the first stage, the cross-validated committees filter (CVCF) is used to remove noise samples to improve the performance of SMOTE. In the second stage, we propose an adaptive SVM, which uses fuzzy self-tuning particle swarm optimization (FPSO) to optimize the parameters of SVM. Compared with other advanced algorithms, our proposed method obtains the best performance with 95.11% accuracy, 95.10% G-mean, 95.02% F1, and 95.10% area under the curve (AUC) for predicting postoperative survival of LCPs.

statements.text: A hybrid improved SMOTE and adaptive support vector machine approach can predict postoperative survival in patients with lung cancer more accurately than other advanced algorithms.

source_article.title: Do precarious female employment and political autonomy affect the under-5 mortality rate? Evidence from 166 countries.
source_article.abstract: The United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) were designed to benefit the globalized world by safeguarding economic and environmental resources necessary for quality health and well-being and moderate growth and development. The study focused specifically on SDG-3 (good health and well-being), SDG-5 (gender equality), and SDG-8 (decent work and economic growth) to identify the most significant influencing factors that can affect the under-5 mortality rate in a large cross-section of 166 countries. The research used three different regression apparatuses to produce consistent and unbiased estimates: cross-sectional, robust least squares, and quantile regression approaches. Additionally, the innovation accounting matrix technique examines the intertemporal relationships between the variables over the time horizon. The data reveal that precarious female employment increases the under-5 mortality rate. On the other hand, women's political autonomy continued economic growth, and higher immunization coverage is supporting factors for achieving healthcare sustainability agenda. The ex-ante analysis indicates that per capita income will significantly impact the under-5 mortality rate, followed by women's political autonomy, insecure female employment, and immunization coverage during the next ten years. The results are consistent with other health indicators such as the health damage function, labour market function, and wealth function. The study suggests that the more unlocking women's potential in political life, the more likely it is to achieve equitable healthcare choices and reduce the mortality rate among children under five. As a result, there is an urgent need for women to have an equitable share of the labour market to appropriately meet their family healthcare demands.

statements.text: Precarious female employment is associated with higher under-5 mortality in countries.
statements.support_summary: One Nigeria birth-history analysis found that overall maternal employment was associated with slightly lower infant and child mortality than unemployment, but also reported higher mortality risks among certain maternal occupations such as sales and agriculture/manual work, suggesting that occupation type and context matter for child survival (PMID: 30873781). A cross-country OLS analysis of 43 African countries within a 109-country pool identified female employment rate as one of several factors associated with under-5 mortality differences, although the abstract does not specify directionality for female employment or address precarious employment directly (PMID: 30873806). Overall, the supporting evidence is indirect and mixed: one study is more about unemployment versus employment with some higher-risk job categories, and the other only flags female employment rate as an important correlate, so there is a discrepancy between the claim's focus on precarious employment and the abstracts' more general employment measures.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The abstracts suggest that maternal employment patterns and certain occupations may relate to under-5 mortality, but they do not directly establish that precarious female employment is associated with higher under-5 mortality across countries.

statements.text: Women’s political autonomy is associated with lower under-5 mortality in countries.
statements.support_summary: Cross-national evidence generally supports an inverse association between women's political empowerment and child mortality. A global panel study across 161 countries found that the Women's Political Empowerment Index was associated with child mortality improvements, although the effect size was small and varied by country income group, indicating a nuanced rather than uniformly strong relationship (PMID: 33307997). A study of 193 countries reported that a broader gender equality construct, including women's representation in leadership and parliament, was significantly associated with lower under-5 mortality, though this is somewhat indirect because the exposure was not limited to political autonomy alone (PMID: 34982333). Evidence from 59 LMICs also linked higher women's empowerment to lower under-5 mortality, but this mainly reflects household/decision-making empowerment rather than specifically political autonomy, so its relevance is supportive but indirect (PMID: 32133162). A US state-level ecological study found better child outcomes with higher women's political participation and status, but it focused on infant mortality and other child well-being measures rather than under-5 mortality across countries, making it only partially relevant (PMID: 16962693).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from cross-national studies showing that women's political empowerment or related gender equality measures are associated with lower child mortality, although some studies are indirect or report small/heterogeneous effects rather than a uniformly strong association.

statements.text: Higher immunization coverage and continued economic growth are associated with lower under-5 mortality in countries.
statements.support_summary: The supporting evidence is mostly indirect but generally consistent with the claim that better child-health intervention coverage and stronger socioeconomic conditions are linked to lower under-5 mortality. A Peru case study found a 58% decline in under-5 mortality alongside increased coverage of essential child health interventions, reduced poverty, and sustained economic growth, and explicitly interpreted this progress as resulting from a combination of economic growth and pro-poor health strategies (PMID: 27198845). Cross-national and ecological analyses also suggest that women's political empowerment is associated with improved immunisation and some child mortality benefits (PMID: 33307997), and that poorer Brazilian regions had both worse under-5 mortality and worse broader development profiles while vaccine coverage was one of the stronger-performing indicators nationally (PMID: 32993666), though these do not directly test the specific claim. Additional indirect support comes from a review reporting associations of paid parental leave with higher immunization rates and lower infant mortality (PMID: 29450093), and from regression analyses in developing countries showing per capita income as a significant predictor of child survivability (PMID: 20168038). A notable limitation is that several studies do not directly analyze immunization coverage and economic growth together against under-5 mortality, so the support is suggestive rather than definitive.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted because the available studies consistently point toward lower child mortality in settings with stronger economic conditions and better child-health intervention coverage, with some evidence specifically involving immunisation. However, the evidence base here is mostly indirect and ecological, and few abstracts directly test the combined association of higher immunization coverage and continued economic growth with under-5 mortality.

matched_article.title: Short-form adaptive measure of financial toxicity from the Economic Strain and Resilience in Cancer (ENRICh) study: Derivation using modern psychometric techniques.
matched_article.abstract: This study sought to evaluate advanced psychometric properties of the 15-item Economic Strain and Resilience in Cancer (ENRICh) measure of financial toxicity for cancer patients. We surveyed 515 cancer patients in the greater Houston metropolitan area using ENRICh from March 2019 to March 2020. We conducted a series of factor analyses alongside parametric and non-parametric item response theory (IRT) assessments using Mokken analysis and the graded response model (GRM). We utilized parameters derived from the GRM to run a simulated computerized adaptive test (CAT) assessment. Among participants, mean age was 58.49 years and 278 (54%) were female. The initial round factor analysis results suggested a one-factor scale structure. Negligible levels of differential item functioning (DIF) were evident between eight items. Three items were removed due to local interdependence (Q3>+0.4). The original 11-point numerical rating scale did not function well, and a new 3-point scoring system was implemented. The final 12-item ENRICh had acceptable fit to the GRM (p<0.001; TLI = 0.94; CFI = 0.95; RMSEA = 0.09; RMSR = 0.06) as well as good scalability and dimensionality. We observed high correlation between CAT version scores and the 12-item measure (r = 0.98). During CAT, items 2 (money you owe) and 4 (stress level about finances) were most frequently administered, followed by items 1 (money in savings) and 5 (ability to pay bills). Scores from these four items alone were strongly correlated with that of the 12-item ENRICh (r = 0.96). These CAT and 4-item versions provide options for quick screening in clinical practice and low-burden assessment in research.

statements.text: Among cancer patients, the final 12-item Economic Strain and Resilience in Cancer measure provided a valid and reliable assessment of financial toxicity with acceptable model fit and good unidimensionality.

source_article.title: A new plasma-adapted hydroxyethylstarch preparation: in vitro coagulation studies using thrombelastography and whole blood aggregometry.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: The lack of acceptance of hydroxyethylstarch (HES) for intravascular volume replacement is most likely due to reports of abnormal coagulation. In a blinded in vitro study, we compared the effects on hemostasis of a new HES, prepared in a balanced solution, with a conventional HES preparation and Ringer's lactate solution. METHODS: Blood was taken from 10 healthy young male volunteers. Blood was diluted by 10%, 30%, and 50% using either 6% HES 130/0.42 prepared in a balanced solution, a conventional nonbalanced 6% HES 130/0.4 or Ringer's lactate solution. Rotation thrombelastography, was performed after adding two activators (thromboplastin-phospholipid to monitor the intrinsic system; tissue factor to monitor the extrinsic system). Whole blood aggregometry adding adenosine diphosphate, collagen, and thrombin receptor-activating protein was used to assess changes of platelet function. RESULTS: Dilution of blood (30% and 50%) resulted in clot formation time that was significantly more prolonged in the nonbalanced than in the balanced HES group. In the 50% diluted sample using the unbalanced HES, maximum clot firmness was significantly more reduced than by 50% dilution using the balanced HES. In the 50% diluent using the nonbalanced HES, adenosine diphosphate-, collagen-, and thrombin receptor activating protein-induced aggregometry was more reduced than in the balanced HES group. CONCLUSIONS: A balanced HES preparation showed fewer negative effects on thrombelastographic data and platelet aggregation than a nonbalanced HES preparation, especially when using higher degrees of dilution. Future clinical studies may show a decreased influence of balanced HES solutions on coagulation.

statements.text: In diluted whole blood from healthy adult male volunteers, a balanced hydroxyethylstarch preparation had fewer adverse effects on clot formation and clot strength than a conventional nonbalanced hydroxyethylstarch preparation.
statements.support_summary: One ex vivo thrombelastography study in blood from healthy volunteers reported that balanced salt colloid formulations caused fewer coagulation abnormalities than saline-based colloids, with balanced salt pentastarch showing the least derangement across tested dilutions (PMID: 16551936). The abstract also notes that smaller-molecular-weight starches and balanced carrier solutions preserved coagulation better than larger starches and saline-based formulations, which is directionally consistent with the claim. However, this study compared several fluids and formulations rather than a clean head-to-head comparison of the exact balanced versus conventional HES preparations in the claim, so support is indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: A study of combined balanced versus unbalanced colloid/crystalloid hemodilution found that a totally balanced fluid concept had similar in vitro coagulation effects to corresponding unbalanced combinations, arguing against a clear advantage of balanced HES for clot formation and strength (PMID: 21610182). Another ex vivo thromboelastometry study found that balanced HES priming solutions did not preserve coagulation parameters, especially the fibrin contribution to clot formation, but this compared balanced HES with gelatin rather than with nonbalanced HES, so its placement as contradiction to this specific claim is a mismatch (PMID: 21177722). A randomized crossover trial in healthy male volunteers found lower ADP-induced platelet aggregation after saline-based tetrastarch than balanced tetrastarch, but no significant differences in rotational thromboelastometry clot formation parameters, suggesting little impact of carrier solution on clot formation/strength at the studied dose and thus only weak contradiction to the claim (PMID: 22791802). Overall, the contradicting set is mixed, with one directly relevant negative study and two only partially relevant or miscategorized relative to the exact claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall slightly more contradicted than supported. The strongest directly relevant evidence reports no meaningful coagulation advantage for balanced versus unbalanced fluid combinations (PMID: 21610182), while the supportive evidence is indirect and not an exact match to the specific head-to-head claim.

statements.text: In diluted whole blood from healthy adult male volunteers, a balanced hydroxyethylstarch preparation impaired platelet aggregation less than a conventional nonbalanced hydroxyethylstarch preparation.
statements.support_summary: One randomized, double-blinded crossover trial in 13 healthy male volunteers found that ADP-induced platelet aggregation decreased after saline-based tetrastarch infusion but not after balanced-solution tetrastarch infusion, and aggregation was significantly lower with saline-based than balanced HES (PMID: 22791802). This directly aligns with the claim that a balanced HES preparation impairs platelet aggregation less than a conventional nonbalanced preparation. However, the same abstract also states that the carrier solution had only little overall impact on platelet aggregation or clot formation, which slightly tempers the strength of support (PMID: 22791802).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. Support comes from a directly relevant randomized crossover study in healthy male volunteers showing less reduction in ADP-induced platelet aggregation with balanced versus saline-based HES, although the evidence base here is limited to a single small study.

statements.text: The coagulation-preserving advantage of a balanced hydroxyethylstarch preparation over a conventional nonbalanced hydroxyethylstarch preparation was greater at higher degrees of hemodilution.
statements.support_summary: One study reported that a balanced salt-based HES caused less impairment of thromboelastography parameters and aPTT than a saline-based HES during liver transplantation, with additional findings of higher calcium and less platelet reduction in the balanced group (PMID: 18289228). However, this study involved only modest in vitro dilution (11%) and a fixed in vivo infusion, so it supports a general coagulation-preserving advantage of balanced HES but does not directly test whether that advantage becomes greater at higher degrees of hemodilution.
statements.contradict_summary: An in vitro ROTEM study found that balanced colloid/crystalloid combinations had coagulation effects similar to corresponding unbalanced combinations, arguing against a specific balanced-fluid advantage across 20% and 40% hemodilution; notably, this evidence is only indirectly relevant because it evaluates combined fluid regimens rather than balanced vs conventional HES alone (PMID: 21610182). Another ex vivo study reported that balanced HES priming solutions did not preserve coagulation, especially the fibrin component of clot formation, and in fact produced larger decreases in clot firmness than gelatin-based solutions (PMID: 21177722). This second paper is also only partially on-point because it compares balanced HES with gelatin rather than with a conventional nonbalanced HES preparation. Overall, the contradicting set mainly challenges the notion of a coagulation benefit from balanced HES, but only weakly addresses the claim's specific emphasis on superiority over nonbalanced HES becoming greater at higher hemodilution.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear, with slight support for a general coagulation-preserving effect of balanced HES but little direct evidence on whether that advantage increases at higher hemodilution. The supporting study does not test multiple dilution levels, and the contradicting studies are partly off-target because they compare different fluid strategies rather than balanced versus conventional nonbalanced HES under escalating hemodilution.

matched_article.title: The local antinociceptive actions of nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs in the mouse radiant heat tail-flick test.
matched_article.abstract: While many preclinical models detect the analgesic activity of nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), the radiant heat tail-flick response has repeatedly been insensitive to this class of drugs. As the tail-flick test involves nociceptive processing at spinal circuits with supraspinal modulation, it seems reasonable to assume that the NSAIDs should not modify strong nociceptive stimuli, since the primary site of action of NSAIDs is likely to be in the periphery. We injected 3-300 mug of diclofenac, dipyrone, ketorolac, lysine acetyl salicylate, and sodium salicylate intradermally into mice tails and evaluated the tail-flick response to radiant heat. These results were compared with intraperitoneally injected controls. We also evaluated the ability of naloxone to reverse the observed effects. Intradermal injection of each NSAID produced a dose-dependent increase in tail-flick latency. Intraperitoneal NSAIDs injection produced no antinociceptive effects. Naloxone pretreatment had no effect on the antinociceptive effects of intradermal diclofenac, ketorolac, lysine acetyl salicylate, and sodium salicylate. Naloxone completely blocked the antinociceptive effects of intradermal dipyrone. Local, but not systemic, administration of NSAIDs produced antinociception in the tail-flick thermal assay. The endogenous opioid system contributes to the peripheral antinociceptive effects of dipyrone, but not to that of diclofenac, ketorolac, lysine acetyl salicylate, or sodium salicylate, suggesting differences in the mechanisms of action among the NSAIDs.

statements.text: In mice subjected to a radiant heat tail-flick test, local intradermal administration of nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs produces antinociception, whereas systemic intraperitoneal administration of the same drugs does not.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Several abstracts report that systemic intraperitoneal NSAID administration does produce thermal antinociception, directly opposing the claim’s assertion that i.p. dosing is ineffective. In mice, multiple studies found i.p. NSAIDs increased tail-flick latency or otherwise showed thermal antinociception, including ketoprofen, diclofenac, piroxicam, paracetamol and related agents in tail-flick assays (PMID: 11854764; PMID: 12056508), as well as dipyrone/metamizol in thermal tests including tail-flick or heat-threshold paradigms (PMID: 9592021; PMID: 23957272). Additional rat studies also showed i.p. NSAIDs or metamizol inhibited tail-flick responses (PMID: 18560039; PMID: 3808743), though these are less directly matched to the mouse claim. One study used hot-plate/formalin rather than tail-flick, so it is only indirect contradiction despite showing i.p. acetylsalicylic acid antinociception (PMID: 9088041). No contradicting abstract addresses the claim’s local intradermal component directly, so the contradiction centers mainly on the statement that systemic i.p. administration does not cause antinociception.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted. While no evidence here evaluates the local intradermal portion, several abstracts—especially in mice and including tail-flick or other thermal assays—show that systemic intraperitoneal NSAIDs can produce antinociception, which conflicts with the claim’s key negative assertion.

statements.text: In mice subjected to a radiant heat tail-flick test, the peripheral antinociceptive effect of intradermal dipyrone depends on the endogenous opioid system, whereas the peripheral antinociceptive effects of intradermal diclofenac, ketorolac, lysine acetyl salicylate, and sodium salicylate do not.
statements.support_summary: One study found that naloxone pretreatment did not alter ketorolac-induced antinociception in mice, suggesting ketorolac's analgesic effect is not mediated by opioid receptors (PMID: 8214659). However, this study used the acetic acid writhing test and systemic/CNS routes rather than the specific intradermal radiant heat tail-flick model in the claim, so it provides only partial, indirect support.
statements.contradict_summary: Several abstracts report opioid involvement for NSAID/dipyrone antinociception, but most differ importantly from the claim's peripheral intradermal mouse tail-flick context. Dipyrone antinociception was reported as naloxone-insensitive in mouse formalin/capsaicin models, which directly conflicts with the claim that dipyrone's peripheral effect depends on the endogenous opioid system (PMID: 9592021). Other studies found naloxone-sensitive antinociception for lysine-acetylsalicylate in rats and for diclofenac, ketorolac, and metamizol after microinjection into the nucleus raphe magnus, implying opioid mediation for drugs the claim says are opioid-independent, but these are central/systemic rat models rather than peripheral intradermal mouse testing (PMID: 15327823; PMID: 28574391). A further dipyrone study addressed cannabinoid/FAAH mechanisms rather than opioid dependence, so its placement as contradiction is a discrepancy and it is only weakly relevant to the claim (PMID: 22954646).
statements.conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear to slightly contradicted. The only supportive study is indirect and limited to ketorolac, while the contradiction set includes one more directly conflicting dipyrone study plus additional opioid-mechanism findings for other NSAIDs, though most are not in the same peripheral intradermal mouse tail-flick model as the claim.

source_article.title: LncRNA-PAX8-AS1 Silencing Decreases Cell Viability, Enhances Apoptosis, and Suppresses Doxorubicin Resistance in Myeloid Leukemia via the miR-378g/ERBB2 Axis.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: Considering the role of lncRNAs reported as regulators in acute myeloid leukemia (AML) progression, the current research aims to investigate the role of PAX8-AS1 in chemo-resistant AML. METHODS: Human AML cells HL60 and human doxorubicin (ADM)-resistant AML cells (HL60/ADM cells) were used to establish in vitro models of chemo-sensitive AML and refractory/recurrent AML, respectively. CCK-8 assay and flow cytometry were used to determine cell resistance to ADM, viability, and apoptosis. PAX8-AS1, miR-378g, and ERBB2 expressions in the models and/or AML patients were quantified via qRT-PCR or Western blot. The miRNA/mRNA axis targeted by PAX8-AS1 was analyzed using Starbase, TargetScan, or GEO and validated through a dual-luciferase reporter assay. The expressions of Bcl-2, Bax, and C Caspase-3 in cells were quantitated by Western blot. RESULTS: The highly expressed PAX8-AS1 was observed in AML patients and HL60 cells, which was more evident in refractory/recurrent AML patients and HL60/ADM cells. Compared with that in ADM-treated parental HL60 cells, the viability of ADM-treated HL60/ADM cells remained strong. PAX8-AS1 overexpression increased viability and Bcl-2 expression, while diminishing apoptosis, Bax, and C Caspase-3 expressions in HL60 cells. However, the abovementioned aspects were oppositely impacted by PAX8-AS1 silencing in HL60/ADM cells. PAX8-AS1 directly targeted miR-378g, whose expression pattern is opposite to that of PAX8-AS1 in AML. MiR-378g upregulation abrogated the effects of PAX8-AS1 overexpression on HL60 cells. MiR-378g downregulation offset PAX8-AS1 silencing-induced effects on HL60/ADM cells. Moreover, ERBB2 was recognized as the target of miR-378g, with a higher expression in HL60/ADM cells than in HL60 cells. CONCLUSION: PAX8-AS1 silencing decreases cell viability, enhances apoptosis, and suppresses ADM resistance in AML via regulating the miR-378g/ERBB2 axis.

statements.text: Silencing lncRNA PAX8-AS1 decreases viability, increases apoptosis, and reduces doxorubicin resistance in acute myeloid leukemia cells.
statements.support_summary: The only cited supporting item is a retraction notice rather than a research abstract, so the abstract itself does not provide usable experimental evidence for the claim (PMID: 37387868). The title of the retracted article states that PAX8-AS1 silencing decreases viability, increases apoptosis, and suppresses doxorubicin resistance in myeloid leukemia, which matches the claim, but this is undermined by the retraction (PMID: 37387868). This is therefore a major discrepancy between the item's categorization as support and the actual reliability of the evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is currently weakly supported at best. Although the title of a retracted paper matches the statement, the only provided evidence is a retraction notice rather than valid primary evidence, so the overall evidence is insufficient and unreliable.

statements.text: lncRNA PAX8-AS1 promotes acute myeloid leukemia cell survival and resistance to doxorubicin, while PAX8-AS1 silencing has the opposite effect.
statements.support_summary: The only cited supporting item is a retraction notice rather than a study abstract with results; it states that it retracts a prior article on PAX8-AS1 silencing in myeloid leukemia (PMID: 37387868). Because no experimental findings are provided in the abstract itself, this item does not offer usable abstract-level evidence that PAX8-AS1 promotes AML cell survival or doxorubicin resistance. In fact, the retracted status is a major discrepancy with the positive Med-V1 labeling and weakens confidence in the underlying claim.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear and very weakly supported overall. The only item supplied is a retraction notice (PMID: 37387868), which does not provide reliable experimental support from its abstract and instead raises concerns about the validity of the claimed findings.

statements.text: The effects of lncRNA PAX8-AS1 on acute myeloid leukemia cell viability, apoptosis, and doxorubicin resistance are mediated through the miR-378g/ERBB2 axis.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only cited item is a retraction notice for a paper that had reported that PAX8-AS1 silencing reduced viability, increased apoptosis, and decreased doxorubicin resistance in myeloid leukemia via the miR-378g/ERBB2 axis (PMID: 37387868). Because the abstract is only a retraction notice and provides no experimental details, it does not directly test the claim but does undermine the reliability of the original report. This creates a discrepancy: the underlying retracted study appears to have aligned with the claim, yet its retraction weakens confidence rather than providing substantive biological contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The claim is not supported by the provided evidence and is weakly undermined overall. This judgment is based on the absence of any supporting studies and the presence of a retraction notice (PMID: 37387868), which reduces confidence in the claimed miR-378g/ERBB2-mediated mechanism without offering direct experimental refutation.

matched_article.title: Root Extract of a Micropropagated Prunus africana Medicinal Plant Induced Apoptosis in Human Prostate Cancer Cells (PC-3) via Caspase-3 Activation.
matched_article.abstract: Prostate cancer is one of the major causes of cancer-related deaths among men globally. Medicinal plants have been explored as alternative treatment options. Herein, we assessed the in vitro cytotoxic effects of 70% ethanolic root extracts of six-month-old micropropagated Prunus africana (PIR) on PC-3 prostate cancer cells as an alternative to the traditionally used P. africana stem-bark extract (PWS) treatment. In vitro assays on PC-3 cells included annexin-V and propidium iodide staining, DAPI staining, and caspase-3 activity analysis through western blotting. PC-3 cells were exposed to PWS and PIR at different concentrations, and dose-dependent antiprostate cancer effects were observed. PC-3 cell viability was determined using CCK-8 assay, which yielded IC50 values of 52.30 and 82.40 μg/mL for PWS and PIR, respectively. Annexin-V and PI staining showed dose-dependent apoptosis of PC-3 cells. Significant (p < 0.001) percent of DAPI-stained apoptotic PC-3 cells were observed in PWS, PIR, and doxorubicin treatment compared with the negative control. PWS treatment substantially elevated cleaved caspase-3 levels in PC-3 cells compared with the PIR treatment. These results provide evidence for the antiprostate cancer potential of PIR and sets a basis for further research to enhance future utilization of roots of young micropropagated P. africana for prostate cancer treatment as an alternative to stem bark. Moreover, micropropagation approach may help provide the required raw materials and hence reduce the demand for P. africana from endangered wild population.

statements.text: In human prostate cancer PC-3 cells, root extract from six-month-old micropropagated Prunus africana showed antiprostate cancer activity by reducing cell viability and inducing apoptosis.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study reports that ethanolic extracts of Pygeum africanum (Prunus africana) inhibited growth of human prostate cancer PC-3 and LNCaP cells, induced apoptosis, and altered cell kinetics in vitro, with additional antitumor effects in a TRAMP mouse model (PMID: 17709901). This supports the general claim that Prunus africana extracts can reduce prostate cancer cell viability and trigger apoptosis in PC-3 cells. However, the abstract describes bark-derived Pygeum africanum extract rather than root extract from six-month-old micropropagated plants, so support for those specific source/preparation details is indirect rather than direct.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because the available abstract shows anti-prostate-cancer activity of Prunus africana in PC-3 cells including growth inhibition and apoptosis induction (PMID: 17709901). However, the evidence is only moderately strong since it does not directly match the specific root extract from six-month-old micropropagated plants described in the claim.

statements.text: In human prostate cancer PC-3 cells, root extract from six-month-old micropropagated Prunus africana activated apoptosis-related caspase-3 signaling, although stem-bark extract produced a stronger effect.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract reports that ethanolic extracts of Pygeum africanum/Prunus africana inhibited PC-3 and LNCaP prostate cancer cell growth and induced apoptosis, with additional changes in cell kinetics and receptor-related signaling (PMID: 17709901). This is only indirect support for the claim, because the abstract does not specify root extract, six-month-old micropropagated material, caspase-3 activation, or that stem-bark extract was stronger than root extract. Thus, the main supportive theme is that Prunus africana extracts can promote apoptosis in PC-3 cells, but the key mechanistic and extract-comparison details of the claim are not addressed.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported overall. The available evidence suggests Prunus africana can induce apoptosis in PC-3 cells, but it does not directly confirm caspase-3 signaling by root extract or the stronger effect of stem-bark extract.

statements.text: Root extract from six-month-old micropropagated Prunus africana may serve as an alternative source of antiprostate cancer activity compared with traditionally used Prunus africana stem-bark extract.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: The Status Quo of Criminal Responsibility for Aflatoxin Pollution in China: From the Perspective of Judgment Analysis.
source_article.abstract: With the development of the economy, the food safety problems caused by aflatoxin have become increasingly prominent. With regard to the control of aflatoxin pollution, the Chinese government has promulgated a series of legal documents on food safety related to aflatoxin pollution, such as the formulation of industry standards for allowable limits of aflatoxin and various penalties for violators. Although these measures have achieved good results to some extent, there are still many legal problems. This study reviews the current situation of aflatoxin pollution control in food in China. The court judgment documents related to aflatoxin pollution from January 1st 2014 to January 1st 2020 are investigated to analyze the accountability status of aflatoxin pollution treatment in China. Furthermore, this study mainly cross verified the above problems by means of the literature survey and an organization interview and proposed solutions on the basis of in-depth analysis of their causes. Finally, some suggestions are put forward to solve the problem of aflatoxin pollution accountability in China.

matched_article.title: A Novel Deep Learning-Based Black Fungus Disease Identification Using Modified Hybrid Learning Methodology.
matched_article.abstract: Currently, countries across the world are suffering from a prominent viral infection called COVID-19. Most countries are still facing several issues due to this disease, which has resulted in several fatalities. The first COVID-19 wave caused devastation across the world owing to its virulence and led to a massive loss in human lives, impacting the country's economy drastically. A dangerous disease called mucormycosis was discovered worldwide during the second COVID-19 wave, in 2021, which lasted from April to July. The mucormycosis disease is commonly known as "black fungus," which belongs to the fungus family Mucorales. It is usually a rare disease, but the level of destruction caused by the disease is vast and unpredictable. This disease mainly targets people already suffering from other diseases and consuming heavy medication to counter the disease they are suffering from. This is because of the reduction in antibodies in the affected people. Therefore, the patient's body does not have the ability to act against fungus-oriented infections. This black fungus is more commonly identified in patients with coronavirus disease in certain country. The condition frequently manifests on skin, but it can also harm organs such as eyes and brain. This study intends to design a modified neural network logic for an artificial intelligence (AI) strategy with learning principles, called a hybrid learning-based neural network classifier (HLNNC). The proposed method is based on well-known techniques such as convolutional neural network (CNN) and support vector machine (SVM). This article discusses a dataset containing several eye photographs of patients with and without black fungus infection. These images were collected from the real-time records of people afflicted with COVID followed by the black fungus. This proposed HLNNC scheme identifies the black fungus disease based on the following image processing procedures: image acquisition, preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification; these procedures were performed considering the dataset training and testing principles with proper performance analysis. The results of the procedure are provided in a graphical format with the precise specification, and the efficacy of the proposed method is established.

statements.text: A hybrid learning-based neural network classifier combining convolutional neural network and support vector machine methods can identify black fungus disease from eye photographs of patients with and without black fungus infection.
statements.support_summary: One review article discusses AI/computer vision approaches for mucormycosis diagnosis and reports that deep features from a ResNet50 model combined with a linear SVM achieved 94.7% accuracy, suggesting that hybrid CNN/SVM-style methods may be useful for black fungus detection (PMID: 35233180). However, the abstract frames this as a broad review of diagnosis challenges and imaging approaches, emphasizing CT scans and general computer vision methods rather than a primary study specifically testing eye photographs from patients with and without infection. Thus, the available supporting evidence is indirect and only partially aligned with the exact claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The only cited evidence is an indirect review suggesting CNN/SVM-type image-analysis methods can aid mucormycosis detection, but it does not clearly validate identification from eye photographs specifically, so support for the exact claim remains limited.

statements.text: A deep learning-based black fungus disease identification approach can effectively classify eye images from patients affected by coronavirus disease with subsequent black fungus infection.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: A modified hybrid learning methodology can provide effective performance for black fungus disease detection in patient eye images.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: RETRACTED: Cardiac-specific overexpression of catalase attenuates lipopolysaccharide-induced myocardial contractile dysfunction: Role of autophagy.
source_article.abstract: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (http://www.elsevier.com/locate/withdrawalpolicy). This article has been retracted at the request of the Editor-in-Chief. After an institutional investigation into the work of Dr. Jun Ren, University of Wyoming subsequently conducted an examination of other selected publications of Dr. Ren's under the direction of the HHS Office of Research Integrity. Based on the findings of this examination, the University of Wyoming recommended this article be retracted due to concerns regarding data irregularities inconsistent with published conclusions. Specifically, University of Wyoming found evidence of data irregularities and image reuse in Figure 2 that significantly affect the results and conclusions reported in the manuscript.

matched_article.title: Tetrathiatriarylmethyl radical with a single aromatic hydrogen as a highly sensitive and specific superoxide probe.
matched_article.abstract: Superoxide (O(2)(•-)) plays crucial roles in normal physiology and disease; however, its measurement remains challenging because of the limited sensitivity and/or specificity of prior detection methods. We demonstrate that a tetrathiatriarylmethyl (TAM) radical with a single aromatic hydrogen (CT02-H) can serve as a highly sensitive and specific O(2)(•-) probe. CT02-H is an analogue of the fully substituted TAM radical CT-03 (Finland trityl) with an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) doublet signal due to its aromatic hydrogen. Owing to the neutral nature and negligible steric hindrance of the hydrogen, O(2)(•-) preferentially reacts with CT02-H at this site with production of the diamagnetic quinone methide via oxidative dehydrogenation. Upon reaction with O(2)(•-), CT02-H loses its EPR signal and this EPR signal decay can be used to quantitatively measure O(2)(•-). This is accompanied by a change in color from green to purple, with the quinone methide product exhibiting a unique UV-Vis absorbance (ε=15,900 M(-1) cm(-1)) at 540 nm, providing an additional O(2)(•-) detection method. More than five-fold higher reactivity of CT02-H for O(2)(•-) relative to CT-03 was demonstrated, with a second-order rate constant of 1.7×10(4) M(-1) s(-1) compared to 3.1×10(3) M(-1) s(-1) for CT-03. CT02-H exhibited high specificity for O(2)(•-) as evidenced by its inertness to other oxidoreductants. The O(2)(•-) generation rates detected by CT02-H from xanthine/xanthine oxidase were consistent with those measured by cytochrome c reduction but detection sensitivity was 10- to 100-fold higher. EPR detection of CT02-H enabled measurement of very low O(2)(•-) flux with a detection limit of 0.34 nM/min over 120 min. HPLC in tandem with electrochemical detection was used to quantitatively detect the stable quinone methide product and is a highly sensitive and specific method for measurement of O(2)(•-), with a sensitivity limit of ~2×10(-13) mol (10 nM with 20-μl injection volume). Based on the O(2)-dependent linewidth broadening of its EPR spectrum, CT02-H also enables simultaneous measurement of O(2) concentration and O(2)(•-) generation and was shown to provide sensitive detection of extracellular O(2)(•-) generation in endothelial cells stimulated either by menadione or with anoxia/reoxygenation. Thus, CT02-H is a unique probe that provides very high sensitivity and specificity for measurement of O(2)(•-) by either EPR or HPLC methods.

statements.text: A tetrathiatriarylmethyl radical with a single aromatic hydrogen, CT02-H, is a highly sensitive and specific probe for superoxide detection in chemical and cellular systems.
statements.support_summary: Indirect supporting evidence indicates that tetrathiatriarylmethyl (TAM) radicals can be useful superoxide probes, but the abstracts do not directly test CT02-H itself. One study reports that tris-(p-carboxyltetrathiaaryl)methyl radical EPR probes are efficiently oxidized by superoxide, with chemistry proposed to underlie previously reported specific O2*- measurement, although the same abstract also notes reactivity with alkylperoxyl radicals, which tempers claims of strict specificity (PMID: 19259606). Another study title and partial abstract describe perthiatriarylmethyl/TAM radicals and dendritic derivatives with high sensitivity and selectivity to superoxide, again supporting the broader TAM-superoxide detection concept rather than CT02-H specifically (PMID: 29505165). Overall, the support is mechanistically and class-level rather than direct evidence for CT02-H in chemical and cellular systems.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is somewhat supported, but mainly by indirect evidence from related TAM radicals rather than direct abstract-level evidence on CT02-H itself. Because there is no contradictory evidence here but the supporting studies do not clearly establish CT02-H's high sensitivity and specificity in both chemical and cellular systems, the overall support remains limited.

statements.text: Compared with the fully substituted tetrathiatriarylmethyl radical CT-03, CT02-H reacts much more rapidly with superoxide and provides substantially higher superoxide detection sensitivity while remaining largely inert to other oxidoreductants.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: CT02-H enables quantitative superoxide measurement by electron paramagnetic resonance signal loss or by detection of a characteristic quinone methide product, and it can also be used to detect extracellular superoxide generation in endothelial cells under oxidative or hypoxia-reoxygenation conditions.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract reports that tris-(p-carboxyltetrathiaaryl)methyl radical EPR probes are efficiently oxidized by superoxide, with selective formation of quinone-methide products, and states that this chemistry explains previously reported specific measurement of superoxide using these probes (PMID: 19259606). This supports the mechanistic parts of the claim: superoxide can be quantified through EPR probe signal loss/oxidation and by detection of a characteristic quinone-methide product. However, the abstract does not specifically name CT02-H or describe detection of extracellular superoxide in endothelial cells under oxidative or hypoxia-reoxygenation conditions, so support for those application-specific aspects is indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only partially supported overall. The available evidence supports the underlying probe chemistry for superoxide detection and quinone-methide formation, but it does not directly confirm CT02-H's use in endothelial-cell extracellular superoxide detection or under hypoxia-reoxygenation conditions.

source_article.title: The effects of sericin in recovering spermatogenesis and sexual hormone levels in diabetic rats.
source_article.abstract: Sericin-S (a hydrolysate from the silk protein sericin) is a natural antioxidant, which may improve spermatogenesis while having high biological safety, thus potentially useful for the treatment of male infertility. Our objective was to determine the effects of sericin-S on the sperm parameters and sexual hormone levels in a diabetic rat model. Thirty-six adult male rats were randomly divided in two groups, inducing diabetes in one of them by intraperitoneal administration of streptozotocin (STZ; 65 mg/kg). Both groups were randomly assigned to three subgroups, receiving oral gavage of saline, 1% or 2% sericin extract for 60 days. Therefore, the experimental design was a 2 × 3 factorial design of STZ and sericin treatments. One day after the last treatment, the rats were euthanized, weighed, the testes were processed (weigh, volume, and histology), and serum samples were processed for measuring sex hormone levels [testosterone, follicle-stimulating hormone, and luteinizing hormone (LH)]. STZ treatment decreased LH concentration and counts of spermatogonia, spermatocytes, and spermatids. Body and testis weights were significantly (p < 0.05) lower in the control group (non-diabetic saline) compared to the treated groups. In non-diabetic rats, sericin treatments increased (p < 0.05) the number of testicular cells (spermatogonia, spermatocytes, spermatids, Sertoli, and Leydig cells) and sex hormone concentrations. In diabetic rats, administration of sericin significantly (p < 0.05) improved sperm cell number and sex hormones levels. In nutshell, sericin can clearly modify sperm parameters and overall sex hormone function, and could improve spermatogenesis in normal and diabetic rats.

statements.text: In diabetic male rats, oral sericin administration improved sperm cell numbers and sex hormone levels compared with saline treatment.
statements.support_summary: One rat study in type 2 diabetic males reported that oral/intragastric sericin for 35 days increased serum testosterone and IGF-1, altered the testicular GH/IGF-1 axis, and was interpreted by the authors as improving spermatogenic function versus untreated diabetic rats (PMID: 26379831). However, the abstract does not explicitly report sperm cell counts or semen parameters, so support for the claim’s sperm-number component is indirect rather than direct. The study does directly support improvement in at least one sex hormone level (testosterone) after sericin treatment in diabetic rats (PMID: 26379831).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, mainly because one diabetic rat study found oral sericin increased testosterone and suggested improved spermatogenic function (PMID: 26379831). However, the evidence is limited and incomplete for the specific claim about improved sperm cell numbers, since the abstract does not directly report sperm counts.

statements.text: In non-diabetic male rats, oral sericin administration increased testicular cell numbers and sex hormone concentrations compared with saline treatment.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In streptozotocin-induced diabetic male rats, diabetes reduced luteinizing hormone levels and counts of spermatogonia, spermatocytes, and spermatids compared with non-diabetic male rats.
statements.support_summary: Several STZ-diabetic rat studies report reduced serum luteinizing hormone versus non-diabetic controls, alongside broader reproductive impairment such as lower testosterone/FSH, poorer sperm parameters, and testicular histopathology (PMID: 25765092, PMID: 29080463, PMID: 31420111, PMID: 20456630, PMID: 36311276, PMID: 37988877). Some abstracts also describe seminiferous epithelial damage involving spermatogonia, spermatocytes, or spermatids, or note reduced numbers of sexual lineage cells, which is directionally consistent with reduced spermatogenic cell counts (PMID: 25765092, PMID: 31420111, PMID: 39386078). However, most supporting abstracts do not explicitly quantify spermatogonia, spermatocytes, and spermatids all together, so support for that part of the claim is mostly indirect rather than direct. One item is too truncated to verify the relevant outcomes from the abstract alone, so its evidentiary value is limited (PMID: 34222060).
statements.contradict_summary: One STZ-diabetic rat study directly conflicts with the luteinizing hormone portion of the claim, reporting increased serum LH, not decreased LH, in diabetic controls relative to normal controls, while still showing reduced testosterone and impaired sperm/fertility outcomes (PMID: 30515996). This abstract does not report spermatogonia, spermatocytes, or spermatid counts, so its contradiction is limited mainly to the LH component rather than the full claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple independent STZ-diabetic rat abstracts consistently report lower LH and testicular/spermatogenic damage, but direct abstract-level evidence for reduced counts of spermatogonia, spermatocytes, and spermatids is limited, and one study specifically reports the opposite LH direction.

matched_article.title: Blood flow restriction augments the skeletal muscle response during very low-load resistance exercise to volitional failure.
matched_article.abstract: The purpose of this study was to compare the acute muscular response with resistance exercise between the following conditions [labeled (% one-repetition maximum/% arterial occlusion pressure)]: high-load (70/0), very low-load (15/0), very low-load with moderate (15/40), and high (15/80) blood flow restriction pressures. Twenty-three participants completed four sets of unilateral knee extension to failure (up to 90 repetitions) with each condition, one condition per leg, each day. Muscle thickness and maximal voluntary contraction (MVC) were measured before (Pre), immediately after (Post-0), and 15 min after (Post-15) exercise and electromyography (EMG) amplitude during exercise. Pre to Post-0 muscle thickness changes in cm [95% CI] were greater with 15/40 [0.57 (0.41, 0.73)] and 15/80 [0.49 (0.35, 0.62)] compared to 70/0 [0.33 (0.25, 0.40)]. Pre to Post-0 MVC changes in Nm [95% CI] were higher with 15/40 [-127.0 (-162.1, -91.9)] and 15/80 [-133.6 (-162.8, -104.4)] compared to 70/0 [-48.4 (-70.1, -26.6)] and 15/0 [-98.4 (-121.9, -74.9)], which were also different. Over the first three repetitions, EMG increased across sets, whereas in the last three repetitions it did not. EMG was also different between conditions and was generally greater during 70/0. Repetitions decreased across sets reaching the lowest for 70/0, and for very low loads decreased with increased pressure. In trained participants exercising to failure, lower load and the application of restriction pressure augment changes in muscle thickness and torque. The EMG amplitude was augmented by load. Training studies should compare these conditions, as the results herein suggest some muscular adaptations may differ.

statements.text: In trained participants performing unilateral knee extension to volitional failure, very low-load resistance exercise with blood flow restriction produced greater acute increases in skeletal muscle thickness and greater acute reductions in maximal voluntary contraction than very low-load resistance exercise without blood flow restriction or high-load resistance exercise without blood flow restriction.
statements.support_summary: One supportive study found that 6 weeks of unilateral low-load knee extensor training to volitional fatigue with blood flow restriction (BFR) produced a greater increase in lateral quadriceps muscle thickness than the contralateral free-flow limb, while anterior quadriceps thickness and muscle function improved similarly in both limbs (PMID: 24612120). However, this study evaluated chronic training adaptations in middle-aged adults rather than the specific acute postexercise increases in muscle thickness or acute decrements in maximal voluntary contraction stated in the claim, and it did not compare against high-load exercise. Thus, it offers only indirect and partial support.
statements.contradict_summary: Several studies more directly challenge the claim. A very low-load training study reported that adding BFR did not augment adaptations versus very low-load exercise alone, and high-load training produced greater muscle thickness gains than all very low-load conditions (PMID: 31486720), though this was chronic rather than acute evidence. More directly, an acute study found no between-condition differences in muscle thickness, edema, or blood flow between low-load BFR and low-load non-BFR exercise (PMID: 34192417), and another acute study concluded that when 30% 1RM exercise was taken to failure, applying BFR did not augment acute responses over exercise alone despite pressure-dependent MVC decrements (PMID: 28251784). A crossover knee-extension study also reported no differences in muscle thickness changes between low-load and practical BFR conditions, with fatigue effects stronger only in some wrap-based conditions rather than clearly in standard cuff BFR versus both low-load and high-load exercise (PMID: 31821993). One additional study on high-load training with or without BFR showed no additive effects of BFR on MVIC or quadriceps size (PMID: 33900254), but this is only indirectly relevant because it does not test the claim’s very low-load BFR comparison.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. The only supporting study is indirect and chronic, whereas multiple more relevant studies—especially acute comparisons—indicate that BFR does not clearly produce greater immediate muscle-thickness increases or greater strength loss than low-load exercise without BFR, and high-load exercise often performs as well as or better than very low-load conditions.

statements.text: In trained participants performing unilateral knee extension to volitional failure, higher resistance load produced greater electromyography amplitude than very low-load resistance exercise, regardless of blood flow restriction.
statements.support_summary: Three studies consistently report higher quadriceps EMG amplitude with higher-load knee extension than with low-load conditions. In a randomized crossover study, high-load knee extensions at 80% 1RM produced greater average and peak EMG than low-load BFR exercise at 30% 1RM, with no EMG difference between two BFR device types (PMID: 33647529). In dynamic knee extensions to volitional failure, EMG amplitude was greater in the high-load condition than both low-load and low-load BFR at the beginning and end of exercise, although end-exercise EMG became similar across conditions as low-load conditions rose over time (PMID: 22895371). In trained young adults performing leg extensions to failure, EMG amplitude remained greater at 80% versus 30% 1RM across reps and sets (PMID: 26159316).
statements.contradict_summary: One cited contradiction does not directly refute the claim: although the authors argue that muscle fibre activation and anabolic signalling are unaffected by load when exercise is taken to task failure, the abstract also states that EMG amplitude and total EMG activity were significantly different between heavier and lighter loads, which is a discrepancy with its placement as contradicting evidence (PMID: 31294822). The other study is only weakly relevant because it compared several BFR implementations and reported EMG differences between specific low-load BFR methods, but the abstract does not show that very low-load exercise exceeded high-load exercise in EMG amplitude; thus it does not directly contradict the claim (PMID: 31821993). Overall, the contradiction set mainly challenges interpretation of EMG as a proxy for fibre activation rather than the narrower observation that higher loads can show greater EMG amplitude.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple abstracts directly show greater EMG amplitude during higher-load knee extension than low-load or low-load BFR conditions, whereas the purported contradictory evidence mostly disputes what EMG means physiologically rather than clearly showing the opposite amplitude pattern.

statements.text: In trained participants performing unilateral knee extension to volitional failure, increasing blood flow restriction pressure during very low-load resistance exercise reduced the number of repetitions completed before failure.
statements.support_summary: Several acute knee-extension studies indicate that adding blood flow restriction (BFR) reduces endurance and repetitions to failure versus free-flow exercise: trained subjects performing unilateral low-load dynamic knee extensions completed significantly more repetitions without occlusion than with occlusion in every set (PMID: 19826283), and both sexes performed about 30% fewer repetitions during low-load BFR knee extensions than during free-flow conditions (PMID: 23828293). Additional unilateral leg-extension evidence also shows fewer repetitions to failure with continuous or intermittent BFR than control (PMID: 24666729), and moderate-intensity eccentric knee extensions with BFR produced markedly fewer repetitions than without BFR (PMID: 28889225). More directly relevant to pressure, one unilateral knee-extension crossover study reported fewer total repetitions under 80% arterial occlusion pressure than under 40% in some standardized cuff/wrap comparisons (PMID: 31821993), although this abstract does not cleanly isolate a simple pressure-only effect across identical modalities. Broader but indirect evidence from other exercises supports a pressure-response pattern: trained men completed fewer repetitions with 80% versus 40% AOP during preacher curls (PMID: 36501840), increasing pressure from 100% to 150% AOP shortened time to task failure in elbow flexors (PMID: 34697249), and a meta-analysis found earlier repetition failure with high (≥50% AOP) but not low BFR pressures during low-load resistance exercise overall (PMID: 34319945).
statements.contradict_summary: Most contradicting items are not direct tests of the claim's acute pressure effect. One acute knee-extension study often appears miscoded as contradictory because it compared low-load BFR with high-load exercise and found more repetitions in both low-load conditions than high-load, but the abstract does not report LL(BFR) outperforming low-load free-flow exercise, so it does not directly contradict the claim (PMID: 22895371). Several other papers instead address chronic training adaptations or ischemic preconditioning rather than acute pressure during very low-load BFR to failure: endurance/repetition capacity increased after weeks of ischemic knee-extension training (PMID: 21385216), athletes improved Reps20 after 5 weeks of vascular occlusion training (PMID: 23412543), and ischemic preconditioning before high-load knee extension increased repetitions or workload irrespective of occlusion level (PMID: 30844989; PMID: 31343550). Another study found that changing pressure pain threshold before 80% AOP knee-extension BFR did not alter repetitions, but this does not test whether increasing BFR pressure itself reduces repetitions (PMID: 36727998). Overall, the contradiction set is weakened by major relevance/miscategorization issues and contains little direct evidence against the specific acute unilateral knee-extension pressure-response claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from acute low-load knee-extension studies showing fewer repetitions with BFR than free flow and from broader pressure-comparison evidence suggesting higher cuff pressures hasten failure, whereas most purported contradictions are indirect, chronic-training, or preconditioning studies that do not directly test the claim.

source_article.title: Image Analysis of TVCDS in Infertile Patients with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome.
source_article.abstract: In order to analyze and examine the TVCDS images of infertile patients, this paper conducted an in-depth study based on the symptoms of polycystic ovary syndrome. Through the sample size estimation method, mathematical analysis, and other methods, the image examination of the polycystic ovary in TVCDS was successfully analyzed. 86 cases of infertile patients with PCS were divided into a control group treated with clomiphene alone and an observation group treated with clomiphene combined with TCM periodic therapy, with 43 patients in each group. The therapeutic effects of the two groups were compared and analyzed. Results show that the treatment effective rate and pregnancy success rate of the observation group were 95.35% and 88.37%, respectively, and those of the control group were 83.72% and 76.74%, respectively. The difference between the two groups was statistically significant (P < 0.05). It was understood that the main pathogenesis of polycystic ovary syndrome is the abnormal balance of kidney, qi, and blood meridians. Thus, the balance of kidney-anemone-chong Ren-uprisal is broken and the result is infertility symptoms or irregular menstruation. After a study on TVCDS in infertile patients, it was observed that the levels of progesterone (P) and luteinizing hormone (LH) in patients with irregular menstruation were significantly increased. The increase was higher than that in the control group, with an overall negative rate of 4.00%, compared with 18.00% of the control group, showing a significant difference. It also indicates that TVCDS image examination has a very significant effect on improving menstrual irregularities and reducing the incidence of adverse reactions.

statements.text: In infertile patients with polycystic ovary syndrome, clomiphene combined with traditional Chinese medicine periodic therapy improved treatment effectiveness and pregnancy success more than clomiphene alone.
statements.support_summary: Several randomized or comparative studies report better reproductive outcomes when clomiphene is combined with traditional Chinese medicine modalities than with clomiphene alone. In a 3-arm RCT, clomiphene plus acupuncture-moxibustion and herbal medicine improved cervical mucus, endometrial measures, clinical pregnancy, and lowered early abortion versus clomiphene alone (PMID: 25854013); electroacupuncture plus clomiphene also showed a higher total effective rate than clomiphene alone in PCOS (PMID: 29701043), and flying needling plus clomiphene improved ovulation versus either treatment alone (PMID: 29231300). Reviews/meta-analyses also suggest higher pregnancy rates for Chinese herbal medicine plus clomiphene versus clomiphene alone, though they emphasize poor trial quality or limited evidence (PMID: 20824862; PMID: 38075051). Additional supportive but less directly aligned studies include a retrospective analysis of Fuke Qianjin tablets plus clomiphene reporting improved pregnancy outcomes (PMID: 37417628) and a trial of Cimicifugae racemosae added to clomiphene that improved cycle outcomes and pregnancy rates, although this agent is not clearly traditional Chinese periodic therapy (PMID: 24592984). One cited study is only indirectly relevant because it compared herbal medicine plus acupuncture after ovarian intervention rather than clomiphene alone (PMID: 30645838), highlighting some mismatch within the support set.
statements.contradict_summary: A large, multicenter randomized clinical trial found that adding active acupuncture to clomiphene did not improve live birth compared with control acupuncture plus clomiphene among women with PCOS, despite clomiphene itself outperforming placebo (PMID: 28655015). This is important contradiction because it directly tests a TCM modality added to clomiphene, uses live birth as the primary outcome, and included about 1000 women. The contradiction is specific to acupuncture rather than all forms of traditional Chinese medicine or herbal periodic therapy, but it raises concern that benefits seen in smaller trials may not generalize.
statements.conclusion: The statement is supported by multiple smaller trials and reviews suggesting improved pregnancy or ovulation outcomes when clomiphene is combined with Chinese medicine approaches, but the evidence is tempered by variable intervention types, some indirect studies, and methodological limitations. Overall the evidence is mixed/unclear because a large high-quality RCT directly contradicts added benefit of acupuncture plus clomiphene on live birth, while supportive evidence is broader but generally less definitive.

statements.text: In infertile patients with polycystic ovary syndrome and irregular menstruation, TVCDS image examination was associated with improved menstrual irregularity outcomes and fewer adverse reactions.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In infertile patients with polycystic ovary syndrome, irregular menstruation was associated with higher progesterone and luteinizing hormone levels than in infertile patients without irregular menstruation.
statements.support_summary: One human study of infertile patients with polycystic ovaries found that those with menstrual disturbances had significantly higher endocrine parameters, including luteinizing hormone, than those without menstrual disturbances and than controls, supporting an association between irregular menstruation and higher LH in this population (PMID: 8345073). However, the abstract does not mention progesterone measurements, so it only partially supports the full claim. The study also links more severe ultrasound morphology with worse endocrine abnormalities, suggesting menstrual disturbance tracks with more pronounced hormonal dysfunction (PMID: 8345073).
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a letrozole-induced PCOS rat study reporting menstrual irregularities alongside decreased progesterone levels (PMID: 37993900). This conflicts with the claim's direction for progesterone, but it is an animal model rather than infertile human patients with PCOS, and the abstract does not report luteinizing hormone, limiting its relevance to the full claim. Thus, it provides only indirect contradiction and there is a species/population mismatch discrepancy (PMID: 37993900).
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The available human evidence supports higher LH with menstrual irregularity in infertile patients with polycystic ovaries, but there is no direct abstract evidence here for higher progesterone, while the only contradiction comes from a less directly relevant rat model.

matched_article.title: Long Noncoding RNA CASC2 Facilitated Wound Healing through miRNA-155/HIF-1α in Diabetic Foot Ulcers.
matched_article.abstract: Diabetic foot ulcers (DFU) are among the serious complications which are closely linked to diabetes mellitus. However, there is still a lack of accurate and effective standard prevention and treatment programs for DFU. In this manuscript, we have investigated the function of lncRNA cancer susceptibility candidate 2 (CASC2)/miR-155/hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha (HIF-1α) in the wound healing of DFU. We have analyzed lncRNA CASC2`s expression in the marginal tissues of ulcers in patients and mice with DFU. Additionally, the interaction relationship and mechanism between lncRNA CASC2, miR-155, and HIF-1α were determined, which proved the effects of lncRNA CASC2/miR-155/HIF-1α on fibroblasts apoptosis, proliferation, and migration. According to our study, the lncRNA CASC2's expression was low in the tissues of ulcers of DFU mice and patients. lncRNA CASC2's overexpression promoted fibroblasts migration, proliferation, and inhibited apoptosis and was beneficial for the healing of wounds, preferably in the DFU mice. In addition, lncRNA CASC2 directly targets miR-155 and HIF-1α functions as miR-155's target gene. Overexpression of miR-155 abrogated the function of lncRNA CASC2. Similarly, HIF-1α's inhibition has reversed the effect of miR-155 downregulation on fibroblasts. In general, overexpression of lncRNA CASC2 facilitated wound healing through miR-155/HIF-1α in DFU.

statements.text: In diabetic foot ulcers, increased lncRNA CASC2 expression promotes fibroblast migration and proliferation while reducing fibroblast apoptosis, supporting wound healing.

source_article.title: Disposable screen-printed sensors for determination of duloxetine hydrochloride.
source_article.abstract: A screen-printed disposable electrode system for the determination of duloxetine hydrochloride (DL) was developed using screen-printing technology. Homemade printing has been characterized and optimized on the basis of effects of the modifier and plasticizers. The fabricated bi-electrode potentiometric strip containing both working and reference electrodes was used as duloxetine hydrochloride sensor. The proposed sensors worked satisfactorily in the concentration range from 1.0 × 10-6-1.0 × 10-2 mol L-1 with detection limit reaching 5.0 × 10-7 mol L-1 and adequate shelf life of 6 months. The method is accurate, precise and economical. The proposed method has been applied successfully for the analysis of the drug in pure and in its dosage forms. In this method, there is no interference from any common pharmaceutical additives and diluents. Results of the analysis were validated statistically by recovery studies.

statements.text: A screen-printed disposable potentiometric sensor can accurately, precisely, and economically determine duloxetine hydrochloride in pure drug samples and dosage forms.
