matched_article.title: Filming a live cell by scanning electrochemical microscopy: label-free imaging of the dynamic morphology in real time.
matched_article.abstract: The morphology of a live cell reflects the organization of the cytoskeleton and the healthy status of the cell. We established a label-free platform for monitoring the changing morphology of live cells in real time based on scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM). The dynamic morphology of a live human bladder cancer cell (T24) was revealed by time-lapse SECM with dissolved oxygen in the medium solution as the redox mediator. Detailed local movements of cell membrane were presented by time-lapse cross section lines extracted from time-lapse SECM. Vivid dynamic morphology is presented by a movie made of time-lapse SECM images. The morphological change of the T24 cell by non-physiological temperature is in consistence with the morphological feature of early apoptosis. To obtain dynamic cellular morphology with other methods is difficult. The non-invasive nature of SECM combined with high resolution realized filming the movements of live cells.

statements.text: Scanning electrochemical microscopy can monitor live human bladder cancer cell morphology in real time without labels.
statements.support_summary: Several studies in live human bladder cancer T24 cells report that scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) can perform time-lapse or time-course measurements in real time, including simultaneous assessment of cell topography and membrane permeability, which is closely related to monitoring cell shape or morphology changes (PMID: 26194058, PMID: 24656893, PMID: 26616202). One study explicitly states that SECM depth-scan imaging quantified both cellular topography and membrane permeability after cisplatin exposure, supporting real-time monitoring of live-cell morphological features (PMID: 26194058). Additional bladder-cancer-cell studies show SECM detected cadmium-associated changes including shrinking in cell size/volume and monitored cell physiology/topography, though these papers emphasize permeability more than morphology per se (PMID: 26826690, PMID: 26616202). A broader review describes SECM as a non-invasive method for analyzing single live cells, including topography and label-free measurements, which indirectly supports the platform capability but is not bladder-cancer-specific (PMID: 30324207). There is some discrepancy in that several supporting papers rely on added redox mediators such as ferrocenemethanol or focus on ROS/permeability rather than direct morphology alone, so support for the specific phrase 'without labels' is only partial in some cases (PMID: 22169205, PMID: 24656893, PMID: 26616202).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The evidence consistently shows SECM can non-invasively monitor live human bladder cancer cells in real time and can capture topography/morphology-related changes, but the label-free aspect is not uniformly established because several studies use exogenous redox mediators and focus more on permeability or physiology than direct morphology.

statements.text: Scanning electrochemical microscopy can detect dynamic membrane movements and morphological changes in live human bladder cancer cells.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies in live human bladder cancer T24 cells report that SECM can monitor time-dependent membrane permeability changes in real time, including rapid cisplatin-induced changes within 5 minutes and cadmium- or chromium-associated shifts in membrane homeostasis, supporting detection of dynamic membrane-related responses (PMID: 26194058, PMID: 24656893, PMID: 26616202, PMID: 29458979). Several abstracts also indicate simultaneous or related topographic/morphologic readouts: cisplatin work explicitly quantified cellular topography alongside permeability (PMID: 26194058), one cadmium study states SECM monitors cell physiology and topography (PMID: 26616202), and another reports Cd-treated T24 cells shrank in size and volume while being mapped by SECM (PMID: 26826690). A broader methodological paper describes SECM as a tool for topography and spatiotemporal activity imaging in single live cells, though it is not specific to bladder cancer cells (PMID: 22264538). Overall, the abstracts consistently support SECM detection of dynamic membrane changes in live bladder cancer cells, with more limited but still present abstract-level support for morphological/topographic changes.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall well supported. Several directly relevant studies in live human bladder cancer T24 cells show SECM detects dynamic membrane permeability changes and, in some cases, concurrent topographic or size/volume changes, while no contradictory evidence was provided.

statements.text: Non-physiological temperature induces morphological changes in live human bladder cancer cells that are consistent with early apoptosis.

source_article.title: The Role of Cavin3 in the Progression of Lung Cancer and Its Mechanism.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: The purpose of this study was to describe the role of Cavin3 in the progression of lung cancer and its underlying mechanism. METHODS: Totally, 200 cases of lung cancer tissues and corresponding paracancer tissues were collected. Cavin3 expression in samples was determined by qRT-PCR, and the correlation with lung cancer stages as well as prognosis was statistically analyzed combined with matched clinical information. To investigate the mechanism of Cavin3 in lung cancer progression, firstly, Cavin3 was detected in lung cancer cell lines A549, PC9, and H520. Then, cells with stable Cavin3 overexpression and Cavin3 knockout were established to determine the effect of Cavin3 overexpression on the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) signaling pathway. Subsequently, cells were harvested for cell proliferation, migration, and invasion assays in vitro, as well as nude mouse transplantation tumor experiment in vivo. RESULTS: Cavin3 was seen to be highly expressed in cancer tissues. Statistical analysis with matched clinical data showed that Cavin3 as a prognostic indicator of lung cancer had important clinical value. In addition, it could be found that high expression of Cavin3 was able to promote cell proliferation, migration, and invasion and also potentiate tumor formation in vivo. CONCLUSION: Cavin3 was highly expressed in lung cancer, and it was capable to promote cell proliferation, invasion, and migration.

statements.text: High Cavin3 expression in lung cancer tissues is associated with lung cancer progression and poor prognosis.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: High Cavin3 expression in lung cancer cells promotes cancer cell proliferation, migration, and invasion.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that cavin3 is inactivated in most cancers and that cavin3 loss impairs DNA repair, while cavin3 overexpression sensitizes cancer cells to UV-induced apoptosis, suggesting cavin3 may act more like a tumor-suppressive stress-response factor than a promoter of malignant behavior (PMID: 34142659). A lung adenocarcinoma study found matrine inhibited A549 cell proliferation and induced apoptosis/autophagy while also suppressing Cavin3 in a dose-dependent manner, which does not cleanly contradict the claim and may be miscategorized because the abstract does not show that higher Cavin3 drives proliferation, migration, or invasion (PMID: 32862598). Overall, the contradicting set mainly suggests cavin3 is linked to apoptosis sensitivity and loss in cancers, but direct evidence in lung cancer migration/invasion is limited.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported, but the evidence is limited. The strongest abstract suggests cavin3 has tumor-suppressive or pro-apoptotic functions rather than promoting aggressive lung cancer behavior, while the other cited lung cancer study is indirect and internally inconsistent regarding Cavin3 directionality.

statements.text: High Cavin3 expression enhances tumor formation in vivo in a lung cancer model.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: TTK, CDC25A, and ESPL1 as Prognostic Biomarkers for Endometrial Cancer.
matched_article.abstract: Endometrial cancer (EC) is one of the most common malignant gynaecological tumours worldwide. This study was aimed at identifying EC prognostic genes and investigating the molecular mechanisms of these genes in EC. Two mRNA datasets of EC were downloaded from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO). The GEO2R tool and Draw Venn Diagram were used to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between normal endometrial tissues and EC tissues. Then, Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway analyses were performed using the Database for Annotation, Visualization and Integrated Discovery (DAVID). Next, the protein-protein interactions (PPIs) of these DEGs were determined by the Search Tool for the Retrieval of Interacting Genes (STRING) tool and Cytoscape with Molecular Complex Detection (MCODE). Furthermore, Kaplan-Meier survival analysis was performed by UALCAN to verify genes associated with significantly poor prognosis. Next, Gene Expression Profiling Interactive Analysis (GEPIA) was used to verify the expression levels of these selected genes. Additionally, a reanalysis of the KEGG pathways was performed to understand the potential biological functions of selected genes. Finally, the associations between these genes and clinical features were analysed based on TCGA cancer genomic datasets for EC. In EC tissues, compared with normal endometrial tissues, 147 of 249 DEGs were upregulated and 102 were downregulated. A total of 64 upregulated genes were assembled into a PPI network. Next, 14 genes were found to be both associated with significantly poor prognosis and highly expressed in EC tissues. Reanalysis of the KEGG pathways found that three of these genes were enriched in the cell cycle pathway. TTK, CDC25A, and ESPL1 showed higher expression in cancers with late stage and higher tumour grade. In summary, through integrated bioinformatics approaches, we found three significant prognostic genes of EC, which might be potential therapeutic targets for EC patients.

statements.text: Endometrial cancer tissues with higher expression of TTK, CDC25A, or ESPL1 are associated with poorer prognosis than endometrial cancer tissues with lower expression of these genes.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support parts of the claim in endometrial cancer, especially for TTK and ESPL1. For ESPL1, integrated bioinformatic analysis reported ESPL1 as a negative independent prognostic factor in endometrial cancer (PMID: 32170852), and a more recent study explicitly concluded that ESPL1 upregulation is associated with poor prognostic outcomes in endometrial cancer, although the provided abstract is truncated (PMID: 38568742). For TTK, multiple studies reported that high TTK expression is linked to lower survival, worse overall and disease-free survival, higher grade/advanced disease, and metastatic or aggressive features in endometrial cancer (PMID: 36829176, PMID: 36660721, PMID: 38195567). One proteomic study adds indirect support by identifying TTK among kinases associated with aggressive uterine endometrial cancer phenotypes, but it is less direct on prognosis itself (PMID: 33627787). No provided abstract directly addresses CDC25A prognostic significance in endometrial cancer, so support for the full three-gene statement is incomplete.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. There are therefore no abstracts here directly disputing an association between higher TTK, CDC25A, or ESPL1 expression and poorer prognosis in endometrial cancer.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted because several endometrial cancer studies consistently associate high TTK and ESPL1 expression with poorer outcomes or more aggressive disease, and no contrary evidence was supplied. However, the evidence is incomplete for the full claim because CDC25A is not directly supported by the provided abstracts.

statements.text: Endometrial cancer tissues show higher expression of TTK, CDC25A, and ESPL1 in more advanced-stage disease and in tumors with higher grade than endometrial cancer tissues from earlier-stage disease and lower-grade tumors.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies support the TTK portion of the claim in endometrial cancer: TTK overexpression was reported to be more prevalent in patients with high-grade and advanced tumors (PMID: 36829176), associated with metastasis and advanced TNM stage (PMID: 38195567), and identified among kinases linked to grade/stage-associated aggressive proteomic signatures in uterine endometrial cancer (PMID: 33627787). For ESPL1, one study reports that its upregulation is associated with poor prognostic outcomes in endometrial cancer, but the abstract as provided does not clearly state higher expression by stage or grade, so this is only indirect support (PMID: 38568742). None of the provided supporting abstracts directly addresses CDC25A, creating an important gap relative to the full three-gene claim. Overall, the evidence mainly supports a stage/grade association for TTK, with weaker and less direct support for ESPL1 and no direct support here for CDC25A.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but only partially. The available abstracts provide fairly consistent evidence that TTK expression is higher in more aggressive endometrial cancer, while support for ESPL1 is indirect and no direct evidence is provided here for CDC25A, so the full multi-gene claim is not strongly established by this set.

statements.text: TTK, CDC25A, and ESPL1 are potential prognostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets in endometrial cancer because increased expression of these genes is associated with aggressive disease and poor survival.
statements.support_summary: Several endometrial cancer studies support parts of the claim, especially for TTK and ESPL1. For ESPL1, integrated bioinformatic analyses identified ESPL1 as a negative independent prognostic factor in endometrial cancer and part of a prognostic model (PMID: 32170852), and a more recent endometrial cancer study explicitly reports that ESPL1 upregulation is associated with poor prognostic outcomes (PMID: 38568742). For TTK, multiple studies report that high TTK expression is linked to lower survival, higher grade, advanced tumors, serous histology, TP53 alterations, EMT-related programs, and poor prognosis, while also proposing TTK inhibition as a therapeutic strategy (PMID: 36829176; PMID: 36660721); additional EC-related omics studies identify TTK among hub genes or grade-associated kinases with possible biomarker/target relevance (PMID: 36905262; PMID: 33627787; PMID: 36232772). However, none of the provided abstracts directly address CDC25A in endometrial cancer, and one cited ESPL1 paper is pan-cancer rather than EC-specific, so support is uneven across the three named genes (PMID: 37006282).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because multiple endometrial cancer studies consistently link TTK and ESPL1 overexpression with aggressive disease features, poor prognosis, and potential therapeutic targeting. However, the evidence set does not directly support CDC25A from the abstracts provided, so the claim is only partially established for all three genes together.

source_article.title: mTOR up-regulation of SNRPA1 contributes to hepatocellular carcinoma development.
source_article.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the second leading cause of cancer-related death worldwide. Recent studies showed that snRNPs were implicated in human cancer development. The role of SNRPA1, which is a member of U2 snRNPs, in HCC, remains undocumented. Here, we found that SNRPA1 was highly expressed in HCC tissue compared with normal adjacent liver tissues. Up-regulation of SNRPA1 was correlated with the clinical stage of HCC and the overall survival of HCC patients. In vitro and in vivo results showed that knockdown of SNPRA1 inhibited the cell proliferation, colony formation and xenografted tumorigenesis of HCC cells. Apoptosis was induced by SNPRA1 down-regulation. Mechanistically, SNPRA1 was stimulated by mTOR activation. In addition, whole-genome microarray analysis identified that 262 genes were up-regulated and 462 genes were down-regulated by SNPRA1 knockdown in HCC cells. qPCR analysis suggested that the fibroblast growth factor-2 (FGF2), Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), β-catenin, Ki-67 and cyclin B1 were down-regulated and caspase 3, p53 as well as p21 were up-regulated after SNRPA1 knockdown. Taken together, our findings implicate that SNPRA1 functions as an oncogene in HCC.

statements.text: In hepatocellular carcinoma, high SNRPA1 expression is associated with more advanced disease and poorer overall survival.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Molecular detection of methicillin heat-resistant Staphylococcus aureus strains in pasteurized camel milk in Saudi Arabia.
matched_article.abstract: Antibiotic- and heat-resistant bacteria in camel milk is a potential public health problem. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is an opportunistic pathogen in humans, dairy cattle and camels. We characterized the phenotype and genotype of methicillin-resistant staphylococcal strains recovered from pasteurized and raw camel milk (as control) distributed in the retail markets of Saudi Arabia. Of the 100 samples assessed between March and May 2016, 20 S. aureus isolates were recovered from pasteurized milk, 10 of which were resistant to cefoxitin, and as such, were methicillin-resistant. However, raw camel milk did not contain methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA). Antimicrobial susceptibility tests showed that the resistance ratio for other antibiotics was 60%. We performed a polymerase chain reaction (PCR) assay using primers for the methicillin-resistant gene mecA and nucleotide sequencing to detect and verify the methicillin-resistant strains. Basic local alignment search tool (BLAST) analysis of the gene sequences showed a 96-100% similarity between the resistant isolates and the S. aureus CS100 strain's mecA gene. Ten of the methicillin-resistant isolates were heat-resistant and were stable at temperatures up to 85°C for 60 s, and three of these were resistant at 90°C for 60 or 90 s. The mean decimal reduction time (D85-value) was 111 s for the ten isolates. Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS)/polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) showed that there was no difference in the total protein profiles for the ten methicillin heat-resistant S. aureus (MHRSA) isolates and for S. aureus ATCC 29737. In conclusion, a relatively high percentage of the tested pasteurized camel milk samples contained S. aureus (20%) and MHRSA (10%).

statements.text: Pasteurized camel milk sold in retail markets in Saudi Arabia contained Staphylococcus aureus contamination, including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus strains.
statements.support_summary: A directly relevant Saudi retail-market study found Staphylococcus aureus in pasteurized camel milk, recovering 20 S. aureus isolates from 100 samples, of which 10 were cefoxitin-resistant and mecA-positive methicillin-resistant strains, strongly supporting the claim (PMID: 32359985). Another camel milk study also detected S. aureus including 5 MRSA isolates among 31 S. aureus recovered from 100 milk samples, but the abstract does not clearly state that the milk was pasteurized retail milk from Saudi Arabia, so it is only partial and context-limited support (PMID: 29041789). Overall, the main supporting theme is that camel milk can contain S. aureus, including MRSA, with one study specifically documenting this in pasteurized camel milk sold in Saudi retail markets (PMID: 32359985).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence is a directly on-point study from Saudi retail markets showing contamination of pasteurized camel milk with S. aureus, including mecA-confirmed MRSA strains (PMID: 32359985), while the second study offers only broader contextual support.

statements.text: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus isolates recovered from pasteurized camel milk showed heat resistance, including survival after high-temperature treatment.
statements.support_summary: One directly relevant study of pasteurized camel milk found 20 S. aureus isolates, of which 10 were cefoxitin-resistant/mecA-positive MRSA, and these MRSA isolates were described as heat resistant, remaining stable up to 85°C for 60 s; 3 isolates also withstood 90°C for 60–90 s (PMID: 32359985). This abstract directly supports the claim that MRSA recovered from pasteurized camel milk showed heat resistance, including survival after high-temperature treatment.
statements.contradict_summary: The cited contradicting study examined S. aureus contamination along a milk value chain in Ethiopia and reported that S. aureus was not isolated from pasteurized milk samples (PMID: 28655298). However, it did not study camel milk, did not test heat resistance, and did not specifically evaluate MRSA survival after high-temperature treatment, so its relevance to the claim is indirect and the contradiction appears to stem from misclassification rather than direct opposing evidence.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest and most directly relevant abstract specifically reports mecA-positive MRSA from pasteurized camel milk with survival at 85–90°C (PMID: 32359985), whereas the supposed contradictory study does not directly address camel milk MRSA heat resistance.

statements.text: Raw camel milk used as a comparator did not contain methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus in the sampled retail market milk specimens.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One camel-milk study directly reports methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus in camel milk: among 100 camel milk samples, 31 S. aureus isolates were recovered, including 5 MRSA isolates, which conflicts with the claim that raw camel milk comparator samples lacked MRSA (PMID: 29041789). A second study found MRSA in retail raw milk samples more generally, but it did not specify camel milk, so it is only indirect and weaker contradiction for this specific claim (PMID: 24404781). Overall, the main contradiction theme is documented MRSA occurrence in camel milk or raw milk, though the evidence is not from the same sampled retail market specimens as the source study.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported. This is driven mainly by direct evidence that some camel milk samples contained MRSA (PMID: 29041789), although the contradiction is tempered because these are different studies and populations than the specific comparator specimens in the source article.

source_article.title: Inhibition of LXRα-dependent steatosis and oxidative injury by liquiritigenin, a licorice flavonoid, as mediated with Nrf2 activation.
source_article.abstract: Liver X receptor-α (LXRα) functions as a major regulator of lipid homeostasis through activation of sterol regulatory element binding protein-1c (SREBP-1c), which promotes hepatic steatosis and steatohepatitis. NF-E2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) is the crucial transcription factor that is necessary for the induction of antioxidant enzymes. This study investigated the potential of liquiritigenin (LQ), a hepatoprotective flavonoid in licorice, to inhibit LXRα-induced hepatic steatosis, and the underlying mechanism of the action. LQ treatment attenuated fat accumulation and lipogenic gene induction in the liver of mice fed a high fat diet. Also, LQ had the ability to inhibit oxidative liver injury, as shown by decreases in thiobarbituric acid reactive substances formation and nitrotyrosinylation. Moreover, LQ treatment antagonized LXRα agonist (T0901317)-mediated SREBP-1c activation, and transactivation of the lipogenic target genes. LQ was found to activate Nrf2, and the ability of LQ to inhibit LXRα-mediated SREBP-1c activation was reversed by Nrf2 deficiency, which supports the inhibitory role of Nrf2 in LXRα-dependent lipogenesis. Consistently, treatment with other Nrf2 activators or forced expression of Nrf2 also inhibited LXRα-mediated SREBP-1c activation. Our results demonstrate that LQ has an efficacy to activate Nrf2, which contributes to inhibiting the activity of LXRα that leads to SREBP-1c induction and hepatic steatosis.

statements.text: Liquiritigenin reduced hepatic fat accumulation and lipogenic gene induction in mice with high-fat-diet-induced liver steatosis.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract reports that isoliquiritigenin, a licorice-derived flavonoid, reduced hepatic steatosis in high-fat-diet-fed mice, with decreased liver fat accumulation and repression of lipogenic genes, alongside improved histopathology and oxidative stress markers (PMID: 20840863). The same study also showed mechanistic suppression of LXRα/SREBP-1c-driven lipogenesis in hepatocytes (PMID: 20840863). However, this is a notable discrepancy with the claim because the article studied isoliquiritigenin rather than liquiritigenin, so the evidence is only indirect for the specific compound named.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported overall. The available evidence is biologically aligned with the claim but comes from a study of isoliquiritigenin rather than liquiritigenin, limiting direct support for the exact statement.

statements.text: Liquiritigenin reduced oxidative liver injury in mice by lowering lipid peroxidation and nitrotyrosine formation.
statements.support_summary: One closely related mouse study found that isoliquiritigenin, a licorice flavonoid related to liquiritigenin, attenuated high-fat diet-induced liver injury and oxidative stress, specifically decreasing thiobarbituric acid-reactive substances and nitrotyrosinylation, which aligns well with reduced lipid peroxidation and nitrotyrosine formation (PMID: 20840863). Additional studies on liquiritigenin or related licorice flavonoids consistently report protection against oxidative hepatic injury through reduced ROS, mitochondrial damage, or lipid peroxidation markers and activation of antioxidant pathways such as AMPK/FXR or Nrf2/NRF2, but they usually do not explicitly measure nitrotyrosine formation (PMID: 26646674, PMID: 29908794, PMID: 37534604). A rat study with isoliquiritigenin also supports broader hepatoprotective antioxidant effects in vivo, though not the specific endpoints in the claim (PMID: 26593436). A key discrepancy is that several supporting papers involve isoliquiritigenin rather than liquiritigenin, or use rats/cell models rather than mice, so they support the general mechanism more than the exact claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest abstract-level support comes from a closely related mouse study showing reduced lipid peroxidation and nitrotyrosine-related oxidative injury markers with a licorice flavonoid (PMID: 20840863), while the remaining evidence consistently supports hepatoprotective antioxidant effects of liquiritigenin or related compounds but is less specific to the exact molecule, species, or endpoints.

statements.text: Liquiritigenin inhibited liver X receptor alpha-driven SREBP-1c activation and downstream lipogenic gene expression through activation of Nrf2.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that isoliquiritigenin, a licorice flavonoid, inhibits LXRα agonist-induced SREBP-1c activation and downstream lipogenic genes in hepatocytes and reduces hepatic steatosis in high-fat-fed mice, but attributes this effect to inhibition of the ASK1-MKK7-JNK1 pathway rather than Nrf2 activation (PMID: 20840863). This evidence also concerns isoliquiritigenin rather than liquiritigenin, which is a related but distinct compound, creating an additional discrepancy with the claim. Thus, the abstract is consistent with the phenotypic effect on LXRα/SREBP-1c-driven lipogenesis but contradicts or fails to support the specific proposed Nrf2-mediated mechanism (PMID: 20840863).
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall weakly contradicted or at least not supported by the provided evidence. The only cited abstract supports inhibition of LXRα/SREBP-1c lipogenic signaling, but in a different compound and through JNK1 inhibition rather than Nrf2 activation, so the key mechanistic part of the claim remains unsupported.

matched_article.title: Differential dependence on cysteine from transsulfuration versus transport during T cell activation.
matched_article.abstract: The synthesis of glutathione, a major cellular antioxidant with a critical role in T cell proliferation, is limited by cysteine. In this study, we evaluated the contributions of the x(C)(-) cystine transporter and the transsulfuration pathway to cysteine provision for glutathione synthesis and antioxidant defense in naïve versus activated T cells and in the immortalized T lymphocyte cell line, Jurkat. We show that the x(C)(-) transporter, although absent in naïve T cells, is induced after activation, releasing T cells from their cysteine dependence on antigen-presenting cells. We also demonstrate the existence of an intact transsulfuration pathway in naïve and activated T cells and in Jurkat cells. The flux through the transsulfuration pathway increases in primary but not in transformed T cells in response to oxidative challenge by peroxide. Inhibition of the transsulfuration pathway in both primary and transformed T cells decreases cell viability under oxidative-stress conditions.

statements.text: Activated T cells induce the x(C)(-) cystine transporter, which reduces their dependence on antigen-presenting cells for cysteine supply compared with naïve T cells.
statements.support_summary: Two abstracts support the claim that T-cell activation induces cystine transport capacity and reduces reliance on antigen-presenting cells for cysteine supply. In activated human CD4+ T cells, cystine/cysteine transporters are strongly upregulated, whereas naïve T cells express little to none, and the authors conclude activated cells can proliferate independently of APC-generated cysteine (PMID: 22355778). A second study more specifically shows that TCR stimulation upregulates the cystine transporter xCT/SLC7A11 and increases cystine uptake in human CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, supporting induction of the x(C)(-) transporter during activation, although it does not directly test APC dependence (PMID: 27594594). Together, the evidence consistently supports activation-associated induction of cystine uptake machinery, with one study directly linking this to reduced dependence on exogenous APC-derived cysteine.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The evidence consistently shows that naïve T cells have low cystine transport capacity, activated T cells upregulate cystine transporters including xCT, and at least one study directly concludes this enables greater independence from antigen-presenting-cell-derived cysteine.

statements.text: Naïve and activated primary T cells and Jurkat T cells possess an intact transsulfuration pathway for cysteine provision and glutathione synthesis.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One abstract directly contradicts the claim by stating that T cells lack cystathionase, cannot convert methionine to cysteine, and therefore require antigen-presenting cells to supply cysteine for activation (PMID: 20028852). The same study further reports that T cells lack an intact xc- transporter for cystine import, reinforcing the idea that their intrinsic cysteine-provision pathway is incomplete rather than intact (PMID: 20028852). This evidence is mechanistically relevant to the claim about transsulfuration and glutathione precursor supply, although it appears to discuss T cells broadly rather than separately addressing naïve, activated, primary, and Jurkat subsets.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported because the only provided evidence explicitly states that T cells lack cystathionase and depend on external cysteine supply rather than an intact internal transsulfuration pathway (PMID: 20028852). However, confidence is moderate rather than maximal because this conclusion rests on a single study abstract and does not specifically parse all T-cell subtypes named in the claim.

statements.text: Inhibition of the transsulfuration pathway reduces T-cell viability under oxidative stress in both primary T cells and Jurkat T cells.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: Study on the Multitarget Synergistic Effects of Kai-Xin-San against Alzheimer's Disease Based on Systems Biology.
source_article.abstract: Kai-Xin-San (KXS), a classical Chinese traditional prescription, was widely applied in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD), while its functional mechanisms still remain unclear. By using systems biology approaches at animal, cellular, and molecular levels, the improvement of KXS on cognitive impairment was achieved by inhibiting abnormal acetylcholinesterase. The function on the nerve skeleton was performed by regulating the Tau phosphorylation pathway. Its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antiapoptotic effects by modulating the aberrant upregulation of ROS, proinflammatory factors, and apoptosis-related proteins in the brain were studied to reveal the synergistic therapeutic efficacy of KXS. Then, formula dismantling in vitro indicated that ginseng was the principal herb, whereas three other herbs served adjuvant roles to achieve the best effect. After that, the in vivo analysis of components into plasma and brain of AD rats showed that 8 of 23 components in blood and 4 of 10 components in brain were from ginseng, respectively, further verifying the principal status of ginseng and the synergistic effects of the formula. Thus, the anti-AD effects of KXS were achieved by multitargets and multichannels. The systems biology approaches presented here provide a novel way in traditional herbal medicine research.

statements.text: Kai-Xin-San improved cognitive impairment in Alzheimer's disease by inhibiting abnormal acetylcholinesterase activity.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support that Kai-Xin-San (KXS) improves cognition in AD models, with the most direct evidence coming from a polysaccharide study showing both significant in vitro acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibition and improved learning/memory in AD-model rats, alongside neuroprotection and anti-inflammatory effects (PMID: 41088919). A recent APP/PS1 mouse study reported that KXS improved learning and memory and measured AChE activity among pathological biochemical indices, but the abstract does not clearly state that AChE inhibition was the causal mechanism, so this is only partial support (PMID: 39947370). A meta-analysis/network pharmacology paper found consistent cognitive benefits in AD models and identified ACHE as a core target with docking to candidate compounds, but this remains indirect mechanistic evidence rather than direct proof of abnormal AChE inhibition in vivo (PMID: 32956608). Another AD rat study showed cognitive improvement and increased hippocampal ChAT expression, which reflects cholinergic modulation but not specifically AChE inhibition; the Med-V1 hint appears somewhat inconsistent here because ChAT is not AChE (PMID: 26896572).
statements.contradict_summary: Multiple AD studies show that KXS improves cognition but attribute the benefit to other mechanisms, including CaMKKβ-AMPK-PGC-1α-mediated mitochondrial homeostasis and anti-apoptotic effects (PMID: 39520951), Aβ-GSK3β-Tau pathway regulation (PMID: 36474082), and mitochondrial autophagy/NLRP3 inflammasome modulation (PMID: 38582153). These abstracts do not directly refute that AChE inhibition may also contribute, but they do weaken the claim that cognitive improvement occurs specifically by inhibiting abnormal AChE activity because alternative mechanisms are emphasized instead. One non-AD rat study found KXS improved memory deficits mainly through antioxidant effects rather than the central cholinergic system, although it also noted some reversal of increased cortical AChE activity, making it only a partial contradiction and less directly relevant to AD (PMID: 27103112). Overall, the main recurring theme is that KXS has cognitive benefit in AD models, but many abstracts point to non-AChE mechanisms or do not mention AChE at all.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, because there is direct abstract-level evidence linking KXS-related treatment to both cognitive improvement and AChE inhibition in an AD model (PMID: 41088919), with additional indirect support from ACHE-target and biochemical studies. However, the evidence is not unequivocal, since several AD studies explain the cognitive benefits through other pathways and do not establish AChE inhibition as the primary mechanism.

statements.text: Kai-Xin-San affected neuronal structural pathology in Alzheimer's disease by regulating the tau phosphorylation pathway.
statements.support_summary: Direct experimental support comes from aged SAMP8 mice, where Kai-Xin-San (KXS) increased AKT phosphorylation, reduced GSK3β and CDK5 activation, inhibited Tau hyperphosphorylation, and decreased neuronal apoptosis, linking tau-pathway regulation to neuronal pathology improvement in an AD-like model (PMID: 35303280). Additional in vivo and network-pharmacology work in Aβ-induced AD rats reported that KXS improved cognition and acted through an Aβ-GSK3β-Tau signaling pathway, which is consistent with regulation of tau phosphorylation but gives less explicit detail on neuronal structural pathology itself (PMID: 36474082). A systems-pharmacology study also identified tau hyperphosphorylation as one of the major AD pathology modules potentially regulated by KXS, but its experimental validation focused mainly on inflammation and cholinergic dysfunction rather than direct tau or structural readouts, so this is more indirect support (PMID: 32317964). Overall, the main theme is that KXS is repeatedly linked to suppression of tau-related signaling, with the strongest abstract-level evidence for downstream neuronal benefit coming from the SAMP8 mouse study (PMID: 35303280).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence directly shows KXS modulating tau-phosphorylation-related kinases and reducing tau pathology with associated neuronal protection in an AD mouse model (PMID: 35303280), while the other studies provide mostly mechanistic and indirect reinforcement rather than contradiction.

statements.text: Kai-Xin-San exerted antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antiapoptotic effects in Alzheimer's disease by reducing abnormal oxidative stress, proinflammatory factors, and apoptosis-related proteins in the brain.
statements.support_summary: Multiple AD-model studies report that Kai-Xin-San (KXS) reduces oxidative stress, inflammation, and cell death-related pathology while improving cognition. Stronger direct support comes from APP/PS1 or AD rat studies showing that KXS suppresses oxidative stress and neuronal apoptosis, improves mitochondrial function, and lowers inflammatory signaling such as NLRP3 inflammasome activity (PMID: 36918872, PMID: 40033867). Other studies further support anti-inflammatory and antiapoptotic effects by showing inhibition of TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB signaling, reduced neuronal apoptosis, altered Bax/Bcl-2 or related markers, and decreased pro-inflammatory cytokines in brain tissue, although some focus more on mitochondrial homeostasis, tau pathology, pyroptosis, or gut-brain mechanisms than the exact claim wording (PMID: 35303280, PMID: 39520951, PMID: 40183082, PMID: 32317964). Additional partial support includes reports of improved antioxidant indices such as ROS/MDA reduction and SOD increase, but some evidence comes from non-brain or non-AD contexts (e.g., C. elegans, aging, depression), so relevance is somewhat indirect and there is a discrepancy between article focus and the claim for these items (PMID: 41075952, PMID: 41067322, PMID: 33790789).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Several animal and mechanistic studies in AD models directly indicate that KXS decreases oxidative stress, suppresses inflammatory mediators/pathways, and reduces apoptosis-related injury in the brain, although some supporting items are only partial or come from adjacent rather than exact AD contexts.

matched_article.title: Rosiglitazone Suppresses Calcium Oxalate Crystal Binding and Oxalate-Induced Oxidative Stress in Renal Epithelial Cells by Promoting PPAR-γ Activation and Subsequent Regulation of TGF-β1 and HGF Expression.
matched_article.abstract: Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor- (PPAR-) γ is a ligand-dependent transcription factor, and it has become evident that PPAR-γ agonists have renoprotective effects, but their influence and mechanism during the development of calcium oxalate (CaOx) nephrolithiasis remain unknown. Rosiglitazone (RSG) was used as a representative PPAR-γ agonist in our experiments. The expression of transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1), hepatocyte growth factor (HGF), c-Met, p-Met, PPAR-γ, p-PPAR-γ (Ser112), Smad2, Smad3, pSmad2/3, and Smad7 was examined in oxalate-treated Madin-Darby canine kidney (MDCK) cells and a stone-forming rat model. A CCK-8 assay was used to evaluate the effects of RSG on cell viability. In addition, intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels were monitored, and lipid peroxidation in renal tissue was detected according to superoxide dismutase and malondialdehyde levels. Moreover, the location and extent of CaOx crystal deposition were evaluated by Pizzolato staining. Our results showed that, both in vitro and in vivo, oxalate impaired PPAR-γ expression and phosphorylation, and then accumulative ROS production was observed, accompanied by enhanced TGF-β1 and reduced HGF. These phenomena could be reversed by the addition of RSG. RSG also promoted cell viability and proliferation and decreased oxidative stress damage and CaOx crystal deposition. However, these protective effects of RSG were abrogated by the PPAR-γ-specific inhibitor GW9662. Our results revealed that the reduction of PPAR-γ activity played a critical role in oxalate-induced ROS damage and CaOx stone formation. RSG can regulate TGF-β1 and HGF/c-Met through PPAR-γ to exert antioxidant effects against hyperoxaluria and alleviate crystal deposition. Therefore, PPAR-γ agonists may be expected to be a novel therapy for nephrolithiasis, and this effect is related to PPAR-γ-dependent suppression of oxidative stress.

statements.text: Rosiglitazone, a PPAR-γ agonist, reduces calcium oxalate crystal deposition and oxalate-induced oxidative stress in renal epithelial cells and in a stone-forming rat model.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a rosiglitazone study showing that ROSI suppressed renal calcium oxalate deposition and reduced oxidative stress/inflammatory injury, although the abstract describes a mouse rather than rat model, which is a minor discrepancy with the claim (PMID: 34691355). Additional indirect support comes from rodent and cell studies showing that PPARγ agonists reduce crystal formation/retention, oxidative stress, apoptosis, and tubular injury: pioglitazone decreased renal crystal formation, inflammation, oxidative stress, and apoptosis in hyperoxaluric rats (PMID: 27022389), while another PPARγ agonist improved oxidative stress, mitochondrial fission, apoptosis, and renal injury in CaOx nephrolithiasis models in vitro and in vivo (PMID: 39325731). A further mechanistic study found that PPARγ activity promotes epithelial redifferentiation and crystal clearance after oxalate exposure, and that agonist treatment partially reversed antagonist-induced worsening of crystal retention (PMID: 30317563). Overall, the recurring theme is that PPARγ activation protects renal tubular epithelial cells from oxalate/CaOx-associated oxidative and inflammatory injury and lowers crystal burden, with the most direct evidence specifically implicating rosiglitazone but in mice rather than rats.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall well supported. The evidence includes one direct rosiglitazone study showing reduced CaOx deposition and oxidative injury plus multiple consistent studies indicating that PPARγ agonism broadly decreases crystal burden and oxalate/CaOx-related oxidative damage, though some supporting studies involve other PPARγ agonists or different rodent species.

statements.text: Rosiglitazone’s protective effects against oxalate-induced renal injury depend on PPAR-γ activation, because a PPAR-γ-specific inhibitor abolishes these benefits.
statements.support_summary: Two studies indirectly support a PPARγ-dependent protective role in oxalate/calcium oxalate kidney injury, though neither directly tests rosiglitazone with a PPARγ-specific inhibitor. In a calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis model, a PPARγ agonist reduced mitochondrial fission, apoptosis, oxidative stress, inflammation, fibrosis, and improved renal function in renal tubular epithelial cells and rat kidneys (PMID: 39325731). In hyperoxaluric rodents, the PPARγ agonist pioglitazone significantly reduced renal crystal formation, inflammation, oxidative stress, apoptosis, and cell injury, supporting that PPARγ activation is protective in this disease context (PMID: 27022389). A key discrepancy is that these abstracts involve PPARγ agonists generally, especially pioglitazone, rather than rosiglitazone, and they do not show that a PPARγ-specific inhibitor abolishes benefit.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that rosiglitazone’s antioxidant protection can occur through a PPARγ-independent mechanism, but it is in endothelial cells under high-glucose stress rather than oxalate-induced renal injury. Specifically, rosiglitazone blocked glucose-induced ROS production via AMPK activation and inhibition of NAD(P)H oxidase, and this effect was prevented by AMPK inhibition but not by PPARγ inhibition (PMID: 17916771). Thus, it contradicts the general idea that rosiglitazone’s protective effects necessarily require PPARγ activation, but its relevance to the specific oxalate-renal injury claim is limited.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, but only indirectly. The supporting studies are disease-relevant and indicate that PPARγ activation is protective in hyperoxaluria/calcium oxalate renal injury, whereas the contradictory study involves a different injury model and cell type, though it does show rosiglitazone can act independently of PPARγ in some contexts.

statements.text: Oxalate-induced suppression of PPAR-γ contributes to increased reactive oxygen species production and altered TGF-β1 and HGF/c-Met signaling, whereas rosiglitazone reverses these changes.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: Breast Cancer Calcifications: Identification Using a Novel Segmentation Approach.
source_article.abstract: Breast cancer is a strong risk factor of cancer amongst women. One in eight women suffers from breast cancer. It is a life-threatening illness and is utterly dreadful. The root cause which is the breast cancer agent is still under research. There are, however, certain potentially dangerous factors like age, genetics, obesity, birth control, cigarettes, and tablets. Breast cancer is often a malignant tumor that begins in the breast cells and eventually spreads to the surrounding tissue. If detected early, the illness may be reversible. The probability of preservation diminishes as the number of measurements increases. Numerous imaging techniques are used to identify breast cancer. This research examines different breast cancer detection strategies via the use of imaging techniques, data mining techniques, and various characteristics, as well as a brief comparative analysis of the existing breast cancer detection system. Breast cancer mortality will be significantly reduced if it is identified and treated early. There are technological difficulties linked to scans and people's inconsistency with breast cancer. In this study, we introduced a form of breast cancer diagnosis. There are different methods involved to collect and analyze details. In the preprocessing stage, the input data picture is filtered by using a window or by cropping. Segmentation can be performed using k-means algorithm. This study is aimed at identifying the calcifications found in bosom cancer in the last phase. The suggested approach is already implemented in MATLAB, and it produces reliable performance.

statements.text: A novel segmentation-based breast cancer diagnosis approach identified breast cancer calcifications in breast imaging and produced reliable performance.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently shows that segmentation- or detection-based CAD methods can identify breast microcalcifications on mammograms with generally strong performance, often using thresholding, wavelets, Hessian-based filters, active contours, CNNs, or U-Net pipelines. Several studies report high sensitivity/accuracy for calcification detection or segmentation, including 96.5% sensitivity with 91.4% shape accuracy for segmented calcifications (PMID: 21989574), 97.76% true-positive ratio with 0.68 false positives/image (PMID: 22581343), 94.4% detection rate and 88.2% accuracy (PMID: 26737753), and deep-learning performance such as 99.99% detection accuracy in one CNN study (PMID: 31093321) and AUCs of 0.95 for segmentation and 0.89 for classification in DeepMiCa (PMID: 37030174). Multiple studies also extend beyond detection to malignancy classification or diagnostic support, with ROC/AUC values around 0.78-0.98 and claims of usefulness as a second reader or decision-support tool (e.g., PMID: 17307670, PMID: 15124996, PMID: 10368015, PMID: 21947904, PMID: 9800710). Some papers are only indirectly relevant because they focus on masses, general mammographic abnormalities, ROI extraction, or non-calcification-specific frameworks rather than calcification segmentation itself (e.g., PMID: 25776767, PMID: 22224592, PMID: 31053963), but the overall theme still favors successful identification of calcifications with reasonably reliable performance. A minor caveat is that at least one abstract notes weaker segmentation for smaller microcalcifications despite acceptable overall adequacy (PMID: 26363676), indicating performance may depend on lesion size.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The evidence base is large and consistently shows that novel segmentation/detection approaches can identify breast calcifications in mammographic images with good to excellent quantitative performance, although some studies are only indirectly aligned with the exact claim and performance may be lower for very small lesions.

statements.text: Breast cancer calcifications can be identified using image preprocessing followed by k-means segmentation in breast imaging data.
statements.support_summary: Two studies provide indirect support that breast calcifications can be detected using image preprocessing plus clustering/segmentation pipelines in mammographic images, though neither abstract specifically reports k-means. One study used multiresolution processing with fuzzy c-means clustering and region growing to detect both tiny and larger calcifications while reducing false positives (PMID: 20940127). Another described a multistage mammogram workflow with preprocessing, thresholding/histogram equalization, ROI localization, clustering, and fuzzy c-means cleanup, followed by wavelet-based microcalcification detection, reporting good sensitivity and reduced false positives (PMID: 28391823). A key discrepancy is that both abstracts rely on fuzzy c-means or other clustering methods rather than explicitly stating k-means segmentation, so support is for the broader preprocessing-plus-segmentation concept more than the exact claimed method.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly to moderately supported overall. The available abstracts support that breast calcifications can be identified through image preprocessing and clustering/segmentation approaches in mammography, but they do not directly demonstrate the specific use of k-means segmentation.

matched_article.title: Bioinformatics Analysis of Potential Biomarkers and Pathway Identification for Major Depressive Disorder.
matched_article.abstract: Aiming at a more comprehensive understanding of the molecular biomarkers and potential mechanisms of major depressive disorder (MDD), from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database, we first obtained mRNA expression profiles and identified 585 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) through the R software, including 263 upregulated genes and 322 downregulated genes. Then, through the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genome and Genome (KEGG) pathway and biological process (BP) analysis, we found that the upregulated and downregulated DEGs were abundant in different pathways, respectively. It was noteworthy that upregulated DEGs were the most significantly enriched in the mTOR signaling pathway. Subsequently, through the protein-protein interaction (PPI) network, we identified seven hub genes, namely, EXOSC2, CAMK2A, PRIM1, SMC4, TYMS, CDK6, and RPA2. Finally, through gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA), we obtained that hypoxia, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, hedgehog signaling, and reactive oxygen species pathway were the enriched pathways for MDD patients. The above data results would provide a new direction for the treatment of MDD patients.

statements.text: Major depressive disorder is associated with altered gene expression profiles that include both upregulated and downregulated genes, indicating dysregulated molecular pathways in affected patients.
statements.support_summary: Multiple transcriptomic studies in MDD consistently report differentially expressed genes in patient brain or blood, often explicitly including both upregulated and downregulated genes, supporting altered expression profiles in MDD; for example, postmortem cortex analysis found 147 upregulated and 402 downregulated genes with synapse- and metabolism-related enrichment (PMID: 37235790), and another study reported 143 upregulated and 77 downregulated genes with enrichment in signaling and HIF-1-related pathways (PMID: 36914737). Several studies further link these DEGs to dysregulated pathways involving synaptic transmission, immune/inflammatory signaling, MAPK, JAK-STAT, Toll-like receptor, cytokine-mediated signaling, mitophagy, ER stress, and circadian biology (PMID: 26728011, PMID: 34414022, PMID: 36353576, PMID: 37098514, PMID: 37633529, PMID: 38198075). Cross-tissue and validation work also supports reproducible MDD-associated signatures, including overlap between brain and blood and confirmation of selected hub genes by qPCR or postmortem assays (PMID: 35030558, PMID: 34414022, PMID: 38604455). Some support items are less direct because they emphasize DEGs or pathway dysregulation without always specifying directionality of change, but they still broadly align with the claim rather than conflict with it (e.g., PMID: 33238838, PMID: 31889427, PMID: 38765844). Overall, the support is strengthened by repeated findings across independent datasets, tissues, and analytic approaches.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. Across many independent studies, MDD is repeatedly associated with differential gene expression signatures—often explicitly containing both increased and decreased transcripts—and these signatures map to dysregulated molecular pathways, especially synaptic, immune-inflammatory, stress-response, and signaling networks.

statements.text: Upregulated genes in major depressive disorder are most strongly associated with mTOR signaling, and additional enriched pathways in major depressive disorder include hypoxia, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, hedgehog signaling, and reactive oxygen species-related processes.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Several studies point to alternative transcriptional themes in MDD rather than the claimed enrichment pattern. An ER stress-focused bioinformatics study found MDD-associated genes were mainly enriched in inflammatory and immune pathways including TNF, NF-κB, and Toll-like receptor signaling, not the claimed mTOR/hypoxia/EMT/hedgehog/ROS set (PMID: 36353576). Another transcriptomic analysis across brain regions emphasized downregulated NPAS4 and negatively enriched glutamatergic signaling modules, again not the claimed pathway profile (PMID: 38062059). A sib-pair peripheral blood study reported mostly downregulated genes with enrichment related to parathyroid hormone and cancer-associated pathways, which is not direct contradiction to the specific claim but is largely off-theme and suggests a discrepancy in its categorization as contradicting evidence (PMID: 34667146). One bioinformatics study did identify 143 upregulated genes and mentioned hypoxia-inducible factor 1 signaling among implicated functions, but it did not report mTOR as the strongest association nor the broader set of pathways in the claim, making it only weak/partial contradiction and also showing limited overlap with the claim (PMID: 36914737).
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported because no supporting studies were provided and the available studies emphasize other MDD-related pathways such as immune/inflammatory, glutamatergic, and other non-mTOR enrichments. However, the contradiction is only moderate because some cited studies are indirect or only partially relevant, and one includes limited overlap via hypoxia-related signaling.

statements.text: Major depressive disorder has potential biomarker candidates among the genes EXOSC2, CAMK2A, PRIM1, SMC4, TYMS, CDK6, and RPA2.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

source_article.title: Analyzing College Students' Reading Behavior by AI Techniques.
source_article.abstract: In order to deeply understand the requirements of artificial in the evaluation of high school reading behavior education, first of all, we compare the differences between reading behavior evaluations empowered by artificial intelligence and unwritten reading evaluations. Subsequently, the connotation of intelligent educational evaluations is revealed and combined. And skill education evaluation is divided into comprehension diagnosis evaluation. Libraries are places for the dissemination of (appellation) wisdom, the might office for the promotion of inn editions, and can provide a multifariousness of lection resources. The aware classroom of the library is to use communicative slaves to transfer readers to enjoy reading, to extract valuable scholarship from compacted documents, and to have the capability to exactly decay and solve problems. By insert the artificial intelligence (AI) techniques, manifold intelligent technologies such as arrange learning and semantic web last to emerge. The intelligent evaluation display plan for intelligently assists the English version of reading evaluation in high schools provides more subjective and accurate evaluations for English reading classroom readings, reduces teachers' knowledge pressure, and improves students' timely and effective erudite audio feedback.

statements.text: Artificial intelligence-assisted reading evaluation for high school English reading can provide more objective and accurate assessment of student reading performance than traditional unwritten reading evaluation.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Artificial intelligence-assisted reading evaluation for high school English reading can reduce teacher workload and improve the timeliness and effectiveness of feedback to students.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study describes a machine learning-based English essay scoring and comment-generation system designed to reduce teacher workload and allow students to practice scoring independently; in testing on 800 college CET-4 mock-test essays, the system reportedly improved scoring accuracy and efficiency compared with traditional models (PMID: 35655503). The abstract also suggests potential benefits for English teaching through more efficient management of scoring and automated evaluation comments, which is consistent with improved feedback processes (PMID: 35655503). However, this evidence is indirect because it concerns college-level English writing assessment rather than high school English reading evaluation, so applicability to the claim is only partial.
statements.contradict_summary: No direct contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is weakly to moderately supported overall. The only available evidence points in the claimed direction for AI-assisted language assessment reducing teacher burden and improving evaluation efficiency, but it is indirect because it addresses college essay scoring rather than high school English reading.

matched_article.title: SegChaNet: A Novel Model for Lung Cancer Segmentation in CT Scans.
matched_article.abstract: Accurate lung tumor identification is crucial for radiation treatment planning. Due to the low contrast of the lung tumor in computed tomography (CT) images, segmentation of the tumor in CT images is challenging. This paper effectively integrates the U-Net with the channel attention module (CAM) to segment the malignant lung area from the surrounding chest region. The SegChaNet method encodes CT slices of the input lung into feature maps utilizing the trail of encoders. Finally, we explicitly developed a multiscale, dense-feature extraction module to extract multiscale features from the collection of encoded feature maps. We have identified the segmentation map of the lungs by employing the decoders and compared SegChaNet with the state-of-the-art. The model has learned the dense-feature extraction in lung abnormalities, while iterative downsampling followed by iterative upsampling causes the network to remain invariant to the size of the dense abnormality. Experimental results show that the proposed method is accurate and efficient and directly provides explicit lung regions in complex circumstances without postprocessing.

statements.text: A U-Net-based segmentation model with channel attention can accurately and efficiently identify malignant lung regions in CT scans.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract describes a U-Net-derived 3D lung tumor CT segmentation model that incorporates squeeze-and-excitation blocks, a spatially adaptive attention mechanism, multi-scale processing, and CRF refinement, and reports strong performance on 759 scans with Dice 0.851, IoU 0.747, sensitivity 0.827, and PPV 0.900, outperforming baseline UNet variants (PMID: 35716533). The same study also reports external validation on NSCLC-Radiomics and LIDC datasets and ablation experiments showing reduced Dice when key modules are removed, supporting the value of the attention-enhanced architecture (PMID: 35716533). A minor discrepancy is that the abstract emphasizes squeeze-and-excitation/spatially adaptive attention and lung tumor segmentation rather than explicitly stating generic channel attention or broader malignant lung region identification, so the support is close but not perfectly exact.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted because the available study reports accurate CT lung tumor segmentation with an attention-enhanced U-Net architecture and shows improvement over standard UNet baselines (PMID: 35716533). However, support is only moderate because the evidence comes from a single study and the attention mechanism described is not stated in exactly the same terms as the claim.

statements.text: A multiscale dense-feature extraction approach can improve lung abnormality segmentation by helping the model remain robust to abnormalities of different sizes.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support the general idea that multiscale feature extraction improves lung-related segmentation, especially when targets vary in size. A COVID-19 lesion CT study explicitly states that multiscale and multilevel features were introduced to handle scale differences, and the proposed network performed better on large, medium, and small lesions (PMID: 35115573). A lung tumor CT study directly reports that its multi-scale strategy was designed for variable tumor size, improved Dice versus ablated versions without the multi-scale module, and retained advantages even as tumor volume decreased (PMID: 35716533). Additional indirect support comes from lung segmentation architectures using multiscale fusion or multiscale convolution/context modules that outperformed baseline/state-of-the-art models, though these abstracts do not directly test robustness to differently sized abnormalities and some focus on lung field rather than abnormality segmentation (PMID: 34157471, PMID: 34148011, PMID: 32602963, PMID: 32163817). Across studies, the recurring theme is that multiscale/dense/context-aware feature extraction improves segmentation performance and can mitigate scale-related challenges, but only a subset directly addresses lung abnormalities of different sizes.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence comes from lung lesion/tumor CT segmentation studies that explicitly link multiscale feature extraction to improved performance across differently sized abnormalities, while the remaining studies provide broader but more indirect support from lung or medical image segmentation tasks.

statements.text: The proposed lung tumor segmentation model can delineate lung regions in complex CT scan cases without requiring postprocessing.
statements.support_summary: Two studies support the feasibility of automated segmentation in difficult lung CT cases, but only indirectly for the specific claim. A deep network for complex lung tumors improved Dice and Hausdorff metrics versus prior models on 502 CT images with difficult-to-segment tumors, showing strong performance in challenging cases, though the abstract does not state that lung-region delineation is done without postprocessing (PMID: 34224147). Another multi-scale SE-UNet achieved good lung tumor segmentation performance across 759 CT scans and external datasets, but it explicitly includes a conditional random field to refine boundaries, which functions as a post-segmentation refinement rather than evidence for a no-postprocessing approach (PMID: 35716533). Overall, the support mainly concerns robust tumor segmentation in complex CT images rather than direct proof of delineating lung regions without postprocessing.
statements.contradict_summary: The contradictory evidence more directly addresses the postprocessing aspect. A thoracic CT OAR segmentation framework explicitly uses a post-processing step to merge segmented organs, including the lungs, which conflicts with the claim that such delineation can be achieved without postprocessing (PMID: 35029804). Other lung/lung-nodule segmentation papers also rely on multistep pipelines with explicit refinement or correction steps such as fuzzy C-means-based boundary extraction, filtering, thresholding, region growing, random walk segmentation, and curvature-based contour correction, indicating that complex CT lung-region delineation often depends on postprocessing or handcrafted stages rather than a pure end-to-end model (PMID: 28749127; PMID: 27635395). These studies are not exact matches to the claimed model, but they consistently suggest that postprocessing is commonly required in related complex CT segmentation tasks.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Supporting studies show good performance in complex lung tumor CT segmentation, but they do not directly establish lung-region delineation without postprocessing, whereas the contradictory literature more explicitly describes postprocessing as part of successful thoracic/lung segmentation pipelines.

source_article.title: Study on the Impact of Online Courses for Pregnant and Lying-In Women on Maternal and Infant Health during the Epidemic.
source_article.abstract: The sudden outbreak of the new crown pneumonia has brought online learning from a supporting role to the center of the teaching stage in an instant. On the basis of the feasibility analysis and demand analysis of the microcourse learning system, this paper uses Sina cloud server to build the WeChat public platform learning online course and designs and implements the microcourse learning system function based on the microcourse public platform. We completed the recording, editing, publishing, and testing of microclass courses and provided services for teachers and pregnant women's microclass learning in order to achieve better learning results. A total of 151 people regularly participate in maternity school courses, accounting for 30.4%. There are 190 people who have never attended the maternity school course, accounting for 38.2%. There are 156 people who occasionally participate in maternity school courses, accounting for 31.4%. The top five sources of health information during pregnancy are books, maternity schools, experience of elders, the Internet, and television. The results of one-way analysis of variance showed that pregnant women of different ages had statistically different scores in the dimensions of knowledge and ideas (P < 0.05). There are statistical differences in the scores of pregnant women with different economic incomes in this dimension (P < 0.05). The women with economic income ≥5000 yuan/month have the highest scores, and those with economic income ≥5000 yuan/month have the lowest scores. The scores of pregnant women who participated in the maternity school were significantly higher than those who did not participate in the maternity school (P < 0.05). There are statistical differences in the scores of maternal and child health basic skills among pregnant women of different age groups (P < 0.05). The women aged ≥35 years old have the highest scores, and those aged 20-24 years old have the lowest scores. The differences in the scores of pregnant women with different economic incomes in this dimension are statistically significant (P＜0.05). The women with economic income ≥5000 yuan/month have the highest score, and those with economic income<2000 yuan/month have the lowest score. Participation in maternity schools has an impact on the scores of this dimension. Pregnant women who regularly participate in maternity schools have the highest average scores, and those who do not participate in maternity schools have the lowest average scores.

statements.text: Pregnant women who participated in maternity school courses had better knowledge, attitudes, and maternal and child health basic skills than pregnant women who did not participate in maternity school courses.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Older pregnant women had better maternal and child health basic skills than younger pregnant women.
statements.support_summary: One cross-sectional study of pregnant women in rural India found that higher reproductive, maternal, newborn, child health, and nutrition knowledge was associated with older age, along with education, literacy, and antenatal care engagement, which directly supports the claim (PMID: 35273055). A PRAMS surveillance report across eight U.S. states found younger women had lower breastfeeding initiation and lower use of the infant back sleep position, although it addressed health behaviors rather than maternal/child health knowledge or basic skills, so it offers only indirect and limited support (PMID: 15229409). Overall, the supporting evidence is led by one directly relevant study and one indirect behavioral disparity report.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item studied skilled birth attendants in Cambodia, not pregnant women, and found younger age associated with higher intrapartum care knowledge scores among providers (PMID: 34108001). Because the population and outcome differ substantially from the claim about pregnant women's maternal and child health basic skills, this is better viewed as a discrepancy or miscategorized item rather than strong contradiction. There is therefore no direct contradictory evidence from pregnant women in the provided abstracts.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is mainly because one directly relevant study in pregnant women links older age with higher maternal/child health knowledge, while the sole contradictory item concerns healthcare workers rather than pregnant women and does not directly test the claim.

statements.text: Pregnant women with higher economic income had better knowledge, attitudes, and maternal and child health basic skills than pregnant women with lower economic income.
statements.support_summary: The supporting studies mostly provide indirect rather than direct evidence that higher economic status is linked to better maternal health-related knowledge or behaviors during pregnancy. A web-based Australian survey found pregnancy nutrition knowledge was positively associated with income among pregnant women, directly supporting an income-knowledge link, though only for nutrition knowledge and with generally low knowledge overall (PMID: 27395383). A Jordan study reported that economic status was associated with greater use of auxiliary dental hygiene devices during pregnancy, but knowledge outcomes were more clearly tied to education than income, making this only partial support for the claim as stated (PMID: 25307819). Large survey-based studies also suggest lower socioeconomic women had poorer maternity care utilization/experiences and that low income was associated with less favorable maternal behaviors such as lower breastfeeding initiation and less use of infant back-sleep positioning, but these abstracts do not directly measure knowledge, attitudes, and basic skills (PMID: 25227878; PMID: 15229409). One Ethiopian study linked rich wealth status with greater intention to use maternity waiting homes, but it analyzed wealth, knowledge, and attitudes as separate predictors rather than showing that wealthier women had better knowledge or attitudes, so its placement as support is somewhat discrepant (PMID: 32393188).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but the support is moderate and partly indirect. The strongest direct evidence is that higher income correlates with better pregnancy nutrition knowledge (PMID: 27395383), while the other studies mainly show socioeconomic differences in care use or health behaviors rather than directly demonstrating better knowledge, attitudes, and maternal/child health basic skills among higher-income pregnant women.

matched_article.title: Machine Learning Based Diabetes Classification and Prediction for Healthcare Applications.
matched_article.abstract: The remarkable advancements in biotechnology and public healthcare infrastructures have led to a momentous production of critical and sensitive healthcare data. By applying intelligent data analysis techniques, many interesting patterns are identified for the early and onset detection and prevention of several fatal diseases. Diabetes mellitus is an extremely life-threatening disease because it contributes to other lethal diseases, i.e., heart, kidney, and nerve damage. In this paper, a machine learning based approach has been proposed for the classification, early-stage identification, and prediction of diabetes.
