Multiple abstracts directly show lower miR-148a expression in HCC tumor tissues compared with matched adjacent/noncancerous liver tissue, whereas the sole 'contradicting' item does not actually provide opposing evidence in its abstract.

statements.text: Lower miR-148a expression in hepatocellular carcinoma is associated with more advanced clinicopathological features, including metastasis and greater tumor burden.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies report that miR-148a is reduced in HCC and that lower expression correlates with more advanced disease features. Tissue-based studies found low miR-148a associated with higher TNM stage, metastasis, capsular infiltration, and greater tumor number/nodes (PMID: 25444499), while serum/plasma or exosomal studies linked lower miR-148a to larger tumor size, advanced TNM/BCLC stage, multinodular lesions, lymph node or distant metastasis, and poorer survival (PMID: 26531720, PMID: 34355070, PMID: 35028121). Other reports connect lower miR-148a with poor differentiation, metastatic or nonmetastatic status, vascular invasion, portal vein tumor thrombus, and aggressive stem cell-like phenotypes, with functional data showing miR-148a suppresses EMT, migration, invasion, proliferation, and metastasis in vitro/in vivo (PMID: 23861222, PMID: 24013226, PMID: 31513595, PMID: 24798342, PMID: 25271001, PMID: 26599259, PMID: 32746934). Several studies are mechanistic or prognostic rather than directly clinicopathologic, but overall the recurring theme is that lower miR-148a accompanies more aggressive HCC behavior and greater tumor burden. No major discrepancy is evident within the supporting set beyond variation in whether associations are direct clinical correlations or indirect functional/prognostic evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: One study of recurrence after curative HCC resection found miR-148a-3p was downregulated in recurrent cases overall, but unlike miR-125b, miR-148a was not significantly associated with early recurrence on the key comparative analysis and was not identified as an independent predictor (PMID: 28984009). This does not directly test the claim's clinicopathologic endpoints such as tumor burden or metastasis, so it functions more as limited non-confirmation than strong contradiction. Thus, the contradicting evidence is sparse and indirect.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported. The evidence includes numerous human studies, spanning tissue and circulating miR-148a, that consistently associate lower miR-148a with metastasis, advanced stage, vascular invasion, poorer differentiation, and greater tumor burden, whereas the lone contradictory item mainly fails to show a recurrence association rather than directly refuting those clinicopathologic correlations.

statements.text: Low miR-148a expression in hepatocellular carcinoma is associated with worse prognosis and may serve as a prognostic biomarker.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies report that miR-148a is downregulated in HCC and that lower tissue, serum, plasma, or exosomal levels are associated with more aggressive disease features and poorer outcomes. Direct prognostic evidence includes shorter overall survival, recurrence-free/progression-free survival, or independent prognostic effects for low miR-148a in serum or tumor tissue in several cohorts (PMID: 26531720, PMID: 31513595, PMID: 32746934, PMID: 24798342, PMID: 34355070). Other studies link low miR-148a to aggressive subtypes, vascular invasion, metastasis, poor differentiation, portal vein tumor thrombus, and recurrence, supporting biological plausibility for worse prognosis (PMID: 25444499, PMID: 25271001, PMID: 23861222, PMID: 24013226). Additional bioinformatic and meta-analytic work suggests miR-148a may contribute to prognostic models or serve as a prognostic candidate, though some of these abstracts do not isolate the direction/effect of miR-148a alone as clearly as the primary cohort studies (PMID: 31210353, PMID: 35197055, PMID: 36065643, PMID: 29291025). Overall, the support is strengthened by recurring findings across independent tissue and circulating biomarker studies, with only a few items offering indirect rather than explicit prognostic evidence.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly partial rather than direct. One liver transplantation study found that higher early-phase circulating miR-148a after reperfusion was associated with recurrence, but survival prediction in that abstract was driven more clearly by miR-1246, and this postoperative injury-related setting may not reflect baseline tumor prognostic biology (PMID: 26918346). Another resection study reported that although miR-148a-3p was downregulated in recurrent cases, it was not significantly associated with early recurrence or disease-free survival in multivariable analysis, unlike miR-125b (PMID: 28984009). These studies therefore temper, but do not strongly overturn, the broader association between low miR-148a and poor prognosis.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The evidence includes several primary HCC studies directly linking low miR-148a expression to poorer survival and more aggressive clinicopathologic features, whereas the contradictory studies are fewer and context-specific or show only lack of significance rather than an opposite association.

matched_article.title: A critical role of mir-199a in the cell biological behaviors of colorectal cancer.
matched_article.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is one of the most common cancer and the leading causes of cancer mortality worldwide. The critical role of hypoxia-inducible factor 1α (HIF-1α) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) are important in the cancer development. The purpose of this study was to investigate the association of miR-199a expression in CRC and non-tumor tissues as well as assessed the effect of miR-199a on biological behaviors including cell proliferation, apoptosis, migration and invasion of CRC cells. The expression of miR-199a was distinctly decreased in colorectal cancer tissues compared with non-neoplastic colorectal tissues. In this study, we found that miR-199a down-regulation was associated with the CRC and metastasis incidence. Advanced study showed that miR-199a up-regulation would lead to decreased CRC proliferation, migration and invasion. However, no significant association of miR-199a treatment and apoptosis rate and cell-cycle were detected in this study. The detection for the mechanisms of miR-199a on the development of CRC showed that the anticarcinogenic effect of miR-199a might be produced through HIF-1α/VEGF pathway. It was found that miR-199a would reduce the proliferation, migration and invasion. However, overexpression of miR-199a on the apoptosis rate and cell cycles showed no significant results. The potential functionary mechanism of miR-199a might through HIF-1α/VEGF pathway. The virtual slide(s) for this article can be found here: http://www.diagnosticpathology.diagnomx.eu/vs/9806714131513041 .

statements.text: Colorectal cancer tissues show lower miR-199a expression than non-neoplastic colorectal tissues, and reduced miR-199a expression is associated with colorectal cancer and metastasis.
statements.support_summary: Two abstracts support reduced miR-199a expression in colorectal cancer tissue. One reports that miR-199a-5p is significantly downregulated in colorectal cancer tissues and that restoring miR-199a-5p suppresses tumor growth, migration, and invasion, consistent with a tumor-suppressive role (PMID: 22903020). Another paper is only indirect from the abstract: it states miR-199a-3p had previously been reported as a biomarker/prognostic indicator, but the abstract itself focuses on NLK and does not directly present lower miR-199a-3p levels or metastasis associations, so its placement as supporting evidence is somewhat uncertain from the abstract alone (PMID: 24972723). Overall, the supporting theme is that at least the miR-199a-5p arm is downregulated in CRC and functionally restrains aggressive behavior.
statements.contradict_summary: One study directly contradicts the claim, reporting that miR-199a-3p is significantly upregulated in colorectal cancer tissues versus non-neoplastic adjacent tissues and that high, not low, expression is associated with lymphatic invasion, lymph node metastasis, liver metastases, later TNM stage, and worse survival (PMID: 23292866). The same study also found that inhibiting miR-199a-3p reduced proliferation and increased apoptosis in CRC cells, supporting an oncogenic rather than tumor-suppressive role for this strand. A major discrepancy across the evidence is strand specificity: supporting evidence mainly concerns miR-199a-5p or indirect mentions of miR-199a-3p, whereas the contradiction is a direct clinicopathologic study of miR-199a-3p.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although there is support that miR-199a-5p is downregulated and suppresses malignant behavior in CRC, the strongest direct clinicopathologic evidence for miR-199a-3p reports the opposite pattern—higher expression in tumors and association of high expression with metastasis—making the overall evidence mixed and strand-dependent.

statements.text: In colorectal cancer cells, increased miR-199a expression reduces cell proliferation, migration, and invasion compared with lower miR-199a expression.
statements.support_summary: Direct supporting evidence comes from a colorectal cancer study showing miR-199a-5p is downregulated in CRC tissues and that restoring miR-199a-5p suppresses tumor cell growth, migration, and invasion, with CAC1 identified as a direct target (PMID: 22903020). Another CRC study reports that circUBAP2 promotes CRC progression by sponging miR-199a; importantly, VEGFA overexpression rescues phenotypes reduced by miR-199a overexpression, supporting an anti-proliferative and anti-migratory/invasive role for miR-199a in CRC cells (PMID: 32767322). One purportedly supporting article is actually discrepant: it shows miR-199a-3p targets NLK, while NLK itself suppresses proliferation, migration, and invasion, so the abstract does not support miR-199a-3p as inhibitory and may even imply the opposite (PMID: 24972723). Overall, the strongest abstract-level support is for miR-199a-5p/miR-199a broadly acting as a suppressor of CRC cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, but evidence is not fully consistent across isoforms.
statements.contradict_summary: A strong contradicting CRC study found miR-199a-3p upregulated in colorectal tumors, associated with lymphatic invasion, lymph node and liver metastasis, advanced TNM stage, and worse survival; moreover, inhibiting miR-199a-3p reduced SW480 cell proliferation, induced cell-cycle arrest, and increased apoptosis, directly contradicting the claim for the 3p isoform (PMID: 23292866). The other contradicting item is a gastric cancer study, not colorectal cancer, showing miR-199a promotes proliferation, migration, and invasion in gastric cancer cells, so it is only indirect and of limited relevance to the stated CRC claim (PMID: 21048306). There is also a notable cross-study discrepancy within CRC because the contradicting 3p study conflicts with the supportive 5p study, suggesting isoform-specific or context-dependent effects rather than a uniform role for all miR-199a species.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is mixed and overall unclear. There is solid direct support for a tumor-suppressive effect of miR-199a-5p/miR-199a in CRC cells, but there is also direct colorectal evidence that miR-199a-3p is associated with more aggressive disease and that its inhibition suppresses proliferation, indicating the claim is not consistently true across miR-199a isoforms or study contexts.

statements.text: The anti-carcinogenic effect of miR-199a in colorectal cancer is likely mediated through the HIF-1α/VEGF pathway.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that miR-199a suppresses colorectal cancer cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, and that VEGFA is a direct downstream target whose overexpression rescues these phenotypes, supporting involvement of a miR-199a/VEGFA axis in CRC (PMID: 32767322). However, this abstract does not mention HIF-1α, so it does not directly contradict the broader idea that miR-199a is anti-carcinogenic, but rather fails to support the specific proposed HIF-1α/VEGF pathway. This indicates a discrepancy with its categorization as contradicting evidence: it is better interpreted as partial mechanistic support for VEGFA involvement, while leaving HIF-1α unaddressed (PMID: 32767322).
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear and insufficient overall. There is no direct supporting evidence provided, and the lone 'contradicting' study actually suggests miR-199a can inhibit CRC progression through VEGFA-related signaling, but it does not test or establish mediation through HIF-1α.

source_article.title: Role of miR-584-5p in Lipopolysaccharide-Stimulated Human Bronchial Epithelial Cell Inflammation and Apoptosis.
source_article.abstract: Acute lung injury (ALI)/acute respiratory distress syndrome is a common clinical syndrome characterized by respiratory failure. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are closely related to ALI and acute respiratory distress syndrome. TargetScan software analysis showed that miR-584-5p can bind to the 3' noncoding region of TLR4, which is involved in the occurrence and development of ALI, thereby affecting the inflammatory pathway and inflammation development. Thus, we aimed to determine whether miR-584-5p affects ALI. Human bronchial epithelial (16-HBE) cells were transfected with miR-584-5p mimics or inhibitors and then stimulated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS).The cell viability, apoptosis, release of proinflammatory factors, mTOR, and NF-κB pathway protein expression were evaluated respectively. Mimic584 increased, whereas inhibitor584 decreased, LPS-stimulated inflammation. The protein expression of inflammatory factors was significantly increased in 16-HBE cells in the mimic584 + LPS group and decreased in the inhibitor584 + LPS group. Mimic584 activated mTOR and the NF-κB-related proteins P65 and p-p65, whereas inhibitor584 inactivated the proteins in 16-HBE cells. Overexpression of miR-584 significantly promoted apoptosis in LPS-stimulated 16-HBE cells. There were no differences in the proliferation and cell cycle of LPS-stimulated 16-HBE cells regardless of mimic584 or inhibitor584 transfection. Collectively, we demonstrated that inhibitor584 can alleviate ALI-induced expression of inflammatory factors via mTOR signaling and the NF-κB pathway. In conclusion, we found that inhibitor584 transfection could be a potential therapeutic strategy for ALI.

statements.text: In lipopolysaccharide-stimulated human bronchial epithelial cells, increased miR-584-5p worsens inflammatory responses and apoptosis, whereas reduced miR-584-5p lessens inflammatory responses.
statements.support_summary: One abstract provides only limited, indirect support. In LPS-induced human bronchial epithelial 16-HBE cells, transfection with either a negative-control miRNA or miR-584 mimic increased inflammatory cytokine/chemokine mRNA levels and reduced cell viability, indicating that the transfection procedure itself may be toxic and pro-inflammatory rather than demonstrating a specific harmful effect of miR-584-5p (PMID: 39099248). Because the abstract explicitly states that both control and miR-584 mimic transfection increased inflammatory factors, this creates an internal discrepancy with the claim-specific interpretation. The abstract also does not directly test miR-584-5p reduction or clearly measure apoptosis, only decreased viability.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The single study mentions miR-584 mimic use in LPS-stimulated HBE cells, but its abstract suggests the observed inflammation and reduced viability may be driven by transfection-related toxicity rather than a specific miR-584-5p effect, and it does not address miR-584-5p knockdown.

statements.text: In lipopolysaccharide-stimulated human bronchial epithelial cells, reduced miR-584-5p decreases inflammatory factor expression through inhibition of mTOR signaling and NF-κB pathway activation.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One study reports that in LPS-induced 16-HBE cells, transfection with either miR-584 mimic or negative-control mimic using Lipofectamine 3000 increased inflammatory cytokine/chemokine expression and reduced cell viability, suggesting the transfection procedure itself can confound inflammatory readouts rather than demonstrating an anti-inflammatory effect of reduced miR-584-5p (PMID: 39099248). This abstract does not report that lowering miR-584-5p decreases inflammatory factors, nor does it provide evidence for inhibition of mTOR signaling or NF-κB pathway activation, so it conflicts with the claim mainly by undermining the experimental interpretation rather than directly testing the stated mechanism (PMID: 39099248).
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported because there is no supporting evidence provided, and the only cited study raises methodological concerns that oppose the proposed anti-inflammatory interpretation of miR-584 manipulation. However, the contradiction is limited because the study does not directly test reduced miR-584-5p or the mTOR/NF-κB mechanism.

statements.text: In lipopolysaccharide-stimulated human bronchial epithelial cells, miR-584-5p overexpression promotes apoptosis without affecting proliferation or cell-cycle progression.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Scalp Electroacupuncture Promotes Angiogenesis after Stroke in Rats by Activation of Wnt/β-Catenin Signal Pathway.
matched_article.abstract: Scalp acupuncture is a contemporary acupuncture method based on the fundamental theories of traditional acupuncture, which has been widely used in patients with stroke in China. However, the effectiveness is controversial due to lack of solid experimental evidence. In this study, a rat model of cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury (CIRI) was established by the middle cerebral artery occlusion/recirculation. The efficacy of scalp acupuncture against CIRI was evaluated by the mNSS scores, TTC staining for brain slices, and laser Doppler perfusion imaging. Immunohistochemical staining for angiogenetic factors indicated the vascularization after CIRI, including VEGF, Ang2, and bFGF. Activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway and p-GSK3β (ser9)/VEGF pathway in the injured brain tissues was assessed by western blotting and qRT-PCR. On the 7, 14, and 21 days after CIRI, scalp acupuncture could reduce the mNSS scores, decrease the cerebral infarction area, and accelerate the recirculation of ischemic brain tissues. VEGF, FLK1, bFGF, and Ang2 were upregulated on both the mRNA and protein levels in the ischemic brain tissues of the AC group, suggesting that the recirculation might result from angiogenesis, which was also confirmed with the IHC staining in the angiogenetic markers of VEGF, Ang2, and bFGF. Moreover, Wnt3a, β-catenin, and cyclin D1 were also upregulated on both the mRNA and protein levels in the ischemic brain tissues of the AC group on day 7, 14, and 21, indicating that the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway was activated after the treatment of scalp acupuncture. In contrast, dikkoppf-1 (DKK1) pretreatment, a specific inhibitor for the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, inactivated the Wnt3a/β-catenin signaling pathway and exacerbated the infarct size induced by the cerebral IR injury on day 7. Together, our findings demonstrated a mechanism whereby scalp acupuncture led to the activation of Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, promoting angiogenetic factor expression and restoring blood perfusion in the ischemic zone.

statements.text: In rats with cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury after middle cerebral artery occlusion/recirculation, scalp electroacupuncture improves neurological deficits, reduces infarct size, and restores blood perfusion in the ischemic brain.
statements.support_summary: Multiple rat MCAO or MCAO/reperfusion studies report that electroacupuncture improves neurological deficit scores and reduces infarct volume, including direct ischemia-reperfusion models after reperfusion (PMID: 24828425, PMID: 27716383, PMID: 34929338, PMID: 33788427, PMID: 26329606, PMID: 33428141). Some evidence also addresses cerebral perfusion or blood flow: electroacupuncture combined with rehabilitation increased regional cerebral blood flow and reduced infarct volume in acute cerebral ischemia rats (PMID: 31475486), while review articles describe acupuncture/scalp acupuncture as capable of enhancing cerebral perfusion/CBF, though these are indirect rather than model-specific evidence (PMID: 36388196, PMID: 37650106). Additional studies support related neuroprotection through reduced apoptosis, inflammation, or neurovascular-unit injury in MCAO/reperfusion rats (PMID: 30091538, PMID: 31037902, PMID: 26994873, PMID: 32142833, PMID: 34865328). However, several items are only partial matches because they involve pretreatment rather than post-injury treatment, standard acupuncture rather than scalp electroacupuncture, or combination therapies rather than electroacupuncture alone (e.g., PMID: 26994873, PMID: 32142833, PMID: 33066914, PMID: 31475486). Overall, the abstracts consistently support benefits of electroacupuncture/acupuncture on neurological outcomes and infarct size in rat cerebral ischemia models, with more limited direct evidence for restored blood perfusion in the specific MCAO/recirculation scalp-electroacupuncture setting.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The evidence base is fairly consistent that electroacupuncture in rat MCAO/ischemia-reperfusion models improves neurological deficits and reduces infarct size, but support for restored ischemic-brain perfusion is more limited and often indirect or comes from combination-treatment/review evidence rather than the exact scalp electroacupuncture post-reperfusion model.

statements.text: In rats with cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury after middle cerebral artery occlusion/recirculation, scalp electroacupuncture increases expression of angiogenic factors in ischemic brain tissue, consistent with enhanced angiogenesis.
statements.support_summary: Several animal studies support that acupuncture/electroacupuncture increases angiogenesis-related factors in ischemic brain tissue. In a rat middle cerebral artery occlusion model, EA increased VEGF protein and mRNA in ischemic brain tissue at 7, 14, and 21 days versus untreated model rats, alongside reduced infarct size and neurological deficits (PMID: 33788427). Another rat cerebral infarction study found acupuncture upregulated VEGF and bFGF and downregulated endostatin in the ischemic penumbra, consistent with a pro-angiogenic shift, though it used manual acupuncture plus thrombolysis rather than scalp EA in MCAO/recirculation (PMID: 34558240). A further acute cerebral ischemia study reported that EA combined with rehabilitation promoted expression of angiogenesis-related factors in ischemic brain tissue and improved regional cerebral blood flow/infarct volume, but the abstract does not isolate the independent effect of EA alone and is less specific about the reperfusion model (PMID: 31475486). A review also describes acupuncture as promoting new vessel formation after ischemic stroke, but this is indirect/background rather than direct experimental confirmation of the specific rat MCAO/recirculation claim (PMID: 36388196).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. Direct rat evidence shows EA can upregulate VEGF in ischemic brain tissue after MCAO-type ischemia (PMID: 33788427), and additional related studies support a broader pro-angiogenic effect of acupuncture, although some are less specific to scalp EA, reperfusion injury, or isolate combined treatments rather than EA alone.

statements.text: In rats with cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury after middle cerebral artery occlusion/recirculation, the beneficial effects of scalp electroacupuncture depend on activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, because inhibition of this pathway worsens injury and blocks the treatment effect.
statements.support_summary: Several rat MCAO studies report that electroacupuncture (EA) improves neurological deficits, reduces infarct volume, and is accompanied by increased Wnt/β-catenin pathway activity. Most directly, EA pretreatment increased β-catenin and neuroprotection, and these benefits were attenuated by the Wnt antagonist Dkk-1, indicating pathway dependence (PMID: 26994873). Additional studies found EA upregulated β-catenin/Wnt1 or related pathway components (e.g., Wnt7a, LEF1) and downregulated inhibitory factors such as GSK-3β, DKK1, or Axin2 while improving stroke outcomes, consistent with a mechanistic role for Wnt signaling (PMID: 26329606, PMID: 33788427, PMID: 32202711). One study linked EA pretreatment neuroprotection to GSK-3β/β-catenin signaling and showed reversal by wortmannin, although this is less direct for specifically testing Wnt/β-catenin inhibition and the abstract’s wording suggests some mechanistic complexity (PMID: 32142833). Overall, the abstracts consistently support that EA benefit is associated with, and in at least one study partly dependent on, Wnt/β-catenin activation.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest support comes from a study directly showing that Wnt pathway antagonism with Dkk-1 blunted EA-induced neuroprotection after MCAO/reperfusion (PMID: 26994873), while multiple additional studies consistently associate EA benefit with activation of Wnt/β-catenin-related signaling, though not all directly test pathway inhibition.

source_article.title: Raf kinase inhibitor protein (RKIP) blocks signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 (STAT3) activation in breast and prostate cancer.
source_article.abstract: Raf kinase inhibitor protein (RKIP) is a member of the phosphatidylethanolamine-binding-protein (PEBP) family that modulates the action of many kinases involved in cellular growth, apoptosis, epithelial to mesenchymal transition, motility, invasion and metastasis. Previously, we described an inverse association between RKIP and signal transducers and activators of transcription 3 (STAT3) expression in gastric adenocarcinoma patients. In this study, we elucidated the mechanism by which RKIP regulates STAT3 activity in breast and prostate cancer cell lines. RKIP over expression inhibited c-Src auto-phosphorylation and activation, as well as IL-6-, JAK1 and 2-, and activated Raf-mediated STAT3 tyrosine and serine phosphorylation and subsequent activation. In MDA-231 breast cancer cells that stably over express RKIP, IL-6 treatment blocked STAT3 phosphorylation and transcriptional activation. Conversely, in RKIP knockdown MDA-231 cells: STAT3 phosphorylation and activation increased in comparison to parental MDA-231 cells. RKIP over expression resulted in constitutive physical interaction with STAT3 and blocked c-Src and STAT3 association. The treatment of DU145 prostate, but not PC3 prostate or MDA-231 breast, cancer cell lines with ENMD-1198 or MKC-1 dramatically increased expression of RKIP. Overexpression of RKIP sensitized PC3 and MDA-231 cells to MTI-induced apoptosis. Moreover, MTI treatment resulted in a decrease in Src-mediated STAT3 tyrosine phosphorylation and activation, an effect that was significantly enhanced by RKIP over expression. In stable RKIP over expressing MDA-231 cells, tumor xenograft growth induced by activated STAT3 is inhibited. RKIP synergizes with MTIs to induce apoptosis and inhibit STAT3 activation of breast and prostate cancer cells. RKIP plays a critical role in opposing the effects of pro-oncogenic STAT3 activation.

statements.text: In breast and prostate cancer cells, RKIP overexpression inhibits STAT3 activation by blocking upstream Src- and cytokine/JAK-dependent STAT3 phosphorylation and by disrupting Src-STAT3 association.
statements.support_summary: The only supporting item is a review stating that RKIP negatively regulates STAT3 and generally acts by associating with and blocking upstream signaling molecules, which is directionally consistent with the claim that RKIP overexpression suppresses STAT3 activation (PMID: 34760627). However, from the abstract alone, this review does not specifically document breast or prostate cancer cells, Src- or JAK-dependent STAT3 phosphorylation, or disruption of Src-STAT3 association, so support for the detailed mechanism is limited (PMID: 34760627).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The available abstract supports a general inhibitory relationship between RKIP and STAT3, but it does not directly substantiate the specific mechanistic details about Src, cytokine/JAK signaling, or Src-STAT3 complex disruption in breast and prostate cancer cells.

statements.text: In breast and prostate cancer cells, increased RKIP expression sensitizes cancer cells to apoptosis induced by microtubule-targeting agents and enhances the agents’ inhibition of STAT3 activation.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract reports that RKIP overexpression in two prostate cancer cell lines enhanced the effects of docetaxel, a microtubule-targeting agent, while also inhibiting proliferation, migration, and invasion (PMID: 29534584). This is consistent with the claim's prostate-cancer component that increased RKIP can sensitize cells to microtubule-targeting therapy, but the abstract does not specifically show apoptosis induction or inhibition of STAT3 activation, and it does not address breast cancer. Thus, the available support is partial and narrower than the full claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly to partially supported by the provided evidence. The single abstract supports enhanced response to a microtubule-targeting agent in prostate cancer cells, but it does not directly address apoptosis, STAT3 inhibition, or breast cancer, which are central parts of the claim.

statements.text: In breast cancer cells, RKIP overexpression suppresses STAT3-driven tumor xenograft growth.
statements.support_summary: The only supporting item is a review stating that RKIP negatively regulates multiple metastatic signaling pathways, including STAT3, in general cancer biology (PMID: 34760627). This provides indirect mechanistic support that RKIP could oppose STAT3-mediated tumor-promoting effects, but the abstract does not specifically report breast cancer cells, RKIP overexpression experiments, or suppression of STAT3-driven xenograft growth. Thus, the available support is thematic rather than direct, and it comes from a review rather than primary xenograft data (PMID: 34760627).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The sole item offers indirect review-level support that RKIP can negatively regulate STAT3, but it does not directly demonstrate suppression of STAT3-driven breast cancer xenograft growth by RKIP overexpression.

matched_article.title: Differential responses of herbivores and herbivory to management in temperate European beech.
matched_article.abstract: Forest management not only affects biodiversity but also might alter ecosystem processes mediated by the organisms, i.e. herbivory the removal of plant biomass by plant-eating insects and other arthropod groups. Aiming at revealing general relationships between forest management and herbivory we investigated aboveground arthropod herbivory in 105 plots dominated by European beech in three different regions in Germany in the sun-exposed canopy of mature beech trees and on beech saplings in the understorey. We separately assessed damage by different guilds of herbivores, i.e. chewing, sucking and scraping herbivores, gall-forming insects and mites, and leaf-mining insects. We asked whether herbivory differs among different forest management regimes (unmanaged, uneven-aged managed, even-aged managed) and among age-classes within even-aged forests. We further tested for consistency of relationships between regions, strata and herbivore guilds. On average, almost 80% of beech leaves showed herbivory damage, and about 6% of leaf area was consumed. Chewing damage was most common, whereas leaf sucking and scraping damage were very rare. Damage was generally greater in the canopy than in the understorey, in particular for chewing and scraping damage, and the occurrence of mines. There was little difference in herbivory among differently managed forests and the effects of management on damage differed among regions, strata and damage types. Covariates such as wood volume, tree density and plant diversity weakly influenced herbivory, and effects differed between herbivory types. We conclude that despite of the relatively low number of species attacking beech; arthropod herbivory on beech is generally high. We further conclude that responses of herbivory to forest management are multifaceted and environmental factors such as forest structure variables affecting in particular microclimatic conditions are more likely to explain the variability in herbivory among beech forest plots.

statements.text: Arthropod herbivory on European beech in temperate European forests is generally high, with substantial leaf damage occurring on beech foliage.
statements.support_summary: One field study across 48 beech forest plots in three German regions found measurable arthropod herbivore abundance and leaf damage on Fagus sylvatica, with damage increasing as beech dominance increased and decreasing with harvest intensity; leaf chewers and miners were positively related to leaf damage, showing that herbivory can be substantial and management-sensitive in some temperate beech forests (PMID: 25938417). However, the abstract does not state that herbivory is generally high across European beech forests overall, so support for the broad claim is only partial.
statements.contradict_summary: Several abstracts suggest beech may experience relatively limited herbivory rather than generally high damage. A study along forest layers reported that herbivory was lowest in upper canopies and, importantly, that among three co-occurring tree species, Fagus sylvatica had the lowest herbivory, which the authors linked to tougher, lower-nitrogen, higher-carbon leaves (PMID: 28099483). Another paper found that beech supports a comparatively low richness of associated insect species, with far fewer insects than oak-dominated forests and a paucity of monophagous species, attributing this partly to beech secondary chemistry; this speaks more directly to insect community richness than to measured leaf damage, so its contradiction to the claim is indirect (PMID: 21686149). A further study showed lower leaf herbivory and sucking damage on beech in Douglas fir mixtures than in beech monocultures, indicating that damage can be reduced by stand composition, but it does not by itself show that herbivory is low in absolute terms, so this is only partial contradiction (PMID: 36477972).
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall somewhat more contradicted than supported. The only supporting study shows that beech herbivory occurs and varies with forest composition and management, but the contradictory evidence more directly suggests that beech often has relatively low herbivory compared with other species or under some canopy and mixture contexts, and one contradiction is only indirect because it concerns insect richness rather than actual damage.

statements.text: Herbivory on European beech differs more by canopy versus understory position and by herbivore guild than by forest management regime, with damage generally greater in the canopy than in the understory.
statements.support_summary: One study on European beech along a vertical forest gradient reports that arthropod herbivory patterns vary with environmental conditions, leaf traits, and feeding guild across canopy versus understory positions, which is consistent with the claim that vertical position and guild matter (PMID: 29373542). However, the provided abstract is truncated and does not clearly state comparisons with forest management regime or explicitly confirm that damage is generally higher in the canopy, so this is only partial support.
statements.contradict_summary: A study of leaf-chewing herbivory along forest layers in European beech found the lowest herbivory levels in the upper canopy and attributed this to tougher, lower-nitrogen, higher-carbon leaves despite warmer temperatures there (PMID: 28099483). This directly conflicts with the claim's direction that damage is generally greater in the canopy than in the understory. The study focuses on vertical gradients and leaf traits rather than forest management regime, so its contradiction is strongest for the canopy-versus-understory damage pattern, not the management component.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported based on the available evidence. The only supporting item gives limited, indirect support for vertical and guild-related differences, whereas the contradicting study directly reports lower herbivory in the upper canopy than the understory.

statements.text: Forest management regime has little consistent effect on arthropod herbivory on European beech, and variation in forest structure and related microclimatic conditions appears more important for explaining differences in herbivory among beech forest plots.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: Two studies directly report management- or composition-related effects on beech herbivory: across 48 German forest plots, herbivore abundance and leaf damage on beech were consistently associated with tree species composition and harvest intensity, with damage increasing with beech dominance and decreasing with harvest intensity (PMID: 25938417). Another study found lower herbivory on beech saplings and mature trees in mixtures with non-native Douglas fir than in beech monocultures, indicating that stand composition can substantially alter leaf damage, although this is partly about species mixture rather than management regime per se (PMID: 36477972). A third paper is only indirectly relevant: it examined plant-insect herbivore network structure across fragments, edges, and interiors, emphasizing fragmentation, host-plant availability, and habitat conditions rather than directly measuring beech herbivory under management regimes, so its placement as contradiction is somewhat discrepant (PMID: 28185607). Overall, the contradicting evidence mainly points to tree composition and harvesting having measurable effects on herbivory, but some of that evidence overlaps with forest structure/context rather than cleanly isolating management regime.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported because no supporting studies were provided, while two abstracts give fairly direct evidence that harvest intensity and tree species composition influence herbivore damage on European beech. However, the contradiction is not absolute, since some evidence conflates management with stand composition or habitat structure rather than decisively ruling out an important role for microclimatic or structural variation.

source_article.title: In vitro and in vivo human gastric cancer inhibition by Trifolirhizin is facilitated via autophagy, mitochondrial mediated programmed cell death, G2/M phase cell cycle arrest and inhibition of m-TOR/PI3K/AKT signalling pathway.
source_article.abstract: PURPOSE: To investigate the anticancer effects of Trifolirhizin in SNU-5 human gastric cancer cells along with evaluation of its effects on autophagy, apoptosis, cell cycle phase distribution and m-TOR/PI3K signalling pathway. METHODS: The antiproliferative effect on gastric cancer cells was assessed by MTT assay. Autophagy was detected by electron microscopy and western blot. Apoptotic cell death was revealed by acridine orange (AO)/ethidium bromide (EB) and annexin V/propidium iodide (PI) staining using flow cytometry. Cell cycle analysis was carried out by flow cytometry. Protein expression was determined by immunoblotting. Xenografted mice models were used to evaluate in vivo the anticancer effects of Trifolirhizin. RESULTS: Trifolirhizin could significantly inhibit the proliferation of the gastric cancer cells. The anticancer activity of Trifolirhizin against the gastric cancer cells was found to be due to induction of autophagy and mitochondrial-mediated apoptosis. It was further observed that both apoptosis and autophagy-related protein expressions sere significantly altered. Further, it was found that Trifolirhizin could inhibit the m-TOR/PI3K/AKT signalling pathway. In vivo evaluation in xenografted mice indicated that Trifolirhizin inhibited significantly both tumor weight and tumor volume. CONCLUSIONS: In conclusion, it can be safely stated that Trifolirhizin has the potential to be developed as a potent anticancer agent against gastric carcinoma provided further in depth evaluation of this compound is performed.

statements.text: Trifolirhizin inhibits proliferation of human gastric cancer cells and suppresses gastric carcinoma tumor growth in xenografted mice.
statements.support_summary: One non-retracted study directly supports the claim: trifolirhizin significantly decreased proliferation of human gastric cancer MKN45 cells in a time- and dose-dependent manner and also inhibited MKN45 xenograft tumor growth in vivo (PMID: 27666116). The abstract further reports apoptosis-related effects and pathway changes, consistent with an antitumor action in gastric cancer cells (PMID: 27666116). A second cited item is a retraction notice for the source article itself, stating that data were unreliable and raw data could not be provided, so although the original article purportedly supported in vitro and in vivo gastric cancer inhibition, this evidence is not reliable and should be treated as a discrepancy rather than valid support (PMID: 34077023). Overall, the support rests mainly on one direct experimental abstract with both cell and xenograft findings.
statements.contradict_summary: No direct contradicting evidence items were provided. However, there is an important reliability concern because the source article was retracted due to unreliable data and inability to provide raw data, which weakens confidence in that particular report even though it is not direct experimental contradiction (PMID: 34077023).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because one non-retracted abstract directly reports reduced proliferation of human gastric cancer cells and suppression of xenograft growth with trifolirhizin (PMID: 27666116). However, confidence is only moderate since the source article itself was retracted for unreliable data, which limits the strength of the overall evidence base (PMID: 34077023).

statements.text: Trifolirhizin induces autophagy and mitochondrial-mediated apoptotic cell death in human gastric cancer cells.
statements.support_summary: One supporting item is actually a retraction notice stating that the original gastric cancer study reporting Trifolirhizin-induced autophagy and mitochondrial-mediated programmed cell death was retracted because some data were unreliable and raw data could not be provided, so this should be treated as conflicting rather than supportive evidence (PMID: 34077023). A separate study in MKN45 human gastric cancer cells found that trifolirhizin inhibited proliferation, showed apoptotic features by Hoechst staining/TUNEL, and examined caspase activation, supporting an anti-cancer and apoptosis-inducing effect in gastric cancer cells, but its abstract does not explicitly report autophagy or clearly specify mitochondrial-mediated apoptosis (PMID: 27666116). Overall, the available support from abstracts is limited to partial evidence for apoptosis in gastric cancer cells, with the strongest mechanistic claim weakened by retraction.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no formal contradicting evidence items. However, the retraction notice indicates that the main article originally asserting autophagy and mitochondrial-mediated programmed cell death in gastric cancer had unreliable data, which substantially undermines confidence in that mechanism claim even though it is not direct experimental contradiction (PMID: 34077023).
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported overall. There is some abstract-level evidence that trifolirhizin induces apoptosis in human gastric cancer cells, but the specific claim about autophagy and mitochondrial-mediated apoptotic death relies on a retracted and unreliable report, leaving the mechanistic evidence insufficient.

statements.text: Trifolirhizin inhibits the m-TOR/PI3K/AKT signaling pathway in human gastric cancer cells.
statements.support_summary: The only cited supporting item is a retraction notice stating that the original gastric cancer study reported Trifolirhizin inhibited the m-TOR/PI3K/AKT pathway, but that article was retracted because part of the data was found unreliable and the authors could not provide raw data (PMID: 34077023). Thus, while the claim is explicitly described in the notice, the abstract primarily undermines the reliability of that evidence rather than providing dependable support. This is a major discrepancy because the item is categorized as support, yet its abstract functions mainly as a warning about invalidated findings.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no direct contradicting evidence items provided. However, the absence of contradiction does not strengthen the claim, especially because the only related evidence is a retraction notice that questions the underlying data.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is unclear and not reliably supportive. Although the claim appears in the retracted study's title and notice, the only available evidence explicitly states that the data were unreliable, so there is no robust basis to conclude that Trifolirhizin inhibits m-TOR/PI3K/AKT signaling in human gastric cancer cells.

matched_article.title: MiR-335-5p Inhibits Cell Proliferation, Migration and Invasion in Colorectal Cancer through Downregulating LDHB.
matched_article.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is a common malignancy which has a high mortality rate around the world. The advancement of new therapeutic strategies is crucial for the efficient treatment of CRC. Many miRNAs play a central role in the progression of cancer cells. There are still few studies on miR-335-5p and CRC. In this study, the potential of miR-335-5p in CRC development and progression was explored. The expression level of miR-335-5p in CRC cell lines and tissues was detected by quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) assay. Cell counting kit-8 (CCK8) assay and colony formation assay were applied for evaluating the ability of cell proliferation. Wound healing assay and Transwell assay were applied for detecting cell migration and invasion ability. Moreover, dual luciferase reporter assay was performed to validate if lactic dehydrogenase B (LDHB) is a downstream target of miR-335-5p. Western blotting was used to estimate the expression of protein. The expression of miR-335-5p was significantly low in CRC tissues and cells. To investigate the function of miR-335-5p in CRC, two CRC cell lines (HCT-116 and SW620) were selected for further experiments. After transfection with mimics and inhibitor to up-regulate or down-regulate miR-335-5p, it was found that overexpression of miR-335-5p obviously decreased cell proliferation and inhibited migration ability and invasion, while the knockdown of miR-335-5p could obtain the opposite results. Then it was validated in dual luciferase reporter assay that LDHB could be a potential directive target gene of miR-335-5p. Moreover, the rescue assay confirmed that miR-335-5p executed its function as a tumor suppressor gene through targeting LDHB in CRC. To sum up, the present study demonstrated that miR-335-5p regulates cell proliferation, migration as well as invasion of CRC cells through down-regulating LDHB, and demonstrated that miR-335-5p/LDHB axis may be an underlying therapeutic strategy in CRC.

source_article.title: Solar-Aligned Pictographs at the Paleoindian Site of Painel do Pilão along the Lower Amazon River at Monte Alegre, Brazil.
source_article.abstract: The archaeological sites near Monte Alegre, along Brazil's lower Amazon River, provide new information on the little-known activities and symbolism of South American Paleoindians toward the end of the Ice Age. While paleoindian sites like Monte Verde in Chile, or Guitarrero Cave in Peru, are located near the pacific coast, Monte Alegre lies much further inland, 680 km upriver from the mouth of the Amazon River and the Atlantic Coast. With excavated wood charcoal radiocarbon dated as early as 13,200 calibrated years ago, the hill-as a source of sandstone and quartz lithics-supplied early pioneers with adequate tools needed for colonizing the interior of the continent. Once there, they painted rock art on the landscape, which bears a record of the sun's horizon positions throughout the year. At just 2° south of the equator, Monte Alegre shows no overt seasonal changes beyond fluctuating rainfall amounts, unlike at higher latitudes where temperature, amount of daylight, foliage, and forms of precipitation markedly change. Near the equator, solar and stellar horizon sightings most visibly track the passage of time and seasonal cycles. However, horizons are often hidden behind high forest canopy throughout much of the Amazon Rainforest; but in the Monte Alegre hill ridges looming above the river, paleoindians could hike above the canopy to peer at the horizon, more effectively synchronizing their activities to ecological cycles. This research suggests that Monte Alegre paleoindians delimited the azimuthal range of the sun in a solar year with notational pictographs aligned to horizon sightings at Painel do Pilão, and leaving a painted grid of tally marks that might have served as a rudimentary early calendar. The broad-reaching implication for early Americans is that through the strategic placement of rock art, these ancient artists fostered predictive archaeorecording from which resources could be optimally extracted, ceremonial activities could be consistently scheduled, and gatherings for social and economic exchange could be more efficiently coordinated.

statements.text: Paleoindians at Painel do Pilão, Monte Alegre, Brazil, created pictographs that align with solar horizon positions and appear to mark the sun’s annual movement.

matched_article.title: Ultra-Deep Sequencing of HIV-1 near Full-Length and Partial Proviral Genomes Reveals High Genetic Diversity among Brazilian Blood Donors.
matched_article.abstract: Here, we aimed to gain a comprehensive picture of the HIV-1 diversity in the northeast and southeast part of Brazil. To this end, a high-throughput sequencing-by-synthesis protocol and instrument were used to characterize the near full length (NFLG) and partial HIV-1 proviral genome in 259 HIV-1 infected blood donors at four major blood centers in Brazil: Pro-Sangue foundation (São Paulo state (SP), n 51), Hemominas foundation (Minas Gerais state (MG), n 41), Hemope foundation (Recife state (PE), n 96) and Hemorio blood bank (Rio de Janeiro (RJ), n 70). A total of 259 blood samples were obtained from 195 donors with long-standing infections and 64 donors with a lack of stage information. DNA was extracted from the peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) to amplify the HIV-1 NFLGs from five overlapping fragments. The amplicons were molecularly bar-coded, pooled, and sequenced by Illumina paired-end protocol. Of the 259 samples studied, 208 (80%) NFLGs and 49 (18.8%) partial fragments were de novo assembled into contiguous sequences and successfully subtyped. Of these 257 samples, 183 (71.2%) were pure subtypes consisting of clade B (n = 167, 65%), C (n = 10, 3.9%), F1 (n = 4, 1.5%), and D (n = 2, 0.7%). Recombinant viruses were detected in 74 (28.8%) samples and consist of unique BF1 (n = 41, 15.9%), BC (n = 7, 2.7%), BCF1 (n = 4, 1.5%), CF1 and CDK (n = 1, 0.4%, each), CRF70_BF1 (n = 4, 1.5%), CRF71_BF1 (n = 12, 4.7%), and CRF72_BF1 (n = 4, 1.5%). Evidence of dual infection was detected in four patients coinfected with the same subtype (n = 3) and distinct subtype (n = 1). Based on this work, subtype B appears to be the prevalent subtype followed by a high proportion of intersubtype recombinants that appeared to be arising continually in this country. Our study represents the largest analysis of the viral NFLG ever undertaken worldwide and provides insights into the understanding the genesis of the HIV-1 epidemic in this particular area of South America and informs vaccine design and clinical trials.

statements.text: Among HIV-1–infected blood donors in Brazil, subtype B was the predominant viral subtype.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies directly in Brazilian blood donors report subtype B as the predominant HIV-1 subtype, including nationwide/multicenter donor cohorts and regional donor studies: 76% clade B in four blood centers (PMID: 23507660), 73.9% in donors from 2001-2003 (PMID: 18184087), 76.7% in Rio de Janeiro donors (PMID: 9928731), 62.2% among recently infected donors with many recombinants (PMID: 25413141), and ≥90% in Northern donor cohorts (PMID: 31498798). A recurring theme is that although subtype B predominates, BF/BF1 and other recombinant forms are common and sometimes substantial, especially in more detailed genome analyses (PMID: 18184087, PMID: 25413141). Several additional non-donor studies from diverse Brazilian regions also report subtype B as the most common clade, generally around 65%-87%, which is broadly consistent with the donor findings but is less directly applicable to the claim (e.g., PMID: 22022460, PMID: 27091699, PMID: 28325060, PMID: 22877156). Some items in the support list are population-mismatched to the claim because they studied patients, pregnant women, or treated cohorts rather than blood donors, so they should be treated as indirect support rather than direct confirmation.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence mainly comes from Southern Brazil, where several studies report subtype C or BC recombinants as dominant in local epidemics rather than subtype B, such as high subtype C prevalence across southern municipalities (PMID: 27384663) and 65.8% subtype C in Florianópolis (PMID: 21622023). One donor-focused study from Pernambuco emphasizes a high burden of BF1 recombinants and novel CRFs among blood donors, but the abstract does not clearly state that subtype B was no longer the single most common subtype, so this is better viewed as highlighting regional complexity than definitively refuting national predominance of subtype B (PMID: 25401747). Other contradiction items focus on recombinant forms or subtype C expansion in southern/non-donor populations without directly testing the national blood-donor claim, making them only indirect contradictions (PMID: 19954300, PMID: 34036794, PMID: 20950149, PMID: 19460312, PMID: 17263643). Overall, the main conflicting theme is strong regional heterogeneity, especially in the South, where subtype C can dominate local epidemics.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence comes from several blood-donor studies across Brazil showing subtype B as the predominant subtype, while the contradictory evidence mainly reflects regional exceptions—especially in Southern Brazil—or studies in populations other than blood donors.

statements.text: Among HIV-1–infected blood donors in Brazil, a substantial proportion of infections were intersubtype recombinant viruses, indicating ongoing viral diversification and recombination.
statements.support_summary: Direct studies in Brazilian blood donors consistently report notable levels of recombinant HIV-1, especially BF1 mosaics: 22.7% intersubtype recombinants in blood donors from 2001-2003 (PMID: 18184087), 37.8% recombinant genomes among recently infected donors from four Brazilian blood centers (PMID: 25413141), and high rates of BF1 recombinants including newly defined CRF70_BF1 and CRF71_BF1 in Pernambuco donors, with CRF71_BF1 alone estimated at 10% locally (PMID: 25401747). Earlier donor surveillance in Rio de Janeiro also found potential mosaic viruses in 9.3% of infected blood donors (PMID: 9928731). Broader Brazilian studies outside donor cohorts repeatedly identify substantial recombinant prevalence, novel CRFs/URFs, dual infections, and region-specific diversification, reinforcing ongoing recombination as a major feature of the epidemic (e.g., PMID: 31024510, PMID: 22012712, PMID: 21678433). A few supporting items are only indirectly relevant because they are not blood-donor studies or are focused on specific recombinant forms without quantifying donor prevalence, but the central donor-based evidence aligns well with the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: Some evidence suggests recombinant prevalence is not uniformly high across Brazil: among blood donors in the Brazilian Amazon, subtype B was highly dominant and BF1 recombinants were only 5.3%, with subtype B exceeding 90% in the sampled northern states (PMID: 31498798). In São Paulo, one newly identified recombinant CRF46_BF1 was estimated to account for just 0.56% of circulating strains, but this only addresses one specific CRF rather than all recombinants and therefore does not directly refute a substantial overall recombinant burden (PMID: 20398371). Another São Paulo study found a very low prevalence of pure subclade F1 (0.56%) after excluding mosaics, so it is not a true contradiction to recombinant prevalence and is somewhat misaligned with the claim (PMID: 19531216). Likewise, a southern Brazil study reported low diversity of BC recombinants, yet still documented many recombinant infections, so it challenges the breadth/diversity aspect more than the existence of substantial recombination overall (PMID: 19460312).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Multiple donor-specific Brazilian studies show that intersubtype recombinant viruses constituted a meaningful fraction of infections, often around 20-38%, and several studies identified new recombinant forms and dual infections, whereas the contradictory evidence mainly reflects regional heterogeneity or low prevalence of particular recombinant lineages rather than a clear absence of substantial recombination.

statements.text: Among HIV-1–infected blood donors in Brazil, dual HIV-1 infection occurred in a small number of patients, including both same-subtype and different-subtype coinfections.
statements.support_summary: The strongest directly relevant support comes from a blood-donor study in Pernambuco, Brazil, which used near full-length genome deep sequencing and found dual infection in 4 patients co-infected with distinct HIV-1 subtypes, explicitly indicating that dual infection occurred in a minority of donors (PMID: 25401747). A broader Brazilian NGS analysis also states that multiple infections were detected in some individuals, reinforcing that dual/multiple infection is a recognized but not dominant phenomenon in Brazil, though it is less specific to blood donors and does not detail same- versus different-subtype coinfection in the abstract (PMID: 31024510). Additional Brazilian studies outside the blood-donor setting found only a few dual infections, including coinfections with the same or distinct subtypes in children/adolescents from São Paulo (3 patients; PMID: 23667488) and 3 dual infections in a Rio cohort, all involving different subtypes (PMID: 10081673), which supports biological plausibility but is less directly matched to the claim population. One older blood-donor study in Rio de Janeiro documented subtype diversity and mosaic viruses, but the abstract does not actually show dual infection per patient, so its placement as supportive is uncertain and should be treated as discrepant/noisy evidence rather than firm support (PMID: 9928731).
statements.contradict_summary: The single contradicting study does not directly measure dual infection in blood donors; instead, it reports many distinct B/F recombinant forms in São Paulo and infers that a high frequency of dual infections between subtypes B and F may be occurring (PMID: 15725753). Because this conclusion is indirect and based on recombinant genome patterns rather than explicit patient-level counts of dual infection, it only weakly contradicts the claim that dual infection occurred in a small number of patients. It also does not address same-subtype coinfection.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The best-matched evidence in Brazilian blood donors directly reports a small number of dual infections and confirms different-subtype coinfection, while indirect contradictory evidence relies on inference from recombination patterns rather than explicit donor-level dual-infection counts.

source_article.title: Pinostrobin ameliorates lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammation and endotoxemia by inhibiting LPS binding to the TLR4/MD2 complex.
source_article.abstract: Pinostrobin is a natural flavonoid with valuable pharmacological properties, including anti-cancer, anti-viral, and anti-oxidant activities. However, the anti-inflammatory effects of pinostrobin have not been well studied. In this study, we investigated whether pinostrobin attenuates lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammation and endotoxemia. Additionally, the target molecule of pinostrobin was identified through molecular docking simulation. Pinostrobin decreased LPS-induced nitric oxide (NO) and prostaglandin E2 production, and reduced the expression of inducible NO synthase and cyclooxygenase-2. Furthermore, pinostrobin inhibited the production of proinflammatory cytokines, including interleukin-12 and tumor necrosis factor-α in LPS-stimulated RAW 264.7 macrophages accompanied by inhibiting nuclear translocation of nuclear factor-κB. The anti-inflammatory effect of pinostrobin was further confirmed in LPS-microinjected zebrafish larvae by diminishing the recruitment of macrophages and neutrophils, and proinflammatory gene expression. Moreover, LPS-microinjected zebrafish larvae showed a decrease in heart rate and an increase in mortality and abnormalities. However, pinostrobin significantly attenuated these adverse effects. Molecular docking showed that pinostrobin fits into myeloid differentiation factor (MD2) and Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) with no traditional hydrogen bonds (pose 1). The 2D ligand interaction diagram showed that pinostrobin forms a carbon hydrogen bond with LYS89 in MD2 and many non-covalent interactions, including π-alkyl or alkyl and van der Waals interactions, indicating that pinostrobin hinders LPS binding between MD2 and TLR4 and consequently inhibits TLR4/MD2-mediated inflammatory responses. These data suggest that pinostrobin attenuates LPS-induced inflammation and endotoxemia by binding to the TLR4/MD2 complex.

matched_article.title: Protodioscin inhibits bladder cancer cell migration and growth, and promotes apoptosis through activating JNK and p38 signaling pathways.
matched_article.abstract: Bladder cancer is one of the most common malignancies of the male genitourinary urinary system. Protodioscin is a steroidal saponin with anti-cancer effects on several types of cancers; however, the anti-cancer activities of protodioscin on bladder cancer have not yet been investigated. Therefore, we aimed to examine the anti-cancer effects of protodioscin on bladder cancer. Two types of bladder cancer cell lines, non-muscle-invasive 5637 cells and muscle-invasive T24 cells, were used to evaluate the effects of protodioscin on cell growth, migration, invasion and epithelial-mesenchymal transition(EMT) marker expressions. Transcriptome analysis was performed by RNA-seq and validated using real-time PCR and western blot; additionally, an in vivo xenograft animal model was established and the anti-tumor effects of protodioscin were tested. Our results demonstrated that protodioscin inhibited cell proliferation, migration, motility and invasion on 5637 and T24 cells. Additionally, protodioscin also induced cell apoptosis and arrested the progression of cell cycle at G2 phase in bladder cancer cells. Moreover, protodioscin inhibited EMT through increased protein expression of E-cadherin and decreased protein expression of N-cadherin and vimentin. RNA-seq analysis indicated that protodioscin regulated mitogen-activated protein kinase(MAPK) and phosphoinositide 3-kinases(PI3K)/protein kinase B(AKT)/mammalian target of rapamycin(mTOR) signaling pathways as further verified by Western blot. Furthermore, protodioscin significantly inhibited tumor growth in vivo. Our results indicated that protodioscin inhibits cell growth, migration and invasion and induces apoptosis and G2 phase cell cycle arrest by activated p38 and JNK signaling pathways in bladder cancer cells, suggesting that protodioscin could be an effective agent for bladder cancer treatment.

source_article.title: Stem Cells Engineered During Different Stages of Reprogramming Reveal Varying Therapeutic Efficacies.
source_article.abstract: Stem cells are emerging as promising treatment strategies for several brain disorders and pathologies. In this study, we explored the potential of creating induced pluripotent stem cell-derived neural stem cells (ipNSC) by using either unmodified or gene-modified somatic cells and tested their fate and therapeutic efficacies in vitro and in vivo. We show that cells engineered in somatic state lose transgene-expression during the neural induction process, which is partially restored by histone deacetylase inhibitor treatment whereas cells engineered at the ipNSC state have sustained expression of transgenes. In vivo, bimodal mouse and human ipNSCs engineered to express tumor specific death-receptor ligand and suicide-inducing therapeutic proteins have profound anti-tumor efficacy when encapsulated in synthetic extracellular matrix and transplanted in mouse models of resected-glioblastoma. This study provides insights into using somatic cells for treating CNS disorders and presents a receptor-targeted cancer therapeutic approach for brain tumors. Stem Cells 2018;36:932-942.

statements.text: Induced pluripotent stem cell-derived neural stem cells engineered at the neural stem cell stage maintain therapeutic transgene expression better than cells engineered in the somatic cell state during neural induction.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
