statements.text: Histone deacetylase inhibitor treatment can partially restore transgene expression in stem cells engineered in the somatic cell state during neural induction.
statements.support_summary: One supporting abstract reports that during fibroblast-to-neural-precursor reprogramming, a drug combination including HDAC inhibitors markedly enhanced activation of a neural stem cell reporter and increased reprogramming efficiency, suggesting chromatin-modifying treatment can improve neural induction in somatic-state engineered cells (PMID: 24128663). However, this study does not explicitly state that HDAC inhibition partially restores silenced transgene expression during neural induction; rather, it shows improved reporter activation and reprogramming outcomes. Thus, the available support is indirect and somewhat discrepant with the specific wording of the claim.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is weakly supported overall. The only cited abstract is consistent with HDAC inhibitor treatment facilitating neural induction in somatic cells, but it does not directly demonstrate partial restoration of transgene expression, so the evidence is indirect rather than definitive.

statements.text: Induced pluripotent stem cell-derived neural stem cells engineered to express tumor-specific death-receptor ligand and suicide-inducing therapeutic proteins can produce strong anti-tumor effects against resected glioblastoma in mouse models when transplanted in a synthetic extracellular matrix.
statements.support_summary: Two studies partially support the claim’s core therapeutic concept in mouse glioblastoma models. In a resection model, biodegradable synthetic extracellular matrix (sECM)-encapsulated engineered stem cells placed in the tumor cavity showed improved retention, released S-TRAIL, induced caspase-mediated apoptosis in residual tumor cells, delayed regrowth, and prolonged survival, directly supporting the synthetic-matrix/resected-GBM aspect, although the abstract does not specify induced pluripotent stem cell-derived neural stem cells or suicide-inducing proteins beyond TRAIL (PMID: 22197831). A separate study showed that engineered induced neural stem cells were tumor-homing, induced apoptosis in glioblastoma cells, reduced orthotopic xenograft growth, and prolonged mouse survival via TRAIL delivery, supporting the engineered neural stem cell anti-glioma concept but not the resection-cavity sECM setting and not clearly the iPSC-derived cell type (PMID: 26830441). Overall, the support is thematically consistent for engineered stem-cell delivery of death-receptor ligands against glioblastoma, but key claim details are only partially matched across the abstracts.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available studies consistently show engineered neural/stem-cell therapies can deliver TRAIL, suppress glioblastoma, and improve survival in mice, including one study in a resection cavity with synthetic extracellular matrix. However, the evidence is only moderate because the exact combination of iPSC-derived neural stem cells plus both death-receptor ligand and suicide-inducing proteins is not fully demonstrated in the provided abstracts.

matched_article.title: Human Mesenchymal Stem Cell Failure to Adapt to Glucose Shortage and Rapidly Use Intracellular Energy Reserves Through Glycolysis Explains Poor Cell Survival After Implantation.
matched_article.abstract: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) hold considerable promise in tissue engineering (TE). However, their poor survival when exogenously administered limits their therapeutic potential. Previous studies from our group demonstrated that lack of glucose (glc) (but not of oxygen) is fatal to human MSCs because it serves as a pro-survival and pro-angiogenic molecule for human MSCs (hMSCs) upon transplantation. However, which energy-providing pathways MSCs use to metabolize glc upon transplantation? Are there alternative energetic nutrients to replace glc? And most importantly, do hMSCs possess significant intracellular glc reserves for ensuring their survival upon transplantation? These remain open questions at the forefront of TE based-therapies. In this study, we established for the first time that the in vivo environment experienced by hMSCs is best reflected by near-anoxia (0.1% O2 ) rather than hypoxia (1%-5% O2 ) in vitro. Under these near-anoxia conditions, hMSCs rely almost exclusively on glc through anerobic glycolysis for ATP production and are unable to use either exogenous glutamine, serine, or pyruvate as energy substrates. Most importantly, hMSCs are unable to adapt their metabolism to the lack of exogenous glc, possess a very limited internal stock of glc and virtually no ATP reserves. This lack of downregulation of energy turnover as a function of exogenous glc level results in a rapid depletion of hMSC energy reserves that explains their poor survival rate. These new insights prompt for the development of glc-releasing scaffolds to overcome this roadblock plaguing the field of TE based-therapies. Stem Cells 2018;36:363-376.

statements.text: Human mesenchymal stem cells require extracellular glucose and anaerobic glycolysis for ATP production under near-anoxic conditions that resemble the in vivo environment after transplantation.
statements.support_summary: Several studies directly report that human or mammalian MSCs tolerate severe hypoxia/near-anoxia only when extracellular glucose is available, with glucose depletion causing ATP loss, reduced viability, and cell death, whereas glucose supplementation improves survival after transplantation-like ischemic stress (PMID: 20716129, PMID: 23225732, PMID: 39324547). Newer biomaterial studies are especially aligned with the claim, showing that glucose-releasing hydrogels or microgels support hMSC survival and function under near-anoxic or ischemic conditions by fueling glycolysis (PMID: 38092861, PMID: 39324547). Mechanistic support also comes from studies showing that hypoxia shifts hMSCs toward anaerobic glycolysis, increases lactate production, and makes ATP more sensitive to glycolysis inhibition than to respiratory inhibition under low oxygen (PMID: 23733376, PMID: 23742825). Additional metabolic studies report glucose consumption with lactate production consistent with anaerobic glycolysis in hMSCs, though these are less specific to transplantation-like near-anoxia (PMID: 19207045, PMID: 25929129). Overall, the main recurring theme is that under oxygen-poor transplantation-like conditions, glucose availability and glycolytic metabolism are central to MSC energy maintenance and survival.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence emphasizes MSC metabolic flexibility and oxidative metabolism rather than an absolute dependence on extracellular glucose or anaerobic glycolysis. One study reports that cultured hMSCs generate more ATP from substrate oxidation than glycolysis and oxidize acetoacetate far more efficiently than glucose, arguing against glucose as the uniquely required fuel, although this work is not specifically centered on near-anoxic transplantation-like conditions and therefore only partially challenges the claim's context (PMID: 28483672). Another study is internally mixed relative to the claim: despite being listed as contradiction, its abstract states MSCs survive hypoxia via anaerobic ATP production and die rapidly with glucose deprivation, but also suggests glycolysis may have prosurvival roles beyond ATP production, creating a discrepancy between categorization and abstract content (PMID: 18308942). A further study links higher stemness phenotypes to oxidative phosphorylation and lower glycolytic activity, supporting metabolic plasticity but not directly testing near-anoxic ATP dependence after transplantation (PMID: 31659213).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple studies, including direct near-anoxia/ischemia and transplantation-relevant models, indicate that MSC survival and ATP maintenance under very low oxygen depend strongly on extracellular glucose and glycolytic metabolism, whereas the contradictory studies mainly show broader metabolic flexibility or are less well matched to the specific near-anoxic context.

statements.text: Human mesenchymal stem cells cannot effectively substitute other exogenous energy substrates, such as glutamine, serine, or pyruvate, for glucose under near-anoxic conditions.
statements.support_summary: One study reports that human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) can survive sustained near-anoxia only when exogenous glucose is present, and that glucose also increases HIF-1α, angiogenic factor expression, in vivo viability, and vascularization after transplantation (PMID: 23225732). This supports the idea that glucose is uniquely important under near-anoxic conditions, but the abstract does not directly test whether other substrates such as glutamine, serine, or pyruvate can substitute for glucose. Thus, the support is indirect and rests mainly on glucose dependence rather than explicit substrate-comparison experiments.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall weakly supported. The available abstract indicates that glucose is required for hMSC survival in near-anoxia, but because it does not directly compare glucose with glutamine, serine, or pyruvate, the specific non-substitutability claim remains only indirectly supported.

statements.text: Human mesenchymal stem cells have very limited intracellular glucose and ATP reserves and fail to adapt their metabolism to glucose shortage, which contributes to poor survival after implantation.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support the idea that nutrient/glucose shortage is a major determinant of MSC death after transplantation-like stress. Human MSCs showed marked death with serum deprivation and especially with prolonged combined hypoxia plus nutrient deprivation, indicating sensitivity to ischemic/nutrient-poor conditions, though this study did not directly measure intracellular glucose or ATP reserves or metabolic adaptation to glucose shortage (PMID: 17518749). More direct support comes from glucose-delivery biomaterial studies showing that transplanted or oxygen/glucose-deprived MSCs survive better, maintain intracellular energy, and preserve paracrine functions when supplied with sustained glucose; one abstract explicitly states MSCs fail to adapt to hypoxia in the absence of glucose (PMID: 39324547), and another similarly frames ischemic post-transplant death as mitigated by nutritive glucose-releasing hydrogels (PMID: 38092861). Overall, the support is strongest for glucose dependence and poor survival under glucose shortage, but evidence for the specific claim of "very limited intracellular glucose and ATP reserves" is mostly indirect from these abstracts.
statements.contradict_summary: A larger body of evidence suggests MSCs have substantial metabolic flexibility rather than a general inability to adapt. Multiple studies report that MSCs tolerate prolonged severe hypoxia or near-anoxia if glucose is available, shifting toward glycolytic/anaerobic metabolism and maintaining viability, proliferation, differentiation potential, or transplantation survival (PMID: 20716129, PMID: 23225732, PMID: 23742825). One study explicitly concludes that metabolic flexibility permits MSC survival in ischemic environments, although its own abstract also notes rapid death under enforced glucose deprivation, making it only a partial contradiction to the specific glucose-shortage aspect of the claim (PMID: 18308942). Other abstracts report adaptive mechanisms under nutrient stress, including autophagy/lipid use during glucose deprivation (PMID: 29725060), metabolic reconfiguration toward glycolysis during immune activation (PMID: 30272389), metabolic plasticity under glucose or glutamine shortage in higher-stemness hMSC subsets (PMID: 31659213), and improved ischemic survival after quiescence preconditioning with maintenance of ATP levels (PMID: 27578059). Thus, the main contradiction is not that glucose shortage is harmless, but that MSCs can reprogram metabolism under several stress contexts, so the absolute claim that they fail to adapt appears overstated from the cited abstracts.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more contradicted than supported. The evidence consistently supports that glucose availability is crucial for post-implantation MSC survival, but many abstracts indicate MSCs can adapt metabolically to hypoxia or other stresses, so the stronger claim that they generally have very limited reserves and fail to adapt to glucose shortage is not well sustained.

source_article.title: Influence of LncRNA UCA1 on glucose metabolism in rats with diabetic nephropathy through PI3K-Akt signaling pathway.
source_article.abstract: OBJECTIVE: The aim of this study was to investigate the influence of long non-coding ribonucleic acid (lncRNA) urothelial carcinoma associated 1 (UCA1) on glucose metabolism in rats with diabetic nephropathy (DN), and to explore its regulatory mechanism. MATERIALS AND METHODS: A total of 30 healthy Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats were selected in this study. All rats were randomly divided into three groups, including the control group, the model group, and the lncRNA UCA1 inhibitor group. The rat model of DN was successfully established via intraperitoneal injection of streptozotocin (STZ). The pathological changes in kidney tissues were detected via hematoxylin-eosin (HE) staining. The levels of blood urea nitrogen (BUN), serum creatinine (Scr), and urinary protein (UP) were detected using the biochemical method. Meanwhile, the content of serum tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) was detected via enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). In addition, the messenger RNA (mRNA) and protein levels of phosphatidylinositol 3-hydroxy kinase (PI3K) and protein kinase B (Akt) in kidney tissues were detected via reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and Western blotting, respectively. RESULTS: The model group showed severe pathological damage to the kidney, compared with the control group. Meanwhile, the levels of BUN, Scr and UP, and the content of serum TNF-α and IL-6 increased significantly in the model group. The mRNA and the protein levels of PI3K and Akt in kidney tissues of the model group were significantly up-regulated as well. LncRNA UCA1 inhibitor group exhibited relieved pathological damage to the kidney, compared with the model group. The levels of BUN, Scr and UP, and the content of serum TNF-α and IL-6 remarkably decreased in UCA1 inhibitor group. Furthermore, the mRNA and the protein levels of PI3K and Akt in kidney tissues of UCA1 inhibitor groups were significantly down-regulated. CONCLUSIONS: LncRNA UCA1 can relieve the pathological damage to the kidney, improve renal function, and alleviate inflammatory response in DN rats. The underlying mechanism may be related to the inhibition of the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway.

statements.text: In rats with streptozotocin-induced diabetic nephropathy, inhibition of lncRNA UCA1 reduces kidney pathological damage and improves renal function compared with untreated diabetic nephropathy rats.

matched_article.title: LncRNA HOTAIR promotes colon cancer development by down-regulating miRNA-34a.
matched_article.abstract: To investigate the characteristics of long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) HOTAIR in colon cancer, and to further explore its function in the development of colon cancer and its potential regulatory mechanisms. Quantitative Real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) was performed to detect HOTAIR expression in 72 colon cancer tissues along with adjacent normal tissues. Meanwhile, the relationship between HOTAIR level and colon cancer pathological parameters and patient prognosis were analyzed, respectively. QRT-PCR further verified the HOTAIR expression in colon cancer cells. Besides, knockdown and overexpression of HOTAIR models were constructed using lentivirus in HT29 and HCT-116 colon cancer cell lines. Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8), 5-ethynyl-2'-deoxyuridine (EDU) and cell colony formation assays were used to analyze the effects of HOTAIR on biological function of colon cancer cell. In addition, dual luciferase reporter gene assay was performed to explore the underlying mechanisms. QRT-PCR results showed that HOTAIR expression in colon cancer was significantly higher than that in normal tissues. The incidence of distant metastasis was higher in patients with high expression of HOTAIR while their survival rate was lower than that of patients with low HOTAIR expression. Meanwhile, cell proliferation, invasion as well as migration ability of the cells in HOTAIR knockdown group was significantly decreased than those in the negative control group. QRT-PCR results showed that mRNA levels of miR-34a and HOTAIR in colon cancer tissues were negatively correlated. Besides, luciferase reporter gene assay revealed that overexpression of miR-34a significantly attenuated the luciferase activity of the wild-type HOTAIR vector group without attenuating the mutant HOTAIR vector group (p>0.05) In addition, the recovery experiment also found a mutual regulation between HOTAIR and miR-34a, together they could affect the malignant progression of colon cancer. HOTAIR expression was significantly increased in colon cancer, which was in association with distant metastasis and poor prognosis of colon cancer. In addition, HOTAIR may promote malignant progression of colon cancer by regulating miR-34a.

statements.text: In colon cancer, high HOTAIR expression is associated with distant metastasis and poorer patient prognosis compared with low HOTAIR expression.
statements.support_summary: A clinical study in 120 colon cancers found that increased HOTAIR expression was significantly associated with lymph node metastasis, organ metastasis, vascular invasion, advanced stage, and worse outcomes including higher recurrence and reduced metastasis-free and overall survival, directly supporting the claim (PMID: 24840737). Several mechanistic and translational studies further suggest that HOTAIR promotes aggressive behavior in colon/colorectal cancer, with HOTAIR knockdown reducing migration, invasion, EMT, tumor growth, and metastatic capacity in cell and mouse models (PMID: 30335892; PMID: 33588137). Additional evidence links HOTAIR expression with poor prognosis in colon adenocarcinoma and colorectal cancer, although these reports are less direct about distant metastasis and in some cases are review-level or broader CRC evidence rather than colon-cancer-specific survival analyses (PMID: 34630664; PMID: 37530984). One cited study mainly associates miR-125a-5p, rather than HOTAIR itself, with clinicopathologic features, so its support for the exact claim is indirect despite describing HOTAIR overexpression in colon tumors (PMID: 26962687). Overall, the strongest support comes from PMID: 24840737, while the remaining studies mainly reinforce biological plausibility and poor-prognosis associations.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The key reason is a directly relevant colon cancer cohort study showing that high HOTAIR is associated with organ metastasis and poorer survival (PMID: 24840737), with multiple additional studies supporting a metastasis-promoting and poor-prognosis role, although several are indirect or mechanistic rather than definitive clinical prognosis studies.

statements.text: In colon cancer cells, HOTAIR promotes proliferation, invasion, and migration compared with reduced HOTAIR expression.
statements.support_summary: Several colorectal/colon cancer studies directly support a pro-tumor role for HOTAIR: HOTAIR knockdown suppressed cell viability/proliferation, migration, invasion, EMT, and in some cases tumor growth/metastasis in vivo, while HOTAIR overexpression reversed inhibitory effects, as shown most clearly in colorectal cancer models by PMID: 33588137 and colon cancer models by PMID: 31953926. Additional CRC studies support the proliferation component, reporting that HOTAIR silencing inhibits proliferation and/or tumor growth through miR-545/EGFR or apoptosis-related pathways (PMID: 28364379, PMID: 26962687). Indirect supportive evidence also links high HOTAIR to poor prognosis and to upstream regulation associated with increased proliferation/invasion in colon adenocarcinoma (PMID: 34630664), and a review summarizes HOTAIR upregulation and growth-promoting roles in CRC (PMID: 37530984). A categorization discrepancy is present because some evidence is indirect or mechanistic rather than a full direct test of proliferation, migration, and invasion together.
statements.contradict_summary: Most items listed as contradiction do not truly contradict the claim because they are from other diseases or cancers (glioblastoma, NSCLC, preeclampsia trophoblasts, gastric cancer) and therefore are not directly relevant to colon cancer biology (PMID: 32176624, PMID: 30720199, PMID: 32320706, PMID: 29236333). Notably, one supposed contradiction in colon cancer actually supports the claim: HOTAIR silencing inhibited proliferation, invasion, and migration of LoVo colon cancer cells and reduced tumor growth, indicating misclassification of PMID: 30335892. Two additional CRC studies also appear misclassified because HOTAIR knockdown reduced proliferation/viability or promoted radiosensitivity by weakening viability, which is directionally supportive rather than contradictory for the proliferation aspect (PMID: 29680837, PMID: 32144238). The main genuinely mixed evidence is PMID: 24840737, which found that HOTAIR strongly promoted migration and invasion and correlated with metastasis/EMT, but had only a limited effect on proliferation, partially contradicting the full breadth of the claim.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant colon/colorectal cancer studies consistently show that reducing HOTAIR decreases proliferation/viability, invasion, and migration, while the nominal contradiction set is largely composed of irrelevant non-colon studies or misclassified supportive CRC papers, with only limited disagreement about the strength of the proliferation effect.

statements.text: In colon cancer, HOTAIR may promote malignant progression by down-regulating miR-34a.

source_article.title: Transduced Tat-SOD fusion protein protects against ischemic brain injury.
source_article.abstract: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are implicated in reperfusion injury after transient focal cerebral ischemia. The antioxidant enzyme, Cu,Zn-superoxide dismutase (SOD), is one of the major means by which cells counteract the deleterious effects of ROS after ischemia. Recently, we reported that when Tat-SOD fusion protein is transduced into pancreatic beta cells it protects the beta cells from destruction by relieving oxidative stress in ROS-implicated diabetes (Eum et al., 2004). In the present study, we investigated the protective effects of Tat-SOD fusion protein against neuronal cell death and ischemic insults. When Tat-SOD was added to the culture medium of neuronal cells, it rapidly entered the cells and protected them against paraquat-induced cell death. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed that Tat-SOD injected intraperitoneally (i.p.) into mice has access to various tissues including brain neurons. When i.p. injected into gerbils, Tat-SOD prevented neuronal cell death in the hippocampus in response to transient fore-brain ischemia. These results suggest that Tat-SOD provides a strategy for therapeutic delivery in various hu-man diseases, including stroke, related to this anti-oxidant enzyme or to ROS.

statements.text: Tat-SOD fusion protein protects neuronal cells against oxidative stress–induced cell death after exposure to paraquat.
statements.support_summary: Direct support comes from studies showing that Tat-SOD enters neural/neuronal-like cells and reduces paraquat-induced damage. In PC12 neural cells, wild-type Tat-SOD1 prevented DNA fragmentation caused by 35 mM paraquat, indicating protection from oxidative stress-induced cell death, while a mutant Tat-SOD1 worsened injury (PMID: 15965076). A later study similarly reported that denatured Tat-SOD regained antioxidant activity in NGF-differentiated PC12 cells and protected them from paraquat-induced DNA fragmentation, with associated suppression of COX-2 and NF-κB activity (PMID: 26786970). Additional but less specific support comes from HeLa cells, where transduced Tat-SOD increased viability after paraquat exposure, suggesting a general protective effect against superoxide-mediated toxicity rather than neuronal-specific evidence (PMID: 11094160). One item concerns Tat-SOD delivered via mesoporous silica nanoparticles protecting cells from ROS/apoptosis, but the abstract does not specifically mention neuronal cells or paraquat, so its placement as support for this precise claim is weaker and somewhat discrepant (PMID: 23289802).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items. No abstract provided here reports that Tat-SOD fails to protect, or worsens, paraquat-induced oxidative death in neuronal cells, aside from the mutant Tat-D101G variant worsening cell death, which does not contradict the claim about Tat-SOD fusion protein in its wild-type/protective form (PMID: 15965076).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The strongest evidence is direct and neuronal-context specific in PC12 cells exposed to paraquat (PMID: 15965076; PMID: 26786970), while the remaining studies provide broader but less specific support for Tat-SOD-mediated protection against oxidative stress.

statements.text: Tat-SOD fusion protein protects hippocampal neurons against transient forebrain ischemia in gerbils.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Tat-SOD fusion protein administered systemically reaches brain neurons and can protect tissues from ischemic injury.
statements.support_summary: Direct support comes from a rat focal cerebral ischemia/reperfusion study showing that intraperitoneal TAT-SOD increased cerebral SOD activity, reduced lipid peroxidation and apoptosis markers, and protected brain tissue when given 2 hours before or 2 hours after injury, consistent with systemic neuroprotection against ischemic damage (PMID: 21983418). A diabetic neuropathy study also found that intraperitoneal Tat-SOD increased radical-scavenging activity, reduced apoptosis signaling, and improved sciatic nerve pathology, supporting systemic delivery and bioactivity in neural tissue, though this was peripheral nerve rather than brain and not an ischemia model (PMID: 22128026). One listed study supports the broader concept of SOD fusion proteins in ischemic brain injury, showing that a different fusion protein, SOD:Tet451, reached ischemic cortex immunohistochemically and reduced infarct volume after focal ischemia/reperfusion; however, this is not Tat-SOD specifically, so its relevance is indirect and represents a discrepancy in categorization (PMID: 9270054). Overall, the main theme is that systemically administered cell-penetrating or neuron-targeted SOD fusion proteins can access neural tissues and reduce oxidative/ischemic injury, with the strongest abstract-level support coming from the cerebral I/R Tat-SOD rat study.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest evidence is a direct in vivo Tat-SOD cerebral ischemia/reperfusion study showing brain biochemical protection and reduced neuronal apoptosis after systemic administration (PMID: 21983418), while the other studies provide indirect but consistent support for neural delivery and antioxidant protection.

matched_article.title: Differential expression patterns of gangliosides in the ischemic cerebral cortex produced by middle cerebral artery occlusion.
matched_article.abstract: Neuronal damage subsequent to transient cerebral ischemia is a multifactorial process involving several overlapping mechanisms. Gangliosides, sialic acid-conjugated glycosphingolipids, reduce the severity of acute brain damage in vitro. However their in vivo effects on the cerebral cortex damaged by ischemic infarct are unknown. To assess the possible protective role of gangliosides we examined their expression in the cerebral cortex damaged by ischemic infarct in the rat. Ischemia was induced by middle cerebral artery (MCA) occlusion, and the resulting damage was observed by staining with 2, 3, 5-triphenylterazolium chloride (TTC). High-performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC) showed that gangliosides GM3 and GM1 increased in the damaged cerebral cortex, and immunofluorescence microscopy also revealed a significant change in expression of GM1. In addition, in situ hybridization demonstrated an increase in the mRNA for ganglioside GM3 synthase. These results suggest that gangliosides GM1 and GM3 may be synthesized in vivo to protect the cerebral cortex from ischemic damage.

statements.text: In rats with middle cerebral artery occlusion causing ischemic cerebral cortex damage, gangliosides GM1 and GM3 increased in the damaged cerebral cortex, suggesting a role in the response to ischemic injury.
statements.support_summary: Most supporting items are indirect: several rat studies report that exogenous GM1 treatment reduces infarct volume, biochemical injury markers, or microvascular damage after cerebral ischemia, implying a protective role for GM1 in ischemic cortex but not showing endogenous increases of GM1 or GM3 in damaged cortex (PMID: 8313942, PMID: 25934141, PMID: 10965984). More directly, a mouse MCAO-reperfusion imaging study found transient induction of GM3, along with altered regional ganglioside profiles, in the ipsilateral hemisphere at the infarct border, supporting the broader idea that gangliosides participate in injury responses, though it did not show increased GM1 and was in mice rather than rats (PMID: 21687673). Overall, the support side mainly speaks to ganglioside involvement in ischemic injury response, but only weakly to the specific claim that both GM1 and GM3 increase in rat damaged cerebral cortex.
statements.contradict_summary: The strongest contradicting evidence is only partially relevant: cultured rat astrocytes showed abundant GM3 and absent GM1, but this was an in vitro astrocyte composition study unrelated to ischemic cortex or MCAO, so its contradiction to the claim is limited (PMID: 2515920). A rat stroke/Aβ model found increased GM2 and GM3 in ischemic regions, while not reporting increased GM1, which cuts against the specific 'GM1 and GM3 increased' formulation but actually supports part of the claim regarding GM3 and ganglioside responses to injury (PMID: 26086081). Another rat ischemia study showed that applied GM1 inhibited ischemia-evoked amino acid release, addressing pharmacologic protection rather than endogenous cortical GM1 levels, so it is not a direct contradiction (PMID: 8580429). Overall, the contradiction set is weakened by several scope mismatches and mixed evidence.
statements.conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear, with slight lean toward support for a general role of gangliosides in ischemic injury response but weak support for the specific statement that both GM1 and GM3 increase in rat MCAO-damaged cortex. Most cited studies are indirect treatment studies or show only GM3 changes, while the contradicting studies are also largely indirect or not well matched to the claim.

statements.text: In rats with ischemic cerebral cortex damage after middle cerebral artery occlusion, ganglioside GM3 synthase mRNA increased, consistent with increased in vivo synthesis of ganglioside GM3.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: The observed increase in gangliosides GM1 and GM3 in the ischemic cerebral cortex suggests that these gangliosides may contribute to protection of the cerebral cortex from ischemic damage.
statements.support_summary: Most supporting studies report that exogenous GM1 reduces ischemic injury in rodent brain, including smaller infarcts, improved neurological outcomes, reduced cortical glutamate/amino acid release, and protection of cortical penumbra or cortex after focal ischemia/reperfusion (PMID: 8313942, 8474009, 8580429, 15754422, 25934141, 26751695, 28392211, 10965984). Several abstracts also suggest mechanisms for GM1-mediated neuroprotection, including modulation of NMDA receptor expression, inhibition of autophagy, suppression of ER-stress/apoptosis, and reduction of oxidative/calcium-related injury (PMID: 15754422, 25934141, 26751695, 28392211). One imaging study found post-ischemic changes in ganglioside distribution and transient induction of GM3 at the infarct border, suggesting gangliosides may participate in injury responses, though it did not directly show that increased GM1/GM3 is protective (PMID: 21687673). Overall, the support is mainly indirect: it strongly supports a protective effect of administered GM1, but provides little direct abstract-level evidence that endogenous increases of both GM1 and GM3 in ischemic cortex themselves mediate protection.
statements.contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is limited and only weakly addresses the claim. One study on cultured astrocytes reported GM1 absence and predominant GM3 expression, but it was not an ischemia model and does not directly test cortical protection from ischemic damage, so its placement as contradiction is questionable (PMID: 2515920). Another MALDI imaging study in a combined Aβ-plus-stroke model found increased GM3 (and GM2) in ischemic regions and interpreted this as part of injury/comorbidity mechanisms rather than protection; however, it did not directly test whether GM3 is harmful or protective and did not refute a protective role of endogenous gangliosides (PMID: 26086081). Thus, the contradiction set contains substantial relevance/miscategorization concerns and offers little direct evidence against the claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The evidence base consistently shows neuroprotective effects of GM1 in ischemic cortex, but direct proof that endogenous increases in both GM1 and GM3 specifically account for cortical protection is limited, making the support moderate rather than definitive.

source_article.title: Clinical evolution of equine leishmaniasis with self-limiting cutaneous disease caused by Leishmania infantum in northeastern Brazil: A case report.
source_article.abstract: Leishmania infantum infections have long been described in humans and dogs worldwide, but characterization of equine cases remains scarce. We describe the clinical evolution of a natural L. infantum infection to contribute to the diagnostic knowledge and epidemiology of equine leishmaniasis (EL). An auction-acquired four-year-old Mangalarga Marchador mare from Pernambuco state, presented a few subcutaneous nodules on the head and neck upon arrival at the purchaser's stud at Bahia state, in November of 2019. They progressed to multiple ulcerated and non-ulcerated nodules and spread to both right limbs in seven weeks. Hematology revealed anemia, lymphocytosis, monocytosis, and elevated plasma fibrinogen. Histopathology of the biopsied nodules identified a granulomatous dermatitis with macrophages containing Leishmania amastigotes. PCR detected Leishmania in skin lesions, but not in blood or spleen aspirate samples; ITS1 PCR-RFLP and DNA sequencing confirmed L. infantum species. A topical antiseptic and insect-repellent therapy and a monthly follow-up were established. All lesions improved progressively, without specific anti-Leishmania treatment, and 14 months later there was a consistent resolution. This first description of EL by L. infantum in an endemic area is relevant to emphasize the need for epidemiological studies, and to enhance clinicians' awareness for differential diagnosis.

statements.text: A horse with natural Leishmania infantum infection developed progressive cutaneous nodules and ulcerated skin lesions that later resolved without specific anti-Leishmania treatment, indicating that equine leishmaniasis can be self-limiting in this setting.
statements.support_summary: The supporting evidence suggests that equine Leishmania infantum infection can produce cutaneous nodules and skin lesions, with a generally mild or self-limiting course in some reports. A field study in horses from Brazil found PCR-confirmed L. infantum infection associated with skin lesions and nodules, though it did not document spontaneous resolution without treatment, so it only partially supports the claim's specific self-healing aspect (PMID: 31666069). A review on feline leishmaniasis explicitly states that equine leishmaniasis appears to be a self-healing skin-dwelling disease and that affected horses may recover without chemotherapy, which is directly consistent with the claim, although this statement appears in a broader review rather than a primary equine case report (PMID: 15305717). Another equine review reports that cutaneous lesions in infected equids may show spontaneous regression and that the disease often has a mild clinical profile, supporting the interpretation that equine leishmaniasis can be self-limiting (PMID: 31498914).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The evidence includes reviews explicitly describing equine leishmaniasis as capable of spontaneous regression or recovery without chemotherapy, while the primary horse study supports the presence of compatible cutaneous lesions but not the specific untreated resolution described in the claim.

statements.text: In the horse with cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania infantum, parasite detection was positive in skin lesions but negative in blood and spleen aspirate, indicating that skin lesions can be the main detectable site of infection.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Leishmania infantum should be considered in the differential diagnosis of cutaneous nodular or ulcerative disease in horses in endemic areas, because equine leishmaniasis can occur in northeastern Brazil.
statements.support_summary: Multiple articles support that horses can develop cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania infantum, making it a plausible differential diagnosis for nodular or ulcerative skin disease in endemic settings. A Brazilian survey found PCR-confirmed L. infantum infection in 14 horses, with reported skin lesions and nodules among infected equines, though this study was from southern Brazil rather than northeastern Brazil (PMID: 31666069). Case-based evidence shows ulcerated cutaneous disease in a horse with PCR-confirmed L. infantum infection in Portugal (PMID: 15991833), and autochthonous equine cases in Brazil with parasites detected in lesions, including L. infantum infection and mixed infection with L. braziliensis in Minas Gerais (PMID: 23845306). A review further states that equine cutaneous leishmaniasis can be caused by L. infantum, that autochthonous cases have been documented in Brazil, and that such skin lesions may be underdiagnosed (PMID: 31498914). A limitation/discrepancy is that the cited evidence supports equine L. infantum cutaneous disease broadly and in Brazil, but does not directly document northeastern Brazil in these abstracts.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall supported, because several abstracts document L. infantum-associated cutaneous disease in horses and autochthonous equine infections in Brazil, supporting inclusion in the differential diagnosis in endemic areas. However, the geographic specificity to northeastern Brazil is only indirectly supported by the provided abstracts rather than directly demonstrated.

matched_article.title: Worldwide meta-analysis on Anaplasma phagocytophilum infections in animal reservoirs: Prevalence, distribution and reservoir diversity.
matched_article.abstract: A wide range of vertebrate species are competent reservoirs of Anaplasma phagocytophilum, where the pathogen is maintained in the enzootic cycle and transmitted to humans through activities of tick vectors. An insight into the role and diversity of these reservoirs is vital in understanding the epidemiology of this pathogen. Here, we determined the prevalence, distribution and reservoir diversity of A. phagocytophilum using a systematic review and meta-analysis. Data pooling was performed by the random-effects model, heterogeneity was assessed by the Cochran's Q-test and publication bias by Egger's regression test. Eighty-nine studies from 33 countries across 5 continents revealed A. phagocytophilum pooled prevalence of 15.18% (95% CI: 11.64, 19.57). Continental estimates varied significantly (p < 0.0001), with a range of 2.88% (95% CI: 0.25, 26.20) in South America to 19.91% (95% CI: 13.57, 28.24) in Europe. Country-based estimates ranged between 2.93% (95% CI: 1.17, 7.16) in Slovakia and 71.58% (95% CI: 25.91, 94.77) in Norway. Studies on A. phagocytophilum were concentrated in Europe (51.69%; 46/89) by continent and the USA (22.47%; 20/89) by country. Prevalence in wildlife (17.64%; 95% CI: 12.21-28.59) was significantly higher (p < 0.001) than that among domestic animals (10.68%; 95% CI: 6.61-16.83). Diverse species of wildlife, domestic animals and birds were infected by A. phagocytophilum. To curtail the public health, veterinary and economic consequences of A. phagocytophilum infections, we recommend an all-inclusive epidemiological approach that targets the human, animal and environmental components of the disease.

statements.text: Animal reservoirs worldwide have a substantial prevalence of Anaplasma phagocytophilum infection, indicating broad geographic distribution of the pathogen in reservoir hosts.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently reports detection of Anaplasma phagocytophilum in a wide range of reservoir or potential reservoir hosts across many regions, including Europe, Asia, Africa, and North America, with especially high prevalences in cervids and some other wildlife. Several studies report substantial infection levels in wild ungulates, such as >60% in deer in Slovakia (PMID: 23770268), 75% in red deer and 54.2% in roe deer in Italy (PMID: 27020736), 97.9% in red deer and 60% in roe deer in Hungary (PMID: 29554900), 94.0% in roe deer in Denmark (PMID: 30245088), and 82% in moose calves in Norway (PMID: 34782490). Additional studies show notable prevalence in domestic or other wild hosts from multiple countries, including sheep/goats/yaks in China (32.3-42.9%; PMID: 23105025), cattle in Iran (15.5%; PMID: 32891899), camels in Iran (34.2%; PMID: 30592905), wild carnivores in Poland (31.6%; PMID: 31590678), gray foxes in California by serology (51%; PMID: 19395744), and baboons in Kenya (18.3% for Anaplasma spp., with A. phagocytophilum confirmed; PMID: 34906141). Reviews also describe A. phagocytophilum as a cosmopolitan or widespread multi-host pathogen with broad host range and geographically dispersed ecotypes (PMID: 26346451, PMID: 23885337, PMID: 25127547), although some supporting items are reviews or focused local studies rather than direct global prevalence surveys.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence mainly shows that prevalence is not uniformly high across all host species and that some proposed reservoirs may be poor or negligible reservoirs in certain settings. In Switzerland, Apodemus spp. and Myodes glareolus rodents failed to transmit A. phagocytophilum to xenodiagnostic ticks, arguing against their reservoir role there (PMID: 24582511), and in Slovakia rodent infection prevalence was only 0.5-0.6%, suggesting rodents were not the main reservoirs in that area (PMID: 25980768). Forest birds in west-central Poland had no detectable A. phagocytophilum in blood or bird-derived ticks, indicating they were not competent reservoirs locally (PMID: 16841867). Low prevalences were also reported in some domestic or carnivore hosts, including dogs in Croatia at 0.3% (PMID: 28905230), horses in Korea at 0.2% (PMID: 30630276), and red foxes in Romania at 2.55%, with authors suggesting only a limited maintenance role (PMID: 26449360). Thus, the main counter-theme is host- and region-specific heterogeneity rather than absence of geographic spread overall.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The evidence base includes many studies from multiple continents showing A. phagocytophilum in numerous animal hosts, often at high prevalence in major wildlife reservoirs such as deer and moose, while the contradictory studies mostly indicate that some hosts (especially certain rodents, birds, dogs, horses, or foxes) are low-prevalence or poor reservoirs in specific local contexts.

statements.text: Wildlife reservoirs have a higher prevalence of Anaplasma phagocytophilum infection than domestic animal reservoirs.
statements.support_summary: Several field studies report very high A. phagocytophilum prevalence in wildlife, especially cervids, often exceeding rates seen in sampled domestic animals. In France, roe deer showed 76% prevalence and were considered major reservoir candidates, although this study did not directly quantify domestic-animal prevalence in the same comparison and also noted livestock-associated genotypes in ticks (PMID: 28807858). In Hungary, prevalence was significantly higher in red deer (97.9%), fallow deer (72.7%), and roe deer (60%) than in mouflons (6.3%), with no positives in water buffalo, directly favoring higher prevalence in wildlife than at least some domestic ungulates (PMID: 29554900). Other studies found similarly high wildlife positivity—red deer 98%, roe deer 95.2%, wild boar 44.8% in France (PMID: 27876073), >60% infection in red and roe deer in Slovakia (PMID: 23770268), and 71% PCR positivity in woodrats versus 7% in domestic dogs in California (PMID: 16584330); gray fox seroprevalence also exceeded dogs in one habitat (PMID: 19395744). However, several supporting items are only indirect or not true reservoir-to-domestic prevalence comparisons, including studies focused on ticks, reservoir competence, or mixed Anaplasma species rather than explicit A. phagocytophilum prevalence contrasts (PMID: 24865688, PMID: 23885337, PMID: 18429692, PMID: 31014863, PMID: 28890111).
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited and mixed in relevance. A Slovakia study found very low A. phagocytophilum prevalence in rodents (0.5% in skin), concluding rodents were not the main reservoirs in that setting, but this does not directly compare wildlife with domestic animal reservoirs and mainly challenges one wildlife reservoir group rather than the overall claim (PMID: 25980768). A China study more directly undermines the claim by detecting A. phagocytophilum in 3/9 wild Siberian roe deer and not reporting it in goats, though the abstract emphasizes greater pathogen diversity in wildlife and the tiny deer sample makes inference on prevalence unstable; the provided contradiction rationale appears discrepant with the abstract numbers (PMID: 31196180). Overall, the contradictory set contains only one partially direct comparison and one study about poor rodent reservoir status rather than domestic-versus-wild prevalence.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple studies, particularly in deer and some other wildlife, report high A. phagocytophilum prevalence and in several cases higher levels than sampled domestic animals, whereas the contradictory evidence is sparse, partly indirect, and includes at least one apparent mismatch between abstract data and the contradiction label.

statements.text: A diverse range of wildlife, domestic animals, and birds are infected with Anaplasma phagocytophilum, supporting a broad reservoir host diversity.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently describes A. phagocytophilum as a multi-host pathogen infecting many wildlife and domestic mammals across regions, with detections in deer, roe deer, moose, wild boar, foxes, rodents, cattle, sheep, goats, yaks, horses, dogs, donkeys, camels, goats, and nonhuman primates (e.g., PMID: 23885337, PMID: 17114686, PMID: 27876073, PMID: 23105025, PMID: 29558990, PMID: 30592905, PMID: 34906141). Several studies explicitly link these infections to reservoir or maintenance roles, especially for cervids and some small mammals, and note distinct ecotypes or shared transmission cycles between wildlife and livestock (PMID: 27876073, PMID: 30606222, PMID: 28784148, PMID: 29310697). Evidence for birds is present but more limited: A. phagocytophilum was directly detected in birds in Spain and birds were cited as maintaining infection in southern Europe (PMID: 16417435, PMID: 17114686), while another Italian field study found A. phagocytophilum in ticks collected from wild birds rather than clearly in bird tissues themselves (PMID: 28890111). Reviews also broadly characterize the organism as widely distributed among wild and domestic animals and sometimes mention movement by migrating birds, but these are less direct than host-detection studies (PMID: 26346451, PMID: 31864403). Overall, the strongest recurring theme is broad mammalian host diversity, with some but less consistent abstract-level support for avian infection/reservoir involvement.
statements.contradict_summary: The contradictory evidence does not generally dispute broad host diversity overall, but instead challenges specific host groups—especially rodents and birds—as important reservoirs in some settings. Two rodent-focused studies found very low or no evidence that rodents transmitted A. phagocytophilum and concluded they were not main reservoirs in those European study areas (PMID: 25980768, PMID: 24582511). Bird-focused studies likewise reported no pathogen DNA in sampled forest birds or their bird-derived ticks in Poland, and experimental infection work suggested American robins and gray catbirds are unlikely to be significant maintenance hosts (PMID: 16841867, PMID: 23427666). A Europe-wide ecotype study found A. phagocytophilum in many vertebrates but noted it was rare in rodents and birds and absent in some taxa such as badgers and lizards, indicating host range is structured rather than uniformly broad across all animals (PMID: 25127547). One listed contradictory item is actually mixed/partly supportive because it explicitly states one clade was found in hosts including humans, ungulates, birds, and dogs, so its placement reflects a discrepancy in categorization rather than clear contradiction (PMID: 25129860).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The evidence strongly supports broad reservoir host diversity across many wildlife and domestic mammal species, while the main caveat is that avian and rodent reservoir roles appear inconsistent, region- and ecotype-dependent, and weaker than the mammalian evidence.

source_article.title: Microbial dynamics and nitrogen retention during sheep manure composting employing peach shell biochar.
source_article.abstract: In this study, the effects of peach shell biochar (PSB) and microbial agent (EM) amendment on nitrogen conservation and bacterial dynamics during sheep manure (SM) composting were examined. Six treatments were performed including T1 (control with no addition), T2 (EM), T3 (EM + 2.5 %PSB), T4 (EM + 5 %PSB), T5 (EM + 7.5 %PSB), and T6 (EM + 10 %PSB). The results showed that the additives amendment reduced NH3 emissions by 6.12%∼32.88% and N2O emissions by 10.96%∼19.76%, while increased total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN) content by 8.15-9.13 g/kg. Meanwhile, Firmicutes were the dominant flora in the thermophilic stages, while Proteobacteria, Actinobacteriota, and Bacteroidota were the dominant flora in the maturation stages. The abundance of Bacteroidota and Actinobacteriota were increased by 17.49%∼32.51% and 2.31%∼12.60%, respectively, which can accelerate the degradable organic materials decomposition. Additionally, redundancy analysis showed that Proteobacteria, Actinobacteriota, and Bacteroidota were positively correlated with NO3--N, TKN, and N2O, but a negative correlation with NH3 and NH4+-N. Finally, results confirmed that (EM + 10 %PSB) additives were more effective to reduce nitrogen loss and improve bacterial dynamics.

statements.text: During sheep manure composting, adding peach shell biochar with an effective microbial agent reduced ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions and improved nitrogen retention compared with no amendment or microbial agent alone.
statements.support_summary: One sheep-manure composting study found that peach shell biochar improved compost conditions and, at higher doses, inhibited excess NH3 release while retaining over 55% of nitrogen versus control, which is consistent with improved nitrogen retention during sheep manure composting, although the abstract does not mention an effective microbial agent or nitrous oxide outcomes (PMID: 38697253). A broader review reported that adding biochar during organic-waste composting commonly reduces greenhouse-gas and odorous emissions, including NH3-related losses, especially around 5-10% dosage, but this is indirect evidence because it is not specific to sheep manure or to biochar combined with a microbial inoculant (PMID: 35385764). Overall, the supporting literature mainly backs the biochar component of the claim, with limited direct abstract-level evidence for the combined peach shell biochar plus microbial agent intervention.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall somewhat supported, mainly because one directly relevant sheep-manure study supports reduced ammonia loss and improved nitrogen retention with peach shell biochar, and a review broadly supports emission reductions from biochar during composting. However, support is incomplete because the provided abstracts do not directly establish the full comparison against microbial agent alone or clearly confirm nitrous oxide reduction from the combined amendment.

statements.text: During sheep manure composting, adding peach shell biochar with an effective microbial agent shifted bacterial communities toward greater abundance of decomposer-associated phyla during maturation, consistent with enhanced degradation of organic materials.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study reports that peach shell biochar amendment in sheep manure compost improved the composting environment, regulated microbial and enzyme activity, advanced and strengthened the thermophilic phase, increased germination index, and promoted decomposition of compost organic matter, especially at 7.5-10% addition rates (PMID: 38697253). However, the abstract does not mention use of an effective microbial agent or directly describe bacterial community shifts toward decomposer-associated phyla during maturation, so this is only indirect support and represents a mismatch with the specific claim (PMID: 38697253).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly supported overall. The available evidence suggests peach shell biochar can enhance sheep manure composting and organic matter degradation, but it does not directly show that biochar plus an effective microbial agent shifted bacterial communities toward decomposer-associated phyla during maturation.

statements.text: During sheep manure composting, the combination of effective microbial agent and the highest peach shell biochar amendment was the most effective treatment for reducing nitrogen loss and improving bacterial community dynamics.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Exploring and comparing the impacts of low temperature to endogenous and exogenous partial denitrification: The nitrite supply, transcription mechanism, and microbial dynamics.
matched_article.abstract: Nitrite supply was pretty significant to exogenous or endogenous partial denitrification (ExPD or EdPD) for their combination with anammox in removing nitrogen. This study investigated how temperature impacted the nitrite supply of ExPD and EdPD, through long-term experiments in two 10 L sequencing batch reactors (SBRs) and 12 batch temperature tests, with sodium acetate as organic. It was demonstrated that low temperature (5-15 °C) favored higher nitrite transformation rate (NTR) for two systems (1.1-1.3 and 1.1-1.2 times higher separately), and ExPD owned higher nitrite-supply ability than EdPD (32.8 % higher NTR). Moreover, quantitative reverse transcription PCR and 16srDNA sequencing were conducted, exploring the inherent mechanism and microbial dynamics. Results presented that more inhibition to transcription and translation of nirSK genes than narG in low temperature induced higher NTR. Besides, compared with ExPD, less microbial dynamics and granule size reduction occurred to EdPD, which was more capable of adapting to low temperature.

statements.text: Low temperature favored nitrite supply in both exogenous partial denitrification and endogenous partial denitrification systems, leading to a higher nitrite transformation rate than warmer conditions.
statements.support_summary: Support is mostly indirect and centered on low-temperature operation being compatible with nitrite accumulation rather than explicit low-vs-warm comparisons. An endogenous partial denitrification granular sludge system achieved high nitrate-to-nitrite transformation (NTR 88.7%) during 105 days under mainstream low-temperature conditions, suggesting strong nitrite supply in EPD at low temperature (PMID: 31228740). An anoxic biofilm reactor maintained substantial nitrite accumulation (>9.7 mg/L) even as temperature declined from 32 to 5°C, consistent with persistent nitrite availability under colder conditions, though not specifically in exogenous/EPD side-by-side comparisons (PMID: 31901514). Additional studies on low-temperature nitrogen removal via nitrite in swine wastewater reactors and low-temperature nitrite-anammox treatment further suggest feasibility of nitrite-based pathways in cold conditions, but the abstracts provided do not clearly show that colder conditions outperformed warmer ones, making these only weakly supportive and somewhat discrepant with the claim's comparative wording (PMID: 32698083; PMID: 31891911).
statements.contradict_summary: One pilot-scale IFAS partial nitritation-anammox study directly reported worse nitrogen removal as temperature decreased from 25 to 15°C, with lower temperatures suppressing ammonium-oxidizing and anammox bacteria and reducing treatment capacity (PMID: 36215833). However, this study concerns PN/A rather than exogenous or endogenous partial denitrification specifically, so it contradicts the broader notion that colder temperatures generally improve nitrite-related transformation but is not a perfect match to the claim.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall weakly supported, but the evidence is not definitive. Most supporting abstracts show that low temperature can sustain or enable nitrite accumulation, especially in endogenous denitrification contexts, yet they rarely directly demonstrate a higher nitrite transformation rate than warmer conditions, while the lone contradictory study is only partially applicable because it evaluates PN/A rather than partial denitrification systems.

statements.text: Exogenous partial denitrification had a greater nitrite-supply capacity than endogenous partial denitrification under the tested conditions.
statements.support_summary: One directly comparative study reported that exogenous partial denitrification (ExPD) achieved a higher nitrite transformation rate than endogenous partial denitrification (EdPD), reaching 83.4% versus 74.4%, which supports greater nitrite-supply capacity for ExPD under those tested conditions (PMID: 38460557). This is the only evidence item that explicitly compares ExPD and EdPD in the provided set.
statements.contradict_summary: The contradicting studies mainly show that endogenous partial denitrification can itself achieve high nitrite accumulation, but they do not directly demonstrate that it outperforms exogenous partial denitrification. One study achieved about 80% nitrate-to-nitrite transformation after a shift from exogenous to endogenous PD with hydrolytic acidification products and intracellular storage compounds contributing to nitrite production, suggesting endogenous PD can be effective, though not providing a head-to-head superiority test against exogenous PD (PMID: 33895667). Another study reported endogenous partial denitrification with a nitrate-to-nitrite transformation ratio around 87%, again showing strong endogenous performance but without direct comparison to exogenous PD (PMID: 27914783). Thus, there is a discrepancy between their categorization as contradiction and what the abstracts actually support: they are better viewed as indirect or contextual evidence rather than direct refutation.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence is a direct comparative study showing higher nitrite transformation for exogenous versus endogenous partial denitrification, whereas the opposing studies mainly establish that endogenous PD can also perform well but do not directly refute the comparative claim.

statements.text: Endogenous partial denitrification was more resilient to low temperature than exogenous partial denitrification, with less microbial community disruption and less granule size reduction.
statements.support_summary: One supporting study reports that an endogenous partial-denitrification granular sludge system operated stably for 105 days under mainstream low-temperature conditions, maintaining high nitrate-to-nitrite transformation and indicating robustness of EPD at low temperature (PMID: 31228740). However, the abstract does not directly compare endogenous versus exogenous partial denitrification, and it does not clearly report microbial community disruption or granule size reduction outcomes, so support for the full comparative claim is only indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported overall. The available evidence suggests endogenous partial denitrification can remain stable at low temperature, but there is no direct abstract-level evidence here showing it is more resilient than exogenous partial denitrification or that it causes less microbial community disruption and less granule size reduction.

source_article.title: Poor condyle position after mandibular reconstruction and a classification system for patients with "VSCU" based on computed tomography: a cross-sectional study with retrospective data collection.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: Although the application of vascularized free bone muscle flap to reconstruct the mandible has become a standardized approach for mandible reconstruction, the results of its reconstruction are not always satisfactory. The purpose of this study was to identify the types of mandibular and condylar defects by analyzing the unsatisfactory cases of mandibular reconstruction in clinical practice, and to provide some clinical experience of reconstruction. METHODS: Our study retrospectively analyzed 364 patients who underwent mandibular resection and vascularized free bone flap reconstruction of the mandible and temporomandibular joint (TMJ). We innovatively proposed a "VSCU" classification system (V: vertical position, S: sagittal position, C: coronal position, U: condylar resection is not required) by analyzing computed tomography (CT) scans of mandibular branches and TMJs. RESULTS: In all, 221 cases of free iliac muscle flap and 143 cases of fibula muscle flap were included in this study, of which 23 cases had unsatisfactory results after TMJ reconstruction. We classified 23 patients with unsatisfactory mandibular reconstruction according to the "VSCU" classification system. The most common type was U + V + SfC (n=8), followed by V - SfC + U + (n=4), V - s + C + U + (n=3), V - sbcou - (n=3), V - SBC + U + (n=2), V - s + C + U - (n=1). The most common classification was insufficient mandibular rami length, followed by condylar sagittal anteriorization. There was no significant change in the position of condyle on the healthy side during mandibular reconstruction involving condyle. P1 on the affected side was 52.28±4.17 mm before operation and 58.94±5.65 mm after operation, P<0.01; P2 was 12.83±3.49 mm before operation and 24.90±7.15 mm after operation. S2 was 4.54±2.84 mm before operation and 19.10±8.54 mm after operation. A2 was 11.46±3.35 mm before operation and 24.15±8.29 mm after operation. The P values were all less than 0.01, and the differences were statistically significant. CONCLUSIONS: We propose to use the "VSCU" classification system for accurate 3-dimensional (3D) analysis and positioning, and then obtain accurate models through computer-aided design and manufacturing (CAD/CAM), which can reduce the occurrence of poor reconstruction effect and unreasonable joint position, and is worthy of clinical promotion.

statements.text: In patients undergoing mandibular resection and vascularized free bone flap reconstruction involving the mandible and temporomandibular joint, unsatisfactory reconstruction was most commonly associated with insufficient mandibular ramus length and condylar sagittal anteriorization.
statements.support_summary: One retrospective study of 337 patients undergoing mandibular reconstruction with vascularized osteocutaneous flaps found that unsatisfactory condylar reconstruction could be categorized by short ramus length and dissatisfaction in sagittal/coronal condylar position, introducing a VSC classification system (PMID: 39175198). The abstract supports that insufficient mandibular ramus length is one recognized pattern of unsatisfactory reconstruction, but it does not clearly state that this was the most common problem; in fact, the reported class counts suggest short ramus length alone was uncommon, while more complex positional dissatisfaction was more frequent (PMID: 39175198). The abstract also refers broadly to sagittal condylar malposition rather than specifically to sagittal anteriorization, so this is only partial support and there is some discrepancy between the claim and the available abstract details (PMID: 39175198).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly to partially supported overall. The sole cited abstract supports that short ramus length and sagittal/coronal condylar malposition are relevant features of unsatisfactory mandibular reconstruction, but it does not clearly show that insufficient ramus length and specifically condylar sagittal anteriorization were the most common associated findings.

statements.text: In patients undergoing mandibular reconstruction involving the condyle, the condyle position on the healthy side did not change significantly during reconstruction.
statements.support_summary: Several imaging-based studies directly support relative stability of the healthy/contralateral condyle after mandibular reconstruction involving the condyle. Prospective and retrospective CT/CBCT studies found that while the operated side changed significantly, the non-tumor/contralateral side showed relatively stable condylar position or no major/no obvious change, especially in mediolateral and anteroposterior dimensions (PMID: 36747208, PMID: 38622579, PMID: 30735641, PMID: 37632578). Additional reconstruction-accuracy studies using CAD/CAM, guides, or rapid prototyping reported satisfactory condylar positioning and minimal postoperative deviation, but many of these do not explicitly isolate the healthy side and therefore provide only indirect support (PMID: 30470467, PMID: 33850857, PMID: 21094572, PMID: 28749839). Some SUPPORT items appear only loosely relevant to the specific claim because they discuss reconstructed/neocondyles, function, or general symmetry rather than contralateral condylar stability (e.g., PMID: 27235153, PMID: 29222966, PMID: 35999876). Overall, the strongest support comes from studies explicitly stating that contralateral/non-tumor condyles had no significant or no major positional change after reconstruction.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence includes newer and condition-specific studies showing that condylar position may not remain stable after reconstruction. A large retrospective CBCT study reported that over time the contralateral side showed inward and medial movement, concluding that condylar position lacks long-term stability after fibula-flap reconstruction with condyle preservation (PMID: 41472372). Other studies found significant deviation of the reconstructed fibular/neocondyle over follow-up or reported severe condylar dislocation as a complication (PMID: 32440937, PMID: 30997360), but these mainly concern the operated/reconstructed side rather than the healthy side and are therefore only partially contradictory to the specific claim. One study categorized as contradiction emphasized decreased contralateral laterotrusion and neocondyle deviation, which reflects function and reconstructed-side position more than direct evidence of healthy-side positional change (PMID: 36088184). Thus, the clearest contradiction to the exact claim is mainly PMID: 41472372, with the other items being less directly aligned to the healthy-side question.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple studies directly report little or no significant change in the contralateral/healthy condyle after mandibular reconstruction, although at least one newer larger study suggests measurable long-term contralateral movement, indicating that stability may depend on follow-up duration and surgical factors.

statements.text: A computed tomography-based VSCU classification system may improve three-dimensional analysis and positioning for mandibular and condylar reconstruction and may help reduce poor reconstruction outcomes and malposition of the temporomandibular joint.
statements.support_summary: One retrospective CT-based study directly relevant to the claim introduced a VSC classification for post-reconstruction condylar positioning after mandibular reconstruction and argued it could refine assessment and enhance surgical outcomes, while also identifying defect site and number of reconstructed segments as risk factors for condylar dislocation (PMID: 39175198). Several indirect studies support the broader premise that CT-based 3D planning improves condylar/TMJ positioning: virtual surgical planning reduced ipsilateral condylar shifts and preserved anatomic relations better than traditional planning in mandibular reconstruction (PMID: 27649463), and computer-assisted free fibular flap reconstruction showed less postoperative condylar displacement and more stable condylar position than traditional techniques (PMID: 38622579). Additional indirect evidence shows CT-guided classification or CAD/3D-template workflows can guide treatment and help restore satisfactory condylar/ramus position in related TMJ or mandibular reconstruction settings (PMID: 21295900, PMID: 30320699). However, most supporting studies do not specifically evaluate the exact VSCU system named in the claim, so support is mainly extrapolated from adjacent CT-based classification and 3D planning literature.
statements.contradict_summary: The main contradicting evidence is a retraction notice for the original VSCU-related study, indicating that the article on poor condyle position after mandibular reconstruction and CT-based VSCU classification has been withdrawn and therefore should not be relied upon as valid evidence for the claim (PMID: 41367729). This does not by itself prove the claim is false, but it substantially weakens confidence in the specific VSCU classification system and creates a major discrepancy with the supporting narrative built around that system.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall unclear to slightly contradicted. Although related CT-based and computer-assisted reconstruction studies suggest better 3D analysis and improved condylar/TMJ positioning are plausible, the direct evidence for the specific VSCU classification is undermined by retraction of the key original study.

matched_article.title: Flow-augmentation STA-MCA bypass for acute and subacute ischemic stroke due to internal carotid artery occlusion and the role of advanced neuroimaging with hemodynamic and flow-measurement in the decision-making: preliminary data.
matched_article.abstract: A major clinical challenge is the adequate identification of patients with acute (<1 week) and subacute (1-6 weeks) ischemic stroke due to internal carotid artery (ICA) occlusion who could benefit from a surgical revascularization after a failure of endovascular and/or medical treatment. Recently, two novel quantitative imaging modalities have been introduced: (I) quantitative magnetic resonance angiography (qMRA) with non-invasive optimal vessel analysis (NOVA) for quantification of blood flow in major cerebral arteries (in mL/min), and (II) blood oxygenation level-dependent (BOLD) functional magnetic resonance imaging to assess cerebrovascular reactivity (CVR). The aim of this study is to present our cohort of patients who underwent surgical revascularization in the acute and subacute phase of ischemic stroke as well as to demonstrate the importance of hemodynamic and flow assessment for the decision-making regarding surgical revascularization in patients with acute and subacute stroke and ICA-occlusion. Symptomatic patients with acute and subacute ischemic stroke because of persistent ICA-occlusion despite optimal medical/endovascular recanalization therapy who were treated at the Neuroscience Clinical Center of the University Hospital Zurich underwent both BOLD-CVR and qMRA-NOVA to study the hemodynamic and collateral vessel status. Patients selected for surgical revascularization according to our previously published flowchart were included in this prospective cohort study. Repeated NOVA and BOLD-CVR investigations were done after bypass surgery as follow up as well as clinical follow up. Continuous BOLD-CVR and qMRA-NOVA variables were compared using paired Student t-test. Between May 2019 and September 2022, superficial temporal artery-middle cerebral artery (STA-MCA) bypass surgery was performed in 12 patients with acute and subacute stroke because of ICA-occlusion despite of optimal endovascular and/or medical treatment prior to the surgery. Impaired BOLD-CVR in the occluded vascular territory [MCA territory: ipsilateral vs. contralateral: -0.03±0.07 vs. 0.11±0.07 %BOLD/mmHgCO2, P<0.001] as well as reduced hemispheric flow with qMRA-NOVA (ipsilateral vs. contralateral: 228.00±54.62 vs. 384.50±70.99 mL/min, P=0.01) were measured indicating insufficient collateralization. Post-operative qMRA-NOVA showed improved hemispheric flow (via bypass) (pre-bypass vs. post-bypass: 236.60±76.45 vs. 334.20±131.33 mL/min, P=0.02) and the 3-month-follow-up with BOLD-CVR showed improved cerebral hemodynamics (MCA territory: pre-bypass vs. post-bypass: -0.01±0.05 vs. 0.06±0.03 %BOLD/mmHgCO2, P=0.02) in all patients studied. Quantitative assessment with BOLD-CVR and qMRA-NOVA allows us to evaluate the pre- and post-operative cerebral hemodynamics and collateral vessel status in patients with acute/subacute stroke due to ICA occlusion who may benefit from surgical revascularization after failure of endovascular/medical treatment.

statements.text: In patients with acute or subacute ischemic stroke due to persistent internal carotid artery occlusion despite medical and/or endovascular treatment, superficial temporal artery-middle cerebral artery bypass can improve cerebral blood flow and cerebrovascular reactivity after surgery.
statements.support_summary: Multiple studies report postoperative hemodynamic improvement after STA-MCA bypass in patients with carotid or intracranial steno-occlusive disease, including improved cerebral blood flow/perfusion, vasodilatory reserve/CVR, or reduced oxygen extraction fraction on SPECT, PET, CT perfusion, MRI, Doppler, or qMRA (PMID: 26023999, 23101451, 30778681, 28131187, 11344908, 31513966). Evidence closer to the claim’s population includes acute/subacute or treatment-refractory ischemia from persistent ICA/large-vessel occlusion, where urgent or early bypass was proposed or associated with improved perfusion/hemodynamics and clinical recovery, though often in small series or case reports rather than controlled trials (PMID: 34996035, 27816279, 31226451, 33475724, 33681773). Additional quantitative flow studies show that STA-MCA bypass can deliver substantial postoperative flow and alter intracranial perfusion/pressure dynamics, supporting biological plausibility for improved blood flow after surgery (PMID: 39126722, 34460923, 31497114, 12403945). Several supporting items are only indirectly relevant because they involve moyamoya, chronic occlusion, MCA rather than ICA disease, double/combined bypass techniques, or emphasize clinical outcomes more than explicit CVR change (e.g., PMID: 39199456, 23735877, 36595057, 38702162). Overall, the recurring theme is that in selected hemodynamically compromised patients, STA-MCA bypass often improves postoperative perfusion metrics, with some evidence specifically including ICA occlusion and failed medical therapy.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and only partially aligned with the claim.
