One study on indirect EC-IC bypass found poor collateral development, persistent perfusion deficits in some patients, and no clear advantage over medical therapy in symptomatic ICA/MCA hemodynamic failure, but this concerns indirect rather than direct STA-MCA bypass and focuses on infarction/TIA outcomes more than postoperative CBF/CVR improvement (PMID: 19199456). Another technical note states that ipsilateral STA-MCA bypass is unsuitable in common carotid artery occlusion because donor flow is inadequate, but this pertains to CCAO rather than the claim’s persistent ICA occlusion after stroke (PMID: 39246756). Thus, the contradiction set contains a discrepancy in applicability: it raises caution about bypass limitations and patient selection, but does not directly refute postoperative improvement in blood flow/CVR after direct STA-MCA bypass for persistent ICA occlusion.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Most relevant studies and case series indicate that STA-MCA bypass can improve postoperative cerebral perfusion and often cerebrovascular reserve/reactivity in carefully selected hemodynamically compromised patients, including some with persistent ICA occlusion or failed prior treatment, whereas the contradictory evidence is sparse and largely indirect or not specific to direct STA-MCA bypass in this population.

statements.text: In patients with acute or subacute ischemic stroke due to internal carotid artery occlusion, impaired cerebrovascular reactivity and reduced hemispheric blood flow identify insufficient collateral circulation and support consideration of surgical revascularization after failed medical and/or endovascular therapy.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support the hemodynamic premise of the claim: impaired cerebrovascular reactivity (CVR), reduced cerebral blood flow, and poor collateral status are linked in symptomatic ICA occlusion, including findings that leptomeningeal collateral activation marks severely impaired ipsilateral BOLD-CVR (PMID: 34112002), that BOLD-CVR mapping identifies hemodynamically relevant unilateral carotid disease (PMID: 34102856), and that reduced rCBF plus impaired vasoreactivity predict bypass usefulness after ICA occlusion (PMID: 12403945). Acute/subacute surgical series further suggest that after failed or unavailable reperfusion therapy, selected patients with persistent perfusion mismatch/hemodynamic impairment and insufficient collaterals may benefit from urgent bypass, including a proposed MRI/CVR-based selection flowchart (PMID: 34996035), urgent STA-MCA bypass after failed thrombectomy or contraindication to thrombectomy (PMID: 33423028), and early bypass in progressing stroke with low rCBF ratios around 70% (PMID: 27816279). Additional observational studies and reviews report improved perfusion, CVR, symptoms, or stroke recurrence outcomes after bypass in patients with symptomatic steno-occlusive disease and exhausted hemodynamic reserve (PMID: 35223090, PMID: 29673881, PMID: 35946362, PMID: 36624923), though many are retrospective, nonrandomized, or not limited specifically to acute ICA occlusion. Some “supporting” items are only indirect or case-based rather than direct tests of the exact claim, which weakens certainty despite a consistent recurring theme that hemodynamic failure/poor collateral compensation can be used to select patients for possible revascularization.
statements.contradict_summary: Randomized trial evidence challenges the clinical inference that identifying hemodynamic insufficiency should lead to bypass: in COSS, patients with symptomatic complete ICA occlusion and elevated OEF had improved hemodynamics after STA-MCA bypass, but no reduction in 2-year ipsilateral stroke recurrence versus medical therapy because of substantial perioperative stroke risk (PMID: 23101451). Similarly, the CMOSS randomized trial found that in symptomatic ICA or MCA occlusion with CT-perfusion-defined hemodynamic insufficiency, adding EC-IC bypass to medical therapy did not significantly reduce the composite outcome of stroke or death compared with medical therapy alone (PMID: 37606672). These studies do not dispute that impaired CVR/blood flow can identify hemodynamic compromise, but they contradict the stronger implication that such findings reliably justify surgical revascularization on outcome grounds.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only partially supported overall: there is substantial observational and physiologic evidence that impaired CVR and reduced hemispheric blood flow indicate inadequate collateral compensation in symptomatic ICA occlusion and may help select patients for rescue revascularization. However, the highest-quality evidence from randomized trials does not show a clear net clinical benefit of EC-IC bypass over medical therapy, so the rationale for considering surgery is plausible but remains not definitively validated for improved outcomes.

statements.text: Quantitative magnetic resonance angiography and blood oxygenation level-dependent functional magnetic resonance imaging can be used to assess preoperative and postoperative cerebral hemodynamics in patients undergoing bypass for acute or subacute ischemic stroke due to internal carotid artery occlusion.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support MRI-based hemodynamic assessment around EC-IC/STA-MCA bypass, though direct evidence for the exact combination of qMRA plus BOLD fMRI in acute/subacute ICA occlusion is limited. A proposed urgent bypass workflow for acute stroke with persistent ICA or M1 occlusion explicitly uses preoperative BOLD-CVR and NOVA qMRA to assess hemodynamic impairment and collateral circulation when selecting bypass candidates (PMID: 34996035). Postoperative or perioperative hemodynamic assessment is supported by qMRA showing increased bypass flow after surgery in acute/subacute/chronic LVO and moyamoya cases (PMID: 39126722), intraoperative BOLD-CVR demonstrating immediate tissue-level hemodynamic feedback after bypass (PMID: 30717072), and additional MRI flow studies showing pre/post bypass flow changes or vasoreactivity improvement in ICA/carotid occlusive disease (PMID: 34460923, PMID: 32862480). Some cited support is indirect or only partial because it addresses postoperative MRA alone, other MR flow techniques rather than qMRA, or populations broader than acute/subacute ICA occlusion (PMID: 29514319, PMID: 30396815, PMID: 16175231).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The strongest evidence comes from studies using preoperative BOLD-CVR and qMRA/NOVA for bypass selection in acute large-vessel occlusion and from peri/postoperative MRI-based flow or CVR studies showing these modalities can track hemodynamic changes after bypass, although several papers provide only partial or indirect support for the exact acute/subacute ICA occlusion setting.

source_article.title: A comparison of tiotropium/olodaterol vs tiotropium alone in terms of treatment effect for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: A meta-analysis.
source_article.abstract: BACKGROUND: Combinations of long-acting bronchodilators with different mechanisms of action are recommended to improve prognosis and reduce risk of adverse events of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). It is unclear whether the new combination therapy with long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) tiotropium (TIO) and long acting beta-agonists (LABA) olodaterol (OLO) was superior to tiotropium alone. METHODS: We measured the efficacy of the TIO/OLO combination vsTIO alone for COPD patients based on electronic databases up to February 2019. After rigorous quality review, data was extracted from eligible trials. All the main outcomes were pooled analysis using RevMan software. RESULTS: A total of 6 randomized controlled trials (RCTs) were identified. The pooled results of our meta-analysis demonstrated that FEV1 [MD = 0.03, 95% CI (-0.01,0.07), P = .18], FVC [MD = -0.03, 95%CI (-0.06,0.00), P = .09] and FEV1%pred [MD = 0.35, 95%CI (-0.30, 0.99), P = .29] all showed no significant difference between the 2 groups. The overall incidence of adverse effects (AEs) [OR = 1.01,95%CI (0.93,1.09), P = .87] and serious AEs [OR = 1.04,95% CI (0.82, 1.32), P = .72] in the combination group was similar to that of the TIO alone group, without statistical significance. CONCLUSION: These studies reported that the TIO/OLO combination did not show superior effects than tiotropium alone for COPD. Additionally, both therapies were well tolerated.

statements.text: In patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, tiotropium/olodaterol does not provide better lung function improvement than tiotropium alone.
statements.support_summary: One supporting item reports an age-stratified analysis of the OTEMTO and TONADO trials and states that there were no differences in relative treatment responses across age groups when comparing tiotropium/olodaterol with monotherapies, including tiotropium (PMID: 27843306). However, this abstract does not clearly state that lung function improvement with tiotropium/olodaterol was equal to tiotropium alone overall; rather, it emphasizes consistency across age groups, so its placement as support is somewhat uncertain. Thus, direct abstract-level support for the claim is limited and indirect.
statements.contradict_summary: Most contradicting evidence indicates that tiotropium/olodaterol improves lung function more than tiotropium alone. A Cochrane review found a small but statistically significant increase in pre-bronchodilator FEV1 with tiotropium plus LABA versus tiotropium alone, with most data derived from olodaterol studies (PMID: 26490945), and a pooled TONADO analysis in patients previously on LAMA monotherapy reported a significant trough FEV1 benefit of +0.074 L for tiotropium/olodaterol versus tiotropium (PMID: 32462607). A systematic review/meta-analysis concluded that tiotropium/olodaterol significantly improved trough FEV1 versus monocomponents (PMID: 29178871), and individual trials/reviews also reported better FEV1, inspiratory capacity, or broader lung-function outcomes with the combination than tiotropium alone (PMID: 26320402, 29750027, 26683033, 27354040, 28383865). Some listed contradiction items are weaker or indirect because they are reviews, economic models, protocols, or focus mainly on symptoms rather than direct lung-function comparisons (e.g., PMID: 27405723, 28537001, 28154373, 28139131), but the overall direction of evidence still favors superior lung-function improvement with the combination.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Direct comparative trial, pooled-analysis, and meta-analytic evidence consistently show that tiotropium/olodaterol provides modest but statistically significant lung-function improvements over tiotropium alone, whereas the lone supporting item is indirect and appears to be a misclassified or overstretched interpretation of the abstract.

statements.text: In patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, tiotropium/olodaterol has a similar overall adverse event and serious adverse event profile to tiotropium alone.
statements.support_summary: Multiple randomized trial analyses consistently report that tiotropium/olodaterol has an adverse event profile comparable to tiotropium alone in COPD. The strongest evidence comes from large 52-week pooled or long-term analyses showing no significant differences in overall AEs, SAEs, cardiovascular events, respiratory events, or major adverse cardiovascular events versus tiotropium, and in one pooled analysis discontinuation due to AEs was actually lower with the combination (PMID: 30261995; PMID: 27993292). Subgroup analyses found similar safety versus monocomponents in patients with renal impairment and in older adults, with no increase in major cardiac events, other AEs, or SAEs (PMID: 29910611; PMID: 33277507). Shorter efficacy trials and reviews also state that AE incidence or tolerability was similar between treatment groups, though these provide less detailed SAE-specific data and some are narrative/review articles rather than primary comparative safety reports (PMID: 25956072; PMID: 26320402; PMID: 26683033; PMID: 28383865; PMID: 27843306).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. I did not identify any abstract here that clearly reports worse overall adverse event or serious adverse event outcomes with tiotropium/olodaterol versus tiotropium alone.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This is based on several large long-term randomized trial analyses directly comparing tiotropium/olodaterol with tiotropium and consistently finding similar overall AE and SAE rates, with no clear safety penalty for the combination.

statements.text: In patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, tiotropium/olodaterol is not superior to tiotropium alone for overall treatment effect.
statements.support_summary: One age-stratified analysis of OTEMTO/TONADO reported no differences in relative treatment responses or safety for tiotropium/olodaterol versus monotherapies across age groups, but this does not clearly show absence of superiority on overall treatment effect and may reflect subgroup consistency rather than head-to-head equivalence (PMID: 27843306). A China cost-effectiveness model found tiotropium/olodaterol produced slightly more QALYs and fewer exacerbations than tiotropium, while being more costly and not cost-effective; this economic result does not support the claim on clinical superiority and is misaligned with the SUPPORT categorization (PMID: 37767047). Overall, the supporting set is weak and includes at least one discrepant item whose abstract actually suggests some clinical benefit with the combination.
statements.contradict_summary: Multiple randomized trials and pooled analyses reported that tiotropium/olodaterol improved lung function, dyspnea, health status, inspiratory capacity, exercise capacity, or physical activity compared with tiotropium alone, including TONADO/OTEMTO analyses and VIVACITO/VESUTO-related reports (PMID: 32462607, 29750027, 25956072, 28154373, 26320402, 32982204, 34908832). A systematic review/meta-analysis of 10 RCTs found significant improvements in lung function and more patients achieving meaningful dyspnea and health-status responses with tiotropium/olodaterol versus monocomponents (PMID: 29178871). Evidence on exacerbations also trends in favor of the combination, including a DYNAGITO Japanese subgroup reporting a lower moderate-to-severe exacerbation rate, though subgroup precision appears limited from the abstract (PMID: 30034230). Several reviews and health-economic studies are also directionally consistent, generally assuming or summarizing better clinical outcomes with tiotropium/olodaterol, though these are lower-level evidence than the RCTs and meta-analysis (PMID: 26683033, 33095662, 27405723, 30863045, 27703341, 28383865, 28139131).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. The strongest evidence consists of multiple randomized comparisons and a meta-analysis showing tiotropium/olodaterol improves several clinically relevant outcomes versus tiotropium alone, whereas the sparse supporting evidence is indirect or discrepant.

matched_article.title: Clinical and pathological analysis of 10 cases of salivary gland epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma.
matched_article.abstract: Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma (EMC) is a rare neoplasm of the salivary glands. The aim of this study is to review and evaluate clinicopathological features and treatment of EMC of salivary gland for better sensitivity and specificity of the diagnosis.The clinical and pathological data of the 10 salivary gland EMC cases from 2008 to 2017 were analyzed.Six cases of EMC were diagnosed to be originated from parotid gland and 4 cases were from the minor salivary gland including palate, tongue, and oropharynx. Seven cases were performed radical surgery and 3 cases had radiotherapy postoperation, 2 cases had a local recurrence. The follow-up period was 4 to 104 months and the survival rate was 100%. Histopathology showed the tumors had a dominant prototypical biphasic tubular structure consisting of inner, cuboidal ductal cells and an outer layer of clear, myoepithelial cells, which grew infiltratively. The immunohistochemistry (IHC) showed the marker proteins CK, S-100, CD117, and Calponin were strongly positive in most EMC.EMC is a rare and low-grade malignant tumor with good overall survival but relatively high tendency for local recurrence. Surgery is the priority choice for EMC therapy. Complete surgical excision and negative margins are necessary for good prognosis. Imaging techniques should be used to assess the neck dissection and it is unclear whether adjuvant radiotherapy is beneficial. To ensure the sensitivity and specificity of the EMC diagnosis, we should perform both pathological and IHC analysis.

statements.text: Salivary gland epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma is a rare, low-grade malignant tumor with good overall survival but a relatively high tendency for local recurrence.
statements.support_summary: Multiple reviews and case series consistently describe salivary gland epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma (EMC) as a rare neoplasm, usually comprising about 0.5-2% of salivary tumors/malignancies, and generally a low-grade malignancy (e.g., PMID: 28635988, PMID: 26195572, PMID: 24987609, PMID: 9568184). The strongest survival evidence comes from a population-based SEER analysis showing disease-specific survival of 91.3% at 5 years and 90.2% at 10 years, supporting generally favorable survival (PMID: 26195572), while reviews also cite 5-year survival around 94% (PMID: 28635988). A recurring theme is substantial local recurrence after resection, often reported in the 23-50% range or around 30%, across reviews and clinicopathologic summaries (PMID: 28635988, PMID: 24987609, PMID: 32645560, PMID: 31741932, PMID: 37275099). Older literature likewise reported 20 local recurrences among 71 patients with only 2 disease-related deaths, consistent with low-grade behavior but meaningful recurrence risk (PMID: 1666513). Some listed supporting studies actually concern myoepithelial carcinoma rather than EMC, which is a related but distinct entity and weakens those particular items as direct support.
statements.contradict_summary: Much of the contradicting set is driven by studies of myoepithelial carcinoma/malignant myoepithelioma rather than EMC, and these often report more aggressive behavior, including poorer survival and/or classification as high-grade, so they are only indirectly applicable to the claim (e.g., PMID: 36504414, PMID: 12775221, PMID: 10677950, PMID: 25970687). Among direct EMC evidence, some case reports/series of minor salivary gland EMC reported no recurrence during limited follow-up or explicitly described good prognosis with low recurrence, which conflicts with the claim's emphasis on relatively high local recurrence (PMID: 34829471, PMID: 20032423, PMID: 32215030). Another nuance is that direct EMC reports acknowledge fatal or aggressive variants and dedifferentiation/high-grade transformation in some cases, indicating that prognosis is not uniformly benign (PMID: 10220800, PMID: 30937832). However, these direct contradictions are mostly based on small case reports or short follow-up, limiting their weight against the broader EMC literature. Overall, the contradiction side is weakened by diagnosis mismatch and limited direct EMC data.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. Direct EMC literature repeatedly characterizes the tumor as rare and generally low-grade with favorable survival, while several reviews and series also report a notable local recurrence rate; contradictory evidence is either indirect because it studies myoepithelial carcinoma rather than EMC, or based on small direct EMC case reports with limited follow-up.

statements.text: For patients with salivary gland epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma, surgery is the preferred treatment, and complete excision with negative margins is important for a good prognosis.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently describes surgery as the primary or mainstay treatment for salivary gland epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma (EMC), often explicitly recommending wide/radical excision with clear or negative margins (e.g., PMID: 28635988, PMID: 24987609, PMID: 35667947, PMID: 31741932). A systematic review of 91 maxillofacial/sinonasal EMC cases found surgery alone was most common and that positive surgical margins were significantly associated with higher recurrence risk, directly supporting the importance of complete excision (PMID: 35667947). Multiple case reports and small series reported no recurrence or good local control after complete resection or wide-margin surgery (e.g., PMID: 20032423, PMID: 16360826, PMID: 27599278, PMID: 32000457), while some reports linked positive margins or recurrence to the need for additional treatment or closer surveillance (PMID: 37636663, PMID: 33655096). Several supporting items are about myoepithelial carcinoma rather than epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma, so their applicability is indirect, but they broadly reinforce the theme that radical surgery is favored in related salivary myoepithelial malignancies (e.g., PMID: 12775221, PMID: 22241311, PMID: 26463034). Overall, the recurring themes are surgical management as standard care, margin status as prognostically important, and adjuvant radiotherapy reserved mainly for selected higher-risk or margin-positive cases.
statements.contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited and mostly does not directly refute the claim. One recurrent myoepithelial carcinoma case achieved short-term tumor control with superselective intra-arterial cisplatin plus radiation after multiple recurrences, suggesting non-surgical salvage can work in difficult recurrent disease, but this does not address initial preferred therapy and is about myoepithelial carcinoma rather than EMC (PMID: 16446069). Another case report of unresectable parapharyngeal EMC showed more than 8 years of disease control with definitive radiotherapy alone, indicating that radiotherapy can be an effective alternative when surgery is not feasible; however, the abstract itself still states that surgery is the mainstay of treatment (PMID: 37649950). Thus, the apparent contradiction is weak and context-specific, reflecting exceptions or salvage scenarios rather than evidence against surgery as preferred treatment.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The evidence repeatedly identifies surgery as the main treatment for salivary gland EMC, and the strongest abstract-level evidence shows that positive margins are associated with recurrence, while the small amount of contrary evidence mainly describes non-surgical management in unresectable or recurrent special cases rather than disproving surgery as preferred care.

statements.text: For patients with salivary gland epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma, the benefit of adjuvant radiotherapy remains unclear.
statements.support_summary: The strongest direct evidence is a population-based SEER analysis of 246 salivary gland EMC cases showing no disease-specific survival benefit from adding radiotherapy to surgery (PMID: 26195572). Several retrospective series on myoepithelial carcinoma or EMC state that radiotherapy efficacy is unproven, appears limited/non-sensitive, or "needs to be investigated," with surgery emphasized as the main treatment (PMID: 22241311, PMID: 12775221, PMID: 26463034). Multiple case reports/reviews also note that there is no established standard treatment or consensus for EMC and that adjuvant radiotherapy is considered selectively for large tumors, positive margins, or aggressive histology rather than as clearly beneficial routine therapy (PMID: 37275099, PMID: 38841034, PMID: 30622928). Some supporting items are indirect or lower quality, and several involve myoepithelial carcinoma rather than epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma specifically, which limits applicability to the exact claim.
statements.contradict_summary: A systematic review of 91 maxillofacial/sinonasal EMC cases found adjuvant radiotherapy was associated with decreased local recurrence and suggested radiotherapy may improve disease-free survival in selected situations, providing the strongest contradiction (PMID: 35667947). One small retrospective series concluded EMC is sensitive to radiotherapy and chemotherapy to some extent and recommended surgery combined with other therapies, though this is based on limited data and the abstract does not isolate the effect of adjuvant radiotherapy (PMID: 16848178). Other contradictory items are mainly case reports stating patients with aggressive features should be considered for adjuvant radiotherapy or showing favorable outcomes after radiotherapy, but these are uncontrolled observations and do not establish general benefit (PMID: 31741932, PMID: 29237344, PMID: 37649950). Overall, the contradictory evidence suggests possible benefit in selected high-risk or unresectable cases, but much of it is lower-level evidence.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. The best direct comparative evidence shows no clear survival benefit from adjuvant radiotherapy in salivary gland EMC, while contradictory evidence mainly suggests possible local-control benefit in selected settings and is based largely on smaller series, a site-restricted review, or case reports.

source_article.title: Recovery of post-tetanic and train-of-four responses at the first dorsal interosseous and adductor pollicis muscles in patients receiving vecuronium.
source_article.abstract: PURPOSE: To compare recovery of accelographical responses to post-tetanic twitch (PTT) and train-of-four (TOF) stimuli obtained at the first dorsal interosseous muscle (DI) with those at the adductor pollicis muscle (AP) after administration of vecuronium 70 micrograms.kg-1. METHODS: Sixty adult patients were randomly assigned to one of four groups: PTT-DI (n = 15), PTT-AP (n = 15), TOF-DI (n = 15), or TOF-AP (n = 15) group. In PTT-DI and PTT-AP groups, responses to PTT were measured accelographically at the DI and at the AP, respectively. In TOF-DI and TOF-AP groups, responses to TOF were measured at the DI and at the AP, respectively. RESULTS: The T1/T0 (T0 = control) was greater in the TOF-DI than in TOF-AP group throughout recovery (P < 0.05), and the T4/T1 was greater in the TOF-DI than in TOF-AP group during the 30-40 min after vecuronium injection (P < 0.05). Time to the return of the first response to PTT (post-tetanic count(1), PTC1) was less in the PTT-DI than in the PTT-AP group (17.7 +/- 4.2 vs 21.7 +/- 5.6 min, mean +/- SD, P = 0.0341). The post-tetanic count PTC (number of single twitch stimuli in response to PTT) was greater in the PTT-DI than in the PTT-AP group during the 10-30 min after vecuronium (P < 0.05). Time to the return of T1 was less in the TOF-DI than in the TOF-AP group (23.1 +/- 6.0 vs 27.6 +/- 4.9 min, P = 0.0334). CONCLUSION: Recovery of responses to PTT and TOF stimuli occurred earlier at the DI than at the AP.

statements.text: In adult patients receiving vecuronium, recovery of post-tetanic responses occurred earlier at the first dorsal interosseous muscle than at the adductor pollicis muscle.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One electromyographic comparison study reported no clinically significant differences between the first dorsal interosseous and adductor pollicis muscles during atracurium or vecuronium blockade in onset time, maximum blockade, train-of-four ratio, or recovery index, arguing against earlier recovery at the first dorsal interosseous muscle (PMID: 2903759). However, the abstract does not explicitly mention post-tetanic response recovery, so the contradiction is indirect rather than perfectly matched to the claim.
statements.conclusion: The claim is more contradicted than supported because there is no supporting evidence here and one relevant study found no meaningful recovery differences between the muscles during vecuronium blockade (PMID: 2903759). Still, the evidence is limited because the available abstract does not specifically report post-tetanic response recovery.

statements.text: In adult patients receiving vecuronium, recovery of train-of-four responses occurred earlier at the first dorsal interosseous muscle than at the adductor pollicis muscle.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: One electromyographic comparison study found no clinically significant differences between the first dorsal interosseous and adductor pollicis muscles during atracurium or vecuronium blockade in onset time, maximum blockade, train-of-four ratio, or recovery index, directly opposing the claim that train-of-four recovery occurred earlier at the first dorsal interosseous muscle (PMID: 2903759). The abstract specifically includes vecuronium, making this contradiction relevant to the stated adult patient context.
statements.conclusion: The claim is more contradicted than supported. The only available study relevant to vecuronium reports no meaningful difference in train-of-four recovery between the first dorsal interosseous and adductor pollicis muscles, and there is no supporting evidence provided.

matched_article.title: Improved postoperative analgesia with preoperative piroxicam.
matched_article.abstract: Piroxicam like other Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory drugs can be used to provide postoperative analgesia. With a half-life of 50 hr given preoperatively its' analgesic effect should continue postoperatively. This study compared the effects of 20 mg piroxicam given at different times in the perioperative period on postoperative analgesic requirement. Following ethical committee approval and written informed consent, 60 ASA I and II patients presenting for inpatient gynaecological laparoscopic surgery were given either 20 mg piroxicam or a placebo po two hours preoperatively, immediately before induction of anaesthesia or one hour postoperatively in a randomised double bind manner. Postoperative Visual Analogue Pain Scores were lower on admission to the recovery ward in patients given piroxicam preoperatively (Group 1), than in the other two treatment groups (groups 2 and 3). Pain scores were 2.72 vs 4.25 vs 6.67 respectively (P < 0.001). Pain scores did not differ at any other times. Time to first analgesic request was greater in the group 1 than in the other two treatment groups; 141 (61) min vs 115 (147) in Group 2 and 30 (36) min in Group 3. Nine patients in Group 1 requested further analgesia compared with 15 in Group 2 and 16 in Group 3. There were no piroxicam-induced side-effects. Piroxicam given two hours preoperatively reduced pain scores, time to first analgesia and postoperative analgesic requirements compared with administration prior to induction or one hour postoperatively.

statements.text: In patients undergoing inpatient gynecological laparoscopic surgery, piroxicam given two hours before surgery reduced immediate postoperative pain compared with piroxicam given immediately before anesthesia induction or one hour after surgery or placebo.
statements.support_summary: One randomized, double-blind study in laparoscopic bilateral inguinal hernia repair found that sublingual piroxicam 40 mg given 2 hours before surgery led to lower postoperative pain scores at 6 and 20 hours and lower 30-hour tramadol consumption than piroxicam given shortly after surgery, supporting a preoperative timing advantage (PMID: 16492824). However, this study was not in gynecological laparoscopy, did not compare administration immediately before anesthesia induction, and did not include placebo in the same comparison, so it only indirectly supports the claim. The abstract also notes that pain scores were low in both groups and that the clinical relevance was uncertain (PMID: 16492824).
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is only weakly supported overall. The available evidence suggests preoperative piroxicam may be more effective than postoperative dosing, but it comes from a different surgical population and does not directly test all timing and placebo comparisons stated in the claim.

statements.text: In patients undergoing inpatient gynecological laparoscopic surgery, piroxicam given two hours before surgery delayed the first request for postoperative analgesia compared with piroxicam given immediately before anesthesia induction or one hour after surgery or placebo.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In patients undergoing inpatient gynecological laparoscopic surgery, piroxicam given two hours before surgery reduced overall postoperative analgesic requirements compared with piroxicam given immediately before anesthesia induction or one hour after surgery or placebo.
statements.support_summary: One randomized, double-blind study in laparoscopic bilateral inguinal hernia repair found that sublingual piroxicam given 2 hours preoperatively produced lower pain scores at 6 and 20 hours and lower cumulative tramadol use over 30 hours than piroxicam given shortly after surgery, supporting a preoperative timing advantage (PMID: 16492824). However, this study was not in gynecologic laparoscopy and did not compare against administration immediately before anesthesia induction or placebo, so it only partially matches the claim. The abstract also notes that pain scores were low in both groups, making the clinical relevance uncertain (PMID: 16492824).
statements.contradict_summary: No direct contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported overall. The available evidence is consistent with the idea that giving piroxicam before laparoscopic surgery can reduce later analgesic use compared with postoperative dosing, but it comes from a different surgical population and does not directly test all timing/placebo comparisons in the claim.

source_article.title: Evaluating traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) Jie Geng and Huang Qi combination on reducing surgical site infections in colorectal cancer surgeries: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
source_article.abstract: Postoperative wound infections (PWIs) pose a significant challenge in colorectal cancer surgeries, leading to prolonged hospital stays and increased morbidity. This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the efficacy of the traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) combination of Jie Geng and Huang Qi in reducing PWIs following colorectal cancer surgeries. Adhering to PRISMA guidelines, we focused on seven randomized controlled trials (RCTs) involving 1256 patients, examining the incidence of PWIs within 30 days post-surgery, alongside secondary outcomes such as length of hospital stay and antibiotic use. The analysis revealed a significant reduction in PWI incidence in the TCM-treated group compared to controls, with a Risk Ratio of 0.21 (95% CI: 0.14 to 0.30, p < 0.01), a notable decrease in hospital stay (Mean Difference: 1.2 days, 95% CI: 0.15 to 1.28 days, p < 0.01) and a significant reduction in antibiotic use (Risk Ratio: 0.24, 95% CI: 0.16 to 0.36, p < 0.01). These findings suggest that Jie Geng and Huang Qi in TCM could be an effective adjunct in postoperative care for colorectal cancer surgeries, underscoring the need for further high-quality RCTs to substantiate these results and explore the underlying mechanisms.

statements.text: In patients undergoing colorectal cancer surgery, the traditional Chinese medicine combination of Jie Geng and Huang Qi as an adjunct to postoperative care was associated with a lower risk of postoperative wound infection compared with control treatment.
statements.support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
statements.contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a retraction notice for a systematic review/meta-analysis on this exact intervention and outcome, stating the article was retracted because of a compromised peer review process and that its findings cannot be relied upon (PMID: 40258679). This does not directly show that Jie Geng plus Huang Qi increases wound infection risk; rather, it undermines confidence in previously published favorable evidence. Thus, the main theme is evidentiary unreliability rather than affirmative clinical contradiction.
statements.conclusion: The claim is not meaningfully supported by the provided evidence, but it is also only weakly contradicted. The sole available article is a retraction notice that chiefly invalidates prior positive evidence rather than demonstrating the treatment is ineffective or harmful.

statements.text: In patients undergoing colorectal cancer surgery, the traditional Chinese medicine combination of Jie Geng and Huang Qi as an adjunct to postoperative care was associated with a shorter postoperative hospital stay compared with control treatment.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: In patients undergoing colorectal cancer surgery, the traditional Chinese medicine combination of Jie Geng and Huang Qi as an adjunct to postoperative care was associated with reduced antibiotic use compared with control treatment.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Investigating low rates of compliance to graduated compression therapy for chronic venous insufficiency: A systematic review.
matched_article.abstract: Chronic venous insufficiency (CVI) is a chronic lower limb progressive disorder with significant burden. Graduated compression therapy is the gold-standard treatment, but its underutilisation, as indicated in recent literature, may be contributing to the growing burden of CVI. The aim of this systematic review is to determine the reasons for poor compliance in patients who are prescribed graduated compression therapy in the management of chronic venous insufficiency. A systematic review of the literature was conducted to identify the reasons for non-compliance in wearing graduated compression therapy in the management of chronic venous insufficiency. The keyword search was conducted through Medline, PubMed, CINAHL, Cochrane library, AMED, and Embase databases from 2000 to April 2023. Qualitative and quantitative studies were included with no study design or language limits imposed on the search. The study populations were restricted to adults aged over 18 years, diagnosed with chronic venous insufficiency. Of the 856 studies found, 80 full-text articles were reviewed, with 14 being eligible for the review. Due to the variability in study designs, the results were summarised rather than subjected to meta-analysis. There are five main overarching themes for non-compliance, which are physical limitations, health literacy, discomfort, financial issues, and psychosocial issues with emerging sub-themes. Graduated compression therapy has the potential to reduce the burden of chronic venous insufficiency if patients are more compliant with their prescription.

statements.text: In adults with chronic venous insufficiency prescribed graduated compression therapy, poor compliance is mainly driven by physical limitations, limited health literacy, discomfort, financial barriers, and psychosocial barriers.
statements.support_summary: Across observational studies and reviews, nonadherence to compression therapy in chronic venous disease/CVI is repeatedly linked to discomfort/heat/itching/pain, difficulty donning or applying garments, and cosmetic or restrictive burden, with examples from large cohorts in tropical and tertiary-care settings (PMID: 30189813; PMID: 17980798; PMID: 28705340). Several studies also support physical limitation as a barrier, especially in older adults, where hand strength, ability to reach the foot, and donning difficulty affected successful use, and assistive devices improved usability (PMID: 25579875; PMID: 26810342; PMID: 28961047). Limited understanding or inadequate comprehension of the need/benefits of compression and broader educational deficits were specifically reported as contributors to nonadherence or incorrect use (PMID: 34163533; PMID: 23806412; PMID: 28961047). Financial barriers were also directly identified in some cohorts, including one where cost was the most prevalent reason for nonadherence and another where high cost was a commonly reported drawback (PMID: 34163533; PMID: 23806412). Psychosocial barriers are supported more indirectly through reports of unattractive appearance, neglect, and an “intangible sense of restriction,” while some included reviews primarily discuss discomfort rather than the full set of claimed drivers, so support for all components is uneven (PMID: 30189813; PMID: 28705340; PMID: 17980798).
statements.contradict_summary: One randomized crossover study in women with early-stage chronic venous disease found very high short-term compliance (95%) and better tolerance with class 1 stockings, suggesting that poor compliance is not inevitable and may be less problematic with lower-pressure stockings in selected patients (PMID: 15153823). However, this abstract does not directly test whether physical limitations, health literacy, cost, discomfort, or psychosocial barriers are the main drivers of poor compliance when noncompliance occurs; it mainly shows a context in which compliance was high. Thus, this is a limited contradiction focused on a specific low-pressure, short-duration setting rather than a broad refutation of the claim.
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. Multiple studies and reviews consistently identify discomfort, application difficulty/physical limitation, inadequate understanding, and cost as important reasons for poor adherence, while evidence for psychosocial barriers is present but less direct, and the main contradictory study reflects a favorable early-stage, low-pressure context rather than negating these barriers generally.

statements.text: Improving patient compliance with prescribed graduated compression therapy in chronic venous insufficiency could reduce the burden of chronic venous insufficiency.
statements.support_summary: The supporting literature consistently shows that compression therapy is beneficial in chronic venous disease/CVI and venous ulcer populations, improving symptoms, edema, hemodynamics, quality of life, ulcer healing, or recurrence prevention when worn or compared with no compression (e.g., PMID: 34308565, PMID: 12563216, PMID: 15153823, PMID: 11060420, PMID: 18063393, PMID: 38451842). Several studies directly connect adherence/compliance to better outcomes: non-compliant patients had significantly higher venous ulcer recurrence in an RCT (PMID: 23078587), and reviews note that poor adherence contributes to impaired healing/recurrence while improved adherence appears to improve health outcomes in VLU/CVI populations (PMID: 34556978). A recurring theme is that adherence is often poor or declines over time because of discomfort, difficulty donning, heat, cost, pain, and poor understanding, suggesting substantial unrealized benefit if compliance improves (PMID: 28705340, PMID: 30189813, PMID: 34163533, PMID: 17980798). Multiple intervention studies suggest compliance can be improved by better fit, lower or tailored pressure, easier-to-don devices, customized stockings, progressive/modular systems, education, or alternative devices, with some also showing better symptom or QoL outcomes alongside improved usability/compliance (PMID: 25579875, PMID: 37708938, PMID: 22592040, PMID: 26810342, PMID: 28961047). Overall, much of the evidence is indirect because many abstracts demonstrate efficacy of compression and barriers to adherence rather than directly testing whether a compliance-improving intervention reduces overall CVI burden, but the pattern is broadly supportive.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The evidence base is broad and consistently shows that compression is effective and that poorer adherence is linked to worse outcomes or lost benefit, although relatively few studies directly test whether adherence-improving strategies reduce the overall burden of chronic venous insufficiency itself.

source_article.title: Effect of Narrative Nursing Combined with Thinking Map Health Education on Parturient Self-Efficacy and Neonatal Nursing Ability.
source_article.abstract: The motivation behind this study is to explore the influence of narrative nursing combined with thinking map health education on parturient self-efficacy and neonatal nursing ability. To verify this claim, we have selected 60 parturients who were treated in the hospital from February 2019 to April 2021. Moreover, these patients were randomly assigned to control and study groups. The former received narrative nursing, and the latter received narrative nursing combined with thinking map health education. The nursing satisfaction, maternal and neonatal nursing ability, maternal health knowledge awareness score, maternal self-efficacy, anxiety, and depression scores were compared. Initially, we have compared the nursing satisfaction: in the study group, 22 cases were satisfied, 8 cases were basically satisfied, and 0 cases were dissatisfied, and the satisfaction rate was 100.00%; in the control group, 12 cases were satisfied, 14 cases were basically satisfied, and 4 cases were dissatisfied, and the satisfaction rate was 86.67%. The nursing satisfaction in the study group was higher compared to the control group (P < 0.05). Secondly, we compared the mastery of neonatal nursing ability. The study group mastered the relevant knowledge of neonatal nursing in 23 cases, basically mastered 6 cases, and did not master 1 case, with a mastery rate of 96.67%. The control group mastered the knowledge of neonatal nursing in 12 cases, basically mastered 10 cases, and did not master 8 cases with a mastery rate of 73.33%. In the comparison between the two groups, the mastery of neonatal nursing ability in the study group was higher compared to the control group (P < 0.05). Considering the scores of maternal health knowledge, the scores of neonatal basic nursing, physiological characteristics, breastfeeding methods, environmental nursing knowledge, vaccination, and physical examination requirements in the study group were higher compared to the control group (P < 0.05). In the comparison of maternal self-efficacy, the total scores for promoting development, health care, safety, feeding, and self-efficacy in the study group were significantly higher compared to the control group (P < 0.05). Finally, we compared the scores for anxiety and depression. Before nursing, there exhibited no significant difference (P > 0.05). After nursing, the anxiety and depression scores decreased. Furthermore, the anxiety and depression scores of the study group were lower compared to the control group (P < 0.05). In the health education of primary parturient, the adoption of narrative nursing combined with thought guiding schema can enhance the level of nursing satisfaction and self-efficacy, strengthen the nursing ability of primiparas and the awareness rate of health knowledge, and reduce the occurrence of a maternal bad mood, which is more in line with the requirements of parturient.

statements.text: Among parturients receiving narrative nursing, adding thinking map health education improves nursing satisfaction compared with narrative nursing alone.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

statements.text: Among parturients receiving narrative nursing, adding thinking map health education improves maternal self-efficacy, maternal health knowledge awareness, and neonatal nursing ability compared with narrative nursing alone.
statements.support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

matched_article.title: Blockchain-Based Digital Twins Collaboration for Smart Pandemic Alerting: Decentralized COVID-19 Pandemic Alerting Use Case.
matched_article.abstract: Emerging technologies such as digital twins, blockchain, Internet of Things (IoT), and Artificial Intelligence (AI) play a vital role in driving the industrial revolution in all domains, including the healthcare sector. As a result of COVID-19 pandemic outbreak, there is a significant need for medical cyber-physical systems to adopt these emerging technologies to combat COVID-19 paramedic crisis. Also, acquiring secure real-time data exchange and analysis across multiple participants is essential to support the efforts against COVID-19. Therefore, we have introduced a blockchain-based collaborative digital twins framework for decentralized epidemic alerting to combat COVID-19 and any future pandemics. The framework has been proposed to bring together the existing advanced technologies (i.e., blockchain, digital twins, and AI) and then provide a solution to decentralize epidemic alerting to combat COVID-19 outbreaks. Also, we have described how the conceptual framework can be applied in the decentralized COVID-19 pandemic alerting use case.

statements.text: A blockchain-based collaborative digital twins framework can support decentralized epidemic alerting for COVID-19 and future pandemics.
statements.support_summary: Supportive evidence is largely indirect but consistently suggests that blockchain can enable decentralized, privacy-preserving, real-time pandemic surveillance, alerting, and collaboration across stakeholders. The strongest abstract-level support comes from a COVID-19 early-warning framework using blockchain and smart contracts that crowdsources surveillance, fuses distributed monitoring results, and reports advantages in decentralized decision-making and applicability to unknown future infectious diseases (PMID: 33846657). Additional conceptual or prototype systems describe decentralized blockchain-based pandemic detection/assistance with alerts and early warnings (PMID: 34664014), blockchain solutions for disease surveillance with real-time updates and reduced bias/delay (PMID: 36061978), and architectures combining blockchain with AI/big data for COVID-19 detection and decentralized stakeholder access (PMID: 39088543). Evidence touching digital twins is more limited and indirect: reviews/reference frameworks link personal digital twins with blockchain and AI and include COVID-19 contagion mitigation as a use case (PMID: 35957477), while broader reviews discuss personal digital twins alongside blockchain for pandemic response (PMID: 35782886).
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. There is therefore no direct abstract-level evidence here arguing that a blockchain-based collaborative digital twins framework cannot support decentralized epidemic alerting. However, many of the cited supportive papers are broad reviews, conceptual proposals, or blockchain-only systems rather than explicit collaborative digital-twin implementations, which limits how directly they support the exact claim.
statements.conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The evidence base includes one notably relevant blockchain-based collaborative early-warning framework for COVID-19 and several related decentralized surveillance/alerting architectures, but direct evidence specifically combining blockchain with collaborative digital twins for epidemic alerting remains limited.

statements.text: Combining blockchain, digital twins, and artificial intelligence can provide secure real-time data exchange and analysis across multiple participants for pandemic response.
statements.support_summary: Most supporting items are reviews, perspectives, or conceptual frameworks suggesting that blockchain and AI can help pandemic response by enabling decentralized, secure data sharing, privacy protection, surveillance, contact tracing, telemedicine, and predictive analytics, but many do not mention digital twins at all (e.g., PMID: 34664014, PMID: 37835793, PMID: 34295908, PMID: 36506475). A smaller subset more directly aligns with the full triad in the claim: a personal digital twin framework explicitly brings together digital twins, blockchain, and AI for smart personalized healthcare and includes COVID-19 mitigation and follow-up use cases (PMID: 35957477), while another review discusses IoT, AI, blockchain, and personal digital twins together for COVID-19 management (PMID: 35782886). Several abstracts specifically support secure or decentralized multi-stakeholder exchange and analysis during the pandemic using blockchain plus AI/big data, such as stakeholder-accessible decentralized repositories and secure architecture for COVID-19 analysis (PMID: 39088543), privacy-preserving blockchain-based pandemic detection with AI/ML alerts (PMID: 34664014), and blockchain-enabled secure sharing of epidemiologic or patient data (PMID: 34295908, PMID: 34511692). However, there is a recurring discrepancy that many articles were categorized as support despite only covering blockchain with AI, IoT, or big data rather than the stated combination including digital twins. Overall, the evidence supports the plausibility of the claim more than direct empirical demonstration, with limited abstracts explicitly showing all three technologies combined for real-time secure exchange across multiple participants.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because multiple abstracts describe blockchain- and AI-enabled secure, decentralized, real-time or near-real-time pandemic data sharing and analysis, and a few explicitly connect these capabilities with digital twins in COVID-19-related healthcare contexts. However, the support is mostly conceptual and indirect rather than strong empirical proof of a fully implemented multi-participant blockchain-digital twin-AI pandemic response system.

source_article.title: SIRT3 regulates cell proliferation and apoptosis related to energy metabolism in non-small cell lung cancer cells through deacetylation of NMNAT2.
source_article.abstract: Lung cancer is the leading cause of death worldwide and associated with dismal prognoses. As a major mitochondrial deacetylase, SIRT3 regulates the activity of enzymes to coordinate global shifts in cellular metabolism and has important implications for tumor growth. Its role as a tumor suppressor or an oncogene in lung cancer is unclear, especially in non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC). To identify the mechanism of SIRT3-interacting proteins, we performed a yeast two-hybrid screen using a human lung cDNA library. One of the positive clones encoded the full-length cDNA of the nicotinamide mononucleotide adenylyltransferase 2 (NMNAT2) gene and the interaction between SIRT3 and NMNAT2 was identified. The interaction on growth, proliferation, apoptosis of NSCLC cell lines, and energy metabolism related to SIRT3 were investigated. Screening from the library resulted in NMNAT2 gene. We found that NMNAT2 interacts with SIRT3 both in vitro and in vivo; SIRT3 binds to NMNAT2 deacetylating it. Downregulation of SIRT3 inhibited acetylation of NMNAT2 and NAD+ synthesis activity of the enzyme. Low expression of SIRT3 significantly inhibited mitotic entry, growth and proliferation of NSCLC cell lines and promoted apoptosis, which was related to energy metabolism involving in the interaction between SIRT3 and NMNAT2. Taken together, our results strongly suggest that the binding of SIRT3 with NMNAT2 is a novel regulator of cell proliferation and apoptosis in NSCLC cell lines, implicating the interaction between SIRT3 and NMNAT2, energy metabolism associated with SIRT3.

statements.text: In non-small cell lung cancer cells, SIRT3 promotes cell proliferation and inhibits apoptosis through regulation of energy metabolism.
statements.support_summary: Several NSCLC-focused studies suggest an oncogenic role for SIRT3, but support for the full claim is only partial. In patient samples and NSCLC cell lines, SIRT3 was upregulated, correlated with Ki-67 and p-Akt, associated with worse survival, and experimentally activated Akt signaling, supporting a pro-malignancy/proliferation role in NSCLC (PMID: 28197634). Mechanistic NSCLC evidence also shows CRIF1 promoted proliferation and inhibited mitochondrial apoptosis while acting through SIRT3-dependent deacetylation of PYCR1, linking SIRT3 to metabolic enzyme regulation, although this does not isolate SIRT3 as the sole driver (PMID: 35716330). More indirect support comes from broader cancer or review papers describing SIRT3 as a regulator of mitochondrial metabolism, proline metabolism, and NSCLC-related energy metabolism/apoptosis pathways, but these are not specific demonstrations of SIRT3 promoting proliferation and inhibiting apoptosis in NSCLC cells (PMID: 31108370, PMID: 22749020, PMID: 27686535, PMID: 39092574).
statements.contradict_summary: Direct contradictory evidence exists in lung cancer models showing tumor-suppressive effects of SIRT3. In lung adenocarcinoma/A549 cells, SIRT3 was downregulated in tumors, and its overexpression inhibited growth and induced apoptosis via Bax/Bcl-2-family changes, AIF translocation, and p53/p21 upregulation, directly opposing the claim in an NSCLC subtype (PMID: 23842789). Another NSCLC study reported that aspirin upregulated SIRT3 and that AMPK/SIRT3 signaling inhibited glycolysis and proliferation, indicating that SIRT3 can suppress NSCLC growth through metabolic regulation rather than promote it (PMID: 35981603). Additional contradiction comes from a lung cancer cisplatin-resistance study where SIRT3 overexpression reduced viability/proliferation and increased apoptosis-related signaling, although the abstract is not explicit about NSCLC subtype and focuses on drug response (PMID: 33655712); studies in small-cell lung cancer or other cancers also show SIRT3 promoting apoptosis and inhibiting proliferation, but these are less directly relevant to the NSCLC-specific claim (PMID: 31894331, PMID: 33231084, PMID: 25227106).
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. Although some NSCLC evidence links SIRT3 to malignancy and metabolic enzyme regulation, more direct functional studies in lung adenocarcinoma/NSCLC report that increasing SIRT3 suppresses proliferation and promotes apoptosis, including through metabolic pathways.

statements.text: In non-small cell lung cancer cells, SIRT3 interacts with NMNAT2 and deacetylates NMNAT2, which supports NMNAT2 activity involved in NAD+ synthesis.
statements.support_summary: The only cited supporting study is a retracted paper reporting that SIRT3 regulates non-small cell lung cancer cell proliferation and apoptosis through deacetylation of NMNAT2 (PMID: 37026521). Based on the available abstract text, this article is primarily a retraction notice describing image/data concerns rather than providing reliable experimental findings, so it offers little trustworthy support for the claimed SIRT3-NMNAT2 interaction, deacetylation, or effects on NMNAT2 activity in NAD+ synthesis. Thus, although the title aligns with the claim, the abstract itself creates a major reliability conflict.
statements.contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
statements.conclusion: The claim is not meaningfully established by the provided evidence. While the title of PMID: 37026521 appears supportive, the paper has been retracted and the abstract supplied is a retraction notice, so the overall evidence here is too weak and unreliable to conclude support.

statements.text: Reduced SIRT3 expression in non-small cell lung cancer cells inhibits growth and mitotic entry while increasing apoptosis.
statements.support_summary: One lung adenocarcinoma study reported that SIRT3 is downregulated in tumors versus adjacent tissue, but experimentally overexpressing SIRT3 in A549 cells inhibited growth and induced apoptosis, implying that reduced SIRT3 would be expected to have the opposite effect; this makes the item discrepant with the claim despite its placement in the support set (PMID: 23842789). Another study in glioma and breast cancer cells under hypoxia found that silencing Sirt3 promoted apoptosis, but it did not study NSCLC growth or mitotic entry, so it provides only indirect, non-NSCLC evidence for the apoptosis component (PMID: 27270321). Overall, the support set contains weak and partly miscategorized evidence, with little direct abstract-level support that reduced SIRT3 in NSCLC inhibits growth or mitotic entry.
statements.contradict_summary: Several NSCLC-focused studies suggest SIRT3 has pro-survival or context-dependent roles opposite to the claim. In NSCLC tissues, SIRT3 was reported to be increased, correlated with Ki-67, p-Akt, and shorter survival, and siRNA-mediated downregulation decreased Akt activation, indicating SIRT3 may promote malignancy signaling, though the abstract does not directly measure apoptosis or mitotic entry (PMID: 28197634). Other lung cancer/NSCLC studies found that low SIRT3 was associated with enhanced proliferation or resistance phenotypes, while SIRT3 overexpression reduced gefitinib resistance and ROS in PC-9/A549 cells (PMID: 37183639) and overexpressed SIRT3 inhibited viability/proliferation with SIRT3 depletion increasing Ki-67 and lowering cleaved caspase-3 in lung cancer models (PMID: 33655712). Mechanistic NSCLC work also linked CRIF1-driven progression to SIRT3-mediated PYCR1 deacetylation, consistent with SIRT3 participating in tumor-promoting pathways in that context (PMID: 35716330). A broader review further states that loss of SIRT3 can support proliferation and tumorigenesis via ROS-mediated metabolic reprogramming, though this is indirect and not NSCLC-specific (PMID: 22749020).
statements.conclusion: The claim is overall more contradicted than supported. Direct NSCLC/lung cancer evidence in the abstracts more often indicates that SIRT3 loss is associated with increased proliferation/survival or that SIRT3 overexpression suppresses growth, while the nominal support evidence is largely indirect or internally inconsistent with the claim.

matched_article.title: Human hepatocellular carcinoma cell-specific miRNAs reveal the differential expression of miR-24 and miR-27a in cirrhotic/non-cirrhotic HCC.
matched_article.abstract: microRNAs (miRs) are 18-25 nucleotide non-coding RNAs that regulate gene expression in several physiological and pathological conditions. To gather more knowledge on microRNAs in human hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) we generated a small RNA library in the human HCC cell line HA22T/VGH by cloning and sequencing the cDNA obtained following the size selection of 18-24 nucleotide RNAs. We determined the expression levels of the most frequently cloned microRNAs by qPCR in HCC tissues and in their peritumoral counterparts from biopsy specimens of 41 HCC patients. The most frequently cloned miRs were miR-24, miR-27a and miR-21, and their expression levels in human HCC tissues indicate that these miRs were dysregulated in HCC. We showed that miR-24 and miR-27a were significantly downregulated in HCCs from cirrhotic liver tissues in comparison to those from non-cirrhotic liver tissues. In cirrhotic HCCs the downregulation of miR-24 was correlated with poorer prognosis in patients with HBV and HCV virus infections. miR-21 was generally upregulated in HCC tissues versus the corresponding peritumoral tissues, particularly in non-cirrhotic HCC. Furthermore, by sequence alignment we identified the human miR orthologue of Mus musculus miR-1199 not yet annotated. Our results outline the differential expression of miRs in cirrhotic and non-cirrhotic HCCs, thereby contributing to advances in the discovery and validation of novel molecular biomarkers of HCC progression.

statements.text: In human hepatocellular carcinoma tissues, miR-24, miR-27a, and miR-21 are dysregulated compared with corresponding peritumoral liver tissues.
statements.support_summary: Several studies support dysregulation of at least parts of the claimed miRNA set in HCC versus non-tumor liver. miR-21 was reported as significantly upregulated in HCC tissue compared with paired adjacent nontumor/non-tumor/benign liver in multiple cohorts, including studies using paired adjacent tissues (PMID: 30191729, PMID: 26261620, PMID: 18223217). miR-27a was described as frequently upregulated in HCC tissues and cell lines in HBV-related HCC, although the abstract does not clearly state comparison with paired peritumoral tissue (PMID: 23621256). One profiling study found miR-24 and miR-27a among miRNAs upregulated in HCC samples versus normal liver, but this was against normal rather than corresponding peritumoral tissue and did not address miR-21 in the abstract (PMID: 25811030). Overall, the abstracts consistently support dysregulation of miR-21 and provide partial/indirect support for miR-27a and miR-24, with a discrepancy that several studies use adjacent nontumor or normal liver rather than explicitly corresponding peritumoral liver.
statements.contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
statements.conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support is strongest for miR-21 upregulation in HCC relative to adjacent non-tumor liver, while evidence for miR-27a and especially miR-24 is more indirect because some abstracts do not use paired peritumoral tissue comparisons or do not assess all three miRNAs together.

statements.text: In hepatocellular carcinoma arising in cirrhotic liver tissue, miR-24 and miR-27a are lower than in hepatocellular carcinoma arising in non-cirrhotic liver tissue.

statements.contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
statements.conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.
